docs.title: NEET PG Study Materials - Complete Guide
docs.text: Comprehensive, high-yield study materials for NEET PG 2026 preparation covering all major subjects.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This repository contains topic-wise study materials designed specifically for NEET PG preparation. Each topic includes:

  • Comprehensive content with diagrams (ASCII format)
  • High-yield NEET PG points
  • Clinical correlations
  • Memory aids and mnemonics
  • Practice MCQs
  • Revision checklists
  • Difficulty ratings and weightage indicators

docs.title: How to Use These Materials
docs.text:
1. Start with high-yield topics (marked with ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐)
  2. Read through each topic systematically
  3. Make notes of memory aids and mnemonics
  4. Attempt practice MCQs after each topic
  5. Use revision checklists to track progress
  6. Revise diagrams by drawing them from memory
  7. Focus on NEET PG High-Yield points (specially marked)

docs.title: 01. Cranial Nerves
docs.text:
File: Anatomy/01_Cranial_Nerves.md

Topics Covered: - All 12 cranial nerves (detailed) - Sensory/Motor/Mixed classification - Exit foramina (high-yield) - Clinical testing methods - UMN vs LMN facial palsy - Reflex arcs - Visual field defects - Cranial nerve syndromes

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (3-5 questions)

docs.title: 02. Brachial Plexus
docs.text:
File: Anatomy/02_Brachial_Plexus.md

Topics Covered: - Roots, Trunks, Divisions, Cords, Branches - Terminal branches (Median, Ulnar, Radial, Axillary, Musculocutaneous) - Nerve injuries and clinical signs - Erb's vs Klumpke's palsy - Carpal tunnel syndrome

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (2-4 questions)

docs.title: 03. Blood Supply of GI Tract
docs.text:
File: Anatomy/03_GI_Blood_Supply.md

Topics Covered: - Celiac trunk, SMA, IMA branches - Foregut, midgut, hindgut blood supply - Collateral circulation - Portosystemic anastomoses - Calot's triangle

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: High (2-3 questions)

docs.title: 01. Cardiac Cycle
docs.text:
File: Cardiac_Cycle_Study_Material.md

Topics Covered: - 7 phases of cardiac cycle - Pressure-volume changes - Wiggers diagram - Heart sounds (S1, S2, S3, S4) - Frank-Starling mechanism - JVP waves

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: High (2-3 questions)

docs.title: 02. Renal Physiology
docs.text:
File: Physiology/01_Renal_Physiology.md

Topics Covered: - GFR and Starling forces - Tubular function (PCT, Loop, DCT, CD) - Countercurrent mechanism - Hormonal regulation (ADH, Aldosterone, RAAS) - Clearance concepts - Diuretics

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: 01. Cell Injury & Adaptation
docs.text:
File: Pathology/01_Cell_Injury_and_Adaptation.md

Topics Covered: - Cellular adaptations (hypertrophy, hyperplasia, atrophy, metaplasia, dysplasia) - Reversible vs irreversible injury - Types of necrosis (6 types) - Apoptosis vs necrosis - Intracellular accumulations - Calcifications

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (3-5 questions)

docs.title: 01. Antimicrobial Agents
docs.text:
File: Pharmacology/01_Antimicrobials.md

Topics Covered: - Beta-lactams (Penicillins, Cephalosporins, Carbapenems, Monobactams) - Glycopeptides (Vancomycin) - Aminoglycosides - Macrolides, Tetracyclines, Fluoroquinolones - Antitubercular drugs - Antifungal drugs

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: 01. Diabetes Mellitus
docs.text:
File: Medicine/01_Diabetes_Mellitus.md

Topics Covered: - Type 1 vs Type 2 diabetes - Diagnostic criteria (HbA1c, FPG, OGTT) - DKA vs HHS - Microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) - Treatment algorithms - Target goals

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: 01. Thyroid Disorders
docs.text:
File: Surgery/01_Thyroid_Disorders.md

Topics Covered: - Thyroid anatomy (surgical importance) - Goiter (simple, multinodular) - Hyperthyroidism (Graves' disease) - Thyroid storm - Thyroid cancer (all types) - Surgical complications

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: 01. Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy
docs.text:
File: ObsGyn/01_Hypertensive_Disorders_of_Pregnancy.md

Topics Covered: - Pre-eclampsia (diagnostic criteria, severe features) - Eclampsia - HELLP syndrome - MgSO4 dosing and monitoring - Antihypertensives in pregnancy - Delivery timing - Prevention (aspirin prophylaxis)

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: 01. Growth & Development
docs.text:
File: Pediatrics/01_Growth_and_Development.md

Topics Covered: - Weight milestones (double, triple, quadruple) - Height/Length milestones - Head circumference - Dentition - Gross motor & fine motor milestones - Language & social development - Primitive reflexes - Immunization schedule (IAP 2023-24)

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Weightage: Very High (5-8 questions)

docs.title: Phase 1: Foundation (Months 1-3)
docs.text:
• Complete all high-yield topics
  • Make comprehensive notes
  • Master diagrams and flowcharts
  • Learn all mnemonics

docs.title: Phase 2: Consolidation (Months 4-6)
docs.text:
• Revise all topics (2nd reading)
  • Solve topic-wise MCQs
  • Create quick revision notes
  • Focus on weak areas

docs.title: Phase 3: Practice (Months 7-9)
docs.text:
• Solve previous year questions
  • Take mock tests
  • Identify pattern of questions
  • Time management practice

docs.title: Phase 4: Revision (Months 10-12)
docs.text:
• Quick revision of all topics
  • Review high-yield points only
  • Solve grand tests
  • Last-minute mnemonics

docs.title: Physiology
docs.text:
• GFR: 125 ml/min
  • Filtration fraction: 20%
  • Cardiac output: HR × SV
  • Ejection fraction: (SV/EDV) × 100 = 55-65%

docs.title: Pediatrics
docs.text:
• Teeth formula: Age (months) - 6
  • Tower of cubes: Age (years) × 3
  • Weight doubles: 5 months, triples: 12 months, quadruples: 24 months

docs.title: Medicine
docs.text:
• Corrected Na+ in DKA: Measured Na+ + 1.6 × [(Glucose-100)/100]
  • Anion gap: Na+ - (Cl- + HCO3-) = 8-12

docs.title: Tips for NEET PG Success
docs.text:
1. Consistency: Study daily (6-8 hours minimum)
  2. Active Recall: Test yourself regularly
  3. Spaced Repetition: Revise at intervals
  4. MCQ Practice: Minimum 50 MCQs daily
  5. Mock Tests: Weekly full-length tests
  6. Error Analysis: Review all mistakes
  7. Time Management: Practice solving in time
  8. Stay Healthy: Sleep 7-8 hours, exercise
  9. Stay Updated: Follow NEET PG pattern changes
  10. Stay Motivated: Remember your goal

docs.title: Version History
docs.text:
Version 1.0 (May 2026) - Initial release - 10 comprehensive topics across all subjects - High-yield content with diagrams - Practice MCQs included - Memory aids and mnemonics

Best of luck for NEET PG 2026! 🎯

Remember: Success in NEET PG requires dedication, consistency, and smart work. Use these materials wisely, revise regularly, and stay focused on your goal.

Study Smart. Stay Consistent. Achieve Your Dreams. ✨

docs.title: CRANIAL NERVES - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: 12 pairs of cranial nerves arising from the brain Mnemonic for names: "**O**h **O**h **O**h **T**o **T**ouch **A**nd **F**eel **V**ery **G**ood **V**elvet **A**h **H**eaven"

docs.title: Sensory Only (3)
docs.text:
1. I - Olfactory
  2. II - Optic
  3. VIII - Vestibulocochlear

docs.title: Motor Only (5)
docs.text:
1. III - Oculomotor
  2. IV - Trochlear
  3. VI - Abducens
  4. XI - Accessory
  5. XII - Hypoglossal

Mnemonic: "**S**ome **S**ay **M**arry **M**oney **B**ut **M**y **B**rother **S**ays **B**ig **B**rains **M**atter **M**ore" (S=Sensory, M=Motor, B=Both)

docs.title: Mixed (4)
docs.text:
1. V - Trigeminal
  2. VII - Facial
  3. IX - Glossopharyngeal
  4. X - Vagus

docs.title: CN I - OLFACTORY (Sensory)
docs.text: Origin: Olfactory epithelium → Cribriform plate → Olfactory bulb Function: Smell Testing: Identify odors (coffee, vanilla) Lesion: Anosmia Causes: Head trauma, meningioma, COVID-19

docs.title: CN II - OPTIC (Sensory)
docs.text:
Origin: Retina → Optic chiasma → Lateral geniculate body Function: Vision Testing: Visual acuity, visual fields, fundoscopy

Visual Field Defects - HIGH YIELD 

Lesion Site          |  Visual Field Defect
---------------------|-------------------------
Optic nerve          |  Ipsilateral blindness
Optic chiasma        |  Bitemporal hemianopia
Optic tract          |  Contralateral homonymous hemianopia
Optic radiation      |  Quadrantanopia
Occipital cortex     |  Macular sparing hemianopia

NEET PG Favorite: Bitemporal hemianopia = Pituitary tumor

docs.title: CN III - OCULOMOTOR (Motor + Parasympathetic)
docs.text:
Origin: Midbrain (at superior colliculus level) Exit: Superior orbital fissure

Functions: - Motor: All extraocular muscles EXCEPT SO & LR - Superior rectus (SR) - Inferior rectus (IR) - Medial rectus (MR) - Inferior oblique (IO) - Levator palpebrae superioris (LPS) - Parasympathetic: Sphincter pupillae, ciliary muscle

Testing: H-pattern eye movements, pupillary reflex

Lesion - Complete CN III Palsy: - Ptosis (LPS paralysis) - Eye "down and out" (SO + LR intact) - Dilated pupil (unopposed sympathetic) - Loss of accommodation

Causes: Posterior communicating artery aneurysm (pupil involved), Diabetes (pupil spared - medical CN III palsy)

NEET PG Pearl: Pupil involvement → Surgical cause (aneurysm)

docs.title: CN IV - TROCHLEAR (Motor)
docs.text:
Origin: Dorsal midbrain (only nerve from dorsal aspect) Exit: Superior orbital fissure Function: Superior oblique (SO) muscle Action: Depression + Intorsion (when eye adducted)

Testing: Ask patient to look down and in (reading position)

Lesion: - Diplopia on looking down - Head tilt to opposite side (compensatory) - Cannot descend stairs

NEET PG: Longest intracranial course, most commonly injured in head trauma

docs.title: CN V - TRIGEMINAL (Mixed)
docs.text:
Origin: Pons Ganglion: Trigeminal (Gasserian) ganglion in Meckel's cave

Three Divisions:

docs.title: V1 - Ophthalmic (Sensory)
docs.text:
• Exit: Superior orbital fissure
  • Supply: Forehead, upper eyelid, cornea, nose tip
  • Branch: Nasociliary → Long ciliary → Corneal reflex (afferent)

docs.title: V2 - Maxillary (Sensory)
docs.text:
• Exit: Foramen rotundum
  • Supply: Lower eyelid, cheek, upper lip, upper teeth, maxillary sinus

docs.title: V3 - Mandibular (Mixed)
docs.text:
• Exit: Foramen ovale
  • Sensory: Lower lip, chin, lower teeth, anterior ⅔ tongue (general sensation)
  • Motor: Muscles of mastication (masseter, temporalis, pterygoids)

Testing: - Sensory: Touch, pain, temperature on face - Motor: Clench teeth (masseter), jaw jerk reflex - Corneal reflex: V1 afferent, VII efferent

Lesion: - Trigeminal neuralgia (Tic douloureux) - Jaw deviates to SAME side (unopposed opposite pterygoid)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Largest cranial nerve - Corneal reflex: Afferent = V1, Efferent = VII - Trigeminal neuralgia: Carbamazepine first-line

docs.title: CN VI - ABDUCENS (Motor)
docs.text:
Origin: Pontomedullary junction Exit: Superior orbital fissure Course: Longest intradural course Function: Lateral rectus (LR) - ABduction

Lesion: - Cannot abduct eye - Diplopia on lateral gaze - False localizing sign in raised ICP

NEET PG: Most commonly affected in raised ICP

docs.title: CN VII - FACIAL (Mixed)
docs.text:
Origin: Pontomedullary junction Exit: Internal acoustic meatus → Facial canal → Stylomastoid foramen

Functions: - Motor: Muscles of facial expression - Sensory: Taste from anterior ⅔ tongue (chorda tympani) - Parasympathetic: Lacrimal, submandibular, sublingual glands - Special: Stapedius muscle (dampens sound)

Branches - "**T**o **Z**anzibar **B**y **M**otor **C**ar" - Temporal - Zygomatic - Buccal - Marginal mandibular - Cervical

Testing: - Raise eyebrows, close eyes tight, show teeth, puff cheeks - Taste on anterior ⅔ tongue

Lesions:

Feature UMN (Stroke) LMN (Bell's palsy) Forehead Spared Affected Eye closure Present Absent (Bell's phenomenon) Nasolabial fold Lost Lost Taste Normal Lost if proximal to chorda tympani Hyperacusis Absent Present (stapedius paralysis)

NEET PG High-Yield: - UMN lesion: Forehead spared (bilateral cortical supply) - LMN lesion: Entire half of face affected - Bell's palsy: Idiopathic LMN facial palsy, treat with steroids + acyclovir - Ramsay Hunt syndrome: VZV reactivation, facial palsy + vesicles in ear

docs.title: CN VIII - VESTIBULOCOCHLEAR (Sensory)
docs.text: Origin: Inner ear Exit: Internal acoustic meatus Two Parts:

docs.title: Cochlear (Hearing)
docs.text:
• Testing: Weber, Rinne tests
  • Weber: Tuning fork on forehead
  • Conductive loss: Lateralizes to affected ear
  • Sensorineural loss: Lateralizes to normal ear
  • Rinne: Air conduction vs Bone conduction
  • Normal/SNHL: AC > BC (positive)
  • Conductive: BC > AC (negative)

docs.title: Vestibular (Balance)
docs.text:
• Testing: Caloric test, Romberg test, nystagmus
  • Lesion: Vertigo, nystagmus, ataxia

NEET PG High-Yield: - Acoustic neuroma: Schwannoma at cerebellopontine angle - Presents with: Hearing loss, tinnitus, facial numbness (V), facial palsy (VII) - MRI: Ice cream cone appearance

docs.title: CN IX - GLOSSOPHARYNGEAL (Mixed)
docs.text:
Origin: Medulla (posterolateral sulcus) Exit: Jugular foramen

Functions: - Motor: Stylopharyngeus muscle - Sensory: Posterior ⅓ tongue (taste + general sensation), pharynx, middle ear - Parasympathetic: Parotid gland (via otic ganglion) - Special: Carotid body & sinus (chemoreceptor & baroreceptor)

Testing: Gag reflex (afferent), taste on posterior ⅓ tongue

Lesion: Loss of gag reflex, loss of taste posterior tongue

NEET PG: Jacobson's nerve (tympanic branch) → Tympanic plexus → Lesser petrosal → Otic ganglion → Parotid

docs.title: CN X - VAGUS (Mixed)
docs.text:
Origin: Medulla Exit: Jugular foramen Course: Longest cranial nerve (extends to abdomen)

Functions: - Motor: All muscles of pharynx (except stylopharyngeus) & larynx (except cricothyroid) - Sensory: Larynx, pharynx, thoracic & abdominal viscera - Parasympathetic: Heart, lungs, GI tract up to splenic flexure

Branches: - Pharyngeal - Superior laryngeal (SLN): External (motor to cricothyroid) + Internal (sensory above vocal cords) - Recurrent laryngeal (RLN): All intrinsic laryngeal muscles except cricothyroid, sensory below vocal cords

Testing: - Say "Ahhh" - Uvula deviates to NORMAL side - Cough reflex, gag reflex (efferent)

Lesion: - Unilateral: Hoarseness, uvula deviates to normal side - Bilateral: Aphonia, stridor, dysphagia

RLN Injury - NEET PG Favorite: - Unilateral: Hoarseness, vocal cord in paramedian position - Bilateral: Respiratory distress (emergency tracheostomy) - Causes: Thyroid surgery, lung cancer, aortic aneurysm - Left RLN more commonly injured (longer course around aortic arch)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Gag reflex: IX afferent, X efferent - SLN injury: Cannot increase pitch (cricothyroid) - RLN injury: Cannot abduct vocal cords (posterior cricoarytenoid)

docs.title: CN XI - ACCESSORY (Motor)
docs.text:
Origin: - Cranial part: Medulla - Spinal part: C1-C5 spinal cord

Exit: Jugular foramen Function: Sternocleidomastoid (SCM) + Trapezius

Testing: - Turn head against resistance (SCM) - Shrug shoulders (trapezius)

Lesion: - Cannot turn head to opposite side - Shoulder droop on same side - Iatrogenic injury: Lymph node biopsy in posterior triangle

NEET PG: SCM turns head to OPPOSITE side

docs.title: CN XII - HYPOGLOSSAL (Motor)
docs.text:
Origin: Medulla Exit: Hypoglossal canal Function: All intrinsic + extrinsic muscles of tongue (except palatoglossus - X)

Testing: Stick out tongue, move side to side

Lesion: - Tongue deviates to SAME side (unopposed opposite genioglossus) - Unilateral: Deviation - Bilateral: Tongue paralysis, dysphagia, dysarthria

NEET PG: Tongue deviates toward the lesion

docs.title: Midbrain (3, 4)
docs.text:
• CN III: Oculomotor nucleus, Edinger-Westphal nucleus
  • CN IV: Trochlear nucleus

docs.title: Pons (5, 6, 7, 8)
docs.text:
• CN V: Motor + Sensory nuclei
  • CN VI: Abducens nucleus
  • CN VII: Facial nucleus, Superior salivatory nucleus
  • CN VIII: Cochlear + Vestibular nuclei

docs.title: Medulla (9, 10, 12) + Spinal cord (11)
docs.text:
• CN IX: Nucleus ambiguus, Inferior salivatory nucleus
  • CN X: Nucleus ambiguus, Dorsal motor nucleus
  • CN XI: Accessory nucleus (C1-C5)
  • CN XII: Hypoglossal nucleus

docs.title: Exit Foramina - HIGH YIELD
docs.text: Foramen Nerves Mnemonic Cribriform plate I - Optic canal II Optic canal: II Superior orbital fissure III, IV, V1, VI **L**azy **F**rench **T**rol **S**it **N**aked **I**n **A**nticipation (Lacrimal, Frontal, Trochlear, Superior div III, Nasociliary, Inferior div III, Abducens) Foramen rotundum V2 R**otundum = **R**ound = **2 Foramen ovale V3 O**vale = **O**pen mouth = mandible = **3 Foramen spinosum Middle meningeal artery - Internal acoustic meatus VII, VIII 7 + 8 = 15 (honeymoon suite) Jugular foramen IX, X, XI 9, 10, 11 = consecutive Hypoglossal canal XII -

docs.title: 1. Cavernous Sinus Syndrome
docs.text: Affected: III, IV, V1, V2, VI Cause: Cavernous sinus thrombosis, ICA aneurysm Features: Ophthalmoplegia, facial pain/numbness

docs.title: 2. Cerebellopontine Angle (CPA) Syndrome
docs.text: Affected: V, VII, VIII Cause: Acoustic neuroma Features: Hearing loss → facial numbness → facial palsy

docs.title: 3. Jugular Foramen Syndrome (Vernet)
docs.text: Affected: IX, X, XI Features: Loss of gag, vocal cord palsy, trapezius weakness

docs.title: 4. Posterior Condylar Syndrome (Collet-Sicard)
docs.text: Affected: IX, X, XI, XII Features: Vernet + tongue deviation

docs.title: 5. Weber Syndrome (Midbrain)
docs.text: Affected: III + corticospinal tract Features: Ipsilateral CN III palsy + Contralateral hemiplegia

docs.title: 6. Millard-Gubler Syndrome (Pons)
docs.text: Affected: VI, VII + corticospinal tract Features: Ipsilateral facial palsy + lateral rectus palsy + Contralateral hemiplegia

docs.title: 7. Wallenberg Syndrome (Lateral Medullary)
docs.text: Affected: IX, X + spinothalamic + sympathetic Features: Dysphagia, hoarseness, Horner's, loss of pain/temp opposite body, ataxia

docs.title: Reflexes - Must Know
docs.text: Reflex Afferent Efferent Response Corneal V1 VII Eye blink Gag IX X Pharynx contracts Jaw jerk V3 V3 Jaw closes Light (Direct) II III Pupil constricts (same side) Light (Consensual) II III Pupil constricts (opposite) Accommodation II III Pupils constrict, convergence

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Which nerve passes through foramen rotundum? Answer: Maxillary nerve (V2)

  2. Tongue deviates to which side in hypoglossal nerve palsy? Answer: Same side as lesion

  3. In Bell's palsy, which muscle paralysis causes hyperacusis? Answer: Stapedius

  4. Afferent limb of corneal reflex? Answer: Ophthalmic nerve (V1)

  5. Which is the longest cranial nerve? Answer: Vagus (X)

  6. Pupil sparing in CN III palsy suggests? Answer: Medical cause (Diabetes)

docs.title: Memory Palace Technique
docs.text:
Imagine walking through your house:

  1. Door (I) - Smell flowers
  2. Window (II) - Look outside
  3. Three lights (III) - Moving up, down, medially
  4. Four-poster bed (IV) - Look down at pillow
  5. Five fingers touching Face (V)
  6. Six-pack looking Lateral (VI)
  7. Seven dwarfs making Facial expressions (VII)
  8. Eight-ball (VIII) - Hear it crack
  9. Nine lives of cat, Tasting food (IX)
  10. Ten commandments (X) - Vagabond travels far
  11. Eleven o'clock, Shoulder shrug time (XI)
  12. Twelve noon, Tongue out panting (XII)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Memorize all 12 cranial nerve names in order
  • Know sensory/motor/mixed classification
  • Understand exit foramina for each nerve
  • Learn all clinical testing methods
  • Memorize UMN vs LMN facial palsy differences
  • Know all reflex arcs
  • Understand visual field defects
  • Learn cranial nerve syndromes
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on cranial nerves
  • Draw cranial nerve nuclei in brainstem

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 3-5 questions)

docs.title: BRACHIAL PLEXUS - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Network of nerves supplying upper limb Roots: C5, C6, C7, C8, T1 Location: Posterior triangle of neck → Axilla → Upper limb

docs.title: Structure - "**R**oots **T**runks **D**ivisions **C**ords **B**ranches"
docs.text:
C5  ─┐
     ├──── Upper Trunk ───┬─── Anterior Division ───┐
C6  ─┘                    │                         ├─── Lateral Cord
                          └─── Posterior Division ──┼─── Posterior Cord
C7  ────── Middle Trunk ──┬─── Anterior Division ───┤
                          └─── Posterior Division ──┤
C8  ─┐                    │                         │
     ├──── Lower Trunk ───┬─── Anterior Division ───┘
T1  ─┘                    └─── Posterior Division ───┘

Mnemonic for organization: "**R**obert **T**aylor **D**rinks **C**old **B**eer"

docs.title: 1. ROOTS (5) - C5, C6, C7, C8, T1
docs.text:
Branches from Roots: - Dorsal scapular nerve (C5) → Rhomboids, Levator scapulae - Long thoracic nerve (C5, C6, C7) → Serratus anterior

NEET PG: Long thoracic nerve injury → Winged scapula

docs.title: Upper Trunk (C5 + C6)
docs.text: Branches: - Suprascapular nerve → Supraspinatus + Infraspinatus - Nerve to subclavius → Subclavius

docs.title: Middle Trunk (C7)
docs.text: No branches

docs.title: Lower Trunk (C8 + T1)
docs.text: No direct branches

docs.title: 3. DIVISIONS (6)
docs.text:
• 3 Anterior divisions
  • 3 Posterior divisions

No branches from divisions - just rearrangement!

docs.title: 4. CORDS (3)
docs.text: Named according to relation with Axillary artery

docs.title: Lateral Cord (Anterior divisions of Upper + Middle trunks)
docs.text: Branches: 1. Lateral pectoral nerve → Pectoralis major 2. Musculocutaneous nerve (terminal) 3. Lateral root of median nerve (terminal)

docs.title: Medial Cord (Anterior division of Lower trunk)
docs.text: Branches: 1. Medial pectoral nerve → Pectoralis major + minor 2. Medial cutaneous nerve of arm 3. Medial cutaneous nerve of forearm 4. Ulnar nerve (terminal) 5. Medial root of median nerve (terminal)

docs.title: Posterior Cord (All 3 Posterior divisions)
docs.text:
Branches: 1. Upper subscapular nerve → Subscapularis 2. Lower subscapular nerve → Subscapularis + Teres major 3. Thoracodorsal nerve → Latissimus dorsi 4. Axillary nerve (terminal) 5. Radial nerve (terminal)

Mnemonic for Posterior cord branches: "**U**pper **L**ower **T**horaco **A**xillary **R**adial"

docs.title: MUSCULOCUTANEOUS NERVE (C5, C6, C7)
docs.text:
Origin: Lateral cord Course: Pierces coracobrachialis → Between biceps & brachialis Motor: - Coracobrachialis - Biceps brachii - Brachialis

Sensory: Lateral cutaneous nerve of forearm

Testing: Elbow flexion with forearm supinated

Injury: Weak elbow flexion, loss of sensation over lateral forearm

docs.title: MEDIAN NERVE (C5-T1)
docs.text:
Origin: Medial + Lateral cords ("M" shaped formation) Course: Arm → Cubital fossa → Forearm (between FDS & FDP) → Carpal tunnel → Hand

Motor:

Forearm (All flexors EXCEPT FCU & medial half FDP): - Pronator teres (PT) - Flexor carpi radialis (FCR) - Palmaris longus (PL) - Flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS) - Lateral half of FDP (to index & middle fingers) - Flexor pollicis longus (FPL) - Pronator quadratus (PQ)

Mnemonic: "**P**lease **F**ind **P**almaris **F**DS **F**DP **F**PL **P**Q"

Hand (Thenar + Lumbricals): - Abductor pollicis brevis (APB) - Flexor pollicis brevis (FPB) - superficial head - Opponens pollicis (OP) - Lateral 2 lumbricals

Mnemonic for Thenar: "**A**ll **F**or **O**ne" (APB, FPB, OP)

Sensory: Palmar side of lateral 3½ digits + nail beds

Branches: - Anterior interosseous nerve (AIN): FPL, lateral FDP, PQ

Testing: - Pen test: Ask to hold pen between thumb & index finger - OK sign: Cannot make "OK" sign (AIN lesion) - Ape hand: Thenar wasting, thumb in plane of hand

Injuries:

Site Features Name Elbow/Forearm Cannot flex DIP of index, cannot flex IP of thumb, loss of pronation AIN syndrome Wrist (Carpal tunnel) Thenar wasting, ape hand, nocturnal pain, +Tinel's, +Phalen's Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Complete Cannot oppose thumb, cannot flex wrist radially, sensory loss Median nerve palsy

NEET PG High-Yield: - Carpal tunnel syndrome: Most common nerve compression, affects median nerve at wrist - Diagnosis: Tinel's sign (tapping), Phalen's test (wrist flexion 60 sec) - Treatment: Wrist splint, steroid injection, surgical release - AIN syndrome: Cannot make "OK" sign, no sensory loss

docs.title: ULNAR NERVE (C8, T1)
docs.text:
Origin: Medial cord Course: Medial arm → Behind medial epicondyle (funny bone) → Ulnar side of forearm → Guyon's canal → Hand

Motor:

Forearm: - Flexor carpi ulnaris (FCU) - Medial half of FDP (to ring & little fingers)

Hand: - Hypothenar muscles: Abductor digiti minimi, Flexor digiti minimi, Opponens digiti minimi - Interossei: Palmar (PAD) + Dorsal (DAB) - Lumbricals: Medial 2 (to ring & little fingers) - Adductor pollicis - Deep head of FPB

Mnemonic: "Ulnar does it A**ll" (All intrinsic hand muscles except LOAF) **LOAF = Median supplies → Lateral 2 lumbricals, Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, Flexor pollicis brevis

Sensory: Medial 1½ digits (both palmar & dorsal)

Testing: - Froment's sign: Paper between thumb & index, thumb IP flexes (using FPL as adductor pollicis weak) - Card test: Cannot adduct/abduct fingers - Wartenberg's sign: Little finger abducted

Injuries:

Site Features Name Elbow Complete ulnar palsy Cubital tunnel syndrome Wrist (Guyon's canal) Spares dorsal sensory branch Cyclist's palsy Complete Claw hand (hyperextended MCP, flexed PIP/DIP of 4th & 5th digits), Froment's +ve Ulnar claw

NEET PG High-Yield: - Claw hand: MORE clawed with distal lesion (Paradox of ulnar nerve) - Proximal lesion: FDP paralyzed → cannot flex → less clawed - Distal lesion: FDP intact → can flex → more clawed - Froment's sign: Pathognomonic for ulnar nerve injury

docs.title: AXILLARY NERVE (C5, C6)
docs.text:
Origin: Posterior cord Course: Quadrangular space (with posterior circumflex humeral artery)

Motor: - Deltoid: Shoulder abduction (15-90°) - Teres minor: Lateral rotation of shoulder

Sensory: Regimental badge area (over lateral deltoid)

Testing: Shoulder abduction

Injury: - Causes: Anterior shoulder dislocation, fracture surgical neck of humerus - Features: Cannot abduct shoulder beyond 15° (supraspinatus does 0-15°) - Sensation loss: Regimental badge area

NEET PG: Quadrangular space contains axillary nerve + posterior circumflex humeral artery

docs.title: RADIAL NERVE (C5-T1)
docs.text:
Origin: Posterior cord (largest branch) Course: Posterior arm (spiral groove of humerus) → Lateral epicondyle → Forearm → Hand

Motor:

Arm: - Triceps brachii (all 3 heads) - Anconeus - Brachioradialis - Extensor carpi radialis longus (ECRL)

Forearm (All extensors): - Extensor carpi radialis brevis (ECRB) - Supinator - Extensor digitorum (ED) - Extensor digiti minimi (EDM) - Extensor carpi ulnaris (ECU) - Abductor pollicis longus (APL) - Extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) - Extensor pollicis longus (EPL) - Extensor indicis (EI)

Sensory: Posterior arm, forearm, dorsal lateral 3½ digits (excluding nail beds)

Branches: - Posterior interosseous nerve (PIN): Deep branch, motor to all extensors in forearm

Testing: - Extend wrist/fingers - Extend thumb at IP joint - Anatomical snuffbox sensation

Injuries:

Site Features Name Axilla Complete palsy: wrist drop, finger drop, thumb drop, triceps weak Crutch palsy Spiral groove Wrist drop, finger drop, triceps spared (branches above) Saturday night palsy Forearm (PIN) Cannot extend fingers/thumb, wrist extension present (ECRL spared) Posterior interosseous syndrome

Saturday Night Palsy: Arm hanging over chair while drunk → radial nerve compression in spiral groove

Wrist Drop: Cannot extend wrist, pathognomonic for radial nerve injury

NEET PG High-Yield: - Most commonly injured nerve in humeral shaft fracture - Wrist drop: Classic sign of radial nerve palsy - Triceps preserved: If injury below spiral groove

docs.title: ERB'S PALSY (Upper Trunk - C5, C6)
docs.text:
Causes: - Birth injury (shoulder dystocia) - Trauma (motorcycle accident)

Muscles affected: - Deltoid, supraspinatus, infraspinatus (shoulder movements) - Biceps, brachialis (elbow flexion) - Supinator, brachioradialis (forearm supination)

Position: "Waiter's Tip" or "Porter's Tip" - Shoulder adducted & internally rotated - Elbow extended - Forearm pronated - Wrist flexed

NEET PG: Birth injury, shoulder dystocia

docs.title: KLUMPKE'S PALSY (Lower Trunk - C8, T1)
docs.text:
Causes: - Birth injury (arm pulled up during delivery) - Trauma (grabbing overhead object while falling)

Muscles affected: - Intrinsic hand muscles - Long finger flexors

Position: "Claw Hand" - Hyperextended MCP - Flexed IP joints

Associated: Horner's syndrome (T1 involvement affects sympathetic chain) - Ptosis - Miosis - Anhidrosis

NEET PG: Associated with Horner's syndrome

docs.title: COMPLETE BRACHIAL PLEXUS INJURY
docs.text: Cause: Severe trauma (motorcycle accident) Features: Entire upper limb paralyzed, anesthetic, flail arm

docs.title: Quadrangular Space
docs.text:
Boundaries: - Superior: Teres minor - Inferior: Teres major - Medial: Long head of triceps - Lateral: Surgical neck of humerus

Contents: - Axillary nerve - Posterior circumflex humeral artery

docs.title: Triangular Space
docs.text:
Boundaries: - Superior: Teres minor - Inferior: Teres major - Lateral: Long head of triceps

Contents: Circumflex scapular artery

docs.title: Triangular Interval
docs.text:
Boundaries: - Superior: Teres major - Medial: Long head of triceps - Lateral: Lateral head of triceps/humerus

Contents: - Radial nerve - Profunda brachii artery

docs.title: Nerve Roots Summary
docs.text: Root Muscles (Key) Reflex Dermatome C5 Deltoid, Biceps Biceps Lateral arm C6 Wrist extensors Brachioradialis Lateral forearm, thumb C7 Triceps, Wrist flexors Triceps Middle finger C8 Finger flexors - Medial forearm, ring/little T1 Intrinsic hand muscles - Medial arm

docs.title: Terminal Branches (Lateral to Medial)
docs.text: "**M**y **A**unt **R**aised **M**y **U**ncle" - **Musculocutaneous - **Axillary - **Radial - **Median - **Ulnar

docs.title: Cords Formation
docs.text:
• **L**ateral cord: **L**ateral + **L**ateral (C5, C6, C7 anterior divisions)
  • **M**edial cord: **M**edial (C8, T1 anterior division)
  • **P**osterior cord: **P**osterior **P**osterior **P**osterior (All 3 posterior divisions)

docs.title: Median vs Ulnar in Hand
docs.text:
LOAF supplied by **M**edian: - **L**ateral 2 lumbricals - **O**pponens pollicis - **A**bductor pollicis brevis - **F**lexor pollicis brevis (superficial head)

Rest supplied by **U**lnar

docs.title: Rotator Cuff Muscles
docs.text: "**S**its **I**n **T**he **S**uper **B**owl" - **S**upraspinatus: Abduction 0-15° - **I**nfraspinatus: Lateral rotation - **T**eres minor: Lateral rotation - **S**ubscapularis: Medial rotation

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Long thoracic nerve palsy causes? Answer: Winged scapula

  2. Nerve passing through carpal tunnel? Answer: Median nerve

  3. Most common site of radial nerve injury? Answer: Spiral groove (mid-shaft humerus fracture)

  4. Erb's palsy involves which roots? Answer: C5, C6 (Upper trunk)

  5. Froment's sign tests which nerve? Answer: Ulnar nerve

  6. Nerve passing through quadrangular space? Answer: Axillary nerve

  7. Inability to make OK sign indicates? Answer: Anterior interosseous nerve lesion

  8. Claw hand is more clawed in? Answer: Distal ulnar nerve lesion (paradox)

docs.title: Clinical Testing Summary
docs.text: Sign/Test Nerve What it shows Wrist drop Radial Cannot extend wrist Ape hand Median Thenar wasting Claw hand Ulnar Hyperextended MCP, flexed IP Waiter's tip C5, C6 Erb's palsy Froment's sign Ulnar Thumb IP flexes when holding paper OK sign Median (AIN) Cannot flex DIP index, IP thumb Tinel's sign Median Tapping wrist causes paresthesia Phalen's test Median Wrist flexion causes symptoms Winged scapula Long thoracic Scapula protrudes posteriorly Wartenberg's sign Ulnar Little finger abducted

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Draw brachial plexus from memory
  • Memorize roots, trunks, divisions, cords, branches
  • Know all branches at each level
  • Understand terminal branches and their supplies
  • Learn clinical features of each nerve injury
  • Memorize Erb's vs Klumpke's palsy
  • Know all clinical tests (Froment's, Tinel's, etc.)
  • Understand carpal tunnel syndrome
  • Learn quadrangular space contents
  • Practice 30+ MCQs on brachial plexus

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 2-4 questions)

docs.title: BLOOD SUPPLY OF GI TRACT - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: GI tract supplied by 3 unpaired branches of abdominal aorta: 1. Celiac trunk (T12) → Foregut 2. Superior mesenteric artery (L1) → Midgut 3. Inferior mesenteric artery (L3) → Hindgut

docs.title: Embryological Divisions
docs.text: Division Extent Artery Venous Drainage Foregut Esophagus → 2<sup>nd</sup> part of duodenum (up to ampulla) Celiac trunk Portal vein Midgut 2<sup>nd</sup> part duodenum → Proximal ⅔ of transverse colon Superior mesenteric Superior mesenteric vein Hindgut Distal ⅓ transverse colon → Upper anal canal Inferior mesenteric Inferior mesenteric vein

docs.title: 1. CELIAC TRUNK (Foregut)
docs.text:
Origin: Abdominal aorta at T12 (aortic hiatus level) Length: 1-2 cm Branches: "LHS" - Left gastric, Hepatic, Splenic

        Celiac Trunk (T12)
              |
     ┌────────┼────────┐
     |        |         |
  Left     Hepatic   Splenic
 Gastric   Artery    Artery

docs.title: A. LEFT GASTRIC ARTERY (Smallest)
docs.text:
Course: Ascends → Esophagogastric junction → Lesser curvature Supplies: - Lower esophagus - Lesser curvature of stomach (upper part)

Anastomoses: Right gastric artery

docs.title: B. COMMON HEPATIC ARTERY
docs.text:
Course: Runs right along upper border of pancreas Branches:

Common Hepatic Artery
         |
    ┌────┴────┐
Proper    Gastroduodenal
Hepatic      (GDA)
    |
 ┌──┴──┐
Right  Left
Hepatic Hepatic
    |
Right Gastric
Cystic (via Right Hepatic)

docs.title: 1. Proper Hepatic Artery
docs.text:
• Right hepatic → Right lobe of liver
  • Cystic artery → Gallbladder (Calot's triangle)
  • Left hepatic → Left lobe of liver
  • Right gastric → Lesser curvature (anastomoses with left gastric)

docs.title: 2. Gastroduodenal Artery (GDA)
docs.text:
Branches: - Right gastroepiploic → Greater curvature (right side) - Superior pancreaticoduodenal (anterior + posterior) → Pancreatic head + duodenum

NEET PG: GDA erosion → massive upper GI bleed in chronic pancreatitis

docs.title: C. SPLENIC ARTERY
docs.text:
Course: Tortuous, along upper border of pancreas → Spleen Branches: 1. Pancreatic branches → Body & tail of pancreas 2. Short gastric arteries (5-7) → Fundus of stomach 3. Left gastroepiploic → Greater curvature (left side)

NEET PG: Most tortuous artery, splenic artery aneurysm common

docs.title: Stomach Blood Supply Summary
docs.text:
Short Gastric
          (from Splenic)
               ↓
    ┌──────────────────┐
    │                  │  ← Left Gastric
    │    STOMACH       │
    │                  │  ← Right Gastric
    └──────────────────┘
         ↑          ↑
    Left GE      Right GE
   (Splenic)    (Hepatic)

Four Arcades: 1. Lesser curvature: Left gastric + Right gastric 2. Greater curvature: Left gastroepiploic + Right gastroepiploic 3. Fundus: Short gastric arteries 4. Cardia: Branches of left gastric

docs.title: 2. SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY (Midgut)
docs.text:
Origin: L1 (1 cm below celiac trunk) Course: Behind neck of pancreas → Between uncinate process & left renal vein Emerges: At lower border of pancreas

Branches (from medial to lateral):

Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA)
         |
    ┌────┼────┬────┬─────┬────┐
    |    |    |    |     |    |
   IPD  Jejunal Ileal Ileo- RCA MCA
        branches     colic

docs.title: 1. Inferior Pancreaticoduodenal (IPD)
docs.text: Supplies: Pancreatic head, duodenum (3rd & 4th parts) Anastomoses: Superior pancreaticoduodenal (from GDA)

docs.title: 2. Jejunal & Ileal Branches (12-15)
docs.text:
Supplies: Jejunum & ileum Arrangement: Arterial arcades in mesentery - Jejunum: 1-2 arcades, long vasa recta - Ileum: 3-4 arcades, short vasa recta

NEET PG: Jejunum has fewer arcades but longer vasa recta

docs.title: 3. Ileocolic Artery
docs.text:
Supplies: - Terminal ileum - Cecum - Appendix (via appendicular artery) - Ascending colon (lower part)

NEET PG: Appendicular artery is end artery (thrombosis → appendiceal gangrene)

docs.title: 4. Right Colic Artery
docs.text: Supplies: Ascending colon Note: May be absent in 10% (then supplied by ileocolic + middle colic)

docs.title: 5. Middle Colic Artery
docs.text: Supplies: Transverse colon (proximal ⅔) Anastomoses: Left colic (forms marginal artery of Drummond)

docs.title: 3. INFERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY (Hindgut)
docs.text:
Origin: L3 (3-4 cm above aortic bifurcation) Course: Descends in front of left common iliac artery

Branches:

Inferior Mesenteric Artery (IMA)
         |
    ┌────┼────┬─────┐
    |    |    |     |
   Left  Sig- Supe-
   Colic moid rior
         Art  Rectal

docs.title: 1. Left Colic Artery
docs.text: Supplies: Distal ⅓ transverse colon, descending colon Branches: Ascending + Descending Anastomoses: Middle colic (forms marginal artery)

docs.title: 2. Sigmoid Arteries (2-3)
docs.text: Supplies: Sigmoid colon

docs.title: 3. Superior Rectal Artery (Terminal branch)
docs.text: Supplies: Upper ⅓ rectum Anastomoses: Middle & inferior rectal arteries

docs.title: 1. Marginal Artery of Drummond
docs.text:
Location: Along mesenteric border of colon Formation: Anastomoses between SMA & IMA branches Significance: Protects colon from ischemia

Weak Point: Griffith's point (splenic flexure - junction of SMA & IMA territories)

docs.title: 2. Arc of Riolan (Meandering Artery)
docs.text: Location: Direct connection between SMA (middle colic) & IMA (left colic) Significance: Alternative collateral if marginal artery blocked

docs.title: 3. Pancreaticoduodenal Arcade
docs.text: Formation: Superior PD (celiac) + Inferior PD (SMA) Significance: Connects celiac trunk & SMA

docs.title: Watershed Areas (Ischemia-Prone) - NEET PG
docs.text:
Area Junction Clinical Significance Splenic flexure SMA + IMA Griffith's point, most common site of ischemic colitis Rectosigmoid IMA + Internal iliac Sudeck's point Duodenum Celiac + SMA Rare ischemia

NEET PG: Splenic flexure most vulnerable to ischemia

docs.title: Arteries (3)
docs.text:
1. Superior rectal (from IMA) → Upper ⅓
  2. Middle rectal (from internal iliac) → Middle ⅓
  3. Inferior rectal (from internal pudendal) → Lower ⅓

docs.title: Veins (3)
docs.text:
1. Superior rectal vein → IMV → Portal system
  2. Middle rectal vein → Internal iliac vein → Systemic
  3. Inferior rectal vein → Internal pudendal → Systemic

Portosystemic Anastomosis: Superior rectal (portal) + Middle & Inferior rectal (systemic)

NEET PG: Hemorrhoids due to portosystemic shunting in portal hypertension

docs.title: Portal Vein Formation
docs.text:
Splenic Vein (from spleen, stomach, pancreas)
              +
Superior Mesenteric Vein (from SMA territory)
              ↓
        PORTAL VEIN
         (behind neck of pancreas)
              ↓
           LIVER

Tributaries of Portal Vein: - Superior mesenteric vein (SMV) - Splenic vein (main tributaries) - Inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) → Usually joins splenic vein - Left gastric vein (coronary vein) - Right gastric vein - Cystic vein

NEET PG: Portal vein formed by SMV + Splenic vein behind neck of pancreas

docs.title: Portosystemic Anastomoses - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Site Portal Systemic Clinical Lower esophagus Left gastric vein Esophageal veins (azygos) Esophageal varices Rectum Superior rectal Middle & Inferior rectal Hemorrhoids Umbilicus Paraumbilical veins Superficial epigastric Caput medusae Retroperitoneum Colic veins Retroperitoneal veins Dilated veins Bare area of liver Portal branches Phrenic veins Dilated veins

NEET PG: 5 sites of portosystemic anastomoses, esophageal varices most dangerous

docs.title: 1. Mesenteric Ischemia
docs.text:
Acute SMA Occlusion: - Sudden severe abdominal pain ("pain out of proportion") - Bloody diarrhea - Peritonitis - Treatment: Emergency laparotomy

Chronic Mesenteric Ischemia: - Postprandial pain (intestinal angina) - Weight loss (fear of eating) - Treatment: Revascularization

NEET PG: "Pain out of proportion to physical findings" = Mesenteric ischemia

docs.title: 2. Ischemic Colitis
docs.text: Most common site: Splenic flexure (Griffith's point) Causes: Atherosclerosis, hypotension, vasculitis Features: Bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, thumbprinting on X-ray

docs.title: 3. Peptic Ulcer Bleeding
docs.text: Posterior gastric ulcer: Can erode into splenic artery Posterior duodenal ulcer: Can erode into gastroduodenal artery

docs.title: 4. Appendicitis
docs.text: Appendicular artery: End artery (no collaterals) Thrombosis: Leads to gangrenous appendicitis

docs.title: Calot's Triangle - NEET PG Favorite
docs.text:
Boundaries: - Inferior: Cystic duct - Superior: Inferior surface of liver - Medial: Common hepatic duct

Contents: - Cystic artery (from right hepatic artery in 75% cases) - Cystic lymph node (Lund's node)

Clinical Significance: Identified during cholecystectomy to avoid CBD injury

NEET PG: Cystic artery normally arises from right hepatic artery

docs.title: Mesenteric Angiography Features
docs.text: Vessel Characteristic Clinical Use Celiac trunk Seagull appearance Identify celiac stenosis SMA Arborization pattern GI bleeding localization IMA Smaller caliber Colonic ischemia evaluation

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Watershed area most prone to ischemia? Answer: Splenic flexure (Griffith's point)

  2. Artery supplying appendix? Answer: Appendicular artery (from ileocolic)

  3. Portal vein formed behind? Answer: Neck of pancreas

  4. Most tortuous artery? Answer: Splenic artery

  5. End artery to appendix? Answer: Appendicular artery

  6. Contents of Calot's triangle? Answer: Cystic artery, cystic lymph node

  7. Marginal artery of Drummond formed by? Answer: Anastomosis between SMA and IMA branches

  8. Most common site of ischemic colitis? Answer: Splenic flexure

docs.title: Celiac Trunk Branches
docs.text: "**L**eft **H**and **S**hake" - **L**eft gastric - **H**epatic - **S**plenic

docs.title: SMA Branches (Medial to Lateral)
docs.text: "I **J**ust **I**magine **I**ce **R**eally **M**elts" - **I**nferior pancreaticoduodenal - **J**ejunal - **I**leal - **I**leocolic - **R**ight colic - **M**iddle colic

docs.title: IMA Branches
docs.text: "**L**eft **S**ide **S**ucks **R**ectally" - **L**eft colic - **S**igmoid - **S**uperior **R**ectal

docs.title: Portosystemic Anastomoses (5 Sites)
docs.text: "**E**very **R**ed **U**mbrella **B**rings **L**uck" - **E**sophagus (varices) - **R**ectum (hemorrhoids) - **U**mbilicus (caput medusae) - **B**are area of liver - **L**igamentum teres

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Memorize 3 main GI arteries and their origins
  • Know foregut, midgut, hindgut divisions
  • Understand celiac trunk branches (LHS)
  • Learn SMA and IMA branch patterns
  • Memorize watershed areas
  • Know portosystemic anastomoses (5 sites)
  • Understand Calot's triangle anatomy
  • Learn marginal artery of Drummond
  • Know rectal blood supply
  • Practice 25+ MCQs on GI blood supply

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: High (expect 2-3 questions)

docs.title: HEART ANATOMY - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Location: Middle mediastinum Shape: Pyramid lying on its side Weight: 250-350g (Male), 200-300g (Female) Size: Closed fist

docs.title: Surface Anatomy
docs.text: Apex: 5th left intercostal space, 9cm from midline (mid-clavicular line) Base: Opposite T6-T9 vertebrae Right border: Right atrium Left border: Left ventricle Superior border: Great vessels

docs.title: Borders (X-ray)
docs.text:
• Right border: Right atrium, SVC
  • Left border: Left ventricle, left atrial appendage, pulmonary trunk
  • Inferior border: Right ventricle (mainly)

NEET PG: Apex beat - 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line

docs.title: RIGHT ATRIUM
docs.text:
Parts: 1. Sinus venarum: Smooth posterior part 2. Atrium proper: Rough anterior part (pectinate muscles) 3. Auricle: Ear-like projection

Openings: - SVC: Superior, no valve - IVC: Inferior, guarded by Eustachian valve (rudimentary) - Coronary sinus: Between IVC & tricuspid valve, guarded by Thebesian valve - Tricuspid orifice: Opening to right ventricle

Important Structures: - Crista terminalis: Ridge between sinus venarum & atrium proper - Fossa ovalis: Remnant of foramen ovale (interatrial septum) - Limbus: Margin of fossa ovalis - SA node: At junction of SVC & RA (near crista terminalis)

NEET PG High-Yield: - SA node location: Junction of SVC & right atrium - Fossa ovalis: Patent in 20-25% (probe patent foramen ovale) - Thebesian valve: Guards coronary sinus opening

docs.title: RIGHT VENTRICLE
docs.text:
Parts: 1. Inflow tract: From tricuspid to apex 2. Outflow tract (Infundibulum): To pulmonary trunk

Internal Features: - Trabeculae carneae: Irregular muscular ridges (3 types) - Ridges, bridges, pillars (papillary muscles) - Papillary muscles: 3 (anterior, posterior, septal) - Chordae tendineae: Attach papillary muscles to tricuspid valve cusps - Moderator band (Septomarginal trabecula): Bridge from septum to anterior papillary muscle - Carries right bundle branch - Supraventricular crest: Separates inflow from outflow tract

Tricuspid Valve: - 3 cusps: Anterior, posterior (inferior), septal (medial) - Annulus: Fibrous ring - Papillary muscles prevent prolapse during systole

Pulmonary Valve: - 3 semilunar cusps: Anterior, right, left - No chordae tendineae - Sinuses of Valsalva: Behind each cusp

NEET PG: - Moderator band: Carries right bundle branch - Most anterior chamber: Right ventricle (injured in stab wounds)

docs.title: LEFT ATRIUM
docs.text:
Parts: 1. Main chamber: Smooth (from embryonic pulmonary veins) 2. Auricle: Rough (pectinate muscles)

Openings: - 4 pulmonary veins: 2 right, 2 left (no valves) - Mitral orifice: To left ventricle

Important Structures: - Smooth walls: No crista terminalis - Interatrial septum: Fossa ovalis (from right side)

Relations (IMPORTANT): - Anterior: Transverse pericardial sinus, aorta, pulmonary trunk - Posterior: Esophagus, descending aorta - Left: Left lung - Right: Right atrium (interatrial septum)

NEET PG: Left atrium posterior to esophagus (enlarged LA → dysphagia)

docs.title: LEFT VENTRICLE
docs.text:
Parts: 1. Inflow tract: From mitral valve 2. Outflow tract: To aorta

Internal Features: - Trabeculae carneae: Finer than right ventricle - Papillary muscles: 2 (anterolateral, posteromedial) - Larger & stronger than RV - Chordae tendineae: To mitral valve - Thickest wall: 8-12 mm (3× thicker than RV)

Mitral Valve: - 2 cusps: Anterior (aortic), Posterior (mural) - Anterior cusp larger - Annulus: Fibrous ring - Each papillary muscle → Chordae to BOTH cusps

Aortic Valve: - 3 semilunar cusps: Right coronary, Left coronary, Non-coronary (posterior) - Sinuses of Valsalva: Behind each cusp - Coronary ostia: In right & left sinuses (just above cusps)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Left ventricle: Thickest wall (high systemic pressure) - Mitral valve: 2 cusps (bicuspid) - Aortic sinuses: Right & left coronary sinuses give coronary arteries

docs.title: Fibrous Skeleton of Heart
docs.text:
Components: 1. 4 fibrous rings: Around all 4 valves 2. Right & left fibrous trigones: Connect rings 3. Membranous interventricular septum: Upper part

Functions: - Attachment for valve cusps - Separation between atria & ventricles (electrical insulation) - Support for myocardium - Only pathway for conduction: AV node → Bundle of His

NEET PG: Fibrous skeleton electrically insulates atria from ventricles

docs.title: Interventricular Septum
docs.text:
Parts: 1. Muscular part (lower ⅔): Thick, muscular 2. Membranous part (upper ⅓): Thin, fibrous

NEET PG High-Yield: - VSD: 90% in membranous part (most common congenital heart defect) - Membranous septum relations: - Right side: Septal cusp of tricuspid valve - Left side: Left ventricular outflow tract

docs.title: RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY (RCA)
docs.text:
Origin: Right aortic sinus

Course: - Right AV groove (atrioventricular groove) - Continues to posterior surface - Ends as posterior descending artery (PDA) in 70% (right dominant)

Branches: 1. SA nodal artery (60% from RCA, 40% from LCx) 2. Right marginal artery: Along right border 3. Posterior descending artery (PDA): In posterior interventricular groove 4. AV nodal artery: From RCA at crux (90%)

Supplies: - Right atrium & ventricle - SA node (60%) - AV node (90%) - Posterior ⅓ of interventricular septum - Posterior wall of LV (if dominant)

docs.title: LEFT CORONARY ARTERY (LCA)
docs.text:
Origin: Left aortic sinus

Course: Short main stem (1-2 cm) → Bifurcates into LAD & LCx

docs.title: A. Left Anterior Descending (LAD)
docs.text:
Course: Anterior interventricular groove → Apex → Posterior surface

Branches: 1. Diagonal branches (1-3): To anterior LV wall 2. Septal perforators: To anterior ⅔ of interventricular septum

Supplies: - Anterior wall of LV - Anterior ⅔ of interventricular septum - Apex - Bundle branches (via septal perforators)

NEET PG: LAD = "Widow maker" (occlusion → massive anterior MI)

docs.title: B. Left Circumflex (LCx)
docs.text:
Course: Left AV groove → Posterior surface

Branches: 1. Left marginal artery (Obtuse marginal): To lateral LV wall 2. SA nodal artery (40%) 3. PDA (10-15% - left dominant system)

Supplies: - Lateral wall of LV - Posterolateral wall - SA node (40%)

docs.title: Coronary Dominance
docs.text:
Type Frequency PDA Origin AV Nodal Artery Right dominant 70% RCA RCA Left dominant 10% LCx LCx Co-dominant 20% Both RCA (usually)

NEET PG: Right dominant system most common (70%)

docs.title: Coronary Veins
docs.text:
Great cardiac vein: Parallels LAD → Continues as coronary sinus Coronary sinus: - Largest vein - Lies in posterior AV groove - Opens into right atrium (between IVC & tricuspid) - Guarded by Thebesian valve

Tributaries: 1. Great cardiac vein 2. Middle cardiac vein (parallels PDA) 3. Small cardiac vein (parallels RCA) 4. Oblique vein of left atrium 5. Posterior vein of LV

Other drainage: - Anterior cardiac veins: Directly into RA - Thebesian veins: Directly into chambers

NEET PG: Coronary sinus drains into right atrium

docs.title: 1. SA NODE (Pacemaker)
docs.text: Location: Junction of SVC & right atrium (subepicardial) Blood supply: SA nodal artery (RCA 60%, LCx 40%) Rate: 60-100 bpm Structure: P cells (pacemaker cells)

docs.title: 2. INTERNODAL PATHWAYS
docs.text: 3 Pathways: Anterior, middle, posterior (Bachmann's bundle) Function: Conduct impulse from SA node to AV node

docs.title: 3. AV NODE
docs.text:
Location: Right atrium, above coronary sinus opening (in triangle of Koch) Blood supply: AV nodal artery (from RCA 90%) Function: Delays impulse (0.1 sec) - allows atrial contraction before ventricular

Triangle of Koch (NEET PG): - Anterior: Tendon of Todaro - Posterior: Coronary sinus opening - Base: Tricuspid valve annulus (septal cusp) - Contains: AV node

docs.title: 4. BUNDLE OF HIS
docs.text: Location: Passes through fibrous skeleton (only electrical connection) Length: 1-2 cm Divides into: Right & left bundle branches

docs.title: 5. BUNDLE BRANCHES
docs.text:
Right bundle branch: - Passes through moderator band - To right ventricle

Left bundle branch: - Divides into anterior & posterior fascicles - To left ventricle

docs.title: 6. PURKINJE FIBERS
docs.text:
Location: Subendocardial Function: Rapid conduction (fastest: 2-4 m/sec) Distribution: Throughout ventricular myocardium

NEET PG High-Yield: - SA node: Pacemaker (60-100 bpm) - AV node: Delays conduction (0.1 sec) - Purkinje fibers: Fastest conduction (2-4 m/sec) - Triangle of Koch: Contains AV node

docs.title: Auscultation Areas
docs.text:
Valve Auscultation Area Position Aortic 2nd right intercostal space Right sternal border Pulmonary 2nd left intercostal space Left sternal border Tricuspid 4th-5th left intercostal space Lower left sternal border Mitral 5th intercostal space Apex (mid-clavicular line)

Mnemonic: "**A**ll **P**eople **T**ake **M**edicine" (from right to left, top to bottom)

docs.title: Mitral Stenosis (MS)
docs.text: Cause: Rheumatic heart disease (most common) Murmur: Mid-diastolic, low-pitched, rumbling (apex) Signs: Opening snap, loud S1, malar flush Complication: Atrial fibrillation, LA thrombus → Embolism

docs.title: Mitral Regurgitation (MR)
docs.text: Causes: RHD, MVP, ischemic (papillary muscle rupture) Murmur: Pansystolic, high-pitched, blowing (apex → axilla) Signs: Soft S1, S3 gallop

docs.title: Aortic Stenosis (AS)
docs.text: Causes: Calcific (elderly), bicuspid valve, RHD Murmur: Ejection systolic, crescendo-decrescendo (2nd right ICS → carotids) Triad: Angina, Syncope, Heart failure (SAD) Signs: Slow-rising pulse, narrow pulse pressure, LV heave

docs.title: Aortic Regurgitation (AR)
docs.text:
Causes: RHD, infective endocarditis, aortic dissection, Marfan Murmur: Early diastolic, high-pitched, blowing (left sternal border) Signs: - Wide pulse pressure, Water-hammer pulse - Head nodding (De Musset's sign) - Corrigan's sign, Quincke's sign, Hill's sign

NEET PG: AR has maximum eponymous signs

docs.title: Fibrous Pericardium
docs.text: Structure: Tough, non-distensible sac Attachments: - Superior: Great vessels - Inferior: Central tendon of diaphragm - Anterior: Sternum (sternopericardial ligaments)

docs.title: Serous Pericardium
docs.text:
Two layers: 1. Parietal layer: Lines fibrous pericardium 2. Visceral layer (Epicardium): Covers heart

Pericardial cavity: Between parietal & visceral layers Fluid: 15-50 ml (lubricant)

docs.title: Transverse Sinus
docs.text:
Location: Behind aorta & pulmonary trunk, in front of SVC & LA Boundaries: - Anterior: Aorta, pulmonary trunk - Posterior: SVC, left atrium - Superior: Pericardial reflection - Inferior: Pericardial reflection

Clinical: Finger passed through during cardiac surgery (to clamp vessels)

docs.title: Oblique Sinus
docs.text:
Location: Behind left atrium Boundaries: - Anterior: Left atrium - Lateral: Pulmonary veins - Inferior: Diaphragm

NEET PG: Transverse sinus used in cardiac surgery

docs.title: Pericardial Effusion
docs.text: Causes: Infection, malignancy, uremia, post-MI Signs: Muffled heart sounds, enlarged cardiac shadow (water bottle shape on X-ray) ECG: Low voltage QRS, electrical alternans

docs.title: Cardiac Tamponade
docs.text:
Triad (Beck's triad): 1. Hypotension 2. Elevated JVP (distended neck veins) 3. Muffled heart sounds

Signs: - Pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in BP during inspiration) - Kussmaul's sign (JVP rises on inspiration)

Treatment: Pericardiocentesis (drain fluid)

NEET PG: Beck's triad + Pulsus paradoxus = Tamponade

docs.title: Constrictive Pericarditis
docs.text:
Causes: TB (most common in developing countries), post-cardiac surgery, radiation Signs: - Kussmaul's sign (JVP rises on inspiration) - Pericardial knock (early diastolic sound) - Calcified pericardium on X-ray

Treatment: Pericardiectomy

docs.title: Myocardial Infarction - Territory
docs.text:
Coronary Artery Territory ECG Leads LAD Anteroseptal V1-V4 LAD (distal) Apical, Anterior V3-V4, I, aVL LCx Lateral I, aVL, V5-V6 RCA Inferior II, III, aVF RCA (proximal) RV infarction V1, V4R LCx or RCA Posterior V7-V9 (or tall R in V1-V2)

NEET PG High-Yield: - LAD occlusion: Anterior MI (most common, worst prognosis) - RCA occlusion: Inferior MI (bradycardia, heart block) - RV infarction: With inferior MI, avoid nitrates (preload dependent)

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. SA node location? Answer: Junction of SVC and right atrium

  2. Most common coronary dominance? Answer: Right dominant (70%)

  3. Most common site of VSD? Answer: Membranous interventricular septum

  4. Moderator band carries? Answer: Right bundle branch

  5. Most anterior chamber of heart? Answer: Right ventricle

  6. Triangle of Koch contains? Answer: AV node

  7. Fastest conducting tissue? Answer: Purkinje fibers (2-4 m/sec)

  8. Beck's triad seen in? Answer: Cardiac tamponade

docs.title: Coronary Dominance
docs.text: "**R**ight is **R**ight" (Right dominant = most common)

docs.title: Auscultation Areas
docs.text: "**A**ll **P**eople **T**ake **M**edicine" - **A**ortic: 2nd right ICS - **P**ulmonary: 2nd left ICS - **T**ricuspid: 4th left ICS - **M**itral: 5th ICS (apex)

docs.title: Beck's Triad (Tamponade)
docs.text: "**H**eart **M**uffled, **J**VP up, **B**P down" - **H**eart sounds muffled - **J**VP elevated - **B**P decreased

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know all 4 chambers and their features
  • Understand coronary artery anatomy
  • Learn coronary dominance patterns
  • Memorize conducting system pathway
  • Know valve auscultation areas
  • Understand pericardial sinuses
  • Learn MI territories by coronary artery
  • Know cardiac tamponade features
  • Practice 30+ MCQs on heart anatomy

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (3-5 questions)

docs.title: LUNGS & RESPIRATORY SYSTEM - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Function: Gas exchange (O2 and CO2) Location: Thoracic cavity Number: 2 (right & left)

docs.title: TRACHEA
docs.text:
Extent: C6 (lower border of cricoid cartilage) → T⅘ (carina) Length: 10-12 cm Structure: 16-20 C-shaped cartilaginous rings Diameter: 2-2.5 cm

Relations (IMPORTANT):

Level Anterior Posterior Neck Isthmus of thyroid (2nd-4th rings), sternothyroid Esophagus Thorax Left brachiocephalic vein, thymus, arch of aorta Esophagus, left recurrent laryngeal nerve

Bifurcation: T⅘ vertebral level (sternal angle) Carina: Ridge at bifurcation (highly sensitive - cough reflex)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Sternal angle (Angle of Louis): Landmark for tracheal bifurcation (T⅘) - Foreign body aspiration: Right main bronchus (wider, shorter, more vertical)

docs.title: Main Bronchi
docs.text:
Feature Right Main Bronchus Left Main Bronchus Length Shorter (2.5 cm) Longer (5 cm) Width Wider Narrower Angle More vertical (25°) More horizontal (45°) Branches 3 lobar bronchi 2 lobar bronchi Eparterial Present (above pulmonary artery) Absent

NEET PG: Right main bronchus - Wider, Shorter, More vertical → Foreign bodies enter right side

docs.title: Lobar (Secondary) Bronchi
docs.text:
Right: 3 (upper, middle, lower) Left: 2 (upper, lower)

Eparterial bronchus: Right upper lobe bronchus (only bronchus above pulmonary artery)

docs.title: Segmental (Tertiary) Bronchi
docs.text: Right lung: 10 segments Left lung: 8-10 segments

docs.title: Right Lung (10 segments)
docs.text:
Upper lobe (3): 1. Apical 2. Posterior 3. Anterior

Middle lobe (2): 4. Lateral 5. Medial

Lower lobe (5): 6. Superior (apical) 7. Medial basal 8. Anterior basal 9. Lateral basal 10. Posterior basal

docs.title: Left Lung (8-10 segments)
docs.text:
Upper lobe (4-5): 1. Apico-posterior 2. Anterior 3. Superior lingular 4. Inferior lingular

Lower lobe (4-5): 5. Superior (apical) 6. Anteromedial basal 7. Lateral basal 8. Posterior basal

NEET PG: Bronchopulmonary segments are surgical units (can be resected individually)

docs.title: General Features
docs.text:
Feature Right Lung Left Lung Lobes 3 (upper, middle, lower) 2 (upper, lower) Fissures 2 (oblique, horizontal) 1 (oblique) Weight Heavier (625g) Lighter (565g) Segments 10 8-10 Cardiac notch Absent Present (lingula)

NEET PG: Right lung - 3 lobes, 2 fissures; Left lung - 2 lobes, 1 fissure

docs.title: 1. Costal Surface
docs.text: Relations: Ribs, intercostal muscles

docs.title: 2. Mediastinal Surface
docs.text: Important structure: Hilum (root of lung)

docs.title: 3. Diaphragmatic Surface (Base)
docs.text: Relations: Diaphragm Note: Right base higher (liver beneath)

docs.title: 4. Apex
docs.text: Extends: 2.5 cm above medial ⅓ of clavicle Relations: Subclavian artery, T1 nerve root

docs.title: Oblique Fissure (Both lungs)
docs.text: Surface marking: 5th rib posteriorly → 6th costal cartilage anteriorly Separates: - Right: Upper & middle lobes from lower lobe - Left: Upper lobe from lower lobe

docs.title: Horizontal Fissure (Right lung only)
docs.text:
Surface marking: 4th costal cartilage (sternal end) → 5th rib (mid-axillary) Separates: Upper lobe from middle lobe

NEET PG: Accessory fissures common (seen on imaging)

docs.title: Structures (Anterior to Posterior)
docs.text:
Arrangement:

Right lung: - Anterior to posterior: Pulmonary artery → Bronchi → Pulmonary veins - Superior to inferior: Bronchus → Pulmonary artery → Pulmonary veins

Left lung: - Anterior to posterior: Pulmonary artery → Bronchi → Pulmonary veins - Superior to inferior: Pulmonary artery → Bronchus → Pulmonary veins

Mnemonic: "A**rtery **B**ronchus **V**ein" (Anterior to Posterior on both sides) **But: Right - BAV (superior to inferior), Left - ABV (superior to inferior)

docs.title: Other structures at hilum:
docs.text:
• Bronchial vessels
  • Pulmonary nerve plexus
  • Lymph nodes
  • Lymphatics

NEET PG High-Yield: - Right: Eparterial bronchus (above artery) - Left: All bronchi below artery (hyparterial)

docs.title: Pulmonary Circulation (Functional)
docs.text:
Pulmonary arteries: Deoxygenated blood from right ventricle Pulmonary veins: Oxygenated blood to left atrium

Right pulmonary artery: Passes behind SVC & ascending aorta Left pulmonary artery: Connected to arch of aorta by ligamentum arteriosum

Pulmonary veins: 4 veins (2 from each lung) → Left atrium

NEET PG: Ligamentum arteriosum = Remnant of ductus arteriosus

docs.title: Bronchial Circulation (Nutritive)
docs.text:
Bronchial arteries: From thoracic aorta - Supply bronchi, visceral pleura, lymph nodes Bronchial veins: Drain into azygos/hemiazygos veins

NEET PG: Bronchial arteries supply bronchi up to terminal bronchioles (not alveoli)

docs.title: LYMPHATIC DRAINAGE - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Superficial plexus: Visceral pleura → Hilum Deep plexus: Along bronchi & vessels

Lymph nodes (in order): 1. Pulmonary nodes: Within lung substance 2. Bronchopulmonary nodes: At hilum 3. Tracheobronchial nodes: Angle of bifurcation (carina) 4. Paratracheal nodes: Along trachea 5. Mediastinal trunks → Thoracic duct (left) or Right lymphatic duct

NEET PG: - Tracheobronchial nodes: Most commonly involved in bronchogenic carcinoma - Right lung drains to right side, Left lung to left side (BUT lower lobes can drain to opposite side)

docs.title: 1. Visceral Pleura
docs.text: Covers: Lung surface (including fissures) Innervation: Autonomic (no pain sensation) Blood supply: Bronchial arteries

docs.title: 2. Parietal Pleura
docs.text:
Parts: 1. Costal pleura: Lines ribs & intercostal muscles 2. Diaphragmatic pleura: Covers diaphragm 3. Mediastinal pleura: Covers mediastinum 4. Cervical pleura (Cupola): Covers apex

Innervation: - Costal & peripheral diaphragmatic: Intercostal nerves (pain sensation) - Central diaphragmatic & mediastinal: Phrenic nerve

Blood supply: Intercostal & internal thoracic arteries

NEET PG: Parietal pleura has pain sensation (visceral does not)

docs.title: Pleural Recesses
docs.text: Definition: Areas where parietal pleura folds on itself (potential spaces)

docs.title: Costodiaphragmatic Recess
docs.text: Location: Between costal & diaphragmatic pleura Importance: Deepest part (accumulation of fluid - pleural effusion) Level: - Mid-clavicular line: 8th rib - Mid-axillary line: 10th rib - Scapular line: 12th rib

docs.title: Costomediastinal Recess
docs.text:
Location: Between costal & mediastinal pleura (anterior) Cardiac notch: Left side (space for heart)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Pleural effusion: Gravitates to costodiaphragmatic recess - Thoracocentesis: Done at costodiaphragmatic recess (safe area)

docs.title: Pleural Margins
docs.text:
Anterior: - Both sides descend behind sternoclavicular joints - Meet at sternal angle (2nd costal cartilage) - Right: Descends to 6th costal cartilage - Left: Deviates laterally at 4th cartilage (cardiac notch)

Inferior (important for thoracocentesis): - Mid-clavicular line: 8th rib - Mid-axillary line: 10th rib - Scapular line: 12th rib

Posterior: 12th rib to T1

Mnemonic for inferior margin: "8-10-12" (MCL-MAL-Scapular line)

docs.title: Lung Margins
docs.text:
Lower border (2 ribs higher than pleura): - Mid-clavicular line: 6th rib - Mid-axillary line: 8th rib - Scapular line: 10th rib

NEET PG: Pleura extends 2 ribs below lung (costodiaphragmatic recess)

docs.title: Inspiration (Active)
docs.text:
Muscles: - Primary: Diaphragm (most important), External intercostals - Accessory: SCM, scalenes, pectoralis major/minor

Mechanism: - Diaphragm contracts → Descends - External intercostals contract → Ribs elevate - Thoracic cavity volume ↑ - Intrathoracic pressure ↓ - Air enters lungs

NEET PG: Diaphragm contributes 75% of inspiration

docs.title: Expiration (Passive at rest)
docs.text:
Muscles (active expiration): - Internal intercostals - Abdominal muscles

Mechanism: - Elastic recoil of lungs - Relaxation of inspiratory muscles - Thoracic cavity volume ↓ - Intrathoracic pressure ↑ - Air expelled

docs.title: Pneumothorax
docs.text:
Definition: Air in pleural cavity

Types: 1. Spontaneous: Primary (young, tall, thin males) or Secondary (COPD, TB) 2. Traumatic: Rib fracture, penetrating injury 3. Tension: One-way valve → Mediastinal shift → Circulatory collapse

Clinical features: - Sudden chest pain, dyspnea - Decreased breath sounds - Hyperresonant percussion - Absent tactile fremitus

NEET PG: - Tension pneumothorax: Emergency (needle decompression at 2nd ICS, MCL) - Treatment: Chest tube insertion (5th ICS, anterior to mid-axillary line)

docs.title: Pleural Effusion
docs.text:
Definition: Fluid in pleural cavity

Types: - Transudate: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome - Exudate: Infection (empyema), malignancy, PE

Clinical features: - Dyspnea - Decreased breath sounds - Dull percussion (Stony dull) - Decreased tactile fremitus

Diagnosis: Light's criteria (exudate vs transudate)

NEET PG: - Thoracocentesis site: 7th-8th ICS, posterior axillary line (costodiaphragmatic recess) - Complications: Pneumothorax, hemothorax, infection

docs.title: Lung Collapse (Atelectasis)
docs.text:
Causes: - Obstructive: Mucus plug, foreign body, tumor - Compressive: Pleural effusion, pneumothorax - Contraction: Pulmonary fibrosis - Adhesive: Surfactant deficiency (ARDS, NRDS)

Clinical: Decreased breath sounds, dull percussion, mediastinal shift toward collapse

NEET PG: Post-operative atelectasis common (inadequate cough, pain)

docs.title: Aspiration Pneumonia
docs.text:
Most common site: Right lower lobe (posterior segment) Reason: - Right main bronchus more vertical - Posterior segment dependent when supine

NEET PG: Foreign body/aspiration → Right lower lobe (posterior segment)

docs.title: Thoracocentesis (Pleural Tap) - PROCEDURE
docs.text:
Indications: Pleural effusion (diagnostic/therapeutic)

Site: 7th-8th ICS, mid-axillary/posterior axillary line Approach: Above the rib (avoid neurovascular bundle below rib)

Contraindications: - Bleeding disorders - Small effusion (<1 cm on US) - Mechanical ventilation

Complications: - Pneumothorax (most common) - Hemothorax - Re-expansion pulmonary edema - Infection

NEET PG High-Yield: - Insert needle ABOVE the rib (neurovascular bundle runs below) - Maximum safe drainage: 1-1.5 L (avoid re-expansion edema)

docs.title: Bronchoscopy
docs.text:
Indications: - Hemoptysis - Persistent cough - Suspected malignancy - Foreign body removal - Biopsy

Anatomy: - Carina easily visualized - Right main bronchus at 25° angle - Left main bronchus at 45° angle

NEET PG: Carina highly sensitive (touch → cough reflex)

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Foreign body most commonly enters? Answer: Right main bronchus (wider, shorter, more vertical)

  2. Number of bronchopulmonary segments in right lung? Answer: 10

  3. Only eparterial bronchus? Answer: Right upper lobe bronchus

  4. Nerve supply of central diaphragmatic pleura? Answer: Phrenic nerve

  5. Most common site for pleural effusion accumulation? Answer: Costodiaphragmatic recess

  6. Thoracocentesis - needle inserted where? Answer: Above the rib (7th-8th ICS)

  7. Most common site for aspiration pneumonia? Answer: Right lower lobe (posterior segment)

  8. Structure at sternal angle (T⅘)? Answer: Tracheal bifurcation (carina)

docs.title: Surface Marking (Inferior Pleural Margin)
docs.text: "8-10-12 rule" - **8**th rib: Mid-clavicular line - **10**th rib: Mid-axillary line - **12**th rib: Scapular line

docs.title: Right vs Left Main Bronchus
docs.text: "**R**ight is **R**eally **W**ide, **S**hort, **V**ertical" - **R**ight: **W**ider, **S**horter, More **V**ertical

docs.title: Hilum Arrangement
docs.text: "ABCD" - **A**rtery **B**ronchus **V**ein (anterior to posterior) But remember: Right = BAV (superior to inferior)

docs.title: Lobes and Fissures
docs.text: "Right has 3-2, Left has 2-1" - Right: 3 lobes, 2 fissures - Left: 2 lobes, 1 fissure

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know tracheal anatomy and relations
  • Understand bronchial tree structure
  • Learn bronchopulmonary segments
  • Memorize lung lobes and fissures
  • Know hilum structures arrangement
  • Understand pleural anatomy
  • Learn surface markings (8-10-12 rule)
  • Know thoracocentesis landmarks
  • Understand aspiration sites
  • Practice 30+ MCQs on respiratory system

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (3-4 questions)

docs.title: DIABETES MELLITUS - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text: Metabolic disorder characterized by chronic hyperglycemia due to: - Insulin deficiency (Type 1) - Insulin resistance (Type 2) - Both

docs.title: 1. TYPE 1 DIABETES (5-10%)
docs.text:
Pathophysiology: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β-cells Age: Usually <30 years (can occur at any age) Presentation: Acute onset, DKA common Body habitus: Lean Insulin: Absolute deficiency Ketosis: Prone Antibodies: Positive (GAD, IA-2, Insulin autoantibodies)

NEET PG: Anti-GAD antibodies = Type 1 DM

docs.title: 2. TYPE 2 DIABETES (90-95%)
docs.text:
Pathophysiology: Insulin resistance + relative insulin deficiency Age: Usually >40 years (increasing in youth) Presentation: Gradual onset, often asymptomatic Body habitus: Obese (80%) Insulin: Resistance ± relative deficiency Ketosis: Resistant (can develop HHS) Antibodies: Negative

NEET PG: Most common type, associated with obesity & metabolic syndrome

docs.title: 3. GESTATIONAL DIABETES (GDM)
docs.text: Definition: Diabetes diagnosed during pregnancy Screening: 24-28 weeks (75g OGTT) Risks: Macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycemia, maternal pre-eclampsia

docs.title: 4. OTHER SPECIFIC TYPES
docs.text:
• MODY (Maturity-Onset Diabetes of Young): Monogenic
  • Secondary: Pancreatitis, Cushing's syndrome, acromegaly, drugs (steroids, thiazides)
  • Pancreatic: Chronic pancreatitis, hemochromatosis, cystic fibrosis

NEET PG: MODY = Young onset, autosomal dominant, no insulin resistance

docs.title: Diagnostic Criteria (ADA 2023) - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Any ONE of the following:

Test Criteria Notes Fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL Fasting = No caloric intake for ≥8 hours 2-hour OGTT ≥200 mg/dL 75g glucose load HbA1c ≥6.5% Reflects 3-month average glucose Random plasma glucose ≥200 mg/dL + symptoms Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss

Prediabetes (IFG/IGT):

Test Prediabetes Normal Fasting glucose 100-125 mg/dL <100 mg/dL 2-hour OGTT 140-199 mg/dL <140 mg/dL HbA1c 5.7-6.4% <5.7%

NEET PG High-Yield: - HbA1c ≥6.5% = Diabetes - HbA1c 5.7-6.4% = Prediabetes - Fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL = Diabetes - HbA1c preferred (no fasting required)

docs.title: Classic Triad
docs.text:
1. Polyuria: ↑ Glucose → Osmotic diuresis
  2. Polydipsia: Compensatory thirst
  3. Polyphagia: Cellular starvation despite hyperglycemia
  4. Weight loss: Especially Type 1 (catabolism)

docs.title: Additional Features
docs.text:
• Blurred vision
  • Recurrent infections (UTI, candidiasis)
  • Slow wound healing
  • Fatigue
  • Acanthosis nigricans (Type 2, insulin resistance)

NEET PG: Acanthosis nigricans = Marker of insulin resistance

docs.title: 1. DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS (DKA)
docs.text:
Triad: 1. Hyperglycemia (>250 mg/dL) 2. Ketosis (↑ Ketones in blood/urine) 3. Metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3, HCO3 <15)

Causes: - Insulin omission (most common) - Infection (pneumonia, UTI) - MI, stroke, pancreatitis - New-onset Type 1 DM

Pathophysiology: 

Insulin deficiency + ↑ Counter-regulatory hormones
         ↓
    Hyperglycemia + Lipolysis
         ↓
    Ketogenesis (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate)
         ↓
    Metabolic acidosis

Clinical Features: - Polyuria, polydipsia - Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain - Kussmaul breathing (deep, rapid) - Fruity odor (acetone) - Dehydration - Altered sensorium

Investigations: - Hyperglycemia: >250 mg/dL - Anion gap metabolic acidosis: pH <7.3, ↑ Anion gap - Ketones: Positive (urine/blood) - K+: Initially high (acidosis) → Drops with treatment

NEET PG: - Kussmaul breathing: Compensatory respiratory alkalosis - Pseudohyponatremia: Corrected Na+ = Measured Na+ + 1.6 × [(Glucose - 100) / 100] - K+ monitoring: Critical (initially high → drops with insulin)

Treatment: 1. IV fluids: Normal saline (1 L in 1st hour) 2. Insulin: Regular insulin IV (0.1 U/kg/hr) 3. K+ replacement: When K+ <5.2 mEq/L (prevent hypokalemia) 4. Treat precipitating cause: Antibiotics for infection

NEET PG High-Yield: - Do NOT give K+ if: K+ >5.2 mEq/L or oliguria - Bicarbonate: Only if pH <6.9 (controversial) - Cerebral edema: Complication in children (too rapid correction)

docs.title: 2. HYPEROSMOLAR HYPERGLYCEMIC STATE (HHS)
docs.text:
Features: - Severe hyperglycemia: >600 mg/dL - Hyperosmolality: >320 mOsm/kg - NO significant ketosis: pH >7.3 - Altered sensorium: Confusion, coma

Occurs in: Type 2 DM (elderly)

NEET PG: HHS vs DKA: - HHS: Higher glucose (>600), no ketosis, higher mortality - DKA: Lower glucose (>250), ketosis present

Treatment: Similar to DKA but: - More fluids (severe dehydration) - Lower insulin doses (more insulin-sensitive)

docs.title: 3. HYPOGLYCEMIA
docs.text:
Definition: Blood glucose <70 mg/dL

Causes: - Excess insulin/sulfonylureas - Missed meals - Exercise - Alcohol

Whipple's Triad: 1. Symptoms of hypoglycemia 2. Low blood glucose 3. Relief with glucose

Symptoms: - Autonomic: Sweating, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, hunger - Neuroglycopenic: Confusion, seizures, coma

Treatment: - Conscious: 15g oral glucose (3 glucose tablets, juice) - Unconscious: IV dextrose (D50) or IM glucagon

NEET PG: Hypoglycemia most common acute complication of DM treatment

docs.title: 1. DIABETIC RETINOPATHY
docs.text:
Most common cause of blindness in adults (20-74 years)

Classification:

A. Non-Proliferative (NPDR): - Mild: Microaneurysms - Moderate: Microaneurysms + dot-blot hemorrhages + hard exudates - Severe: 4-2-1 rule: - Hemorrhages in 4 quadrants - Venous beading in 2 quadrants - IRMA in 1 quadrant

B. Proliferative (PDR): - Neovascularization (NVD - disc, NVE - elsewhere) - Vitreous hemorrhage - Retinal detachment

Macular Edema: Leading cause of vision loss

NEET PG High-Yield: - Screening: Annual fundoscopy from diagnosis (Type 2), 5 years after diagnosis (Type 1) - Treatment: Laser photocoagulation, anti-VEGF (bevacizumab, ranibizumab) - 4-2-1 rule: Severe NPDR (high risk of progression)

docs.title: 2. DIABETIC NEPHROPATHY
docs.text:
Leading cause of ESRD worldwide

Stages (Mogensen):

Stage Name GFR Albuminuria Pathology 1 Hyperfiltration ↑ - Glomerular hypertrophy 2 Silent Normal/↑ - Thickening of GBM 3 Microalbuminuria Normal/↓ 30-300 mg/day Mesangial expansion 4 Overt nephropathy ↓ >300 mg/day Nodular glomerulosclerosis 5 ESRD ↓↓ Massive End-stage kidney

Pathology: - Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules: Nodular glomerulosclerosis (pathognomonic) - Hyaline arteriolosclerosis (both afferent & efferent)

NEET PG: - Microalbuminuria: Earliest clinical sign (30-300 mg/day) - ACE inhibitors/ARBs: Slow progression (reduce intraglomerular pressure) - Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules: Diabetic nephropathy

Screening: Annual urine albumin-creatinine ratio

docs.title: 3. DIABETIC NEUROPATHY
docs.text:
A. Peripheral Neuropathy (Most common) - Distal symmetric polyneuropathy: "Glove & stocking" distribution - Symptoms: Numbness, tingling, burning pain (worse at night) - Loss of: Vibration, proprioception, ankle reflex - Complications: Diabetic foot ulcers, Charcot joint

B. Autonomic Neuropathy - Cardiovascular: Resting tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, silent MI - GI: Gastroparesis, diarrhea, constipation - GU: Erectile dysfunction, neurogenic bladder - Sudomotor: Anhidrosis

C. Mononeuropathy - Cranial nerves: CN III (pupil-sparing), CN VI - Median, ulnar, peroneal nerves

NEET PG: - Loss of ankle reflex: Earliest sign - Treatment: Gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine (for neuropathic pain) - CN III palsy: Pupil-sparing (microvascular, not aneurysm)

docs.title: 1. Coronary Artery Disease (CAD)
docs.text:
• 2-4× risk in diabetics
  • Silent MI: Autonomic neuropathy

docs.title: 2. Stroke
docs.text: • 2-3× risk

docs.title: 3. Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD)
docs.text:
• Claudication, gangrene
  • Diabetic foot: Neuropathy + PAD + infection

NEET PG: Diabetes = CAD equivalent (same treatment as CAD)

docs.title: Diabetic Foot - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Wagner Classification: - Grade 0: No ulcer, high-risk foot - Grade 1: Superficial ulcer - Grade 2: Deep ulcer (tendon/joint) - Grade 3: Abscess/osteomyelitis - Grade 4: Forefoot gangrene - Grade 5: Whole foot gangrene

Management: - Debridement - Antibiotics (if infected) - Offloading (avoid weight-bearing) - Glycemic control - Revascularization (if PAD)

NEET PG: Neuropathy + PAD + infection = Diabetic foot

docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes
docs.text:
Insulin (lifelong)

Regimens: 1. Basal-bolus: Most physiologic - Basal: Long-acting (glargine, detemir) - once/twice daily - Bolus: Rapid-acting (lispro, aspart) - before meals

  1. Premixed insulin: Fixed ratio (70/30, 50/50)

NEET PG: Basal-bolus most physiologic, best HbA1c control

docs.title: Type 2 Diabetes
docs.text:
Stepwise Approach:

1st Line: Metformin - Mechanism: ↓ Hepatic gluconeogenesis, ↑ Peripheral glucose uptake - Advantages: Weight loss, ↓ CV risk, no hypoglycemia - Adverse effects: GI upset, lactic acidosis (rare) - Contraindications: eGFR <30 ml/min, contrast studies, acidosis

2nd Line (if HbA1c target not met): - SGLT2 inhibitors (Empagliflozin, Dapagliflozin) - ↓ Glucose reabsorption in kidney - Benefits: Weight loss, ↓ CV risk, ↓ HF hospitalization - Adverse effects: UTI, genital infections, euglycemic DKA

  • GLP-1 agonists (Liraglutide, Semaglutide)
  • ↑ Insulin, ↓ Glucagon, ↓ Appetite
  • Benefits: Weight loss, ↓ CV risk

  • Adverse effects: Nausea, pancreatitis

  • DPP-4 inhibitors (Sitagliptin, Vildagliptin)

  • ↑ GLP-1, ↑ Insulin

  • Neutral on weight, CV

  • Sulfonylureas (Glimepiride, Gliclazide)

  • ↑ Insulin secretion

  • Adverse effects: Hypoglycemia, weight gain

  • Thiazolidinediones (Pioglitazone)

  • ↑ Insulin sensitivity
  • Adverse effects: Weight gain, edema, heart failure, fractures

3rd Line: Insulin (if inadequate control)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Metformin: 1st line, contraindicated if eGFR <30 - SGLT2i: Preferred if HF or CKD - GLP-1 agonist: Preferred if obesity or ASCVD - Sulfonylureas: Risk of hypoglycemia - Pioglitazone: Contraindicated in heart failure

docs.title: Target Goals (ADA 2023)
docs.text:
