docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
• Boggy uterus despite massage
  • Intermittent bleeding

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Placental inspection: Missing cotyledon/vessels going to edge
  • Bimanual examination: Uterus larger than expected
  • Ultrasound: Echogenic material in uterine cavity

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
• Manual removal of placenta (under anesthesia)
  • Uterine curettage (if fragments)
  • Oxytocics

NEET PG: Retained placenta = Not delivered within 30 minutes → Manual removal

docs.title: Abnormal Placentation
docs.text:
Type Definition Incidence Placenta accreta Adherent to myometrium (no decidua basalis) 80% Placenta increta Invades into myometrium 15% Placenta percreta Penetrates through serosa (bladder, bowel) 5%

Risk Factors: - Previous cesarean section (MOST IMPORTANT) - Placenta previa - Multiparity - Previous uterine surgery

Diagnosis: - Antenatal: USG, MRI (loss of retroplacental clear zone) - Delivery: Placenta does not separate, bleeding

Management: - Cesarean hysterectomy (placenta left in situ) - Avoid forceful placental removal (massive hemorrhage)

NEET PG: Placenta accreta = Previous cesarean + Placenta previa (high risk)

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
Pre-existing: - Von Willebrand disease - Hemophilia carrier - ITP - Anticoagulant therapy

Acquired: - DIC (most common in obstetrics) - HELLP syndrome - Massive transfusion (dilutional coagulopathy)

docs.title: DIC in Obstetrics - Causes
docs.text:
"ASAP" - **A**bruption (placental abruption) - MOST COMMON - **S**epsis - **A**mniotic fluid embolism - **P**re-eclampsia/HELLP

Clinical Features: - Generalized oozing (IV sites, gums) - No clot formation - Petechiae, ecchymoses

Diagnosis: - ↑ PT, ↑ aPTT - ↓ Platelets (<100,000) - ↓ Fibrinogen (<200 mg/dL) - ↑ D-dimer, ↑ FDP

Management: - Treat underlying cause - FFP (fresh frozen plasma) - Cryoprecipitate (if fibrinogen <100 mg/dL) - Platelet transfusion (if <50,000)

NEET PG: Placental abruption = Most common obstetric cause of DIC

docs.title: Signs of Shock
docs.text:
• Tachycardia (>100 bpm)
  • Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg)
  • Tachypnea
  • Oliguria (<30 mL/hr)
  • Altered consciousness
  • Cold, clammy skin

docs.title: Blood Loss Estimation (often underestimated)
docs.text:
Blood Loss Clinical Features 500-1000 mL Tachycardia (100-110), normal BP 1000-1500 mL Tachycardia (110-120), mild hypotension, anxiety 1500-2000 mL Tachycardia (>120), hypotension, oliguria, confusion >2000 mL Severe shock, collapse, anuria

NEET PG: Tachycardia is earliest sign of hypovolemia

docs.title: Active Management of Third Stage of Labor (AMTSL)
docs.text:
Components (after delivery of baby): 1. Oxytocin 10 units IM/IV immediately after anterior shoulder 2. Controlled cord traction (after signs of placental separation) 3. Uterine massage after placental delivery

Benefits: ↓ PPH by 60%, ↓ severe PPH, ↓ need for transfusion

NEET PG: AMTSL = Oxytocin + Controlled cord traction + Uterine massage (reduces PPH)

docs.title: Initial Resuscitation (ABCD)
docs.text: A**irway: Maintain patency **B**reathing: O2 15 L/min **C**irculation: - **2 large-bore IV cannulae (14-16G) - Rapid IV fluids (crystalloids: NS/RL) - Blood sampling: CBC, coagulation profile, cross-match **D**rugs: Uterotonics

docs.title: 1. TONE (Uterine Atony) - MOST COMMON
docs.text:
Step-by-step approach:

Step 1: Uterine Massage - Bimanual uterine compression - Rub fundus to stimulate contraction

Step 2: Uterotonics (in order of use)

A. Oxytocin (First-line): - 10 units IM/IV bolus - 20-40 units in 1L NS IV infusion - Mechanism: Contracts uterus - Side effects: Hypotension (if rapid IV), water intoxication

B. Methylergometrine (Second-line): - 0.2 mg IM (can repeat after 15 min) - Mechanism: Sustained uterine contraction - Contraindications: Hypertension, pre-eclampsia, heart disease - Side effects: HTN, nausea, vomiting

C. Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) (Third-line): - 250 μg IM (can repeat every 15 min, max 8 doses) - Mechanism: Uterine contraction - Contraindications: Asthma (bronchospasm) - Side effects: Diarrhea, fever, bronchospasm

D. Misoprostol (PGE1): - 800-1000 μg (PR/sublingual) - Mechanism: Uterine contraction - Advantage: Stable at room temperature, cheap - Side effects: Fever, shivering

NEET PG High-Yield: - First-line: Oxytocin - Methylergometrine: Contraindicated in hypertension - Carboprost: Contraindicated in asthma

Step 3: Uterine Tamponade - Intrauterine balloon (Bakri balloon, Foley catheter) - Fill with 300-500 mL saline - Provides tamponade effect

Step 4: Conservative Surgical

Uterine compression sutures: - B-Lynch suture: Brace suture (most common) - Cho suture

Pelvic vessel ligation: - Uterine artery ligation - Internal iliac (hypogastric) artery ligation

Step 5: Radiological - Uterine artery embolization (if available, hemodynamically stable)

Step 6: Hysterectomy (Last Resort) - Subtotal hysterectomy preferred (faster, less blood loss) - Total hysterectomy if cervical involvement

NEET PG: Management ladder = Massage → Oxytocin → Methylergometrine → Carboprost → Balloon → Surgery

docs.title: 2. TRAUMA
docs.text:
Cervical/Vaginal Lacerations: - Speculum examination (good lighting, anesthesia) - Suturing (absorbable sutures)

Uterine Rupture: - Emergency laparotomy - Repair (if feasible) or Hysterectomy

Uterine Inversion: - Manual replacement (Johnson's maneuver): - Cup inverted fundus in palm - Push upward through cervix - Do NOT remove placenta if still attached (causes bleeding) - Tocolytics (relax uterus): Terbutaline, nitroglycerin, halothane - After replacement: Oxytocics

NEET PG: Uterine inversion = Manual replacement BEFORE removing placenta

docs.title: 3. TISSUE
docs.text:
Retained Placenta: - Manual removal (under anesthesia) - Controlled cord traction - Oxytocics after removal

Retained Placental Fragments: - Uterine curettage (gentle) - Ultrasound guidance

docs.title: 4. THROMBIN
docs.text: DIC Management: - Treat underlying cause (deliver baby, treat sepsis) - Component therapy: - FFP: 15-20 mL/kg (if PT/aPTT >1.5× normal) - Cryoprecipitate: If fibrinogen <100 mg/dL (10 units) - Platelets: If <50,000 (10 units) - PRBC: Maintain Hb >7 g/dL

docs.title: Blood Transfusion
docs.text:
Indications: - Hb <7 g/dL - Ongoing bleeding - Hemodynamic instability

Massive Transfusion Protocol: - PRBC : FFP : Platelets = 1 : 1 : 1 ratio

NEET PG: Massive transfusion = 1:1:1 ratio (PRBC:FFP:Platelets)

docs.title: Immediate
docs.text:
1. Hypovolemic shock
  2. DIC
  3. Renal failure (acute tubular necrosis)
  4. ARDS
  5. Multi-organ failure
  6. Death

docs.title: Long-term
docs.text:
1. Sheehan syndrome (Pituitary necrosis):
  2. Failure of lactation
  3. Amenorrhea
  4. Hypothyroidism

  5. Adrenal insufficiency

  6. Asherman syndrome: Intrauterine adhesions (if aggressive curettage)

  7. Anemia

NEET PG: Sheehan syndrome = Postpartum pituitary necrosis (failure of lactation, amenorrhea)

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
1. Retained products of conception (most common)
  2. Endometritis
  3. Subinvolution of uterus
  4. Gestational trophoblastic disease

docs.title: Clinical Features
docs.text:
• Delayed bleeding (>24 hours)
  • Foul-smelling lochia (if infection)
  • Fever (endometritis)
  • Open cervical os

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Ultrasound: Retained products (echogenic material)
  • Speculum exam: Visualize cervix, rule out trauma

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
• Antibiotics (if infection): Broad-spectrum
  • Uterine curettage (if retained products)
  • Oxytocics

NEET PG: Secondary PPH = Usually retained products → Curettage

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Most common cause of PPH? Answer: Uterine atony (70%)

  2. Definition of PPH? Answer: Blood loss ≥500 mL (vaginal) or ≥1000 mL (cesarean) within 24 hours

  3. Contraindication to methylergometrine? Answer: Hypertension, pre-eclampsia

  4. Contraindication to carboprost? Answer: Asthma

  5. First-line uterotonic? Answer: Oxytocin

  6. Bleeding despite contracted uterus suggests? Answer: Genital tract trauma (do speculum exam)

  7. Most common obstetric cause of DIC? Answer: Placental abruption

  8. Uterine inversion management? Answer: Manual replacement (Johnson's maneuver) + Tocolytics

docs.title: Causes of PPH - "4 Ts"
docs.text:
• **T**one (Atony) - 70%
  • **T**rauma
  • **T**issue (Retained)
  • **T**hrombin (Coagulopathy)

docs.title: Uterotonics (Order of Use)
docs.text: "**O**xytocin **M**ary **C**arried **M**isoprostol" - **O**xytocin (1st line) - **M**ethylergometrine (2nd line) - **C**arboprost (3rd line) - **M**isoprostol

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text: "Methyl is Hyper, Carbo makes you Wheeze" - Methylergometrine: Hypertension - Carboprost: Asthma (wheeze)

docs.title: AMTSL
docs.text: "OCM" - **O**xytocin - **C**ontrolled cord traction - **M**assage

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know definition of PPH (primary vs secondary)
  • Learn "4 Ts" causes with percentages
  • Understand risk factors for uterine atony
  • Know perineal tear classification
  • Learn AMTSL components
  • Memorize uterotonics (order, contraindications)
  • Understand management ladder for atonic PPH
  • Know management of uterine inversion
  • Learn complications (Sheehan syndrome)
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on PPH

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 5-7 questions)

docs.title: CELL INJURY & ADAPTATION - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Cells respond to stress by: 1. Adaptation (reversible) 2. Injury (reversible or irreversible) 3. Death (irreversible)

docs.title: 1. HYPERTROPHY
docs.text:
Definition: Increase in cell size → Increase in organ size Mechanism: ↑ Protein synthesis, ↑ organelles Type: Physiological or Pathological

Examples:

Physiological Pathological Skeletal muscle (exercise) Left ventricular hypertrophy (HTN, AS) Uterus in pregnancy Bladder wall (BPH) Right ventricular hypertrophy (COPD)

NEET PG: Cardiac myocytes undergo only hypertrophy (cannot divide)

docs.title: 2. HYPERPLASIA
docs.text:
Definition: Increase in cell number → Increase in organ size Mechanism: ↑ Cell division (mitosis) Requirement: Cells must be capable of division

Types:

docs.title: A. Physiological Hyperplasia
docs.text:
• Hormonal: Breast during pregnancy, endometrium in menstrual cycle
  • Compensatory: Liver regeneration after partial hepatectomy

docs.title: B. Pathological Hyperplasia
docs.text:
• Endometrial hyperplasia (excess estrogen)
  • Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)
  • Thyroid goiter (TSH stimulation)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Hyperplasia can be precancerous (e.g., endometrial hyperplasia → Endometrial carcinoma) - Viral warts, Molluscum: Hyperplasia due to viral infection

docs.title: 3. ATROPHY
docs.text:
Definition: Decrease in cell size and number Mechanism: ↓ Protein synthesis, ↑ protein degradation (ubiquitin-proteasome, autophagy)

Causes - "**D**isuse **D**enervation **D**ecreased blood **I**schemia **N**utrition **H**ormone **P**ressure **A**ging"

Cause Example Disuse Immobilized limb, bedridden patient Denervation Polio, nerve injury Decreased blood supply Atherosclerosis Inadequate nutrition Marasmus, kwashiorkor Loss of endocrine stimulation Post-menopause (endometrium, breast) Pressure Brain atrophy from expanding tumor Aging Brain, reproductive organs

Microscopy: Lipofuscin (brown atrophy) - residual bodies in atrophic cells

NEET PG: Lipofuscin = "Wear and tear pigment"

docs.title: 4. METAPLASIA
docs.text:
Definition: Reversible change where one differentiated cell type is replaced by another Mechanism: Reprogramming of stem cells Significance: Adaptation to chronic stress, but PRECANCEROUS

Types:

docs.title: Epithelial Metaplasia
docs.text: Location From → To Cause Bronchi Ciliated columnar → Squamous Smoking Esophagus (Barrett's) Squamous → Columnar (intestinal type) GERD Cervix Columnar → Squamous Chronic irritation Bladder Transitional → Squamous Chronic infection, stones

docs.title: Mesenchymal Metaplasia
docs.text:
• Myositis ossificans: Muscle → Bone (after trauma)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Barrett's esophagus: Squamous → Columnar (intestinal metaplasia) - Risk factor for adenocarcinoma of esophagus - Marker: Goblet cells on biopsy - Bronchial metaplasia: Predisposes to squamous cell carcinoma

docs.title: 5. DYSPLASIA
docs.text:
Definition: Disordered growth, loss of uniformity and orientation Features: - Loss of polarity - Nuclear pleomorphism - ↑ Mitotic figures - Loss of cellular maturation

Grades: Mild → Moderate → Severe (Carcinoma in situ)

Reversibility: Can reverse if stimulus removed (e.g., smoking cessation)

NEET PG: - Dysplasia ≠ Cancer (no invasion of basement membrane) - Carcinoma in situ: Severe dysplasia but no invasion - Cervical dysplasia (CIN): HPV-related, precancerous

docs.title: Causes of Cell Injury
docs.text:
1. Hypoxia: Most common cause
  2. Physical agents: Trauma, temperature, radiation, electric shock
  3. Chemical agents: Toxins, drugs, poisons
  4. Infectious agents: Bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites
  5. Immunological: Autoimmune diseases, hypersensitivity
  6. Genetic: Sickle cell, inborn errors of metabolism
  7. Nutritional: Deficiency, excess

docs.title: Reversible Injury
docs.text:
Features: - Reduced ATP - Cell swelling - Blebs on cell membrane - Chromatin clumping - Mitochondria: Swollen but intact

Microscopy: - Cellular swelling (hydropic degeneration) - Fatty change (steatosis) - in liver, heart, kidney

NEET PG: Reversible if cause removed

docs.title: Irreversible Injury → NECROSIS
docs.text:
Point of No Return: - Severe mitochondrial damage (↓↓ ATP) - Membrane damage (lysosomal rupture) - Massive Ca2+ influx

Features: - Nucleus: Pyknosis → Karyorrhexis → Karyolysis - Cytoplasm: Eosinophilic (pink on H&E) - Cell membrane: Ruptured - Mitochondria: Disrupted

docs.title: 1. COAGULATIVE NECROSIS
docs.text:
Mechanism: Protein denaturation > Enzyme digestion Architecture: Preserved (ghost outline) Microscopy: Anucleate cells with preserved outline Common sites: All organs EXCEPT brain

Example: Myocardial infarction

NEET PG: Most common type of necrosis

docs.title: 2. LIQUEFACTIVE NECROSIS
docs.text:
Mechanism: Enzyme digestion > Protein denaturation Result: Liquid, viscous mass Common sites: Brain (lack of supporting stroma), Abscess (bacterial infection)

Example: - Brain infarction → Cystic space - Bacterial abscess → Pus

NEET PG: Brain infarction = Liquefactive necrosis

docs.title: 3. CASEOUS NECROSIS
docs.text:
Appearance: "Cheese-like" (caseous) Architecture: Lost (amorphous) Microscopy: Granular, amorphous debris Pathognomonic: Tuberculosis

NEET PG: Caseous necrosis = TB until proven otherwise

docs.title: 4. FAT NECROSIS
docs.text: Types:

docs.title: A. Enzymatic Fat Necrosis
docs.text:
Cause: Acute pancreatitis (lipase release) Mechanism: Lipase digests fat → Fatty acids + Glycerol Result: Chalky white deposits (Ca2+ soaps) Microscopy: Shadowy fat cells with basophilic Ca2+ deposits

NEET PG: Acute pancreatitis → ↑ Serum lipase → Fat necrosis

docs.title: B. Traumatic Fat Necrosis
docs.text: Cause: Trauma to adipose tissue (breast) Mechanism: Physical disruption of fat cells Result: Focal enzyme release by adipocytes

docs.title: 5. FIBRINOID NECROSIS
docs.text:
Sites: Blood vessel walls Causes: Immune-mediated injury - Polyarteritis nodosa - Acute rheumatic fever - Malignant hypertension

Microscopy: Bright pink (eosinophilic), amorphous material (fibrin deposition)

NEET PG: Vasculitis, malignant hypertension

docs.title: 6. GANGRENOUS NECROSIS
docs.text:
Definition: Necrosis + putrefaction (bacterial overgrowth)

Types:

Type Underlying Appearance Example Dry gangrene Coagulative Dry, shriveled Diabetic foot Wet gangrene Liquefactive Wet, swollen Gas gangrene (Clostridium) Gas gangrene Liquefactive Crepitus, foul smell C. perfringens infection

NEET PG: Gas gangrene = Clostridium perfringens

docs.title: APOPTOSIS vs NECROSIS
docs.text:
Feature Apoptosis Necrosis Cell size Reduced (shrinkage) Increased (swelling) Nucleus Fragmentation (karyorrhexis) Pyknosis → Karyolysis Membrane Intact Disrupted Contents Retained Released (inflammation) Inflammation NO YES Outcome Apoptotic bodies → Phagocytosed Enzymatic digestion Energy ATP-dependent ATP-independent Physiologic Yes (normal process) No (always pathologic)

NEET PG: Key difference = Apoptosis has NO inflammation

docs.title: Physiological Apoptosis
docs.text:
1. Embryogenesis: Interdigital web removal, organ development
  2. Hormone-dependent involution: Endometrium (menstruation), mammary gland (post-lactation)
  3. Cell turnover: Intestinal epithelium
  4. Immune system: Negative selection of T cells in thymus

docs.title: Pathological Apoptosis
docs.text:
1. DNA damage: Radiation, chemotherapy (p53-mediated)
  2. Viral infections: Hepatitis, HIV
  3. Atrophy: After duct obstruction (pancreas, kidney)

docs.title: 1. INTRINSIC PATHWAY (Mitochondrial)
docs.text:
Trigger: DNA damage, growth factor withdrawal, hypoxia Mechanism: - Bcl-2 family proteins (Bax, Bak → pro-apoptotic) - Mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization - Cytochrome c release - Apoptosome formation (Cytochrome c + Apaf-1 + Caspase-9) - Executioner caspases (3, 6, 7) activation

Regulators: - Pro-apoptotic: Bax, Bak, Bad, Bid - Anti-apoptotic: Bcl-2, Bcl-xL

NEET PG: Bcl-2 (anti-apoptotic) overexpressed in follicular lymphoma (t(14;18))

docs.title: 2. EXTRINSIC PATHWAY (Death Receptor)
docs.text:
Trigger: Fas ligand (FasL), TNF Mechanism: - FasL binds Fas (CD95) receptor - FADD (Fas-associated death domain) recruitment - Caspase-8 activation - Executioner caspases activation

NEET PG: Used by cytotoxic T cells (CTL) to kill infected/cancer cells

docs.title: Morphology of Apoptosis
docs.text:
Light Microscopy: - Cell shrinkage - Chromatin condensation (pyknosis) - Nuclear fragmentation (karyorrhexis) - Apoptotic bodies (membrane-bound fragments)

Electron Microscopy: - Chromatin condensation at nuclear periphery - Membrane blebs - Intact organelles

Detection: - TUNEL assay: Detects DNA fragmentation - Annexin V staining: Detects phosphatidylserine on outer membrane

docs.title: Steatosis (Fatty Change)
docs.text:
Sites: Liver, heart, kidney Causes: Alcohol, obesity, diabetes, toxins, hypoxia Mechanism: - ↑ Fatty acid entry - ↑ Fatty acid synthesis - ↓ Fatty acid oxidation - ↓ Lipoprotein secretion

Microscopy: Clear vacuoles in cytoplasm (H&E), red with Sudan stain

NEET PG: - Alcohol: Most common cause of fatty liver - Reversible if cause removed

docs.title: Cholesterol
docs.text:
• Atherosclerosis: Foam cells in arterial intima
  • Xanthomas: Cholesterol deposits in skin

docs.title: 2. PROTEINS
docs.text:
Causes: - Reabsorption droplets (PCT in nephrotic syndrome) - Russell bodies (immunoglobulins in plasma cells) - Mallory bodies (alcoholic hepatitis - eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions) - Neurofibrillary tangles (Alzheimer's - tau protein)

NEET PG: Mallory bodies = Alcoholic hepatitis

docs.title: 3. GLYCOGEN
docs.text: Examples: - Diabetes: Glycogen in renal tubules, liver - Glycogen storage diseases (Von Gierke, Pompe)

docs.title: Exogenous
docs.text:
• Carbon (anthracosis): Coal miners
  • Tattoos: Tattoo pigment in macrophages

docs.title: Endogenous
docs.text:
A. Lipofuscin - "Wear and tear pigment" - Brown, granular, autofluorescent - Sites: Liver, heart, brain (aging)

B. Melanin - Sites: Skin, hair, eyes - Deficiency: Albinism - Excess: Melanoma

C. Hemosiderin - Iron storage form - Prussian blue stain (blue color) - Causes: - Local: Hemorrhage, bruises - Systemic: Hemochromatosis, transfusions

NEET PG: - Hemosiderin: Prussian blue positive - Hemochromatosis: "Bronze diabetes" (↑ iron deposition)

docs.title: 1. DYSTROPHIC CALCIFICATION
docs.text:
Definition: Ca2+ deposition in dead/dying tissue Serum Ca2+: Normal

Causes: - Atherosclerotic plaques - Old TB lesions (Ghon complex) - Heart valves (calcific aortic stenosis) - Fat necrosis (pancreatitis)

Mechanism: ↑ pH in necrotic tissue → Ca2+ precipitation

NEET PG: Normal serum Ca2+, calcification in abnormal tissue

docs.title: 2. METASTATIC CALCIFICATION
docs.text:
Definition: Ca2+ deposition in normal tissue Serum Ca2+: Elevated (hypercalcemia)

Causes of Hypercalcemia: - Hyperparathyroidism - Vitamin D toxicity - Sarcoidosis - Malignancy (bone metastases, PTHrP) - Renal failure

Sites (tissues with alkaline environment): - Kidneys (nephrocalcinosis) - Gastric mucosa - Lungs - Blood vessels

NEET PG: High serum Ca2+, calcification in normal tissue

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Most common type of necrosis? Answer: Coagulative necrosis

  2. Type of necrosis in brain infarction? Answer: Liquefactive necrosis

  3. Hallmark of caseous necrosis? Answer: Tuberculosis

  4. Barrett's esophagus is an example of? Answer: Metaplasia (squamous → columnar)

  5. Wear and tear pigment? Answer: Lipofuscin

  6. Apoptosis vs necrosis - key difference? Answer: Apoptosis has NO inflammation

  7. Stain for hemosiderin? Answer: Prussian blue

  8. Anti-apoptotic protein? Answer: Bcl-2

docs.title: Types of Necrosis
docs.text: "**C**ows **L**ike **C**heese **F**at **F**ood **G**enerally" - **C**oagulative (MI) - **L**iquefactive (Brain) - **C**aseous (TB) - **F**at (Pancreatitis) - **F**ibrinoid (Vasculitis) - **G**angrenous (Ischemic limb)

docs.title: Causes of Atrophy
docs.text: "**D**on't **D**o **D**rugs **I**n **N**ew **H**ampshire **P**lease **A**nymore" - **D**isuse - **D**enervation - **D**ecreased blood supply - **I**nadequate nutrition - **N**o hormones - **H**igh pressure - **P**ressure - **A**ging

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Understand all cellular adaptations
  • Differentiate hypertrophy vs hyperplasia
  • Know all types of necrosis with examples
  • Understand apoptosis pathways
  • Differentiate apoptosis vs necrosis
  • Learn intracellular accumulations
  • Know dystrophic vs metastatic calcification
  • Memorize high-yield examples
  • Practice 35+ MCQs on cell injury

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 3-5 questions)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Inflammation: Complex biological response to harmful stimuli (pathogens, damaged cells, irritants)

Cardinal Signs (Celsus + Virchow): 1. Rubor (Redness) - Vasodilation 2. Tumor (Swelling) - Exudation 3. Calor (Heat) - ↑ Blood flow 4. Dolor (Pain) - Mediators (bradykinin, PGE2) 5. Functio laesa (Loss of function) - Combined effect

NEET PG: 5 cardinal signs of inflammation

docs.title: ACUTE INFLAMMATION
docs.text: Duration: Minutes to days Hallmark: Neutrophils (PMNs) Vascular changes + Cellular infiltration

docs.title: 1. Transient Vasoconstriction
docs.text: Duration: Few seconds Mechanism: Neurogenic

docs.title: 2. Vasodilation
docs.text: Duration: Sustained Mediators: Histamine, nitric oxide, prostaglandins Result: ↑ Blood flow → Redness & heat

docs.title: 3. Increased Vascular Permeability
docs.text:
Mechanism: Endothelial contraction, retraction, direct injury Result: Exudation of fluid → Edema & swelling

Stages of increased permeability: 1. Immediate transient (5-30 min): Histamine, bradykinin (most common) 2. Immediate sustained (hours): Severe injury 3. Delayed prolonged (2-12 hours): Sunburn, x-ray, bacterial toxins

NEET PG: Histamine causes immediate transient increase in permeability

docs.title: Serous Exudate
docs.text: Composition: Low protein (<3 g/dl), few cells Examples: Skin blister, pleural effusion (viral pleuritis)

docs.title: Fibrinous Exudate
docs.text:
Composition: High fibrin content Examples: Fibrinous pericarditis, lobar pneumonia (pneumococcus) Organization: Scarring (adhesions)

NEET PG: Fibrinous exudate → Organization → Adhesions

docs.title: Purulent Exudate (Suppurative)
docs.text:
Composition: Pus (neutrophils + necrotic debris + bacteria) Examples: Abscess, empyema, pyogenic infections Organisms: Staph aureus, Strep pyogenes, E. coli

Abscess: Localized collection of pus Empyema: Pus in body cavity (pleural, peritoneal)

docs.title: Hemorrhagic Exudate
docs.text: Composition: RBCs + other inflammatory cells Examples: Necrotizing infections (plague, anthrax), viral hemorrhagic fevers

docs.title: 1. Margination
docs.text: Definition: Leukocytes move to periphery of blood vessel Mechanism: Slowing of blood flow (vasodilation, ↑ viscosity)

docs.title: 2. Rolling
docs.text:
Molecules: Selectins (E-selectin, P-selectin on endothelium; L-selectin on leukocytes) Ligands: Sialyl-Lewis X (on leukocytes) Result: Leukocytes roll along endothelium

NEET PG: Selectins mediate rolling

docs.title: 3. Adhesion (Firm attachment)
docs.text:
Molecules: - Integrins on leukocytes (LFA-1, Mac-1, VLA-4) - ICAM-1, VCAM-1 on endothelium

Upregulated by: Cytokines (TNF, IL-1), bacterial products (LPS)

NEET PG: Integrins (LFA-1, Mac-1) mediate firm adhesion

docs.title: 4. Transmigration (Diapedesis)
docs.text: Definition: Migration through endothelium (between cells) Molecules: PECAM-1 (CD31) at intercellular junctions

docs.title: 5. Chemotaxis
docs.text:
Definition: Directional migration toward chemical gradient

Chemotactic Agents: - Bacterial products: fMLP (N-formyl-methionyl peptides), LPS - Complement: C5a (most potent) - Cytokines: IL-8, chemokines - Lipids: LTB4 (leukotriene B4)

NEET PG High-Yield: - C5a: Most potent chemotactic agent - LTB4: Lipid chemotactic agent - IL-8: Chemokine for neutrophils

docs.title: Recognition & Attachment
docs.text:
Mechanisms: 1. Opsonization (coating of particle): - IgG (Fc receptor) - C3b (complement receptor CR1) 2. Direct binding: Mannose receptors, scavenger receptors

NEET PG: Opsonization enhances phagocytosis (IgG & C3b = main opsonins)

docs.title: Engulfment
docs.text: Process: Pseudopods surround particle → Phagosome formation

docs.title: Killing & Degradation
docs.text:
A. Oxygen-Dependent (Respiratory Burst)

NADPH oxidase system: 

NADPH + 2O2 → 2O2- (superoxide) + NADP+ + H+
         ↓ (Superoxide dismutase)
        H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide)
         ↓ (Myeloperoxidase + Cl-)
        HOCl (hypochlorous acid) - MOST POTENT

NEET PG: - NADPH oxidase: Generates superoxide (respiratory burst) - Myeloperoxidase (MPO): H2O2 + Cl- → HOCl (bleach) - HOCl: Most potent bactericidal agent

Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD): - Defect: NADPH oxidase (X-linked or autosomal recessive) - Features: Recurrent catalase-positive bacterial/fungal infections (Staph, Serratia, Aspergillus, Nocardia) - Test: NBT test (nitroblue tetrazolium) - negative

NEET PG: CGD = Defective NADPH oxidase, recurrent Staph infections, negative NBT test

B. Oxygen-Independent - Lysozyme: Degrades bacterial cell wall - Lactoferrin: Binds iron - Defensins: Antimicrobial peptides - Proteases: Elastase, cathepsins

docs.title: Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency (LAD)
docs.text:
LAD-1: - Defect: CD18 (β2 integrin) deficiency - Affected molecules: LFA-1, Mac-1, CR3 - Features: Recurrent bacterial infections, delayed umbilical cord separation, leukocytosis (no margination), poor wound healing - Diagnosis: Flow cytometry (absent CD18)

LAD-2: - Defect: Sialyl-Lewis X (selectin ligand) - Features: Similar to LAD-1 + mental retardation

NEET PG High-Yield: - LAD-1: Defective β2 integrin (CD18) - Features: Delayed cord separation, leukocytosis, recurrent infections - No pus formation (neutrophils can't migrate)

docs.title: Chemical Mediators of Inflammation - HIGH YIELD
A. Vasoactive Amines
docs.text:
1. Histamine Source: Mast cells, basophils, platelets Stored: Preformed in granules Release: IgE (Type I hypersensitivity), C3a, C5a, trauma, cold Actions: - Vasodilation (H1 & H2 receptors) - ↑ Vascular permeability (H1 - venules) - Bronchoconstriction (H1) Duration: Short-lived (degraded by histaminase)

2. Serotonin (5-HT) Source: Platelets, mast cells (rodents) Actions: Similar to histamine

NEET PG: Histamine = Immediate transient permeability (first few minutes)

docs.title: B. Arachidonic Acid Metabolites
docs.text:
Source: Cell membrane phospholipids (via phospholipase A2)

Two pathways:

docs.title: 1. Cyclooxygenase (COX) Pathway → Prostaglandins & Thromboxanes
docs.text:
COX-1: Constitutive (gastric mucosa, platelets, kidneys) COX-2: Inducible (inflammatory cells)

Products: - PGE2, PGI2 (Prostacyclin): - Vasodilation - Potentiate edema (with histamine) - Pain & fever (PGE2) - TXA2 (Thromboxane A2): - Vasoconstriction - Platelet aggregation

NEET PG: - NSAIDs: Inhibit COX → ↓ PGE2 (anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, analgesic) - Aspirin: Irreversibly inhibits COX (antiplatelet effect - TXA2) - COX-2 selective: Less GI side effects (celecoxib)

docs.title: 2. Lipoxygenase Pathway → Leukotrienes
docs.text:
5-LOX (in leukocytes): - LTB4: Chemotaxis & leukocyte adhesion (most potent) - LTC4, LTD4, LTE4 (slow-reacting substance of anaphylaxis): - Bronchoconstriction - Vasoconstriction - ↑ Vascular permeability

NEET PG High-Yield: - LTB4: Neutrophil chemotaxis - LTC4, LTD4, LTE4: Bronchoconstriction (asthma) - Zileuton: 5-LOX inhibitor - Montelukast: Leukotriene receptor antagonist (asthma)

docs.title: C. Cytokines
docs.text:
1. TNF (Tumor Necrosis Factor) & IL-1 Source: Activated macrophages Actions: - Endothelial activation (↑ selectins, integrins) - Fever (act on hypothalamus) - Acute phase proteins (liver) - Systemic effects (shock in high doses)

2. IL-6 Actions: Acute phase protein synthesis (CRP, fibrinogen)

3. Chemokines IL-8 (CXCL8): Neutrophil chemotaxis MCP-1: Monocyte chemotaxis

NEET PG: TNF & IL-1 = Major pro-inflammatory cytokines

docs.title: D. Nitric Oxide (NO)
docs.text:
Source: Endothelial cells, macrophages Enzyme: Nitric oxide synthase (NOS) - eNOS: Constitutive (endothelium) - iNOS: Inducible (macrophages, inflammation)

Actions: - Vasodilation (smooth muscle relaxation) - Antimicrobial (in macrophages) - Inhibits platelet aggregation

NEET PG: NO = Endothelium-derived relaxing factor (EDRF)

docs.title: A. Complement System - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Activation pathways: 1. Classical: Antigen-antibody complexes (IgG, IgM) 2. Alternative: Bacterial cell walls (LPS, endotoxin) - NO antibody 3. Lectin: Mannose-binding lectin (MBL)

All converge at: C3 convertase → C3a + C3b

Functions: - C3a, C5a: Anaphylatoxins (histamine release, chemotaxis, vasodilation) - C5a: Most potent (chemotaxis >> C3a) - C3b: Opsonization (enhance phagocytosis) - C5b-9: Membrane attack complex (MAC) (cell lysis)

NEET PG High-Yield: - C5a: Most potent anaphylatoxin & chemotactic agent - C3b: Main opsonin - MAC (C5b-9): Lysis of Gram-negative bacteria, Neisseria

Complement deficiencies: - C1, C2, C4: SLE-like syndrome - C3: Recurrent pyogenic infections - C5-C9: Recurrent Neisseria infections - C1 inhibitor: Hereditary angioedema

NEET PG: C5-C9 deficiency = Recurrent Neisseria meningitidis infections

docs.title: B. Kinin System
docs.text:
Hageman factor (Factor XII) activation → Prekallikrein → Kallikrein → High molecular weight kininogen (HMWK) → Bradykinin

Bradykinin: - ↑ Vascular permeability - Vasodilation - Pain (most potent pain mediator) - Bronchoconstriction

Degraded by: ACE (angiotensin-converting enzyme)

NEET PG: Bradykinin = Pain mediator, degraded by ACE

docs.title: C. Coagulation & Fibrinolytic Systems
docs.text:
Factor XII (Hageman factor) activates: - Coagulation cascade → Thrombin - Kinin system → Bradykinin - Fibrinolytic system → Plasmin

Thrombin: Converts fibrinogen → Fibrin (fibrinous exudate)

NEET PG: Factor XII links inflammation, coagulation, kinin, fibrinolytic systems

docs.title: Outcomes of Acute Inflammation
docs.text:
1. Complete resolution: Removal of stimulus, no tissue damage
  2. Healing by scarring (fibrosis): Substantial tissue destruction
  3. Abscess formation: Purulent inflammation (pyogenic bacteria)
  4. Progression to chronic inflammation: Persistent stimulus

NEET PG: Resolution = Best outcome (restitutio ad integrum)

docs.title: CHRONIC INFLAMMATION
docs.text: Duration: Weeks to years Hallmark: Mononuclear cells (lymphocytes, macrophages, plasma cells) Tissue destruction + Repair (fibrosis, angiogenesis)

docs.title: Causes
docs.text:
1. Persistent infections: TB, syphilis, fungi, viruses
  2. Prolonged toxic exposure: Silica (silicosis), asbestos (asbestosis)
  3. Autoimmune diseases: RA, SLE, IBD
  4. Unknown: Sarcoidosis, Crohn's disease

NEET PG: Chronic inflammation can start de novo (NOT always from acute)

docs.title: 1. Macrophages (Most Important)
docs.text:
Derived from: Blood monocytes Activated by: IFN-γ (from T cells), bacterial products, cytokines

Functions: - Phagocytosis - Antigen presentation (MHC II) - Cytokine secretion: IL-1, IL-6, TNF, IL-12 - Growth factors: PDGF, FGF, TGF-β (fibrosis)

Products cause: - Tissue injury (ROS, proteases) - Fibrosis (TGF-β, PDGF)

Activated macrophages → Epithelioid cells (in granulomas)

NEET PG: Macrophage = Central cell in chronic inflammation

docs.title: 2. Lymphocytes
docs.text: T cells: Cell-mediated immunity, activate macrophages (IFN-γ) B cells: Antibody production → Plasma cells

docs.title: 3. Plasma Cells
docs.text: Function: Antibody secretion Seen in: Chronic inflammation, autoimmune diseases

docs.title: 4. Eosinophils
docs.text: Seen in: Parasitic infections, allergic reactions, tumors (Hodgkin lymphoma) Products: Major basic protein (MBP), eosinophil cationic protein

docs.title: 5. Mast Cells
docs.text:
Products: Histamine, heparin, tryptase, cytokines

NEET PG: Mast cells = Type I hypersensitivity (IgE-mediated)

docs.title: GRANULOMATOUS INFLAMMATION - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Definition: Chronic inflammation with epithelioid cell granulomas

Granuloma: Focal collection of activated macrophages (epithelioid cells) ± giant cells

docs.title: 1. Foreign Body Granuloma
docs.text: Cause: Inert foreign material (sutures, talc, silica) Giant cells: Foreign body giant cells (haphazard nuclei)

docs.title: 2. Immune Granuloma
docs.text:
Cause: T cell-mediated immunity (poorly degradable antigens) Examples: TB, sarcoidosis, leprosy, Crohn's disease, fungal infections

Structure: - Center: Epithelioid cells (activated macrophages with abundant pink cytoplasm) - Periphery: Lymphocytes, fibroblasts - Giant cells: Langhans giant cells (horseshoe-shaped nuclei at periphery) - Caseous necrosis: TB (caseating granuloma)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Caseating granuloma: TB, histoplasmosis - Non-caseating granuloma: Sarcoidosis, Crohn's, leprosy, cat-scratch disease - Langhans giant cells: TB, sarcoidosis (nuclei at periphery) - Foreign body giant cells: Nuclei randomly distributed

docs.title: Specific Granulomatous Diseases
docs.text:
Disease Type Features Tuberculosis Caseating Langhans cells, central caseous necrosis, acid-fast bacilli Sarcoidosis Non-caseating Schaumann bodies, asteroid bodies, epithelioid granulomas Leprosy Non-caseating Foamy macrophages (lepra cells in lepromatous), no giant cells Cat-scratch disease Necrotizing Stellate abscess, Bartonella henselae Crohn's disease Non-caseating Transmural inflammation, fissures

NEET PG: - Schaumann bodies: Sarcoidosis (lamellated calcified structures) - Asteroid bodies: Sarcoidosis (star-shaped inclusions in giant cells)

docs.title: 1. Fever
docs.text:
Pyrogens: - Exogenous: Bacterial LPS (endotoxin) - Endogenous: IL-1, IL-6, TNF

Mechanism: Act on hypothalamus → ↑ PGE2 → ↑ Temp set point

NEET PG: NSAIDs reduce fever by inhibiting PGE2 synthesis

docs.title: 2. Acute Phase Response
docs.text:
Mediators: IL-6, IL-1, TNF Liver produces: - Positive acute phase proteins (↑): CRP, fibrinogen, serum amyloid A (SAA), haptoglobin, ferritin - Negative acute phase proteins (↓): Albumin, transferrin

CRP (C-reactive protein): - Opsonin (binds C-polysaccharide of pneumococcus) - Marker of inflammation - Normal <1 mg/dL

ESR (Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate): - ↑ in inflammation (due to ↑ fibrinogen)

NEET PG: - CRP: Best acute phase reactant (rises within 6 hours) - ESR: Slow to rise, non-specific

docs.title: 3. Leukocytosis
docs.text:
Neutrophilia: Bacterial infections Eosinophilia: Parasites, allergies Lymphocytosis: Viral infections, chronic inflammation

Left shift: ↑ Band forms (immature neutrophils) in blood → Indicates acute bacterial infection

NEET PG: Left shift = Acute bacterial infection

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Hallmark cell of acute inflammation? Answer: Neutrophils

  2. Most potent chemotactic agent? Answer: C5a

  3. Mediator of rolling in leukocyte extravasation? Answer: Selectins

  4. Mediator of firm adhesion? Answer: Integrins (LFA-1, Mac-1)

  5. Defect in Chronic Granulomatous Disease? Answer: NADPH oxidase

  6. Hallmark of chronic inflammation? Answer: Mononuclear cells (macrophages, lymphocytes, plasma cells)

  7. Caseating granuloma seen in? Answer: Tuberculosis

  8. Best acute phase reactant? Answer: CRP

docs.title: Cardinal Signs
docs.text: "**R**ed **T**omato **C**an **D**estroy **F**ood" - **R**ubor (redness) - **T**umor (swelling) - **C**alor (heat) - **D**olor (pain) - **F**unctio laesa (loss of function)

docs.title: Leukocyte Extravasation
docs.text: "**M**ary **R**olls **A**long **T**o **C**hurch" - **M**argination - **R**olling (selectins) - **A**dhesion (integrins) - **T**ransmigration - **C**hemotaxis

docs.title: Caseating Granulomas
docs.text: "**T**wo **H**ot dogs" - **T**uberculosis - **H**istoplasmosis

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know cardinal signs of inflammation
  • Understand vascular changes sequence
  • Learn leukocyte extravasation steps
  • Memorize chemical mediators
  • Know arachidonic acid pathways
  • Understand complement functions
  • Differentiate acute vs chronic inflammation
  • Learn granulomatous diseases
  • Know acute phase proteins
  • Practice 35+ MCQs on inflammation

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Neoplasm: New growth with uncontrolled proliferation, autonomous (independent of physiological signals), irreversible

Tumor: Swelling (can be neoplastic or non-neoplastic)

Cancer: Malignant neoplasm

docs.title: BENIGN vs MALIGNANT TUMORS
docs.text:
Feature Benign Malignant Differentiation Well-differentiated Poorly differentiated/anaplastic Growth rate Slow Fast Mitoses Rare, normal Numerous, atypical Nuclear features Normal Hyperchromatic, pleomorphic, ↑ N:C ratio Capsule Usually present Absent Local invasion Absent (expansile growth) Present (infiltrative) Metastasis Never Characteristic Necrosis Rare Common Recurrence Rare (after excision) Common Effect on host Minimal (unless location/hormone) Cachexia, death

NEET PG High-Yield: - Metastasis = Hallmark of malignancy - Benign tumors NEVER metastasize - Anaplasia: Lack of differentiation (pleomorphism, ↑ N:C ratio, atypical mitoses)

docs.title: Benign Tumors
docs.text:
Epithelial: - -oma: Adenoma, papilloma - Cyst: Cystadenoma

Mesenchymal: - Cell type + -oma: - Lipoma (fat) - Fibroma (fibrous tissue) - Chondroma (cartilage) - Osteoma (bone) - Leiomyoma (smooth muscle) - Rhabdomyoma (skeletal muscle)

NEET PG: Benign tumors end in "-oma" (with exceptions)

docs.title: Malignant Tumors
docs.text:
Epithelial (Carcinoma): - Squamous cell carcinoma: From squamous epithelium - Adenocarcinoma: From glandular epithelium - Transitional cell carcinoma: From transitional epithelium (bladder)

Mesenchymal (Sarcoma): - Cell type + -sarcoma: - Liposarcoma (fat) - Fibrosarcoma (fibrous tissue) - Chondrosarcoma (cartilage) - Osteosarcoma (bone) - Leiomyosarcoma (smooth muscle) - Rhabdomyosarcoma (skeletal muscle)

Mixed: - Teratoma: All 3 germ layers (mature = benign, immature = malignant) - Carcinosarcoma: Mixed carcinoma + sarcoma (e.g., uterus)

NEET PG: - Carcinoma: Epithelial malignancy (80% of cancers) - Sarcoma: Mesenchymal malignancy (rare)

docs.title: Exceptions to Nomenclature
docs.text:
Benign but NOT "-oma": - Hamartoma: Disorganized tissue (normal to site) - e.g., pulmonary hamartoma - Choristoma: Normal tissue in abnormal location - e.g., pancreatic tissue in stomach wall

Malignant but end in "-oma": - Melanoma (malignant) - Lymphoma (malignant) - Mesothelioma (malignant) - Hepatoma (hepatocellular carcinoma) - Seminoma (testicular cancer)

NEET PG: Melanoma, lymphoma, mesothelioma are MALIGNANT (despite "-oma")

docs.title: Oncogenes (Accelerators)
docs.text:
Definition: Mutated proto-oncogenes → Promote cell proliferation

Mechanisms of activation: 1. Point mutation: RAS (most common oncogene in human cancers) 2. Gene amplification: MYCN (neuroblastoma), HER2/NEU (breast cancer) 3. Chromosomal translocation: BCR-ABL (CML), c-MYC (Burkitt's lymphoma)

Common oncogenes:

Oncogene Tumor Mechanism RAS Colon, pancreas, lung Point mutation (most common oncogene) MYC Burkitt's lymphoma t(8;14) translocation BCR-ABL CML t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome HER2/NEU (ERBB2) Breast cancer Gene amplification N-MYC Neuroblastoma Amplification (poor prognosis) RET MEN 2, medullary thyroid Ca Point mutation

NEET PG High-Yield: - RAS: Most common oncogene (30% of cancers) - Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22): BCR-ABL in CML - Burkitt's lymphoma: c-MYC translocation t(8;14) - HER2: Breast cancer (trastuzumab target)

docs.title: Tumor Suppressor Genes (Brakes)
docs.text:
Definition: Inhibit cell proliferation, promote apoptosis Loss of function → Cancer ("two-hit hypothesis" - Knudson)

Common tumor suppressor genes:

Gene Tumors Function Syndrome p53 Most cancers (50%) Cell cycle arrest, apoptosis, DNA repair Li-Fraumeni RB Retinoblastoma, osteosarcoma Cell cycle regulation (G1/S checkpoint) Familial retinoblastoma APC Colorectal cancer Wnt signaling inhibition FAP (Familial Adenomatous Polyposis) BRCA1/BRCA2 Breast, ovarian cancer DNA repair Familial breast/ovarian cancer WT1 Wilms tumor Kidney development WAGR syndrome NF1 Neurofibromatosis RAS inhibition Neurofibromatosis type 1 VHL Renal cell carcinoma HIF degradation Von Hippel-Lindau PTEN Breast, prostate, endometrial PI3K/AKT pathway inhibition Cowden syndrome

NEET PG High-Yield: - p53: "Guardian of the genome" (most commonly mutated gene in cancer) - RB: Retinoblastoma, osteosarcoma - APC: Familial adenomatous polyposis → Colorectal cancer - BRCA1/BRCA2: Breast & ovarian cancer (prophylactic mastectomy/oophorectomy) - Two-hit hypothesis: Both alleles must be lost for tumor suppressor gene loss

docs.title: DNA Repair Genes
docs.text:
Function: Repair DNA damage

Defects: - HNPCC (Lynch syndrome): Mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2) - Tumors: Colorectal cancer (right-sided), endometrial, ovarian - Xeroderma pigmentosum: Nucleotide excision repair - Tumors: Skin cancers (UV sensitivity) - BRCA1/BRCA2: Homologous recombination repair - Tumors: Breast, ovarian

NEET PG: HNPCC (Lynch syndrome) = Mismatch repair defect → Right-sided colon cancer

docs.title: HALLMARKS OF CANCER (Hanahan & Weinberg)
docs.text:
1. Sustained proliferative signaling (oncogenes)
  2. Evading growth suppressors (loss of tumor suppressors)
  3. Resisting cell death (apoptosis evasion)
  4. Enabling replicative immortality (telomerase activation)
  5. Inducing angiogenesis (VEGF)
  6. Activating invasion & metastasis
  7. Genome instability & mutation
  8. Tumor-promoting inflammation
  9. Deregulating cellular energetics (Warburg effect)
  10. Avoiding immune destruction

NEET PG: Know at least 6 hallmarks

docs.title: METASTASIS - HIGH YIELD
docs.text: Definition: Spread of tumor to distant sites (hallmark of malignancy)

docs.title: 1. Lymphatic Spread
docs.text:
Common in: Carcinomas Spread: Regional lymph nodes → Distant lymph nodes

Sentinel lymph node: First draining lymph node - Breast cancer: Axillary lymph node - Melanoma: Regional lymph node basin

NEET PG: Carcinomas spread via lymphatics (sarcomas via blood)

docs.title: 2. Hematogenous Spread
docs.text:
Common in: Sarcomas, some carcinomas (RCC, HCC, follicular thyroid) Target organs: Liver, lungs, bone, brain

Portal of entry: - Liver: GI tumors (via portal vein), breast, lung - Lungs: Most tumors (first capillary bed) - Bone: Breast, prostate, lung, kidney, thyroid - Brain: Lung, breast, melanoma, RCC

NEET PG: Lung = Most common site of hematogenous metastasis (first capillary bed)

docs.title: 3. Seeding (Transcoelomic)
docs.text:
Spread: Across body cavities (peritoneal, pleural, pericardial) Examples: - Ovarian cancer → Peritoneal cavity (Krukenberg tumor - ovarian met to stomach) - Gastric cancer → Peritoneum, ovaries (Krukenberg) - Mesothelioma → Pleural cavity

NEET PG: Krukenberg tumor = Ovarian metastasis (usually from stomach/GI)

docs.title: Common Metastatic Patterns - MUST KNOW
docs.text:
Tumors with bone metastases (mnemonic: "P**lease **T**ake **K**ids **B**ut **L**eave **T**hem"): - **P**rostate - **T**hyroid (follicular) - **K**idney (RCC) - **B**reast - **L**ung - **T**estis

Osteoblastic (sclerotic): - Prostate (most common) - Breast (mixed)

Osteolytic: - Breast (most common) - Lung, kidney, thyroid, multiple myeloma

NEET PG: - Prostate: Osteoblastic metastases - Breast: Mixed (osteolytic > osteoblastic) - Lung, kidney, thyroid: Osteolytic

docs.title: TUMOR MARKERS - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Marker Tumor Notes AFP (α-fetoprotein) Hepatocellular Ca, yolk sac tumor, testicular (non-seminoma) Neural tube defects (high in maternal serum) β-hCG Choriocarcinoma, hydatidiform mole, testicular (choriocarcinoma) Also elevated in pregnancy CEA Colorectal Ca, pancreatic, gastric, lung Non-specific, monitoring recurrence CA 125 Ovarian Ca (epithelial), endometrial Screening NOT recommended CA 19-9 Pancreatic Ca, cholangiocarcinoma, gastric Highest in pancreatic Ca CA 15-3 Breast Ca Monitoring, not screening PSA Prostate Ca, BPH, prostatitis Screening controversial Calcitonin Medullary thyroid Ca Also used for screening in MEN 2 Thyroglobulin Differentiated thyroid Ca (papillary, follicular) Post-thyroidectomy monitoring S-100 Melanoma, schwannoma, Langerhans cell histiocytosis Neural crest origin NSE Small cell lung Ca, neuroblastoma Neuroendocrine tumors LDH Germ cell tumors, lymphoma, melanoma Non-specific, prognostic Chromogranin A Neuroendocrine tumors (carcinoid, pheochromocytoma) 5-HIAA Carcinoid tumor In urine (serotonin metabolite) VMA, HVA Neuroblastoma, pheochromocytoma In urine (catecholamine metabolites)

NEET PG High-Yield: - AFP: Hepatocellular Ca, yolk sac tumor - CA 125: Ovarian Ca (not for screening) - CA 19-9: Pancreatic Ca - PSA: Prostate (DRE + PSA for screening) - Calcitonin: Medullary thyroid Ca - S-100: Melanoma

docs.title: PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES
docs.text: Definition: Symptom complexes due to cancer but NOT from tumor mass/metastasis

docs.title: Endocrine Syndromes
docs.text:
Syndrome Hormone Tumor Cushing's syndrome ACTH Small cell lung Ca (most common), carcinoid SIADH ADH Small cell lung Ca, pancreatic Ca Hypercalcemia PTHrP Squamous cell lung Ca, breast, RCC, multiple myeloma Hypoglycemia Insulin-like factors Hepatocellular Ca, mesenchymal tumors Polycythemia Erythropoietin RCC, HCC, cerebellar hemangioblastoma Carcinoid syndrome Serotonin (5-HT) Carcinoid tumor (small intestine, bronchial)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Small cell lung cancer: ACTH (Cushing's), ADH (SIADH) - Squamous cell lung cancer: PTHrP (hypercalcemia) - RCC: EPO (polycythemia), PTHrP (hypercalcemia)

docs.title: Neurological Syndromes
docs.text:
Syndrome Antibody Tumor Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome Anti-VGCC Small cell lung Ca Myasthenia gravis Anti-AChR Thymoma Cerebellar degeneration Anti-Yo, Anti-Hu Ovarian, breast, small cell lung Limbic encephalitis Anti-Hu Small cell lung

NEET PG: - Lambert-Eaton: Small cell lung Ca (improves with exercise) - Myasthenia gravis: Thymoma (worsens with exercise)

docs.title: Hematological Syndromes
docs.text:
• Trousseau syndrome: Migratory thrombophlebitis (pancreatic, lung Ca)
  • DIC: Acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL), mucin-secreting adenocarcinoma
  • Polycythemia: RCC, HCC

docs.title: Dermatological Syndromes
docs.text:
Syndrome Tumor Acanthosis nigricans GI malignancies (gastric adenocarcinoma) Dermatomyositis Lung, ovarian, breast Erythema gyratum repens Lung cancer Tripe palms Gastric, lung

NEET PG: Acanthosis nigricans = Gastric adenocarcinoma

docs.title: Grading
docs.text: Definition: Histological assessment of differentiation Basis: Cytology, architecture, mitoses Grades: 1 (well-differentiated) → 4 (poorly differentiated/anaplastic)

docs.title: Staging
docs.text:
Definition: Extent of tumor spread System: TNM classification - T: Primary tumor size - N: Regional lymph node involvement - M: Distant metastasis

NEET PG: - Grading: Degree of differentiation (microscopic) - Staging: Extent of spread (clinical + imaging) - Prognosis: Staging > Grading

docs.title: Tumor Antigens
docs.text:
1. Tumor-specific antigens (TSA): Unique to tumor (viral antigens, neoantigens)
  2. Tumor-associated antigens (TAA): Overexpressed in tumor (CEA, AFP, HER2)

docs.title: Immune Evasion Mechanisms
docs.text:
1. Reduced MHC expression
  2. PD-L1 expression (inhibits T cells)
  3. Immunosuppressive microenvironment (Tregs, MDSCs)
  4. Antigen loss variants

NEET PG: PD-L1 expression → Immune evasion (target for immunotherapy)

docs.title: Immunotherapy
docs.text:
Checkpoint inhibitors: - Anti-PD-1/PD-L1: Pembrolizumab, nivolumab (melanoma, lung cancer) - Anti-CTLA-4: Ipilimumab (melanoma)

CAR-T cells: Engineered T cells (B-cell lymphomas, ALL)

NEET PG: Checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1) for melanoma, lung cancer

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Hallmark of malignancy? Answer: Metastasis

  2. Most common oncogene mutated in cancers? Answer: RAS

  3. Guardian of the genome? Answer: p53

  4. Philadelphia chromosome seen in? Answer: CML (BCR-ABL)

  5. Carcinomas spread via? Answer: Lymphatics

  6. Most common site of hematogenous metastasis? Answer: Lungs

  7. Tumor marker for ovarian cancer? Answer: CA 125

  8. Small cell lung cancer paraneoplastic syndrome? Answer: SIADH, Cushing's (ACTH), Lambert-Eaton

docs.title: Malignant "-oma"
docs.text: "**M**eaningful **L**ymph **M**elanoma **M**eso **H**epatoma **S**eminoma" - **M**elanoma - **L**ymphoma - **M**esothelioma - **H**epatoma - **S**eminoma

docs.title: Bone Metastases
docs.text: "**P**lease **T**ake **K**ids **B**ut **L**eave **T**hem" - **P**rostate, **T**hyroid, **K**idney, **B**reast, **L**ung, **T**estis

docs.title: Small Cell Lung Ca Syndromes
docs.text: "S**mall **C**ell **L**oves **SIADH, Cushing's, Lambert-Eaton"

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know benign vs malignant features
  • Understand nomenclature rules
  • Learn oncogenes and tumor suppressors
  • Know metastatic routes
  • Memorize tumor markers
  • Understand paraneoplastic syndromes
  • Learn grading vs staging
  • Know Philadelphia chromosome
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on neoplasia

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: GROWTH & DEVELOPMENT - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text: Growth: Increase in physical size (quantitative) Development: Functional maturation (qualitative)

docs.title: Principles of Development
docs.text:
1. Cephalocaudal: Head to toe (head control → sitting → standing)
  2. Proximodistal: Center to periphery (shoulder → elbow → wrist → fingers)
  3. Mass to specific: Gross motor → Fine motor
  4. Continuous: From conception to maturity
  5. Sequential: Fixed order (cannot skip stages)
  6. Individual variation: Rate varies (timing may differ)

NEET PG: Development follows predictable sequence but rate varies

docs.title: Weight
docs.text:
Birth weight: 2.5-4 kg (Average: 3.3 kg) Low birth weight (LBW): <2.5 kg Very low birth weight (VLBW): <1.5 kg Extremely low birth weight (ELBW): <1 kg

Weight Gain: - Birth weight regained: By 10 days - Birth weight doubles: By 4-5 months - Birth weight triples: By 12 months - Birth weight quadruples: By 2 years

Average weight gain: - 0-3 months: 30g/day - 3-6 months: 20g/day - 6-12 months: 10g/day - 1-3 years: 2 kg/year - 3-puberty: 3-3.5 kg/year

NEET PG High-Yield: - Birth weight doubles by 5 months - Birth weight triples by 12 months - Birth weight quadruples by 2 years

docs.title: Length/Height
docs.text:
Birth length: 50 cm (Average)

Length/Height milestones: - Birth length increases by 50%: By 1 year (75 cm) - Birth length doubles: By 4 years (100 cm) - Birth length triples: By 13 years (150 cm)

Average growth: - First year: 25 cm - Second year: 12.5 cm (half of 1st year) - Third year: 9 cm - 4 years-puberty: 5-6 cm/year

NEET PG: Length at 1 year = 75 cm (1.5× birth length)

docs.title: Head Circumference (HC)
docs.text:
Birth HC: 35 cm (Average: 33-37 cm)

Milestones: - Equals chest circumference: 1 year (46 cm) - Chest > Head: After 1 year

Growth: - First year: 12 cm (2 cm/month for first 3 months, then 1 cm/month) - Second year: 2.5 cm - 3-5 years: 1 cm/year

Adult HC: 54-55 cm

NEET PG High-Yield: - HC = CC at 1 year (both 46 cm) - Microcephaly: HC <2 SD below mean - Macrocephaly: HC >2 SD above mean

docs.title: Chest Circumference (CC)
docs.text:
Birth CC: 32 cm (2-3 cm less than HC)

Milestones: - Equals HC: 1 year (46 cm) - Exceeds HC: After 1 year

docs.title: Primary (Deciduous) Teeth
docs.text:
Total: 20 teeth (10 upper + 10 lower)

Eruption: - First tooth: 6 months (lower central incisor) - All 20 teeth: 24-30 months (2-2.5 years)

Formula for number of teeth: Age in months - 6 (valid from 6-30 months) Example: At 12 months → 12-6 = 6 teeth

Order of eruption: 1. Lower central incisors (6 months) 2. Upper central incisors (8 months) 3. Upper lateral incisors (10 months) 4. Lower lateral incisors (12 months) 5. First molars (14 months) 6. Canines (18 months) 7. Second molars (24 months)

docs.title: Permanent Teeth
docs.text:
Total: 32 teeth

Eruption: - First permanent tooth: 6 years (first molar - "6-year molar") - Last tooth: 17-25 years (third molar - wisdom tooth)

NEET PG: - First deciduous tooth: 6 months (lower central incisor) - Number of teeth formula: Age (months) - 6 - First permanent tooth: 6 years (first molar)

docs.title: Gross Motor Development
docs.text:
Age Milestone Birth Flexed posture 1 month Lifts chin off bed (prone) 2 months Lifts chest off bed (prone) 3 months Head control (holds head steady) 4 months Rolls prone to supine 6 months Sits with support, Rolls supine to prone 7-8 months Sits without support 9 months Crawls, Stands with support 10 months Creeps (hands & knees) 12 months Stands without support, Walks with one hand held 15 months Walks alone (independently) 18 months Runs, Walks upstairs with help 2 years Walks up & down stairs (2 feet per step), Runs well, Kicks ball 3 years Stands on one foot (few seconds), Rides tricycle 4 years Hops on one foot, Walks downstairs (alternating feet) 5 years Skips

NEET PG High-Yield: - 3 months: Head control - 6 months: Sits with support - 9 months: Stands with support - 12 months: Stands alone, walks with one hand held - 15 months: Walks alone (most important)

docs.title: Fine Motor & Adaptive Development
docs.text:
Age Milestone Birth Grasp reflex 3 months Hands open (no more fisting), Holds rattle 4 months Reaches for objects 6 months Palmar grasp (whole hand), Transfers objects hand to hand 9 months Immature pincer grasp (thumb & index finger) 12 months Mature pincer grasp (tips of thumb & index) 15 months Builds tower of 2 cubes 18 months Builds tower of 3-4 cubes, Scribbles spontaneously 2 years Builds tower of 6 cubes, Imitates vertical line 3 years Builds tower of 9 cubes, Copies circle 4 years Builds tower of 10+ cubes, Copies cross (+) 5 years Copies triangle, Draws a person (6 parts)

Tower of Cubes Formula: Age (years) × 3 cubes (approximate) - 2 years: 6 cubes - 3 years: 9 cubes - 4 years: 12 cubes

NEET PG: - 6 months: Palmar grasp - 9 months: Immature pincer - 12 months: Mature pincer grasp

docs.title: Language Development
docs.text:
Age Milestone Birth Cries 2 months Social smile (smiles at mother) 3 months Coos (vowel sounds: aaa, ooo) 6 months Babbles (consonants: ba-ba, da-da - non-specific) 9 months Mama/Dada (non-specific) 12 months 1 word with meaning (Mama/Dada - specific), Understands "No" 15 months 4-6 words 18 months 10-20 words, Points to body parts 2 years 50 words, 2-word phrases, Refers to self by name 3 years 250 words, 3-word sentences, Knows age & gender 4 years 1500 words, Tells stories, Asks questions 5 years 2000+ words, Complete sentences

NEET PG High-Yield: - 2 months: Social smile - 6 months: Babbles - 12 months: 1 word with meaning - 2 years: 50 words, 2-word phrases - 3 years: 3-word sentences

docs.title: Social & Personal Development
docs.text:
Age Milestone 2 months Social smile 6 months Recognizes mother, Stranger anxiety begins 9 months Waves bye-bye, Plays peek-a-boo 12 months Comes when called, Helps in dressing 15 months Drinks from cup, Uses spoon (messy) 18 months Toilet training starts, Explores environment 2 years Puts on clothes, Dry by day, Parallel play 3 years Dry by night, Cooperative play, Rides tricycle 4 years Plays with others, Dresses & undresses fully 5 years Follows rules in games

NEET PG: - 9 months: Waves bye-bye, plays peek-a-boo - 2 years: Dry by day - 3 years: Dry by night, cooperative play

docs.title: Denver Developmental Screening Test (DDST-II)
docs.text:
Assesses: 4 domains 1. Gross motor (25%) 2. Fine motor-adaptive (25%) 3. Language (25%) 4. Personal-social (25%)

Age range: 0-6 years

NEET PG: DDST-II assesses 4 domains equally

docs.title: Developmental Quotient (DQ)
docs.text:
Formula: DQ = (Developmental age / Chronological age) × 100

Interpretation: - Normal: 85-115 - Borderline: 70-84 - Delayed: <70

docs.title: Motor
docs.text:
• No head control by 4 months
  • Not sitting by 9 months
  • Not walking by 18 months
  • Hand dominance before 18 months (suggests hemiparesis)

docs.title: Language
docs.text:
• No babbling by 9 months
  • No single words by 18 months
  • No 2-word phrases by 2.5 years

docs.title: Social
docs.text:
• No social smile by 3 months
  • No eye contact

NEET PG: Hand dominance before 18 months = Abnormal (suspect cerebral palsy)

docs.title: Primitive Reflexes
docs.text:
Reflex Appears Disappears How to Elicit Response Moro Birth 4-6 months Sudden head drop Abduction → Adduction of arms Grasp Birth 3-4 months Place finger in palm Grasps finger Rooting Birth 4 months Touch cheek Turns head to that side Sucking Birth 4 months Touch lips Sucking movements ATNR Birth 6 months Turn head to one side Fencing posture Stepping Birth 2 months Hold upright, feet touch surface Stepping movements Parachute 8-9 months Persists Sudden forward thrust Arms extend forward

NEET PG High-Yield: - Moro reflex: Absent in Erb's palsy (affected side) - Parachute reflex: Appears at 8-9 months (protective reflex) - ATNR: Asymmetric tonic neck reflex (Fencing posture) - Persistence of primitive reflexes: Suggests cerebral palsy

docs.title: WHO Growth Standards (0-5 years)
docs.text:
Uses: Weight, length/height, head circumference

Interpretation: - Normal: Between -2 SD to +2 SD - Underweight/Stunting: <-2 SD - Overweight/Obesity: >+2 SD

docs.title: IAP Growth Charts (5-18 years)
docs.text: Uses: Weight for age, height for age, BMI

docs.title: Weight for Age
docs.text:
• Normal: >80% of expected
  • Grade I malnutrition: 71-80%
  • Grade II: 61-70%
  • Grade III: 51-60%
  • Grade IV: <50%

docs.title: Mid-Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC)
docs.text:
Age: 6 months - 5 years

Interpretation: - Green (≥13.5 cm): Normal - Yellow (12.5-13.4 cm): Moderate malnutrition - Red (<12.5 cm): Severe malnutrition

NEET PG: MUAC <12.5 cm = Severe acute malnutrition (SAM)

docs.title: Birth
docs.text:
• BCG: Left deltoid, intradermal
  • OPV-0 (or IPV)
  • Hepatitis B-1

docs.title: 6 Weeks
docs.text:
• OPV-1, IPV-1
  • DTP-1 (or DTaP)
  • Hib-1
  • Hepatitis B-2
  • PCV-1
  • Rotavirus-1

docs.title: 10 Weeks
docs.text:
• OPV-2, IPV-2
  • DTP-2
  • Hib-2
  • PCV-2
  • Rotavirus-2

docs.title: 14 Weeks
docs.text:
• OPV-3, IPV-3
  • DTP-3
  • Hib-3
  • Hepatitis B-3
  • PCV-3
  • Rotavirus-3

docs.title: 6 Months
docs.text: • Influenza-1 (annual thereafter)

docs.title: 9-12 Months
docs.text:
• MMR-1 (9-12 months)
  • Typhoid conjugate vaccine (9-12 months)
  • Hepatitis A-1 (12 months)

docs.title: 12-15 Months
docs.text: • PCV booster

docs.title: 15-18 Months
docs.text:
• DTP booster-1
  • IPV booster-1
  • Hib booster
  • MMR-2
  • Varicella-1

docs.title: 18-19 Months
docs.text: • Hepatitis A-2

docs.title: 4-6 Years
docs.text:
• DTP booster-2
  • IPV booster-2
  • MMR-3 (optional)
  • Varicella-2
  • Typhoid booster

docs.title: 10-12 Years
docs.text:
• Tdap/Td
  • HPV (2-3 doses for girls)

NEET PG High-Yield: - BCG: Given at birth, left deltoid, intradermal - OPV vs IPV: IPV preferred (no vaccine-associated paralytic polio) - MMR: Live vaccine, contraindicated in immunocompromised - PCV: Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (13-valent) - Rotavirus: Live oral vaccine (maximum age 8 months for last dose)

docs.title: Vaccine-Preventable Diseases
docs.text:
Vaccine Disease Prevented BCG Tuberculosis (severe forms - meningitis, miliary) OPV/IPV Poliomyelitis DTP Diphtheria, Tetanus, Pertussis Hib H. influenzae type B (meningitis, pneumonia, epiglottitis) Hepatitis B Hepatitis B PCV Pneumococcal infections Rotavirus Rotavirus gastroenteritis MMR Measles, Mumps, Rubella Varicella Chickenpox HPV Cervical cancer (Human papillomavirus)

NEET PG: BCG protects against severe TB (meningitis, miliary), NOT pulmonary TB

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. When does birth weight double? Answer: 4-5 months

  2. When does HC equal CC? Answer: 1 year (both 46 cm)

  3. First deciduous tooth erupts at? Answer: 6 months

  4. Child walks alone at? Answer: 15 months

  5. Mature pincer grasp develops at? Answer: 12 months

  6. Social smile appears at? Answer: 2 months

  7. Hand dominance before 18 months suggests? Answer: Cerebral palsy (hemiparesis)

  8. Parachute reflex appears at? Answer: 8-9 months

docs.title: Gross Motor Milestones
docs.text: "3-6-9-12-15 months" - 3: Head control - 6: Sits with support - 9: Stands with support - 12: Stands alone - 15: Walks alone

docs.title: Weight Milestones
docs.text: "Double, Triple, Quadruple (DTQ)" - **D**ouble: 5 months - **T**riple: 12 months (1 year) - **Q**uadruple: 24 months (2 years)

docs.title: Pincer Grasp
docs.text: "9 is Im-mature, 12 is Mature" - 9 months: Immature pincer - 12 months: Mature pincer

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Memorize weight & length milestones
  • Know head circumference growth
  • Learn dentition formulas
  • Understand gross motor milestones (3-6-9-12-15)
  • Know fine motor development
  • Memorize language milestones
  • Learn primitive reflexes (appearance & disappearance)
  • Know immunization schedule
  • Understand red flags in development
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on growth & development

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 5-8 questions)

docs.title: NEONATAL JAUNDICE - NEET PG Study Material
Definition
docs.text:
Yellow discoloration of skin and sclera due to ↑ serum bilirubin (>5 mg/dL)

Visible jaundice: When bilirubin >5-7 mg/dL

Clinical examination: Progresses cephalocaudal (head → toe)

NEET PG: Jaundice visible when bilirubin >5 mg/dL

docs.title: Bilirubin Metabolism in Newborn
docs.text:
RBC breakdown → Heme
      ↓
Unconjugated bilirubin (indirect, lipid-soluble)
      ↓
Transported bound to albumin
      ↓
Liver uptake + Conjugation (via UDP-glucuronyl transferase)
      ↓
Conjugated bilirubin (direct, water-soluble)
      ↓
Excreted in bile → Intestine
      ↓
Bacterial flora → Urobilinogen → Excreted

Why newborns prone to jaundice? 1. ↑ RBC breakdown (shorter RBC lifespan: 70-90 days vs 120 days) 2. ↑ RBC mass (higher hematocrit) 3. Immature liver enzyme (UDP-glucuronyl transferase) 4. ↑ Enterohepatic circulation (β-glucuronidase in gut) 5. Delayed gut colonization (sterile gut → no bacterial conversion)

docs.title: By Onset
docs.text:
Type Onset Causes Early <24 hours ALWAYS PATHOLOGICAL (hemolysis, sepsis) Physiological 2-3 days Normal adaptation Late >2 weeks Breast milk jaundice, hypothyroidism, cholestasis

NEET PG High-Yield: Jaundice in first 24 hours is ALWAYS pathological (requires investigation)

docs.title: By Bilirubin Type
docs.text:
1. Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia (Indirect > Direct): - Increased production or decreased conjugation - Most common in neonates

2. Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia (Direct >2 mg/dL or >20% of total): - Cholestasis, biliary obstruction - ALWAYS PATHOLOGICAL

docs.title: 1. PHYSIOLOGICAL JAUNDICE
docs.text:
Definition: Benign, self-limiting jaundice due to normal neonatal adaptation

Characteristics: - Onset: 2-3 days (NOT <24 hours, NOT >2 weeks) - Peak: Day 3-5 - Duration: 1 week (term), 2 weeks (preterm) - Bilirubin: <12 mg/dL (term), <15 mg/dL (preterm) - Type: Unconjugated - Baby: Well, feeding normally

Pathophysiology: - ↑ RBC breakdown - Immature liver conjugation - ↑ Enterohepatic circulation

Management: - No treatment needed - Continue breastfeeding - Monitor

NEET PG: Physiological jaundice appears after 24 hours, peaks on day 3-5, resolves by 1 week

docs.title: 2. BREASTFEEDING JAUNDICE (Early Onset)
docs.text:
Definition: Jaundice in first week due to inadequate breastfeeding

Onset: Days 2-5

Cause: - Insufficient milk intake → Dehydration - ↓ Caloric intake → ↓ Hepatic uptake - ↑ Enterohepatic circulation

Diagnosis: - Poor weight gain - Fewer wet diapers (<6/day) - Concentrated urine

Management: - Increase frequency of breastfeeding (8-12 times/day) - Do NOT stop breastfeeding - Lactation support - Supplementation if needed

NEET PG: Breastfeeding jaundice = Inadequate intake, improve feeding (do NOT stop breastfeeding)

docs.title: 3. BREAST MILK JAUNDICE (Late Onset)
docs.text:
Definition: Prolonged jaundice (>2 weeks) in well-fed, thriving breastfed baby

Onset: After 1 week (peaks at 2-3 weeks)

Duration: Can persist 3-12 weeks

Cause: - β-glucuronidase in breast milk → ↑ Deconjugation → ↑ Enterohepatic circulation - Pregnanediol in breast milk (inhibits conjugation)

Diagnosis: - Thriving baby, good weight gain - Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia - Bilirubin falls if breastfeeding stopped (diagnostic test)

Management: - Continue breastfeeding (safe, no treatment needed) - Reassure parents - If bilirubin very high: Temporarily stop breastfeeding × 24-48 hours (bilirubin drops) → Resume

NEET PG High-Yield: - Breast milk jaundice: Prolonged (>2 weeks), thriving baby, continue breastfeeding - Breastfeeding jaundice: Early (<1 week), poor feeding, increase feeding frequency

docs.title: A. ABO Incompatibility (Most Common)
docs.text:
Setup: - Mother: Blood group O - Baby: Blood group A or B

Pathophysiology: - Maternal anti-A or anti-B IgG antibodies cross placenta - Bind to fetal RBCs → Hemolysis

Features: - Onset: First 24 hours (can be delayed to 2-3 days) - Severity: Mild to moderate (less severe than Rh) - Subsequent pregnancies: Can occur in first pregnancy - Anemia: Mild - Direct Coombs test (DAT): Weakly positive or negative

NEET PG: - ABO incompatibility: Most common hemolytic disease - Can occur in first pregnancy (unlike Rh) - Mother O, Baby A/B

docs.title: B. Rh Incompatibility
docs.text:
Setup: - Mother: Rh-negative - Baby: Rh-positive

Pathophysiology: - Prior sensitization (previous pregnancy, transfusion, abortion) - Maternal anti-D IgG crosses placenta → Hemolysis

Features: - First pregnancy: Usually NOT affected (unless prior sensitization) - Subsequent pregnancies: Worsening severity - Onset: First 24 hours - Severity: Severe (can cause hydrops fetalis) - Anemia: Severe - Direct Coombs test: Strongly positive

Complications: - Severe anemia - Hydrops fetalis (in utero) - Kernicterus (if untreated)

Prevention: - Anti-D immunoglobulin (RhoGAM) to Rh-negative mother: - At 28 weeks gestation - Within 72 hours of delivery (if baby Rh+) - After any sensitizing event (abortion, amniocentesis, APH)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Rh disease: Severe, first baby usually spared, subsequent pregnancies worsen - Anti-D given at 28 weeks + within 72 hours of delivery - Direct Coombs test strongly positive

docs.title: C. G6PD Deficiency
docs.text:
Inheritance: X-linked recessive (males affected)

Trigger: Oxidative stress (infection, drugs, fava beans)

Drugs to avoid: - Antimalarials (primaquine, chloroquine) - Sulfonamides - Nitrofurantoin - Aspirin - Vitamin K (large doses)

Features: - Acute hemolysis - Heinz bodies, bite cells on blood smear

Diagnosis: G6PD enzyme assay (after acute episode resolves)

NEET PG: G6PD deficiency = X-linked, avoid oxidant drugs

docs.title: D. Hereditary Spherocytosis
docs.text:
Inheritance: Autosomal dominant

Features: - Spherical RBCs (lack central pallor) - ↑ Osmotic fragility - Splenomegaly (later)

Diagnosis: Osmotic fragility test

docs.title: 5. CRIGLER-NAJJAR SYNDROME
docs.text:
Type I (Severe): - Complete absence of UDP-glucuronyl transferase - Bilirubin: Very high (>20 mg/dL) - Onset: First days of life - Treatment: Phototherapy (lifelong), liver transplant - Prognosis: High risk of kernicterus

Type II (Mild): - Partial deficiency of enzyme - Bilirubin: 6-20 mg/dL - Treatment: Phenobarbital (induces enzyme) - Prognosis: Better

NEET PG: Crigler-Najjar Type I = Lifelong phototherapy, Type II = Phenobarbital responsive

docs.title: 6. GILBERT SYNDROME
docs.text:
Inheritance: Autosomal recessive

Pathophysiology: Mild ↓ UDP-glucuronyl transferase

Features: - Mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (<3 mg/dL) - Triggered by stress, fasting, illness - Benign, no treatment needed

NEET PG: Gilbert syndrome = Benign, no treatment needed

docs.title: 7. PREMATURITY
docs.text:
Premature infants more prone to jaundice: - Immature liver enzymes - ↑ RBC breakdown - Delayed gut colonization

Bilirubin levels: Higher and prolonged compared to term infants

docs.title: 8. POLYCYTHEMIA
docs.text:
Causes: Twin-twin transfusion, maternal diabetes, delayed cord clamping

Mechanism: ↑ RBC mass → ↑ Breakdown → ↑ Bilirubin

NEET PG: Maternal diabetes → Polycythemia → Jaundice

docs.title: 9. CEPHALOHEMATOMA/BRUISING
docs.text:
Large extravascular blood collection → Breakdown → ↑ Bilirubin

NEET PG: Birth trauma → Cephalohematoma → Jaundice

docs.title: 10. SEPSIS
docs.text:
Infection → Hemolysis + impaired hepatic function → Jaundice

Features: Sick baby, fever/hypothermia, poor feeding, lethargy

docs.title: CONJUGATED HYPERBILIRUBINEMIA (Cholestatic Jaundice)
docs.text:
Definition: Direct bilirubin >2 mg/dL OR >20% of total bilirubin

Significance: ALWAYS PATHOLOGICAL (requires urgent investigation)

docs.title: Biliary Obstruction
docs.text:
1. Biliary Atresia (Most Common): - Onset: After 2 weeks - Features: Dark urine, pale/acholic stools, hepatomegaly - Investigation: USG (absent/small gallbladder), HIDA scan (no excretion), liver biopsy - Treatment: Kasai procedure (hepatoportoenterostomy) - best if <60 days - Prognosis: Poor if delayed (liver transplant needed)

NEET PG: Biliary atresia = Acholic stools, do Kasai before 60 days

2. Choledochal Cyst: - Congenital cystic dilatation of bile duct - Triad: Jaundice + Abdominal mass + Pain - Diagnosis: USG - Treatment: Surgical excision

3. Inspissated Bile Syndrome: - Thick bile plugs obstruct ducts - Associated with hemolysis, TPN

docs.title: Hepatocellular
docs.text:
1. Neonatal Hepatitis: - Idiopathic (most common) - Viral: CMV, HSV, Rubella, Hepatitis B - Features: Conjugated jaundice, hepatomegaly - Diagnosis: Viral serology, liver biopsy - Treatment: Supportive

2. Metabolic Disorders: - Galactosemia: Reducing substances in urine, hepatomegaly, cataracts - Tyrosinemia - α1-antitrypsin deficiency

3. TORCH Infections: - Toxoplasma, Rubella, CMV, HSV - Features: IUGR, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, microcephaly

NEET PG: Conjugated jaundice + acholic stools = Biliary atresia (do Kasai <60 days)

docs.title: KERNICTERUS (Bilirubin Encephalopathy)
docs.text:
Definition: Permanent neurological damage due to bilirubin deposition in basal ganglia

Risk Factors: - Very high unconjugated bilirubin (>25-30 mg/dL) - Prematurity - Acidosis, hypoxia, sepsis - Hypoalbuminemia - Hemolysis

Clinical Phases:

Phase 1 (Early - first days): - Poor feeding, lethargy - Hypotonia - High-pitched cry

Phase 2 (Mid - 1 week): - Hypertonia, opisthotonos - Fever - Shrill cry

Phase 3 (Advanced - after 1 week): - Irreversible damage

Chronic Sequelae (Tetrad): 1. Choreoathetoid cerebral palsy (movement disorder) 2. Sensorineural hearing loss (high-frequency) 3. Upward gaze palsy 4. Dental enamel hypoplasia

Prevention: - Aggressive phototherapy - Exchange transfusion if indicated

NEET PG High-Yield: - Kernicterus: Unconjugated bilirubin >25-30 mg/dL - Tetrad: Choreoathetosis + Hearing loss + Gaze palsy + Dental dysplasia

docs.title: All Cases
docs.text:
1. Serum bilirubin (Total, Direct, Indirect)
  2. Blood group (Mother & Baby) + Rh type
  3. Direct Coombs test (DAT): Hemolysis
  4. CBC: Anemia, polycythemia, sepsis
  5. Peripheral smear: Spherocytes, fragmented RBCs
  6. Reticulocyte count: ↑ in hemolysis

docs.title: If Indicated
docs.text:
• G6PD assay: If hemolysis suspected
  • Sepsis workup: Blood culture, CRP
  • Thyroid function: If prolonged jaundice
  • Urine for reducing substances: Galactosemia
  • Liver function tests: If conjugated jaundice
  • USG abdomen: Biliary atresia, choledochal cyst

NEET PG: Direct Coombs test (DAT) positive = Immune hemolysis

docs.title: General Measures
docs.text:
• Adequate hydration & nutrition
  • Increase feeding frequency (↓ enterohepatic circulation)
  • Avoid drugs that displace bilirubin from albumin (sulfonamides, aspirin)

docs.title: Phototherapy
docs.text:
Mechanism: Converts unconjugated bilirubin → Water-soluble isomers (excreted without conjugation)

Light: Blue-green light (wavelength 425-475 nm) most effective

Indications (depends on age, risk factors):

Term healthy newborn:

Age Phototherapy Exchange Transfusion <24 hours ≥12 mg/dL ≥20 mg/dL 24-48 hours ≥15 mg/dL ≥25 mg/dL 49-72 hours ≥18 mg/dL ≥25 mg/dL >72 hours ≥20 mg/dL ≥25 mg/dL

Preterm/sick: Lower thresholds

Technique: - Baby undressed (only diaper) - Eye protection (prevent retinal damage) - Monitor temperature (risk of hypothermia/hyperthermia) - Ensure adequate hydration

Side Effects: - Bronze baby syndrome: Grayish-brown discoloration (if conjugated hyperbilirubinemia) - Loose stools - Skin rash - Dehydration

NEET PG: Phototherapy contraindicated in conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (bronze baby syndrome)

docs.title: Exchange Transfusion
docs.text:
Indication: - Bilirubin >25 mg/dL (term) - Failure of phototherapy - Signs of kernicterus - Severe hemolysis

Procedure: - Remove baby's blood, replace with donor blood (double volume exchange) - Via umbilical vein

Complications: - Infection, thrombosis - Electrolyte imbalance (hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia) - Necrotizing enterocolitis - Thrombocytopenia

NEET PG: Exchange transfusion if bilirubin >25 mg/dL or signs of kernicterus

docs.title: IV Immunoglobulin (IVIG)
docs.text:
Indication: Isoimmune hemolysis (Rh, ABO incompatibility) with rising bilirubin despite phototherapy

Dose: 0.5-1 g/kg IV

Mechanism: Blocks Fc receptors → ↓ Hemolysis

NEET PG: IVIG used in isoimmune hemolysis (reduces need for exchange transfusion)

docs.title: Approach to Neonatal Jaundice
docs.text:
Jaundice detected
      ↓
Check onset, bilirubin level, baby's condition
      ↓
├─ <24 hours → PATHOLOGICAL
│    ↓
│  Investigate (Blood group, Coombs, CBC, smear)
│    ↓
│  Hemolysis likely → Phototherapy/Exchange
│
├─ 2-14 days, well baby, unconjugated
│    ↓
│  Likely physiological/breast milk jaundice
│    ↓
│  Monitor, continue breastfeeding
│
└─ >2 weeks OR conjugated
     ↓
   Prolonged/cholestatic jaundice
     ↓
   Investigate (LFT, USG, TORCH, metabolic screen)
     ↓
   Treat underlying cause

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Jaundice visible when bilirubin is? Answer: >5 mg/dL

  2. Jaundice in first 24 hours indicates? Answer: Pathological (likely hemolysis)

  3. Most common cause of hemolytic jaundice in newborn? Answer: ABO incompatibility

  4. Direct Coombs test positive in? Answer: Rh incompatibility (isoimmune hemolysis)

  5. Breast milk jaundice management? Answer: Continue breastfeeding (reassure parents)

  6. Phototherapy contraindicated in? Answer: Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (bronze baby syndrome)

  7. Bilirubin level for exchange transfusion? Answer: >25 mg/dL (term baby)

  8. Features of kernicterus? Answer: Choreoathetosis, hearing loss, gaze palsy, dental dysplasia

docs.title: Causes of Jaundice <24 hours
docs.text: "HIS" - **H**emolysis (ABO, Rh, G6PD) - **I**nfection (sepsis, TORCH) - **S**ubgaleal hemorrhage/Cephalohematoma

docs.title: Kernicterus Tetrad
docs.text: "CHUD" - **C**horeoathetoid CP - **H**earing loss - **U**pward gaze palsy - **D**ental dysplasia

docs.title: Breastfeeding vs Breast Milk Jaundice
docs.text: "Early Eat, Late Leak" - Breastfeeding jaundice: Early (<1 week), inadequate eating - Breast milk jaundice: Late (>1 week), substance in breast milk leaking

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know bilirubin metabolism in newborns
  • Understand physiological vs pathological jaundice
  • Learn causes of jaundice by onset (<24h, 2-14 days, >2 weeks)
  • Differentiate breastfeeding vs breast milk jaundice
  • Know ABO vs Rh incompatibility differences
  • Understand kernicterus features and prevention
  • Learn phototherapy indications and complications
  • Know exchange transfusion criteria
  • Memorize conjugated hyperbilirubinemia causes
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on neonatal jaundice

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 5-7 questions)

docs.title: ANTIMICROBIAL AGENTS - NEET PG Study Material
By Mechanism of Action
docs.text:
1. Cell Wall Synthesis Inhibitors: Beta-lactams, Glycopeptides
  2. Protein Synthesis Inhibitors: Aminoglycosides, Macrolides, Tetracyclines
  3. DNA Synthesis Inhibitors: Fluoroquinolones
  4. RNA Synthesis Inhibitors: Rifampicin
  5. Folate Synthesis Inhibitors: Sulfonamides, Trimethoprim
  6. Cell Membrane Disruptors: Polymyxins, Daptomycin

docs.title: Mechanism of Action
docs.text:
• Inhibit transpeptidase (penicillin-binding proteins)
  • Prevent peptidoglycan cross-linking
  • Activate autolysins
  • Bactericidal

NEET PG: Beta-lactams are bactericidal (kill bacteria)

docs.title: Natural Penicillins
docs.text:
Penicillin G (Benzylpenicillin) - IV/IM route (acid-labile) - Spectrum: Gram-positive cocci, Treponema, Actinomyces - Uses: Streptococcal pharyngitis, syphilis, gas gangrene - DOC for: Syphilis, actinomycosis

Penicillin V (Phenoxymethyl penicillin) - Oral (acid-stable) - Streptococcal pharyngitis

NEET PG: Benzathine penicillin G - Long-acting (IM, for syphilis, RF prophylaxis)

docs.title: Anti-Staphylococcal Penicillins
docs.text:
Cloxacillin, Dicloxacillin, Nafcillin, Oxacillin - β-lactamase resistant (bulky side chain) - Uses: MSSA (Methicillin-sensitive Staph aureus)

NEET PG: Cloxacillin = DOC for MSSA

docs.title: Broad-Spectrum Penicillins (Aminopenicillins)
docs.text:
Ampicillin, Amoxicillin - Spectrum: Gram-positive + some Gram-negative (H. influenzae, E. coli, Proteus) - Uses: UTI, respiratory infections, endocarditis prophylaxis, Listeria - Amoxicillin: Better oral absorption than ampicillin

NEET PG: - Ampicillin: DOC for Listeria monocytogenes - Amoxicillin: First-line for H. pylori (with clarithromycin + PPI)

docs.title: Anti-Pseudomonal Penicillins
docs.text:
Piperacillin, Ticarcillin - Spectrum: Pseudomonas, broad Gram-negative - Always combined with β-lactamase inhibitor (Piperacillin-Tazobactam)
