NEET PG: Piperacillin-Tazobactam = Broad-spectrum, covers Pseudomonas

docs.title: β-Lactamase Inhibitors
docs.text:
Clavulanic acid, Sulbactam, Tazobactam - No intrinsic antimicrobial activity - Irreversibly inhibit β-lactamases

Combinations: - Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Augmentin) - Ampicillin-Sulbactam - Piperacillin-Tazobactam

NEET PG: β-lactamase inhibitors restore activity against β-lactamase-producing bacteria

docs.title: 2. CEPHALOSPORINS
docs.text: Mechanism: Same as penicillins (inhibit cell wall synthesis) Structure: β-lactam ring + dihydrothiazine ring

docs.title: 1<sup>st</sup> Generation
docs.text:
Cefazolin, Cephalexin, Cefadroxil - Spectrum: Gram-positive cocci, some Gram-negative - Uses: Skin infections, surgical prophylaxis (cefazolin)

NEET PG: Cefazolin = Surgical prophylaxis

docs.title: 2<sup>nd</sup> Generation
docs.text: Cefuroxime, Cefoxitin, Cefaclor - Spectrum: ↑ Gram-negative coverage (H. influenzae, Moraxella) - Cefoxitin: Anaerobic coverage (B. fragilis)

docs.title: 3<sup>rd</sup> Generation
docs.text:
Ceftriaxone, Cefotaxime, Ceftazidime - Spectrum: Excellent Gram-negative, crosses BBB - Ceftriaxone/Cefotaxime: Meningitis, gonorrhea, typhoid - Ceftazidime: Pseudomonas coverage

NEET PG High-Yield: - Ceftriaxone: DOC for meningitis (Neisseria, Pneumococcus, H. influenzae), gonorrhea, typhoid - Crosses BBB - Long half-life (once-daily dosing)

docs.title: 4<sup>th</sup> Generation
docs.text: Cefepime - Spectrum: Gram-positive + Gram-negative + Pseudomonas - Use: Febrile neutropenia

docs.title: 5<sup>th</sup> Generation
docs.text:
Ceftaroline - Spectrum: MRSA coverage + Gram-negatives

NEET PG: Only cephalosporin covering MRSA

docs.title: 3. CARBAPENEMS
docs.text:
Imipenem, Meropenem, Ertapenem, Doripenem

Spectrum: Broadest-spectrum antibiotics - Gram-positive + Gram-negative + Anaerobes + Pseudomonas - Resistant to most β-lactamases

Uses: Severe hospital-acquired infections, multidrug-resistant organisms

NEET PG High-Yield: - Imipenem: Always given with cilastatin (inhibits renal dehydropeptidase) - Meropenem: Preferred for CNS infections (lower seizure risk) - Ertapenem: NO Pseudomonas coverage

Adverse Effects: Seizures (Imipenem > Meropenem)

docs.title: 4. MONOBACTAMS
docs.text:
Aztreonam - Spectrum: Gram-negative ONLY (including Pseudomonas) - No cross-reactivity with other β-lactams - Use: Penicillin-allergic patients with Gram-negative infections

NEET PG: Safe in penicillin allergy (no cross-reactivity)

docs.title: Adverse Effects of β-Lactams
docs.text:
1. Hypersensitivity (most common): Rash, anaphylaxis
  2. Cross-reactivity: 10% between penicillins & cephalosporins
  3. No cross-reactivity: Aztreonam
  4. Diarrhea: C. difficile colitis
  5. Interstitial nephritis: Methicillin
  6. Hemolytic anemia: High-dose penicillin
  7. Seizures: Imipenem (high doses)
  8. Disulfiram-like reaction: Cefoperazone (has methylthiotetrazole side chain)

NEET PG: Cefoperazone & Cefotetan → Disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol

docs.title: VANCOMYCIN
docs.text:
Mechanism: Inhibits cell wall synthesis (binds D-Ala-D-Ala of peptidoglycan)

Spectrum: Gram-positive ONLY (cannot cross Gram-negative outer membrane)

Uses: - MRSA infections (DOC) - C. difficile colitis (oral route) - Endocarditis (with gentamicin) - Penicillin-allergic patients

Route: IV (poorly absorbed orally) - except for C. difficile (oral)

Adverse Effects: 1. Red man syndrome: Histamine release (NOT allergy) - Prevention: Slow infusion, antihistamines 2. Nephrotoxicity: Especially with aminoglycosides 3. Ototoxicity 4. Thrombophlebitis

NEET PG High-Yield: - DOC for MRSA - Oral vancomycin = DOC for C. difficile colitis - Red man syndrome ≠ Allergy (slow infusion prevents it)

docs.title: AMINOGLYCOSIDES
docs.text:
Gentamicin, Tobramycin, Amikacin, Streptomycin, Neomycin

Mechanism: - Inhibit 30S ribosomal subunit - Cause misreading of mRNA - Bactericidal (concentration-dependent)

Spectrum: Aerobic Gram-negative (E. coli, Pseudomonas, Klebsiella)

Uses: - Severe Gram-negative infections (sepsis, UTI) - Synergy with β-lactams: Endocarditis, enterococcal infections - Gentamicin: Most commonly used - Amikacin: Resistant Gram-negatives - Streptomycin: TB (second-line), plague, tularemia - Neomycin: Topical use only (too toxic systemically), hepatic encephalopathy (oral)

NEET PG: Aminoglycosides require O2 to enter bacteria (ineffective against anaerobes)

Adverse Effects: 1. Nephrotoxicity: Acute tubular necrosis (dose-related, reversible) 2. Ototoxicity: Irreversible (vestibular > cochlear) 3. Neuromuscular blockade: Worsens myasthenia gravis 4. Teratogenic: Fetal ototoxicity

NEET PG: - Once-daily dosing (concentration-dependent killing, long PAE) - Monitor: Trough levels (nephrotoxicity), peak levels (efficacy) - Contraindicated: Pregnancy, myasthenia gravis

docs.title: MACROLIDES
docs.text:
Erythromycin, Azithromycin, Clarithromycin

Mechanism: Inhibit 50S ribosomal subunit (block translocation) Effect: Bacteriostatic (bactericidal at high concentrations)

Spectrum: Gram-positive, atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella)

Uses: - Erythromycin: Atypical pneumonia, Campylobacter enteritis, acne (topical), prokinetic - Azithromycin: CAP, STDs (Chlamydia, gonorrhea), traveler's diarrhea, MAC prophylaxis (HIV) - Clarithromycin: H. pylori, MAC, atypicals

NEET PG High-Yield: - Azithromycin: Long half-life, good tissue penetration, once-daily dosing - Alternative to penicillin in allergic patients - Erythromycin: Motilin agonist (prokinetic effect)

Adverse Effects: 1. GI upset: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (erythromycin > azithromycin) 2. QT prolongation: Torsades de pointes 3. Hepatotoxicity: Cholestatic jaundice (erythromycin estolate) 4. Drug interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitor (increases levels of warfarin, statins, cyclosporine)

NEET PG: Erythromycin is a motilin agonist (used as prokinetic)

docs.title: TETRACYCLINES
docs.text:
Tetracycline, Doxycycline, Minocycline, Tigecycline

Mechanism: Inhibit 30S ribosomal subunit (block aminoacyl-tRNA binding) Effect: Bacteriostatic

Spectrum: Broad (Gram+, Gram-, atypicals, Rickettsiae, spirochetes)

Uses: - Doxycycline: - Atypical pneumonia (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia) - Rickettsial infections (DOC) - Lyme disease - Malaria prophylaxis - Acne - Brucellosis - Tigecycline: MRSA, VRE, multidrug-resistant Gram-negatives

NEET PG High-Yield: - DOC for Rickettsiae (scrub typhus, RMSF) - DOC for Chlamydia trachomatis (genital infections)

Adverse Effects: 1. GI upset 2. Tooth discoloration & bone growth inhibition: Contraindicated in children <8 years & pregnancy 3. Photosensitivity: Severe sunburn 4. Hepatotoxicity: Pregnant women 5. Fanconi syndrome: Expired tetracycline 6. Chelation: Binds Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+ (avoid dairy, antacids)

NEET PG: - Contraindicated: Children <8 years, pregnancy - Avoid with milk/antacids (chelation)

docs.title: FLUOROQUINOLONES
docs.text:
Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin, Norfloxacin

Mechanism: Inhibit DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) & topoisomerase IV Effect: Bactericidal (concentration-dependent)

Spectrum:

Drug Gram-negative Gram-positive Atypicals Anaerobes Ciprofloxacin ++++ (Pseudomonas) + ++ - Levofloxacin +++ +++ +++ + Moxifloxacin ++ +++ +++ +++

Uses: - Ciprofloxacin: UTI, GI infections (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter), anthrax, Pseudomonas - Levofloxacin: CAP, TB (second-line) - Moxifloxacin: CAP, TB (second-line), better anaerobic coverage - Norfloxacin: UTI only (does not achieve systemic levels)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Best bioavailability among antibiotics (oral ≈ IV) - Ciprofloxacin: Best for Pseudomonas - Contraindicated: Children, pregnancy (cartilage damage)

Adverse Effects: 1. Tendinitis & tendon rupture: Achilles tendon (elderly, steroids) 2. Cartilage damage: Contraindicated in children & pregnancy 3. QT prolongation 4. CNS: Seizures, confusion (especially in elderly) 5. Photosensitivity 6. Hepatotoxicity 7. Peripheral neuropathy

NEET PG: Achilles tendon rupture = Fluoroquinolones

docs.title: First-Line Drugs (RIPES)
docs.text: **R**ifampicin, **I**soniazid, **P**yrazinamide, **E**thambutol, **S**treptomycin

docs.title: RIFAMPICIN
docs.text:
Mechanism: Inhibits RNA polymerase

Uses: TB, Leprosy, Meningococcal prophylaxis, Staphylococcal infections

Adverse Effects: - Orange/red discoloration of body fluids (tears, urine, sweat) - Hepatotoxicity - CYP450 inducer: ↓ Effectiveness of OCP, warfarin, antiretrovirals

NEET PG: Most potent bactericidal anti-TB drug

docs.title: ISONIAZID (INH)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Inhibits mycolic acid synthesis (via inhibition of enoyl-ACP reductase)

Adverse Effects: - Peripheral neuropathy: Give pyridoxine (Vit B6) prophylaxis - Hepatotoxicity: Most common cause of anti-TB drug-induced hepatitis - Drug-induced lupus

NEET PG: Give pyridoxine with INH to prevent neuropathy

docs.title: PYRAZINAMIDE (PZA)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Unknown (requires acidic pH)

Adverse Effects: - Hepatotoxicity - Hyperuricemia (inhibits uric acid excretion) → Gout

NEET PG: Causes hyperuricemia & gout

docs.title: ETHAMBUTOL
docs.text:
Mechanism: Inhibits arabinosyl transferase (cell wall synthesis)

Adverse Effects: - Optic neuritis: Decreased visual acuity, red-green color blindness (dose-related, reversible)

NEET PG: Monitor visual acuity & color vision

docs.title: STREPTOMYCIN
docs.text:
Mechanism: Aminoglycoside (30S inhibition)

Adverse Effects: Ototoxicity, nephrotoxicity

NEET PG: Now second-line (replaced by ethambutol in first-line regimen)

docs.title: TB Treatment Regimens
docs.text:
Category 1 (New smear-positive PTB): - Intensive phase (2 months): HRZE (daily) - Continuation phase (4 months): HR (daily) - Total: 6 months

Category 2 (Relapse, failure, TAD after default): - Intensive phase (3 months): HRZES (2 months) + HRZE (1 month) - Continuation phase (5 months): HRE - Total: 8 months

NEET PG: Standard ATT = 6 months (2HRZE + 4HR)

docs.title: POLYENES
docs.text:
Amphotericin B, Nystatin

Mechanism: Binds ergosterol in fungal cell membrane → Pore formation → Leakage

Uses: - Amphotericin B: Systemic mycoses (DOC for serious fungal infections), cryptococcal meningitis, mucormycosis - Nystatin: Topical use (oral thrush, vaginal candidiasis)

Adverse Effects (Amphotericin B): - Nephrotoxicity ("Ampho-terrible") - Infusion-related: Fever, chills, rigors ("shake and bake") - Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia - Anemia

NEET PG: - Liposomal formulation: ↓ Nephrotoxicity - DOC for mucormycosis

docs.title: AZOLES
docs.text:
Fluconazole, Itraconazole, Voriconazole, Posaconazole

Mechanism: Inhibit 14α-demethylase (fungal CYP450) → ↓ Ergosterol synthesis

Uses: - Fluconazole: Candida (except C. krusei), cryptococcal meningitis (AIDS), prophylaxis - Itraconazole: Aspergillus, Blastomyces, Histoplasma, Sporothrix, onychomycosis - Voriconazole: DOC for Aspergillus, Candida (including fluconazole-resistant)

Adverse Effects: - Hepatotoxicity - Drug interactions: CYP450 inhibitors - Voriconazole: Visual disturbances, photosensitivity

NEET PG: - Voriconazole: DOC for Aspergillus - Fluconazole: Ineffective against Aspergillus, C. krusei

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. DOC for MRSA? Answer: Vancomycin

  2. Only cephalosporin covering MRSA? Answer: Ceftaroline (5th generation)

  3. DOC for Listeria? Answer: Ampicillin

  4. Antibiotic causing red man syndrome? Answer: Vancomycin

  5. DOC for Rickettsiae? Answer: Doxycycline

  6. Antibiotic safe in penicillin allergy? Answer: Aztreonam

  7. Most common adverse effect of aminoglycosides? Answer: Nephrotoxicity & ototoxicity

  8. Anti-TB drug causing optic neuritis? Answer: Ethambutol

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know mechanism of all major antibiotic classes
  • Memorize spectrum of coverage
  • Learn drug of choice for common infections
  • Understand adverse effects
  • Know TB treatment regimens
  • Learn resistance mechanisms
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on antimicrobials

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 5-7 questions)

docs.title: CARDIOVASCULAR DRUGS - NEET PG Study Material
Classification
docs.text:
1. Diuretics
  2. Sympathoplegics (β-blockers, α-blockers, Central acting)
  3. Vasodilators (ACE-I, ARBs, CCBs, Direct vasodilators)
  4. Others

docs.title: Thiazide Diuretics
docs.text:
Prototype: Hydrochlorothiazide, Chlorthalidone, Indapamide

Site of action: Distal convoluted tubule (DCT) Mechanism: Block Na-Cl cotransporter (NCC)

Effects: - Moderate diuresis - ↓ BP (first-line for hypertension) - ↑ Ca2+ reabsorption (prevent kidney stones)

Uses: - Hypertension (first-line, especially elderly) - Edema (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome) - Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (paradoxical effect) - Hypercalciuria (recurrent kidney stones)

Adverse effects: - Hypokalemia (most common) - Hyponatremia - Hypercalcemia - Hyperglycemia (worsen diabetes) - Hyperuricemia (precipitate gout) - Hyperlipidemia - Hypochloremic alkalosis - Erectile dysfunction

Contraindications: Gout, hypokalemia

NEET PG High-Yield: - First-line for hypertension (JNC 8) - Hypercalcemia (opposite of loop diuretics) - Used in nephrogenic DI (paradoxical) - 4 Hypers: Hyperglycemia, Hyperlipidemia, Hyperuricemia, Hypercalcemia

docs.title: Loop Diuretics
docs.text:
Drugs: Furosemide, Bumetanide, Torsemide, Ethacrynic acid

Site: Thick ascending limb of loop of Henle (TAL) Mechanism: Block Na-K-2Cl cotransporter (NKCC2)

Effects: - Most efficacious diuretics ("high ceiling") - ↑ Ca2+ excretion (hypocalcemia) - ↓ Renal concentrating ability

Uses: - Acute pulmonary edema (IV furosemide - DOC) - Acute renal failure (maintain urine output) - Hypercalcemia (↑ Ca2+ excretion) - CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome - Cerebral edema

Adverse effects: - Hypokalemia (arrhythmias) - Hypocalcemia - Hypomagnesemia - Metabolic alkalosis - Ototoxicity (especially with aminoglycosides) - Hyperuricemia (gout)

NEET PG High-Yield: - DOC for acute pulmonary edema - Most efficacious diuretics - Hypocalcemia (opposite of thiazides) - Ototoxicity (ethacrynic acid > furosemide) - Used in hypercalcemia

docs.title: A. Aldosterone Antagonists
docs.text:
Drugs: Spironolactone, Eplerenone

Site: Collecting duct Mechanism: Block aldosterone receptors

Effects: - Mild diuresis - ↓ K+ excretion (hyperkalemia risk) - Anti-androgenic (spironolactone)

Uses: - Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn's syndrome) - CHF (mortality benefit - RALES trial) - Cirrhosis with ascites (DOC) - Hirsutism, acne (spironolactone - anti-androgen)

Adverse effects: - Hyperkalemia (most serious) - Gynecomastia (spironolactone - anti-androgen) - Metabolic acidosis

NEET PG: - Spironolactone: Mortality benefit in CHF - DOC for ascites in cirrhosis - Gynecomastia (spironolactone, NOT eplerenone)

docs.title: B. ENaC Blockers
docs.text:
Drugs: Amiloride, Triamterene

Mechanism: Block epithelial Na+ channels (ENaC)

Uses: - With thiazides (prevent hypokalemia) - Lithium-induced nephrogenic DI

NEET PG: Amiloride blocks ENaC (direct effect, not via aldosterone)

docs.title: Osmotic Diuretics
docs.text:
Drug: Mannitol

Mechanism: Osmotic effect (not reabsorbed)

Uses: - Cerebral edema (↓ ICP) - Acute glaucoma (↓ intraocular pressure) - Prevent acute renal failure (during surgery, rhabdomyolysis)

Adverse effects: - Pulmonary edema (initial ↑ plasma volume) - Hyponatremia (dilutional)

Contraindications: Anuria, pulmonary edema, CHF

NEET PG: - DOC for cerebral edema & acute glaucoma - Contraindicated in anuria (will worsen)

docs.title: Beta-Blockers
docs.text:
Classification: 1. Non-selective (β1 + β2): Propranolol, nadolol, timolol 2. Selective β1: Atenolol, Metoprolol, bisoprolol, esmolol 3. α + β blockers: Labetalol, carvedilol

Mnemonic for selective β1: "**A**ccepted **M**etoprolol **B**isoprolol **E**smolol"

Mechanism: Block β-adrenergic receptors - ↓ Heart rate (β1) - ↓ Contractility (β1) - ↓ Renin release (β1) - ↓ AV conduction (β1)

Uses: - Hypertension (especially with CAD, post-MI) - Angina (↓ myocardial O2 demand) - Post-MI (↓ mortality) - CHF (bisoprolol, metoprolol, carvedilol - mortality benefit) - Arrhythmias (SVT, AF, ventricular arrhythmias) - Hyperthyroidism (symptomatic relief) - Migraine prophylaxis (propranolol) - Essential tremor (propranolol) - Glaucoma (timolol eye drops) - Variceal bleeding (propranolol - ↓ portal pressure)

Adverse effects: - Bronchospasm (β2 blockade - avoid in asthma) - Bradycardia, AV block - Mask hypoglycemia (in diabetics) - Worsen peripheral vascular disease - Fatigue, depression - Erectile dysfunction - Rebound hypertension (abrupt withdrawal)

Contraindications: - Asthma, COPD (non-selective) - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree) - Cardiogenic shock - Severe bradycardia

NEET PG High-Yield: - Mortality benefit: Post-MI & CHF - Contraindicated in asthma (bronchospasm) - Selective β1: Atenolol, Metoprolol (safer in COPD) - Labetalol: α + β blocker (DOC in pregnancy) - Esmolol: Ultra-short acting (IV, for arrhythmias)

docs.title: ACE Inhibitors
docs.text:
Drugs: Enalapril, Lisinopril, Ramipril, Captopril, Perindopril

Mechanism: - Inhibit ACE (Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme) - Block: Angiotensin I → Angiotensin II - ↓ Degradation of bradykinin (accumulates)

Effects: - ↓ BP (vasodilation) - ↓ Aldosterone (↓ Na+ retention, ↑ K+) - ↓ Cardiac remodeling - Renoprotective (↓ intraglomerular pressure)

Uses: - Hypertension (first-line, especially with diabetes/CKD) - CHF (mortality benefit - reduce afterload) - Post-MI (prevent remodeling) - Diabetic nephropathy (slow progression) - Proteinuria (↓ intraglomerular pressure)

Adverse effects: - Dry cough (10-20%) - Due to ↑ bradykinin (most common side effect) - Hyperkalemia (↓ aldosterone) - Angioedema (rare but serious - due to ↑ bradykinin) - Acute renal failure in bilateral renal artery stenosis - Teratogenic (fetal renal dysgenesis - contraindicated in pregnancy) - First-dose hypotension - Taste alteration (captopril)

Contraindications: - Pregnancy (Category D) - Bilateral renal artery stenosis - Hyperkalemia - Angioedema (history)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Dry cough: Most common side effect (switch to ARB) - Teratogenic (avoid in pregnancy) - Renoprotective (slow diabetic nephropathy) - Contraindicated in bilateral RAS (GFR depends on efferent constriction) - Mortality benefit: CHF & post-MI

docs.title: Angiotensin Receptor Blockers (ARBs)
docs.text:
Drugs: Losartan, Telmisartan, Valsartan, Irbesartan, Candesartan

Mechanism: Block AT1 receptors (Angiotensin II receptors)

Advantages over ACE-I: - No cough (do not affect bradykinin) - No angioedema (rare) - Better tolerated

Uses: Same as ACE inhibitors - Hypertension (alternative to ACE-I) - CHF - Diabetic nephropathy - Post-MI

Adverse effects: Similar to ACE-I (but no cough/angioedema) - Hyperkalemia - Teratogenic (contraindicated in pregnancy) - Hypotension

NEET PG: - Alternative to ACE-I (if cough develops) - No cough or angioedema (doesn't affect bradykinin) - Losartan: Also ↓ uric acid (good for gout with HTN)

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
1. Dihydropyridines (vascular-selective): - Amlodipine, Nifedipine, Felodipine, Nicardipine, Nimodipine

2. Non-dihydropyridines (cardiac-selective): - Verapamil, Diltiazem

Mechanism: Block L-type Ca2+ channels - Dihydropyridines: Peripheral vasodilation (↓ afterload) - Verapamil/Diltiazem: ↓ Heart rate, ↓ contractility, ↓ AV conduction

docs.title: Dihydropyridines (Amlodipine, Nifedipine)
docs.text:
Uses: - Hypertension (especially elderly, isolated systolic HTN) - Variant (Prinzmetal's) angina (coronary vasospasm) - Raynaud's phenomenon

Adverse effects: - Reflex tachycardia (nifedipine > amlodipine) - Peripheral edema (ankle swelling) - Flushing, headache - Gingival hyperplasia

NEET PG: - DOC for variant angina (Prinzmetal's) - Reflex tachycardia (peripheral vasodilation) - Safe in asthma (unlike β-blockers)

docs.title: Non-Dihydropyridines (Verapamil, Diltiazem)
docs.text:
Uses: - Hypertension - Angina (stable, unstable) - Supraventricular arrhythmias (SVT, AF rate control) - Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (verapamil)

Adverse effects: - Constipation (verapamil - most common) - Bradycardia, AV block - Worsening of heart failure (negative inotrope)

Contraindications: - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree) - Do NOT combine with β-blockers (↑ risk of bradycardia, heart block)

NEET PG: - Verapamil: Constipation (most common side effect) - DOC for SVT (verapamil/diltiazem) - Contraindicated with β-blockers (additive cardiac depression)

docs.title: Nitrates
docs.text:
Drugs: Nitroglycerin (sublingual, IV, transdermal), Isosorbide dinitrate, Isosorbide mononitrate

Mechanism: - Release nitric oxide (NO) - ↑ cGMP → Smooth muscle relaxation - Venodilation > Arteriodilation

Effects: - ↓ Preload (venodilation) - ↓ myocardial O2 demand - ↓ Afterload (mild arteriodilation) - Coronary vasodilation

Uses: - Acute angina (sublingual nitroglycerin - DOC) - Prophylaxis of angina (long-acting nitrates) - Acute MI (IV nitroglycerin) - Acute pulmonary edema (↓ preload) - Esophageal spasm

Adverse effects: - Headache (most common - cerebral vasodilation) - Postural hypotension (reflex tachycardia) - Tolerance (with continuous use - give nitrate-free interval) - Methemoglobinemia (high doses)

Contraindications: - Sildenafil (Viagra) - Severe hypotension (both ↑ cGMP) - Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy - Severe aortic stenosis - Right ventricular infarction (preload-dependent)

NEET PG High-Yield: - DOC for acute angina (sublingual nitroglycerin) - Headache: Most common side effect - Tolerance: Give nitrate-free interval (10-12 hrs) - Contraindicated with sildenafil (PDE-5 inhibitor)

docs.title: Drugs that ↓ Mortality in CHF
docs.text:
1. ACE inhibitors (or ARBs)
  2. β-blockers (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol)
  3. Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone)
  4. ARNI (Angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor - sacubitril/valsartan)
  5. SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin)

NEET PG: These 5 drug classes ↓ mortality in CHF

docs.title: Cardiac Glycosides (Digoxin)
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Inhibits Na+/K+-ATPase pump - ↑ Intracellular Na+ → ↑ Ca2+ (via Na+/Ca2+ exchanger) - Positive inotrope (↑ contractility) - Negative chronotrope (↓ heart rate via ↑ vagal tone) - ↓ AV conduction

Uses: - CHF with AF (rate control + inotropy) - Systolic HF (improve symptoms, no mortality benefit)

Adverse effects: - Arrhythmias (most serious): VPCs, VT, AV block - GI: Nausea, vomiting, anorexia - CNS: Confusion, visual disturbances (yellow-green vision - xanthopsia) - Gynecomastia

Toxicity: - Predisposing factors: Hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, hypomagnesemia, renal failure - ECG: Scooping of ST segment, T wave inversion - Treatment: Stop digoxin, Digoxin-specific Fab antibodies, correct electrolytes

Contraindications: WPW syndrome, AV block

NEET PG High-Yield: - No mortality benefit in CHF (improves symptoms only) - Yellow-green vision (xanthopsia) - classic toxicity - Hypokalemia worsens toxicity (competes for Na+/K+-ATPase) - Treatment of toxicity: Digoxin-specific Fab antibodies

docs.title: IA (Moderate blockade, ↑ APD)
docs.text:
Drugs: Quinidine, Procainamide, Disopyramide

Uses: Atrial & ventricular arrhythmias Adverse effects: - Quinidine: Cinchonism, torsades de pointes, diarrhea - Procainamide: Drug-induced lupus, agranulocytosis

docs.title: IB (Weak blockade, ↓ APD)
docs.text:
Drugs: Lidocaine, Mexiletine

Uses: Ventricular arrhythmias (post-MI) Route: IV only (high first-pass metabolism) Adverse effects: CNS (seizures), cardiac depression

NEET PG: Lidocaine for ventricular arrhythmias (IV)

docs.title: IC (Strong blockade, no change in APD)
docs.text:
Drugs: Flecainide, Propafenone

Uses: Supraventricular arrhythmias, AF Contraindications: Structural heart disease, post-MI (↑ mortality)

docs.title: Class II: Beta-Blockers
docs.text:
Drugs: Propranolol, atenolol, metoprolol, esmolol

Uses: SVT, AF (rate control), ventricular arrhythmias

NEET PG: Esmolol - ultra-short acting (t½ = 9 min)

docs.title: Class III: Potassium Channel Blockers (↑ APD)
docs.text:
Drugs: Amiodarone, Sotalol, Dofetilide, Ibutilide

Amiodarone: - Most effective antiarrhythmic - Uses: All arrhythmias (atrial & ventricular), refractory VF/VT - Adverse effects: - Pulmonary fibrosis (most serious) - Thyroid dysfunction (hypo/hyperthyroidism - contains iodine) - Hepatotoxicity - Corneal deposits (reversible) - Photosensitivity (blue-gray skin) - Bradycardia

NEET PG High-Yield: - Most effective antiarrhythmic - Pulmonary fibrosis - most serious side effect - Contains iodine (thyroid dysfunction)

docs.title: Class IV: Calcium Channel Blockers
docs.text:
Drugs: Verapamil, Diltiazem

Uses: SVT, AF (rate control)

docs.title: Others (Not in Vaughan Williams)
docs.text:
Adenosine: - DOC for acute SVT (AVNRT, AVRT) - Ultra-short t½ (< 10 seconds) - Mechanism: Activates K+ channels, ↑ AV block - Adverse effects: Flushing, dyspnea, chest pain (transient) - Contraindicated: Asthma (bronchospasm), WPW with AF

NEET PG: Adenosine - DOC for acute SVT (ultra-short acting)

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. First-line antihypertensive in elderly? Answer: Thiazide diuretic

  2. DOC for acute pulmonary edema? Answer: IV Furosemide

  3. Most common side effect of ACE inhibitors? Answer: Dry cough

  4. Antihypertensive contraindicated in pregnancy? Answer: ACE inhibitors, ARBs

  5. DOC for acute angina? Answer: Sublingual nitroglycerin

  6. Drugs with mortality benefit in CHF? Answer: ACE-I, β-blockers, Spironolactone, ARNI, SGLT2-I

  7. Most effective antiarrhythmic? Answer: Amiodarone

  8. DOC for acute SVT? Answer: Adenosine

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know diuretic sites of action
  • Understand ACE-I vs ARB differences
  • Learn β-blocker contraindications
  • Know CCB classification
  • Memorize drugs with CHF mortality benefit
  • Understand digoxin toxicity
  • Learn Vaughan Williams classification
  • Know antiarrhythmic drug uses
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on CVS drugs

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (6-8 questions)

docs.title: CHOLINERGIC & ANTICHOLINERGIC DRUGS - NEET PG Study Material
Synthesis & Breakdown
docs.text:
Choline + Acetyl-CoA --[ChAT]--> Acetylcholine
Acetylcholine --[AChE]--> Choline + Acetate

ChAT: Choline acetyltransferase (synthesis enzyme) AChE: Acetylcholinesterase (breakdown enzyme)

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
1. Muscarinic Receptors (M1-M5) - G-protein coupled receptors - Location: All parasympathetic effector organs - Subtypes: - M1: Gastric parietal cells, CNS (memory) - M2: Heart (↓ HR, ↓ conduction) - M3: Smooth muscle, glands (contraction, secretion) - M4, M5: CNS

2. Nicotinic Receptors (NN, NM) - Ion channel receptors - NN (Neural): Autonomic ganglia, adrenal medulla - NM (Muscle): Neuromuscular junction (skeletal muscle)

NEET PG: M2 on heart, M3 on smooth muscle & glands

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
A. Direct-acting (receptor agonists): 1. Choline esters: Acetylcholine, Bethanechol, Carbachol, Methacholine 2. Alkaloids: Pilocarpine, Muscarine

B. Indirect-acting (AChE inhibitors): 1. Reversible: Neostigmine, Physostigmine, Pyridostigmine, Edrophonium, Rivastigmine, Donepezil 2. Irreversible: Organophosphates (Malathion, Parathion), Echothiophate

docs.title: BETHANECHOL
docs.text:
Properties: - Selective M3 agonist - Not hydrolyzed by AChE (resistant) - Mainly affects GI & bladder

Actions: - ↑ GI motility - ↑ Bladder contraction - ↑ Salivation

Uses: - Postoperative urinary retention - Postoperative paralytic ileus - Neurogenic bladder

NEET PG High-Yield: Bethanechol = Urinary retention, paralytic ileus

docs.title: CARBACHOL
docs.text:
Properties: - Both muscarinic + nicotinic agonist - Not hydrolyzed by AChE

Uses: - Glaucoma (topical - ↓ IOP)

docs.title: PILOCARPINE
docs.text:
Properties: - Tertiary amine (crosses BBB) - Muscarinic agonist

Actions: - Miosis (pupil constriction) - Ciliary muscle contraction (↓ IOP) - ↑ Salivation, sweating

Uses: - Glaucoma (open-angle & emergency angle-closure) - Xerostomia (dry mouth - Sjögren's syndrome)

Adverse effects: - Sweating, salivation - Diarrhea

NEET PG: Pilocarpine = Glaucoma, causes miosis

docs.title: Reversible AChE Inhibitors
docs.text:
NEOSTIGMINE

Properties: - Quaternary compound (doesn't cross BBB) - Both muscarinic + nicotinic effects - Duration: 2-4 hours

Actions: - ↑ ACh at NMJ (muscle strength) - ↑ ACh at parasympathetic sites

Uses: 1. Myasthenia gravis (diagnosis & treatment) 2. Reversal of non-depolarizing NM blockade 3. Postoperative urinary retention/ileus

Adverse effects: - Muscarinic effects: SLUDGE - **S**alivation - **L**acrimation - **U**rination - **D**efecation - **G**I upset - **E**mesis (vomiting) - Bradycardia - Bronchospasm

Antidote for overdose: Atropine (muscarinic antagonist)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Neostigmine = Myasthenia gravis, reversal of NM block - Overdose antidote = Atropine

PHYSOSTIGMINE

Properties: - Tertiary amine (crosses BBB) - Duration: 2-4 hours

Uses: - Glaucoma (now rarely used) - Atropine poisoning (crosses BBB to reverse CNS effects)

NEET PG: Physostigmine crosses BBB (vs neostigmine)

PYRIDOSTIGMINE

Properties: - Longer duration than neostigmine (4-6 hours) - Less muscarinic side effects

Uses: - Myasthenia gravis (chronic treatment - preferred over neostigmine)

EDROPHONIUM

Properties: - Very short duration (5-10 minutes) - Rapid onset

Uses: - Tensilon test (diagnosis of myasthenia gravis) - Differentiate myasthenic crisis vs cholinergic crisis

Tensilon Test: - Give edrophonium IV - Myasthenic crisis: Symptoms improve (need more AChE inhibitor) - Cholinergic crisis: Symptoms worsen (too much AChE inhibitor)

NEET PG: Edrophonium = Tensilon test for MG diagnosis

DONEPEZIL, RIVASTIGMINE, GALANTAMINE

Uses: - Alzheimer's disease (mild to moderate) - ↑ ACh in brain (improves cognition)

NEET PG: Donepezil = Alzheimer's disease

docs.title: Irreversible AChE Inhibitors
docs.text:
ORGANOPHOSPHATES (Insecticides)

Examples: Malathion, Parathion, Sarin (nerve gas)

Mechanism: - Irreversible binding to AChE - Phosphorylation of enzyme - "Aging" occurs (cannot be reversed after 24-48 hours)

Toxicity (Poisoning):

Acute symptoms: - Muscarinic: SLUDGE + miosis, bradycardia, bronchospasm - Nicotinic: Muscle fasciculations → paralysis - CNS: Confusion, seizures, coma, respiratory failure

NEET PG High-Yield: Organophosphate poisoning presentation

Treatment: 1. Atropine (high dose, repeat) - Blocks muscarinic effects - Give until atropinization (dry mouth, dilated pupils, ↑ HR) 2. Pralidoxime (2-PAM) - Reactivates AChE (if given BEFORE aging) - Reverses nicotinic effects - Must give within 24-48 hours 3. Supportive: Ventilation, decontamination

NEET PG High-Yield: - Organophosphate poisoning = Atropine + Pralidoxime (2-PAM) - Pralidoxime reactivates AChE (must give early)

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
A. Muscarinic Antagonists: 1. Natural alkaloids: Atropine, Scopolamine (Hyoscine) 2. Synthetic: - GI: Dicyclomine, Propantheline, Pirenzepine - Respiratory: Ipratropium, Tiotropium - Urinary: Oxybutynin, Tolterodine - Parkinson's: Benztropine, Trihexyphenidyl

B. Nicotinic Antagonists: 1. Ganglionic blockers: Hexamethonium, Trimethaphan 2. Neuromuscular blockers: Covered separately

docs.title: ATROPINE - Prototype
docs.text:
Source: Atropa belladonna (Deadly nightshade)

Mechanism: Competitive antagonist at muscarinic receptors

docs.title: Pharmacological Actions
docs.text:
1. Eye: - Mydriasis (pupil dilation) - Cycloplegia (paralysis of accommodation) - ↑ Intraocular pressure (contraindicated in glaucoma)

2. Cardiovascular: - Tachycardia (blocks M2 on heart) - ↑ AV conduction - May cause initial bradycardia (central effect)

3. Respiratory: - Bronchodilation - ↓ Secretions (dry airways)

4. GI: - ↓ Motility (antispasmodic) - ↓ Secretions - Relaxes smooth muscle

5. Urinary: - Urinary retention (relaxes detrusor, contracts sphincter)

6. Glands: - ↓ Salivation (dry mouth) - ↓ Sweating (hot, dry skin) - ↓ Lacrimation

7. CNS (high doses): - Restlessness, confusion - Hallucinations, delirium

NEET PG: Remember effects by blocking parasympathetic

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Ophthalmic: - Mydriatic (fundoscopy) - Cycloplegic refraction - Uveitis (prevent synechiae)

2. Cardiac: - Sinus bradycardia - AV block - Asystole (ACLS protocol)

3. Anesthesia: - Premedication (↓ salivary & bronchial secretions) - Prevent vagal reflexes during surgery

4. Organophosphate poisoning (antidote)

5. GI: - Peptic ulcer (now rarely used) - IBS (antispasmodic)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Atropine = Bradycardia, organophosphate poisoning - Premedication in anesthesia

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
"Hot as a hare, Blind as a bat, Dry as a bone, Red as a beet, Mad as a hatter"

  • Hot: Hyperthermia (↓ sweating)
  • Blind: Mydriasis, cycloplegia
  • Dry: Dry mouth, skin (↓ secretions)
  • Red: Flushing (vasodilation)
  • Mad: CNS effects (confusion, hallucinations)

Contraindications: - Glaucoma (↑ IOP) - Prostatic hypertrophy (urinary retention) - Paralytic ileus

NEET PG: Learn the mnemonic for atropine toxicity

docs.title: Atropine Poisoning
docs.text:
Symptoms: Same as adverse effects (above mnemonic)

Treatment: - Physostigmine (crosses BBB, reverses CNS + peripheral effects) - Supportive care, cooling

NEET PG: Physostigmine for atropine poisoning (central + peripheral)

docs.title: SCOPOLAMINE (Hyoscine)
docs.text:
Differences from Atropine: - Stronger CNS effects (sedation, amnesia) - Less tachycardia - Antiemetic effect

Uses: - Motion sickness (transdermal patch) - Premedication (sedation + amnesia)

NEET PG: Scopolamine = Motion sickness

docs.title: Selective Muscarinic Antagonists
docs.text:
IPRATROPIUM, TIOTROPIUM

Properties: - M3 antagonist - Quaternary compounds (poor systemic absorption) - Inhaled bronchodilators

Uses: - COPD (first-line) - Asthma (add-on therapy)

Advantages: - Minimal systemic effects - No cardiac effects

NEET PG: Ipratropium/Tiotropium = COPD

PIRENZEPINE

Properties: - Selective M1 antagonist - ↓ Gastric acid secretion

Uses: - Peptic ulcer (now replaced by PPIs)

OXYBUTYNIN, TOLTERODINE

Properties: - M3 antagonist - Relax bladder smooth muscle

Uses: - Overactive bladder (urge incontinence) - Nocturnal enuresis

Adverse effects: - Dry mouth, constipation - Confusion (elderly)

NEET PG: Oxybutynin = Overactive bladder

BENZTROPINE, TRIHEXYPHENIDYL

Uses: - Parkinson's disease (tremor, rigidity) - Drug-induced parkinsonism (antipsychotics)

Mechanism: ↓ Cholinergic activity (restore dopamine-ACh balance)

NEET PG: Anticholinergics for Parkinson's tremor

docs.title: Ganglionic Blockers
docs.text:
Examples: Hexamethonium, Trimethaphan

Mechanism: Block NN receptors at autonomic ganglia

Effects: - ↓ BP (vasodilation) - Inhibit both sympathetic & parasympathetic

Uses: - Hypertensive emergency (now obsolete) - Controlled hypotension (surgery)

NEET PG: Hexamethonium = Ganglionic blocker (historical)

docs.title: High-Yield Comparisons
docs.text: Drug Receptor Use Key Point Bethanechol M3 agonist Urinary retention, ileus Resistant to AChE Pilocarpine M agonist Glaucoma Causes miosis Neostigmine AChE inhibitor Myasthenia gravis, NM reversal Quaternary (doesn't cross BBB) Physostigmine AChE inhibitor Atropine poisoning Tertiary (crosses BBB) Edrophonium AChE inhibitor Tensilon test Very short acting Atropine M antagonist Bradycardia, organophosphate poisoning "Hot, blind, dry, red, mad" Scopolamine M antagonist Motion sickness Stronger CNS effects Ipratropium M3 antagonist COPD Inhaled, minimal systemic

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Drug of choice for postoperative urinary retention? Answer: Bethanechol

  2. Antidote for atropine poisoning? Answer: Physostigmine (crosses BBB)

  3. Antidote for organophosphate poisoning? Answer: Atropine + Pralidoxime (2-PAM)

  4. Tensilon test uses which drug? Answer: Edrophonium

  5. Drug for motion sickness? Answer: Scopolamine

  6. First-line inhaled bronchodilator for COPD? Answer: Tiotropium (or Ipratropium)

  7. Pralidoxime works by? Answer: Reactivating acetylcholinesterase

  8. Contraindication for atropine? Answer: Glaucoma (↑ IOP)

docs.title: Atropine Toxicity
docs.text: "Hot as a hare, Blind as a bat, Dry as a bone, Red as a beet, Mad as a hatter"

docs.title: Muscarinic Effects (SLUDGE)
docs.text:
• **S**alivation
  • **L**acrimation
  • **U**rination
  • **D**efecation
  • **G**I upset
  • **E**mesis

docs.title: Organophosphate Poisoning Treatment
docs.text: "2-PAM and Atropine Rapidly" (PAR) - **P**ralidoxime (2-PAM) - **A**tropine - **R**apidly (within 24-48 hours)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know cholinergic receptor types (M1-M3, NN, NM)
  • Understand direct vs indirect cholinergic agonists
  • Learn uses of bethanechol, pilocarpine, neostigmine
  • Know organophosphate poisoning treatment
  • Understand atropine actions & uses
  • Learn atropine toxicity mnemonic
  • Know selective anticholinergics (ipratropium, oxybutynin)
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on cholinergic drugs

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: ADRENERGIC AGONISTS (Sympathomimetics) - NEET PG Study Material
Catecholamine Synthesis
docs.text:
Tyrosine
  ↓ [Tyrosine hydroxylase] ← Rate-limiting step
DOPA
  ↓ [DOPA decarboxylase]
Dopamine
  ↓ [Dopamine β-hydroxylase]
Norepinephrine (NE)
  ↓ [PNMT - only in adrenal medulla]
Epinephrine (E)

NEET PG: Tyrosine hydroxylase = Rate-limiting enzyme

docs.title: Catecholamine Metabolism
docs.text:
Enzymes: 1. MAO (Monoamine oxidase) - intracellular 2. COMT (Catechol-O-methyltransferase) - extracellular

End product: VMA (Vanillylmandelic acid) - excreted in urine

NEET PG: VMA in urine - diagnostic for pheochromocytoma

docs.title: Classification & Location
docs.text:
Receptor Location Effect Mechanism α1 Vascular smooth muscle Vasoconstriction Gq → ↑ IP3, Ca²⁺ Radial muscle (iris) Mydriasis GU sphincters Contraction α2 Presynaptic nerve terminals ↓ NE release (negative feedback) Gi → ↓ cAMP Pancreatic β-cells ↓ Insulin secretion Platelets Aggregation β1 Heart ↑ HR, ↑ contractility, ↑ conduction Gs → ↑ cAMP Kidney (JGA) ↑ Renin release β2 Bronchial smooth muscle Bronchodilation Gs → ↑ cAMP Vascular smooth muscle (skeletal) Vasodilation Uterus Relaxation Liver Glycogenolysis β3 Adipose tissue Lipolysis Gs → ↑ cAMP Bladder (detrusor) Relaxation

NEET PG High-Yield: - α1: Vasoconstriction - β1: Heart ("1 heart") - β2: Lungs ("2 lungs")

docs.title: By Mechanism
docs.text:
A. Direct-acting: Directly stimulate receptors - Epinephrine, Norepinephrine, Dopamine - Phenylephrine, Clonidine - Salbutamol, Dobutamine

B. Indirect-acting: Release stored NE - Amphetamine, Tyramine, Ephedrine (partial)

C. Mixed: Both direct + indirect - Ephedrine, Pseudoephedrine

docs.title: By Chemical Structure
docs.text:
A. Catecholamines (3,4-dihydroxybenzene): - Epinephrine, Norepinephrine, Dopamine - Dobutamine, Isoprenaline - Characteristics: - Cannot cross BBB (polar) - Metabolized by MAO & COMT - Short duration of action - Cannot be given orally (first-pass metabolism)

B. Non-catecholamines: - Phenylephrine, Ephedrine, Amphetamine - Salbutamol, Terbutaline - Characteristics: - Can cross BBB - Longer duration - Can be given orally

NEET PG: Catecholamines have short duration, can't cross BBB

docs.title: EPINEPHRINE (Adrenaline)
docs.text: Receptor activity: α1, α2, β1, β2 (all receptors)

docs.title: Cardiovascular Effects (Dose-dependent)
docs.text:
Low dose (β effects predominate): - β1: ↑ HR, ↑ contractility, ↑ cardiac output - β2: Vasodilation (skeletal muscle vessels) - Net effect: ↑ SBP, ↓ DBP, ↓ Total peripheral resistance (TPR)

High dose (α effects predominate): - α1: Vasoconstriction (all vessels) - Net effect: ↑ SBP, ↑ DBP, ↑ TPR

NEET PG: Low-dose epinephrine ↓ DBP (β2 vasodilation)

docs.title: Other Actions
docs.text:
Respiratory: - Bronchodilation (β2) - powerful - ↓ Mediator release (mast cells)

Metabolic: - ↑ Glycogenolysis (β2) → hyperglycemia - ↑ Lipolysis (β3) - ↑ Glucagon, ↓ insulin secretion

Eye: Mydriasis (α1)

GU: Uterine relaxation (β2)

CNS: Minimal (doesn't cross BBB)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Anaphylaxis ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Drug of choice - IM/IV administration - Reverses bronchospasm, hypotension, angioedema

2. Cardiac arrest - ACLS protocol - IV/Intraosseous/Endotracheal

3. Acute asthma (severe) - Nebulized/IM - Rapid bronchodilation

4. With local anesthetics - ↓ Absorption (vasoconstriction) - Prolongs duration - ↓ Systemic toxicity - Contraindicated: Fingers, toes, penis (risk of gangrene)

5. Glaucoma - ↓ Aqueous humor production

NEET PG High-Yield: - Epinephrine = Drug of choice for anaphylaxis - Don't use with LA in digits

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• Anxiety, tremor, palpitations
  • Cerebral hemorrhage (hypertensive crisis)
  • Arrhythmias (ventricular)
  • Pulmonary edema
  • Hyperglycemia

Contraindications: - Hypertension - Hyperthyroidism - Coronary artery disease - Closed-angle glaucoma

docs.title: NOREPINEPHRINE (Noradrenaline)
docs.text: Receptor activity: α1, α2, β1 (NO β2)

docs.title: Cardiovascular Effects
docs.text:
• α1: Intense vasoconstriction (all vessels)
  • β1: ↑ Contractility
  • Net effect:
  • ↑ SBP, ↑ DBP, ↑ TPR
  • Reflex bradycardia (baroreceptor response to ↑ BP)
  • ↓ or no change in cardiac output

NEET PG: NE causes reflex bradycardia (vs epinephrine ↑ HR)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
Shock (cardiogenic, septic): - Potent vasoconstrictor - Maintains BP - Preferred in septic shock (over dopamine)

NEET PG: NE = Drug of choice for septic shock

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• Severe hypertension
  • Tissue necrosis if extravasation (α1-mediated)
  • Antidote: Phentolamine (α-blocker) - local infiltration
  • Reflex bradycardia
  • Reduced organ perfusion (intense vasoconstriction)

NEET PG: Phentolamine for NE extravasation

docs.title: DOPAMINE
docs.text: Receptor activity (dose-dependent): - Low dose (1-2 mcg/kg/min): D1 receptors - Medium dose (2-10 mcg/kg/min): β1 receptors - High dose (>10 mcg/kg/min): α1 receptors

docs.title: Effects by Dose
docs.text:
Low dose ("Renal dose"): - D1: Renal & mesenteric vasodilation - ↑ Renal blood flow - ↑ GFR, ↑ Na⁺ excretion

Medium dose: - β1: ↑ Contractility, ↑ HR, ↑ cardiac output - Minimal vasoconstriction

High dose: - α1: Vasoconstriction - Similar to norepinephrine

NEET PG: Dopamine dose-dependent effects

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Shock (cardiogenic, septic) - Start medium dose - Adjust based on response

2. Heart failure (acute) - ↑ Cardiac output

NEET PG: Dopamine for cardiogenic shock

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• Tachycardia, arrhythmias
  • Nausea, vomiting (D2 receptors - CTZ)
  • Tissue necrosis (if extravasation)
  • ↑ Pulmonary shunt (worsen hypoxemia)

docs.title: DOBUTAMINE
docs.text: Receptor activity: β1 >> β2 (selective β1 agonist)

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
• β1: ↑ Contractility (↑ inotropy)
  • Minimal ↑ HR (less than dopamine)
  • β2: Mild vasodilation
  • Net: ↑ Cardiac output, ↓ preload, ↓ afterload

NEET PG: Dobutamine = ↑ contractility without much ↑ HR

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Acute heart failure (decompensated) - ↑ Cardiac output - Preferred over dopamine (less arrhythmogenic)

2. Cardiogenic shock

3. Stress echocardiography - Assess cardiac reserve

NEET PG: Dobutamine preferred over dopamine in heart failure

docs.title: ISOPRENALINE (Isoproterenol)
docs.text: Receptor activity: β1, β2 (pure β-agonist, NO α)

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
• β1: ↑ HR, ↑ contractility, ↑ cardiac output
  • β2: Bronchodilation, vasodilation
  • Net: ↑ SBP, ↓ DBP, ↓ TPR

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Heart block (AV block, bradycardia) - Emergency, until pacemaker

2. Shock (rarely used now)

NEET PG: Isoprenaline for AV block (emergency)

docs.title: PHENYLEPHRINE
docs.text: Receptor activity: Pure α1 agonist

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
• α1: Vasoconstriction
  • ↑ BP (systolic + diastolic)
  • Reflex bradycardia
  • Mydriasis (if topical on eye)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Hypotension (shock, anesthesia-induced)

2. Nasal decongestant - Vasoconstriction of nasal mucosa

3. Mydriatic (eye drops) - Pupil dilation (fundoscopy)

4. Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) - ↑ BP → reflex vagal stimulation → ↓ HR

NEET PG: Phenylephrine = Pure α1 agonist, causes reflex bradycardia

docs.title: CLONIDINE
docs.text: Receptor activity: α2 agonist (central + peripheral)

docs.title: Mechanism & Actions
docs.text:
Central α2 agonist: - Stimulates α2 in medulla - ↓ Sympathetic outflow - ↓ BP, ↓ HR

Peripheral α2: - Initial vasoconstriction (transient ↑ BP)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Hypertension - Especially with renal disease

2. Opioid withdrawal - ↓ Sympathetic symptoms

3. ADHD (off-label)

4. Prophylaxis of migraine

NEET PG: Clonidine = Central α2 agonist, antihypertensive

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• Sedation, dry mouth
  • Rebound hypertension (if stopped abruptly)
  • Bradycardia

NEET PG: Clonidine rebound hypertension if stopped suddenly

docs.title: Short-Acting β2 Agonists (SABA)
docs.text:
SALBUTAMOL (Albuterol), TERBUTALINE, LEVOSALBUTAMOL

Receptor: Selective β2

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
• Bronchodilation (β2 on bronchial smooth muscle)
  • Uterine relaxation (tocolytic)
  • Tremor (β2 on skeletal muscle)
  • ↑ K⁺ uptake (hypokalemia)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Asthma (acute attack) - Reliever/Rescue medication - Nebulized/MDI

2. COPD (symptomatic relief)

3. Hyperkalemia (emergency) - Shifts K⁺ into cells

4. Preterm labor (tocolytic - terbutaline)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Salbutamol = Asthma reliever - Salbutamol for hyperkalemia (shifts K⁺ intracellularly)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• Tremor (most common)
  • Tachycardia, palpitations
  • Hypokalemia
  • Hyperglycemia

NEET PG: SABA cause tremor, hypokalemia

docs.title: Long-Acting β2 Agonists (LABA)
docs.text:
SALMETEROL, FORMOTEROL

Duration: 12 hours

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
Asthma & COPD (chronic maintenance): - Controller medication - Always used with inhaled corticosteroids (not alone) - Given twice daily

NEET PG: LABA never used alone in asthma (risk of death)

docs.title: AMPHETAMINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Indirect (releases stored NE, blocks reuptake)

Actions: - CNS stimulation (alertness, ↓ appetite) - Cardiovascular: ↑ BP, ↑ HR - Tolerance develops

Uses: - ADHD (methylphenidate preferred) - Narcolepsy - Weight loss (off-label, not recommended)

Adverse effects: - Addiction, dependence - Psychosis - Hypertension, arrhythmias

NEET PG: Amphetamine = ADHD, narcolepsy, abuse potential

docs.title: EPHEDRINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Mixed (direct + indirect)

Actions: - Similar to epinephrine but: - Oral bioavailability (non-catecholamine) - Longer duration - CNS effects (crosses BBB)

Uses: - Nasal decongestant - Hypotension (anesthesia-induced) - Bronchodilator (now rarely used)

NEET PG: Ephedrine = Mixed-acting, nasal decongestant

docs.title: High-Yield Drug Comparison
docs.text: Drug Receptors Key Use Key Point Epinephrine α, β1, β2 Anaphylaxis DOC for anaphylaxis Norepinephrine α, β1 Septic shock Causes reflex bradycardia Dopamine D1, β1, α1 (dose-dependent) Cardiogenic shock Dose-dependent effects Dobutamine β1 >> β2 Heart failure ↑ Contractility, preferred in HF Isoprenaline β1, β2 AV block Pure β-agonist Phenylephrine α1 Nasal decongestant Pure α1, reflex bradycardia Salbutamol β2 Asthma (acute) SABA, reliever Salmeterol β2 Asthma (maintenance) LABA, never alone Clonidine α2 (central) Hypertension Rebound HTN if stopped

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Drug of choice for anaphylaxis? Answer: Epinephrine

  2. Drug of choice for septic shock? Answer: Norepinephrine

  3. Which sympathomimetic causes reflex bradycardia? Answer: Norepinephrine, Phenylephrine

  4. Antidote for norepinephrine extravasation? Answer: Phentolamine (local infiltration)

  5. Beta-blocker for acute asthma attack? Answer: Salbutamol (SABA)

  6. LABA should never be used alone in asthma because? Answer: Increases risk of asthma-related death

  7. Selective β1 agonist used in heart failure? Answer: Dobutamine

  8. Central α2 agonist causing rebound hypertension? Answer: Clonidine

docs.title: Receptor Selectivity
docs.text:
• β1 = "1 heart"
  • β2 = "2 lungs"

docs.title: Epinephrine Effects (FAST)
docs.text:
• **F**ight or flight
  • **A**naphylaxis treatment
  • **S**hock reversal
  • **T**achycardia

docs.title: Catecholamine Characteristics (3 C's)
docs.text:
• **C**an't cross BBB
  • **C**an't be given orally
  • **C**an't last long (short duration)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know adrenergic receptor locations & effects
  • Understand catecholamine vs non-catecholamine differences
  • Learn epinephrine uses (anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest)
  • Know NE vs epinephrine (reflex bradycardia)
  • Understand dopamine dose-dependent effects
  • Learn SABA vs LABA uses
  • Know clonidine mechanism (central α2)
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on adrenergic agonists

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: ADRENERGIC ANTAGONISTS (Sympatholytics) - NEET PG Study Material
By Receptor Selectivity
docs.text:
A. α-Blockers: 1. Non-selective: Phentolamine, Phenoxybenzamine, Tolazoline 2. Selective α1: Prazosin, Terazosin, Doxazosin, Tamsulosin, Alfuzosin 3. Selective α2: Yohimbine, Mirtazapine

B. β-Blockers: 1. Non-selective: Propranolol, Nadolol, Timolol 2. Selective β1: Atenolol, Metoprolol, Bisoprolol, Esmolol 3. Mixed (α + β): Labetalol, Carvedilol

C. α & β Blockers: Labetalol, Carvedilol

docs.title: PHENTOLAMINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Competitive α1 & α2 antagonist

Actions: - α1 blockade: Vasodilation → ↓ BP - α2 blockade: ↑ NE release → reflex tachycardia - ↑ Cardiac output

NEET PG: α2 blockade prevents negative feedback → ↑ NE release → tachycardia

Clinical Uses:

1. Pheochromocytoma: - Diagnosis: Phentolamine test (↓ BP if positive) - Preoperative management (before surgery) - Hypertensive crisis

2. Norepinephrine extravasation: - Local infiltration to prevent tissue necrosis

3. Clonidine overdose (reverses α2 effects)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Phentolamine = Pheochromocytoma, NE extravasation - Given BEFORE β-blocker in pheochromocytoma

Adverse Effects: - Reflex tachycardia (α2 blockade) - Orthostatic hypotension - Nasal congestion

docs.title: PHENOXYBENZAMINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Irreversible (non-competitive) α1 & α2 antagonist

Actions: - Long duration (24-48 hours) - Similar to phentolamine but longer acting

Clinical Uses:

1. Pheochromocytoma (preoperative preparation): - Drug of choice for medical management - Start 1-2 weeks before surgery - Allows volume expansion

NEET PG: Phenoxybenzamine = DOC for pheochromocytoma (preop)

Adverse Effects: - Postural hypotension - Reflex tachycardia - Ejaculation failure

docs.title: SELECTIVE α1-BLOCKERS
docs.text:
PRAZOSIN, TERAZOSIN, DOXAZOSIN

Mechanism: Selective competitive α1 antagonist

Actions: - α1 blockade: Vasodilation (arteriolar + venous) - ↓ Peripheral resistance - ↓ BP - No reflex tachycardia (α2 intact - negative feedback preserved) - Relaxes bladder neck & prostate smooth muscle

NEET PG: α1-selective blockers don't cause tachycardia (α2 intact)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Hypertension: - Effective but NOT first-line - Useful in combination therapy - First-dose hypotension (common - dose at bedtime)

2. Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH): - ↓ Bladder outlet obstruction - Improves urine flow

NEET PG High-Yield: - Prazosin = Hypertension + BPH - First-dose phenomenon (severe hypotension after first dose)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• First-dose hypotension (give at bedtime)
  • Orthostatic hypotension
  • Dizziness, headache
  • Nasal congestion
  • NO reflex tachycardia

NEET PG: First-dose hypotension with α1-blockers (prazosin)

docs.title: TAMSULOSIN, ALFUZOSIN
docs.text:
Properties: - Selective for α1A (prostate > vascular) - Less orthostatic hypotension than prazosin

Uses: - BPH (preferred over prazosin) - Less BP effect

NEET PG: Tamsulosin = BPH (uroselective)

docs.title: SELECTIVE α2-BLOCKERS
docs.text:
YOHIMBINE

Mechanism: α2 antagonist

Uses: - Erectile dysfunction (↑ sympathetic flow) - Research purposes

NEET PG: Yohimbine = α2 blocker (limited use)

docs.title: PROPRANOLOL - Prototype
docs.text:
Mechanism: Non-selective β1 & β2 antagonist

Pharmacological Actions:

1. Cardiovascular (β1): - ↓ Heart rate (negative chronotropy) - ↓ Contractility (negative inotropy) - ↓ AV conduction - ↓ Cardiac output - ↓ BP

2. Renin-angiotensin (β1): - ↓ Renin release → ↓ BP

3. Respiratory (β2): - Bronchoconstriction (↑ airway resistance) - Contraindicated in asthma/COPD

4. Metabolic (β2): - ↓ Glycogenolysis → masks hypoglycemia symptoms - ↓ Lipolysis - Caution in diabetes

5. Eye: - ↓ Intraocular pressure (↓ aqueous humor production)

6. CNS: - Crosses BBB (lipophilic) - Sedation, depression, nightmares

NEET PG: Non-selective β-blockers contraindicated in asthma

docs.title: Clinical Uses of Propranolol
docs.text:
1. Hypertension: - Effective, but NOT first-line alone - Useful in combination

2. Angina pectoris: - ↓ Myocardial O2 demand (↓ HR, ↓ contractility) - ↑ Diastolic filling time

3. Myocardial infarction: - ↓ Mortality (especially post-MI) - ↓ Arrhythmias, ↓ reinfarction

4. Arrhythmias: - Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) - Atrial fibrillation (rate control) - Ventricular arrhythmias

5. Heart failure (chronic, stable): - Paradoxically beneficial (long-term) - Carvedilol, metoprolol, bisoprolol preferred

6. Hyperthyroidism (thyroid storm): - Controls sympathetic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia, anxiety) - Does NOT affect thyroid hormone levels - Blocks T4 → T3 conversion (peripheral)

7. Migraine prophylaxis

8. Essential tremor

9. Performance anxiety

10. Portal hypertension: - ↓ Portal pressure - Prevents variceal bleeding

11. Glaucoma (timolol - topical)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Propranolol = Thyroid storm (symptomatic), migraine prophylaxis - β-blockers ↓ mortality in MI - Portal hypertension prophylaxis

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Cardiovascular: - Bradycardia, heart block - Worsening heart failure (acute use) - Hypotension

2. Respiratory: - Bronchospasm (β2 blockade)

3. Metabolic: - Masks hypoglycemia symptoms (tachycardia, tremor) - Doesn't mask sweating (cholinergic) - Hyperglycemia (↓ insulin release) - Dyslipidemia (↑ TG, ↓ HDL)

4. CNS: - Fatigue, depression - Nightmares, insomnia

5. Peripheral: - Cold extremities (vasoconstriction) - Raynaud's phenomenon

6. Sexual: - Erectile dysfunction

NEET PG High-Yield: - β-blockers mask hypoglycemia symptoms (except sweating) - Contraindicated in asthma, COPD

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Asthma, COPD (bronchospasm) - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree) - Severe bradycardia (<60 bpm) - Acute heart failure (decompensated) - Cardiogenic shock

Relative: - Diabetes (especially on insulin) - Peripheral vascular disease - Depression

NEET PG: β-blockers absolutely contraindicated in asthma

docs.title: β-Blocker Withdrawal
docs.text:
NEVER stop abruptly → Rebound phenomena: - ↑ Angina attacks - ↑ Arrhythmias - Hypertensive crisis - MI

Mechanism: Upregulation of β-receptors

NEET PG: β-blocker withdrawal → rebound angina, MI

docs.title: Other Non-Selective β-Blockers
docs.text:
NADOLOL: - Long-acting (once daily) - Doesn't cross BBB (hydrophilic) - Less CNS effects

TIMOLOL: - Used topically for glaucoma

NEET PG: Timolol = Topical for glaucoma

docs.title: SELECTIVE β1-BLOCKERS (Cardioselective)
docs.text:
ATENOLOL, METOPROLOL, BISOPROLOL, ESMOLOL

Advantages over non-selective: - Safer in asthma/COPD (relatively - still caution) - Safer in diabetes (less metabolic effects) - Safer in peripheral vascular disease

Note: Selectivity is dose-dependent (lost at high doses)

NEET PG: β1-selective safer (but not absolutely safe) in asthma

docs.title: ATENOLOL
docs.text:
Properties: - Selective β1 - Hydrophilic (doesn't cross BBB) - Long-acting (once daily) - Renal excretion (dose adjustment in renal failure)

Uses: - Hypertension - Angina - Post-MI

NEET PG: Atenolol = Hydrophilic, once daily, renal excretion

docs.title: METOPROLOL
docs.text:
Properties: - Selective β1 - Lipophilic (crosses BBB) - Hepatic metabolism

Uses: - Hypertension - Angina - Heart failure (chronic) - Post-MI

NEET PG: Metoprolol = One of 3 β-blockers proven in heart failure

docs.title: BISOPROLOL
docs.text:
Uses: - Heart failure (chronic) - proven benefit - Hypertension

NEET PG: Bisoprolol = Heart failure (along with carvedilol, metoprolol)

docs.title: ESMOLOL
docs.text:
Properties: - Very short-acting (t½ = 9 minutes) - Metabolized by RBC esterases - IV only

Uses: - Perioperative hypertension/tachycardia - Supraventricular tachycardia (acute) - Thyroid storm

NEET PG: Esmolol = Ultra-short acting, IV, perioperative use

docs.title: LABETALOL
docs.text:
Mechanism: α1 + β (non-selective) antagonist - Ratio: β:α = 3:1 (oral), 7:1 (IV)

Actions: - ↓ BP (α1 blockade - vasodilation) - ↓ Heart rate (β1 blockade) - No reflex tachycardia (α blockade prevents)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Hypertensive emergencies: - Pregnancy (pre-eclampsia, eclampsia) - safe in pregnancy - Pheochromocytoma crisis (after α-blockade)

2. Chronic hypertension in pregnancy

NEET PG High-Yield: - Labetalol = Hypertensive emergency in pregnancy (safe) - Pheochromocytoma (after phenoxybenzamine)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• Postural hypotension
  • Fatigue
  • Bronchospasm (less than pure β-blockers)
  • Hepatotoxicity

docs.title: CARVEDILOL
docs.text:
Mechanism: α1 + β (non-selective) antagonist + antioxidant

Actions: - Similar to labetalol - Additional antioxidant properties - ↓ Vascular remodeling

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Heart failure (chronic): - Proven mortality benefit - One of 3 β-blockers for heart failure

2. Hypertension

3. Post-MI

NEET PG: Carvedilol = Heart failure (proven benefit)

docs.title: Heart Failure
docs.text:
Proven beneficial (chronic, stable HF): - Carvedilol - Metoprolol (succinate) - Bisoprolol

Mechanism: ↓ Cardiac remodeling, ↓ apoptosis

NEET PG: Only 3 β-blockers proven in HF (Carvedilol, Metoprolol, Bisoprolol)

docs.title: Thyroid Storm
docs.text:
β-blockers (propranolol preferred): - Controls sympathetic symptoms - Blocks T4 → T3 conversion (peripheral) - Does NOT affect thyroid hormone production

NEET PG: Propranolol in thyroid storm (symptomatic + ↓ T4→T3)

docs.title: Migraine Prophylaxis
docs.text: Propranolol, metoprolol, timolol: - Mechanism unclear - Prevents attacks (not acute treatment)

docs.title: Glaucoma
docs.text:
Timolol (topical): - ↓ Aqueous humor production - ↓ IOP

Caution: Systemic absorption → bradycardia, bronchospasm

NEET PG: Timolol eye drops for glaucoma

docs.title: HIGH-YIELD COMPARISONS
docs.text: Drug Selectivity Key Use Special Feature Phentolamine α (non-selective) NE extravasation, pheochromocytoma Competitive, short-acting Phenoxybenzamine α (non-selective) Pheochromocytoma (preop) Irreversible, long-acting Prazosin α1 Hypertension, BPH First-dose hypotension Tamsulosin α1A BPH Uroselective Propranolol β (non-selective) Thyroid storm, portal HTN Crosses BBB, lipophilic Atenolol β1 Hypertension Hydrophilic, once daily, renal excretion Metoprolol β1 Heart failure Lipophilic Bisoprolol β1 Heart failure Proven benefit in HF Esmolol β1 Perioperative HTN/tachycardia Ultra-short acting (IV) Labetalol α1 + β Pregnancy HTN Safe in pregnancy Carvedilol α1 + β Heart failure Antioxidant properties

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Drug of choice for pheochromocytoma preoperative management? Answer: Phenoxybenzamine

  2. Antidote for norepinephrine extravasation? Answer: Phentolamine (local infiltration)

  3. α1-blocker causing first-dose hypotension? Answer: Prazosin

  4. Drug of choice for BPH with minimal BP effects? Answer: Tamsulosin

  5. β-blocker contraindicated in asthma? Answer: Propranolol (non-selective)

  6. β-blocker safest in asthma? Answer: β1-selective (atenolol, metoprolol) - still use caution

  7. Ultra-short acting β-blocker for perioperative use? Answer: Esmolol

  8. Drug of choice for hypertensive emergency in pregnancy? Answer: Labetalol

  9. Three β-blockers proven in heart failure? Answer: Carvedilol, Metoprolol (succinate), Bisoprolol

  10. Topical β-blocker for glaucoma? Answer: Timolol

docs.title: α1-Blockers for BPH (T-A-D)
docs.text:
• **T**amsulosin
  • **A**lfuzosin
  • **D**oxazosin (also terazosin, prazosin)

docs.title: β-Blockers Proven in Heart Failure (CMB)
docs.text:
• **C**arvedilol
  • **M**etoprolol (succinate)
  • **B**isoprolol

docs.title: Non-Selective β-Blocker Uses (MAPP-GET)
docs.text:
• **M**igraine prophylaxis
  • **A**ngina
  • **P**ortal hypertension
  • **P**ost-MI
  • **G**laucoma (timolol)
  • **E**ssential tremor
  • **T**hyroid storm

docs.title: β-Blocker Contraindications (ABC)
docs.text:
• **A**sthma/COPD
  • **B**radycardia/Heart block
  • **C**ardiogenic shock (acute HF)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know α-blocker classification (non-selective vs α1-selective)
  • Understand phentolamine vs phenoxybenzamine (pheochromocytoma)
  • Learn prazosin first-dose hypotension
  • Know β-blocker classification (non-selective vs β1-selective)
  • Understand propranolol actions & uses
  • Learn β-blocker contraindications (asthma, heart block)
  • Know 3 β-blockers for heart failure
  • Understand labetalol use in pregnancy
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on adrenergic antagonists

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: SKELETAL MUSCLE RELAXANTS - NEET PG Study Material
A. Neuromuscular Blockers (Peripherally Acting)
docs.text:
1. Non-depolarizing (Competitive):
  2. Long-acting: Tubocurarine, Pancuronium
  3. Intermediate-acting: Atracurium, Vecuronium, Rocuronium
  4. Short-acting: Mivacurium
  5. Depolarizing: Succinylcholine (Suxamethonium)

docs.title: B. Centrally Acting Muscle Relaxants
docs.text:
• Baclofen
  • Diazepam
  • Tizanidine
  • Dantrolene (acts on muscle directly)

docs.title: Normal Transmission
docs.text:
Motor nerve action potential
         ↓
   ACh release
         ↓
   ACh binds to nicotinic (NM) receptors
         ↓
   Na⁺ influx → Depolarization
         ↓
   Muscle contraction
         ↓
   ACh hydrolyzed by AChE

Key point: TWO α-subunits of nicotinic receptor must bind ACh for channel opening

NEET PG: Neuromuscular blockers act at nicotinic (NM) receptors

docs.title: TUBOCURARINE - Prototype
docs.text: Mechanism: Competitive antagonist at nicotinic (NM) receptors - Blocks ACh binding - Prevents depolarization - Reversible

docs.title: Pharmacological Actions
docs.text:
1. Neuromuscular Blockade (Sequential paralysis):

Order of paralysis: 

Small, rapidly moving muscles FIRST
         ↓
Eyes, face → Fingers → Limbs → Trunk → Intercostals → Diaphragm (LAST)

Recovery order: Reverse (diaphragm recovers first)

NEET PG High-Yield: Diaphragm paralyzed LAST, recovers FIRST

2. Autonomic Effects: - Ganglionic blockade (weak) - Histamine release → hypotension, bronchospasm - No effect on consciousness, pain sensation

3. No CNS effects (doesn't cross BBB - quaternary)

NEET PG: Patient awake & aware during paralysis (no anesthesia)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Adjunct to general anesthesia: - Muscle relaxation (surgery) - Facilitates intubation - ↓ Anesthetic dose requirement

2. Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT): - Prevents fractures during seizures

3. Controlled ventilation

NEET PG: Muscle relaxants used with anesthesia (patient still conscious)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• Prolonged apnea (respiratory paralysis)
  • Histamine release: Hypotension, bronchospasm, flushing
  • Ganglionic blockade

NEET PG: Tubocurarine releases histamine

docs.title: Reversal of Non-Depolarizing Block
docs.text:
Anticholinesterases (↑ ACh at NMJ): - Neostigmine (most common) - Pyridostigmine - Edrophonium

Always give with: - Atropine or Glycopyrrolate (prevent muscarinic effects)

NEET PG High-Yield: Neostigmine + Atropine to reverse non-depolarizing block

docs.title: PANCURONIUM
docs.text:
Properties: - Long-acting - No histamine release - Vagolytic (↑ HR) - releases NE

Uses: Long surgeries

NEET PG: Pancuronium causes tachycardia (vagolytic)

docs.title: VECURONIUM
docs.text:
Properties: - Intermediate-acting - No histamine release - No cardiovascular effects (most stable) - Hepatic elimination

Uses: Preferred for most surgeries

NEET PG: Vecuronium = Hemodynamically stable

docs.title: ATRACURIUM
docs.text:
Properties: - Intermediate-acting - Hofmann elimination (spontaneous degradation - non-enzymatic) - Ester hydrolysis - Safe in renal/hepatic failure - Releases histamine (mild)

Uses: - Renal/hepatic impairment - ICU sedation

NEET PG High-Yield: Atracurium = Safe in renal/hepatic failure (Hofmann elimination)

docs.title: ROCURONIUM
docs.text:
Properties: - Intermediate-acting - Rapid onset (similar to succinylcholine) - No histamine release

Uses: - Rapid sequence intubation (alternative to succinylcholine)

Reversal: - Sugammadex (specific reversal agent - encapsulates rocuronium)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Rocuronium = Rapid sequence intubation (non-depolarizing) - Sugammadex = Specific reversal for rocuronium

docs.title: MIVACURIUM
docs.text:
Properties: - Short-acting - Metabolized by plasma cholinesterase (like succinylcholine) - Prolonged in cholinesterase deficiency

NEET PG: Mivacurium metabolized by plasma cholinesterase

docs.title: SUCCINYLCHOLINE (Suxamethonium)
docs.text:
Structure: Two ACh molecules joined together

Mechanism: Depolarizing (NOT competitive) 1. Binds to nicotinic (NM) receptors 2. Causes persistent depolarization 3. Initial fasciculations (muscle twitching) 4. Desensitization → flaccid paralysis 5. Metabolized by plasma cholinesterase

NEET PG: Succinylcholine = Depolarizing, causes fasciculations

docs.title: Pharmacological Actions
docs.text:
Phase I Block (Depolarizing): - Initial fasciculations - NOT reversible by anticholinesterases - Enhanced by anticholinesterases

Phase II Block (Desensitization): - After prolonged use - Resembles non-depolarizing block - May be reversed by anticholinesterases

NEET PG: Phase I succinylcholine block NOT reversed by neostigmine

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Rapid sequence intubation: - Fastest onset (30-60 seconds) - Shortest duration (5-10 minutes) - Emergency situations

2. Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT)

3. Endoscopy (short procedures)

NEET PG High-Yield: Succinylcholine = DOC for rapid intubation (fastest onset)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Fasciculations: - Muscle pain (postoperative myalgia)

2. Hyperkalemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - K⁺ release from muscles - DANGEROUS in: - Burns (>24 hours old) - Denervation injuries (stroke, spinal cord injury) - Crush injuries - Prolonged immobilization - Neuromuscular diseases - Can cause cardiac arrest

3. Malignant hyperthermia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Hypermetabolic crisis - Triggers: Succinylcholine + Volatile anesthetics - Symptoms: ↑ Temperature, ↑ CO2, muscle rigidity, acidosis, hyperkalemia - Treatment: Dantrolene (IV)

4. Prolonged apnea: - Plasma cholinesterase deficiency (genetic) - Duration: Hours instead of minutes

5. Bradycardia: - Especially in children, after 2nd dose

6. ↑ Intraocular pressure: - Contraindicated in open eye injury

7. ↑ Intragastric pressure: - Risk of aspiration

8. ↑ Intracranial pressure

NEET PG High-Yield: - Succinylcholine contraindicated in burns, denervation (hyperkalemia) - Contraindicated in open eye injury (↑ IOP) - Malignant hyperthermia treated with Dantrolene

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Burns (>24 hours old) - Denervation (stroke, spinal cord injury) - Crush injuries - Neuromuscular diseases (muscular dystrophy, myasthenia gravis) - Open eye injury (↑ IOP) - Malignant hyperthermia (personal/family history) - Hyperkalemia

Relative: - Plasma cholinesterase deficiency

NEET PG: Know contraindications (burns, denervation, eye injury)

docs.title: BACLOFEN
docs.text:
Mechanism: GABA-B receptor agonist (spinal cord)

Actions: - ↓ Muscle spasticity - ↓ Polysynaptic reflexes

Uses: - Spasticity (multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy)

Adverse effects: - Sedation, drowsiness - Muscle weakness - Withdrawal seizures (if stopped abruptly)

NEET PG: Baclofen = GABA-B agonist for spasticity

docs.title: DIAZEPAM
docs.text:
Mechanism: GABA-A receptor potentiation

Uses: - Muscle spasm (acute) - Status epilepticus - Anxiety

NEET PG: Diazepam for acute muscle spasm

docs.title: TIZANIDINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Central α2 agonist (like clonidine)

Uses: - Spasticity (multiple sclerosis)

Adverse effects: - Sedation - Hypotension

docs.title: DANTROLENE - Direct-Acting
docs.text:
Mechanism: Acts directly on skeletal muscle - Blocks ryanodine receptor (Ca²⁺ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum) - ↓ Muscle contraction

Uses:

1. Malignant hyperthermia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ONLY effective treatment - IV administration - Life-saving

2. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)

3. Spasticity (chronic - oral)

Adverse effects: - Hepatotoxicity (chronic use) - Muscle weakness

NEET PG High-Yield: Dantrolene = ONLY treatment for malignant hyperthermia

docs.title: Factors Potentiating Non-Depolarizing Block
docs.text:
1. Volatile anesthetics (halothane, isoflurane)
  2. Aminoglycosides (gentamicin)
  3. Magnesium sulfate
  4. Hypothermia
  5. Acidosis
  6. Myasthenia gravis

NEET PG: Aminoglycosides potentiate muscle relaxants

docs.title: Factors Potentiating Succinylcholine
docs.text:
1. Anticholinesterases (neostigmine) - Phase I
  2. Organophosphates
  3. Plasma cholinesterase deficiency

docs.title: HIGH-YIELD COMPARISONS
docs.text: Feature Non-Depolarizing Depolarizing (Succinylcholine) Mechanism Competitive antagonist Persistent depolarization Fasciculations NO YES Initial effect Flaccid paralysis Fasciculations → paralysis Reversal Anticholinesterases (neostigmine) NOT reversible (Phase I) Onset Slower (except rocuronium) Fastest (30-60 sec) Duration Long to intermediate Shortest (5-10 min) Hyperkalemia NO YES (dangerous) Malignant hyperthermia NO YES Contraindications Myasthenia gravis Burns, denervation, eye injury Drug Type Duration Key Feature Tubocurarine Non-depolarizing Long Histamine release Pancuronium Non-depolarizing Long Tachycardia (vagolytic) Vecuronium Non-depolarizing Intermediate Hemodynamically stable Atracurium Non-depolarizing Intermediate Safe in renal/hepatic failure Rocuronium Non-depolarizing Intermediate Rapid onset, sugammadex reversal Succinylcholine Depolarizing Very short Fastest onset, hyperkalemia risk

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Drug of choice for rapid sequence intubation? Answer: Succinylcholine (fastest onset)

  2. How to reverse non-depolarizing neuromuscular block? Answer: Neostigmine + Atropine

  3. Specific reversal agent for rocuronium? Answer: Sugammadex

  4. Muscle relaxant safe in renal and hepatic failure? Answer: Atracurium (Hofmann elimination)

  5. Treatment for malignant hyperthermia? Answer: Dantrolene (IV)

  6. Contraindications for succinylcholine? Answer: Burns (>24 hr), denervation, open eye injury, hyperkalemia

  7. Which muscle paralyzed last, recovers first? Answer: Diaphragm

  8. Drug causing fasciculations before paralysis? Answer: Succinylcholine

  9. Muscle relaxant causing tachycardia? Answer: Pancuronium

  10. GABA-B agonist for spasticity? Answer: Baclofen

docs.title: Order of Paralysis
docs.text: "Small Fast muscles First, Big Slow muscles Last" - Eyes/Face → Fingers → Limbs → Trunk → Intercostals → Diaphragm

docs.title: Succinylcholine Contraindications (BODEN)
docs.text:
• **B**urns (>24 hours)
  • **O**pen eye injury
  • **D**enervation
  • **E**levated potassium
  • **N**euromuscular disease

docs.title: Malignant Hyperthermia Triggers (SS)
docs.text:
• **S**uccinylcholine
  • **S**evofluraneother volatile anesthetics)

docs.title: Non-Depolarizing Reversal (NAG)
docs.text:
• **N**eostigmine
  • **A**tropine (or)
  • **G**lycopyrrolate

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Understand neuromuscular junction physiology
  • Know non-depolarizing vs depolarizing differences
  • Learn succinylcholine contraindications
  • Understand malignant hyperthermia (triggers, treatment)
  • Know reversal agents (neostigmine, sugammadex)
  • Learn order of muscle paralysis
  • Understand atracurium (Hofmann elimination)
  • Know dantrolene mechanism & uses
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on muscle relaxants

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: LOCAL ANESTHETICS - NEET PG Study Material
DEFINITION
docs.text:
Local anesthetics: Drugs that reversibly block nerve conduction when applied locally, producing loss of sensation without loss of consciousness.

Site of action: Peripheral nerves (NOT CNS)

NEET PG: Local anesthetics block nerve conduction, patient remains conscious

docs.title: Nerve Conduction - Normal
docs.text:
Resting state: Inside negative (-70 mV)
         ↓
   Stimulus → Na+ channels open
         ↓
   Na+ influx → Depolarization
         ↓
   Action potential propagation

docs.title: Local Anesthetic Action
docs.text:
Mechanism: 1. Block voltage-gated Na⁺ channels (from inside of nerve membrane) 2. Prevent Na⁺ influx 3. Prevent depolarization → No action potential 4. Reversible blockade

Form: Must be in uncharged (base) form to cross nerve membrane - Inside nerve: Becomes charged (ionized) form → Active - pKa important (determines ionization)

NEET PG High-Yield: Local anesthetics block Na⁺ channels (reversible)

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Basic structure: 

Lipophilic     Intermediate     Hydrophilic
(Aromatic ring) — (Ester/Amide) — (Amine group)

docs.title: Classification by Linkage
docs.text:
A. Esters (COO): - Procaine - Cocaine - Tetracaine - Benzocaine - Chloroprocaine

B. Amides (NHCO): - Lidocaine (Lignocaine) - Bupivacaine - Ropivacaine - Prilocaine - Mepivacaine - Etidocaine

NEET PG High-Yield: Esters vs Amides (different metabolism, allergy)

docs.title: Absorption
docs.text:
Factors affecting absorption: 1. Vascularity of site (↑ vascularity → ↑ absorption → ↓ duration) 2. Vasoconstrictor (epinephrine) → ↓ absorption → ↑ duration 3. Dose & concentration

Order of vascularity (fastest to slowest absorption): 

IV > Tracheal > Intercostal > Caudal > Epidural > Brachial plexus > Subcutaneous

NEET PG: Adding epinephrine ↑ duration, ↓ systemic toxicity

docs.title: Metabolism
docs.text:
Esters: - Hydrolyzed by plasma cholinesterase (pseudocholinesterase) - Rapid metabolism - PABA (para-aminobenzoic acid) formed → Allergic reactions - Prolonged in cholinesterase deficiency

Amides: - Metabolized in liver (by microsomal enzymes) - Slower metabolism - Less allergic (no PABA) - Prolonged in liver disease

NEET PG High-Yield: - Esters → Plasma cholinesterase → PABA → Allergic - Amides → Liver → Less allergic

docs.title: Distribution
docs.text:
Factors: - Protein binding: ↑ Binding → ↑ duration (bupivacaine > lidocaine) - Lipid solubility: ↑ Lipid solubility → ↑ potency (bupivacaine > lidocaine)

NEET PG: Bupivacaine more potent & longer duration than lidocaine

docs.title: ORDER OF NERVE BLOCKADE
docs.text:
Small diameter, myelinated fibers blocked FIRST

Sequence: 

Autonomic (B fibers)
    ↓
Pain, temperature (C fibers, Aδ)
    ↓
Touch, pressure (Aβ)
    ↓
Motor (Aα) - LAST

Clinical: Sympathetic block > Sensory block > Motor block

NEET PG: Pain blocked before motor function (can move but no pain)

docs.title: LIDOCAINE (Lignocaine) - Prototype
docs.text:
Classification: Amide

Properties: - Most widely used - Moderate potency, duration - Rapid onset

Potency: Moderate (1x) Duration: 1-2 hours (without epinephrine), 2-4 hours (with epinephrine) Maximum dose: - Without epinephrine: 4-5 mg/kg (max 300 mg) - With epinephrine: 7 mg/kg (max 500 mg)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Local infiltration anesthesia

2. Nerve blocks (dental, peripheral)

3. Epidural/Spinal anesthesia

4. Topical (mucous membranes)

5. IV - Antiarrhythmic ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Class IB antiarrhythmic - Ventricular arrhythmias (VT, VF) - Digoxin-induced arrhythmias - Post-MI arrhythmias

NEET PG High-Yield: - Lidocaine max dose: 4-5 mg/kg (plain), 7 mg/kg (with epi) - IV lidocaine for ventricular arrhythmias

docs.title: BUPIVACAINE
docs.text:
Classification: Amide

Properties: - High potency (4x lidocaine) - Long duration (4-8 hours) - High protein binding (95%) - High lipid solubility - Slow onset

Maximum dose: 2-3 mg/kg (max 175 mg without epi, 225 mg with epi)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Epidural anesthesia (labor, surgery)

2. Spinal anesthesia

3. Peripheral nerve blocks

NEET PG: Bupivacaine = Long-acting, high potency

docs.title: Cardiotoxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Most cardiotoxic local anesthetic: - Binds to cardiac Na⁺ channels - Difficult to resuscitate if overdose - Arrhythmias, cardiac arrest

Treatment: - Lipid emulsion (20% intralipid) - specific antidote - CPR

NEET PG High-Yield: Bupivacaine cardiotoxicity treated with lipid emulsion

docs.title: ROPIVACAINE
docs.text:
Classification: Amide

Properties: - Similar to bupivacaine - Less cardiotoxic (safer) - Pure S-enantiomer

Uses: Epidural, nerve blocks (preferred over bupivacaine)

NEET PG: Ropivacaine less cardiotoxic than bupivacaine

docs.title: PROCAINE
docs.text:
Classification: Ester

Properties: - First synthetic local anesthetic - Low potency - Short duration - Rapid hydrolysis (plasma cholinesterase) - Allergenic (PABA formation)

Uses: - Spinal anesthesia (now rarely used) - Infiltration

NEET PG: Procaine = First synthetic LA, ester, allergenic

docs.title: COCAINE
docs.text:
Classification: Ester

Properties: - Natural (only naturally occurring LA) - Vasoconstrictor (blocks NE reuptake) - UNIQUE - No need for epinephrine - CNS stimulant (euphoria, addiction) - High abuse potential

Uses: - Topical (ENT procedures - nose, throat) - Vasoconstriction + anesthesia

Adverse effects: - Addiction - Myocardial infarction, stroke - Hypertension, arrhythmias

NEET PG High-Yield: - Cocaine = ONLY LA with vasoconstrictor property - Natural LA, high abuse potential

docs.title: TETRACAINE
docs.text:
Classification: Ester

Properties: - High potency (10x procaine) - Long duration - Slow onset

Uses: - Spinal anesthesia (preferred ester) - Topical (eye drops)

NEET PG: Tetracaine = Spinal anesthesia (ester)

docs.title: BENZOCAINE
docs.text:
Classification: Ester

Properties: - NOT ionizable (poor water solubility) - Topical use ONLY - Cannot be injected

Uses: - Topical (mucous membranes, skin) - Lozenges (sore throat)

Adverse effect: - Methemoglobinemia (especially in infants)

NEET PG: Benzocaine = Topical only, methemoglobinemia risk

docs.title: PRILOCAINE
docs.text:
Classification: Amide

Properties: - Similar to lidocaine - Least toxic systemically

Adverse effect: - Methemoglobinemia (metabolite: o-toluidine) - Treatment: Methylene blue

Uses: - EMLA cream (Eutectic Mixture of Local Anesthetics: Prilocaine + Lidocaine) - Topical anesthesia before IV cannulation

NEET PG High-Yield: - Prilocaine → Methemoglobinemia - EMLA cream = Prilocaine + Lidocaine

docs.title: EPINEPHRINE (Adrenaline)
docs.text:
Concentration: 1:200,000 (5 mcg/ml)

Actions: - Vasoconstriction (α1) - ↓ Systemic absorption - ↑ Duration of action - ↓ Systemic toxicity - ↓ Bleeding
