Contraindications: - Digits (fingers, toes) - Penis - Ear, nose tip - Risk of ischemic necrosis (end-arteries)

NEET PG: Don't use epinephrine in digits (risk of gangrene)

docs.title: SODIUM BICARBONATE
docs.text: Action: - ↑ pH → ↑ uncharged form - Faster onset

docs.title: HYALURONIDASE
docs.text:
Action: - Breaks down connective tissue - ↑ Diffusion - Larger area of anesthesia

Use: Ophthalmic blocks

docs.title: 1. Topical (Surface) Anesthesia
docs.text:
Drugs: Lidocaine, benzocaine, tetracaine, cocaine

Sites: Mucous membranes (eye, nose, throat, urethra)

Advantage: Painless application

docs.title: 2. Infiltration Anesthesia
docs.text:
Method: Direct injection into tissue

Uses: Minor surgery, suturing, dental

Drug: Lidocaine (most common)

docs.title: 3. Nerve Block (Regional)
docs.text:
Method: Injection near specific nerve/plexus

Types: - Peripheral nerve blocks: Brachial plexus, femoral, sciatic - Field block: Block multiple nerves around surgical site

Uses: Limb surgery, dental

docs.title: 4. Spinal (Subarachnoid) Anesthesia
docs.text:
Method: Injection into CSF (subarachnoid space) - Between L3-L4 or L4-L5

Drugs: Bupivacaine (heavy/hyperbaric), lidocaine, tetracaine

Spread: Depends on: - Baricity (specific gravity relative to CSF) - Hyperbaric (heavy): Spreads downward (gravity) - Isobaric: No preferential spread - Hypobaric (light): Spreads upward - Patient position - Dose, volume

Advantages: - Profound anesthesia - Muscle relaxation - Small dose required

Uses: Lower abdominal, pelvic, lower limb surgery

NEET PG: Spinal anesthesia = Subarachnoid, hyperbaric spreads downward

Complications:

1. Hypotension ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common - Sympathetic blockade → Vasodilation - Treatment: IV fluids, vasopressors (ephedrine, phenylephrine)

2. Post-dural puncture headache (PDPH): - CSF leak - Worse when upright, better lying down - Treatment: Bed rest, hydration, epidural blood patch

3. Total spinal (too high level): - Respiratory paralysis - Hypotension - Loss of consciousness - Treatment: Ventilation, vasopressors

4. Neurological (rare): - Cauda equina syndrome - Arachnoiditis

NEET PG High-Yield: - Spinal anesthesia most common complication = Hypotension - PDPH = CSF leak, worse upright, epidural blood patch

docs.title: 5. Epidural Anesthesia
docs.text:
Method: Injection into epidural space (outside dura) - Usually lumbar region

Drugs: Bupivacaine, ropivacaine, lidocaine (larger doses than spinal)

Advantages: - Catheter can be placed → continuous infusion - Segmental block possible - Less hypotension than spinal

Uses: - Labor analgesia (most common) - Thoracic, abdominal, pelvic surgery - Chronic pain management

NEET PG: Epidural = Outside dura, catheter possible, less hypotension

Complications: - Hypotension (less than spinal) - Dural puncture (wet tap) → PDPH - Intravascular injection → Systemic toxicity - Total spinal (if dural puncture unrecognized)

docs.title: Spinal vs Epidural
docs.text:
Feature Spinal Epidural Site Subarachnoid (in CSF) Epidural space (outside dura) Dose Small Large Onset Rapid (2-5 min) Slower (15-20 min) Duration Fixed Variable (catheter) Hypotension More common Less common Headache More common Less (unless dural puncture) Motor block Complete Variable (can be segmental)

NEET PG: Spinal faster onset, smaller dose; Epidural catheter possible

docs.title: 6. IV Regional Anesthesia (Bier's Block)
docs.text:
Method: 1. Exsanguinate limb (Esmarch bandage) 2. Inflate tourniquet (above systolic BP) 3. Inject LA IV (distal to tourniquet) 4. LA diffuses into tissues

Drug: Lidocaine (prilocaine safer)

Uses: Upper limb surgery (short procedures)

Complications: - Tourniquet pain (limits duration to ~1 hour) - Systemic toxicity (if tourniquet deflated too early)

NEET PG: Bier's block = IV regional, tourniquet essential

docs.title: CNS Toxicity (Occurs FIRST)
docs.text:
Low blood levels: - Circumoral numbness, tingling - Lightheadedness, tinnitus - Visual disturbances

Higher levels: - Seizures (cortical excitation) - CNS depression (coma, respiratory arrest)

NEET PG: CNS toxicity occurs before cardiac toxicity

docs.title: Cardiovascular Toxicity
docs.text:
Higher blood levels: - Myocardial depression - Arrhythmias (VT, VF) - Hypotension - Cardiac arrest

NEET PG: Bupivacaine most cardiotoxic

docs.title: Treatment of Systemic Toxicity
docs.text:
1. Stop injection

2. Airway management: - 100% O₂ - Ventilation

3. Seizures: - Benzodiazepines (diazepam, midazolam) - NOT thiopental (↑ cardiotoxicity)

4. Cardiovascular support: - IV fluids - Vasopressors

5. Specific antidote ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - 20% Lipid emulsion (Intralipid) - Especially for bupivacaine toxicity

6. CPR (prolonged may be needed)

NEET PG High-Yield: Lipid emulsion for local anesthetic toxicity (especially bupivacaine)

docs.title: ALLERGIC REACTIONS
docs.text:
Esters > Amides (PABA formation)

Treatment: - Antihistamines - Corticosteroids - Epinephrine (if anaphylaxis)

NEET PG: Esters more allergenic than amides

docs.title: HIGH-YIELD DRUG COMPARISON
docs.text: Drug Class Potency Duration Key Feature Lidocaine Amide 1x 1-2 hr Most widely used, IV antiarrhythmic Bupivacaine Amide 4x 4-8 hr Long-acting, cardiotoxic Ropivacaine Amide 4x 4-8 hr Less cardiotoxic than bupivacaine Procaine Ester 0.25x 0.5-1 hr First synthetic, allergenic Cocaine Ester 1x 0.5-1 hr Natural, vasoconstrictor, abuse Tetracaine Ester 10x 3-4 hr Spinal anesthesia Benzocaine Ester - - Topical only, methemoglobinemia Prilocaine Amide 1x 1-2 hr Methemoglobinemia, EMLA cream

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Mechanism of action of local anesthetics? Answer: Block voltage-gated Na⁺ channels

  2. Why add epinephrine to local anesthetics? Answer: Prolong duration, reduce systemic toxicity

  3. Where is epinephrine contraindicated? Answer: Digits (fingers, toes, penis, ear, nose)

  4. Max dose of lidocaine with epinephrine? Answer: 7 mg/kg (max 500 mg)

  5. Most cardiotoxic local anesthetic? Answer: Bupivacaine

  6. Antidote for bupivacaine toxicity? Answer: 20% Lipid emulsion

  7. Local anesthetic with intrinsic vasoconstrictor property? Answer: Cocaine

  8. Drug causing methemoglobinemia? Answer: Prilocaine, Benzocaine

  9. Treatment for methemoglobinemia? Answer: Methylene blue

  10. Most common complication of spinal anesthesia? Answer: Hypotension

  11. Treatment for post-dural puncture headache? Answer: Epidural blood patch

  12. First sign of local anesthetic systemic toxicity? Answer: CNS symptoms (circumoral numbness, tinnitus, seizures)

docs.title: Amide Local Anesthetics (Two "I"s)
docs.text:
• L**i**doca**i**ne
  • Bup**i**vaca**i**ne
  • Pr**i**loca**i**ne
  • Rop**i**vaca**i**ne
  • Mep**i**vaca**i**ne

docs.title: Epinephrine Contraindications (PPEN)
docs.text:
• **P**enis
  • **P**halanges (fingers, toes)
  • **E**ar
  • **N**ose

docs.title: Order of Nerve Block (ASMT)
docs.text:
• **A**utonomic
  • **S**ensory (pain, temperature)
  • **M**otor
  • **T**ouch (last)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Understand mechanism (Na⁺ channel blockade)
  • Know ester vs amide differences (metabolism, allergy)
  • Learn lidocaine max doses (with/without epinephrine)
  • Understand bupivacaine cardiotoxicity & treatment
  • Know cocaine unique properties (vasoconstrictor)
  • Learn spinal vs epidural anesthesia differences
  • Understand complications of spinal (hypotension, PDPH)
  • Know systemic toxicity treatment (lipid emulsion)
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on local anesthetics

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (4-6 questions)

docs.title: GENERAL ANESTHETICS - NEET PG Study Material
DEFINITION
docs.text:
General anesthesia: Reversible state of CNS depression producing: 1. Loss of consciousness 2. Analgesia (pain relief) 3. Amnesia (memory loss) 4. Muscle relaxation 5. Loss of reflexes

NEET PG: General anesthesia = Reversible CNS depression with loss of consciousness

docs.title: Stage I: Analgesia (Induction)
docs.text:
Characteristics: - Conscious but drowsy - Analgesia present - Amnesia may occur

Clinical: From start of anesthetic to loss of consciousness

docs.title: Stage II: Excitement (Delirium)
docs.text:
Characteristics: - Loss of consciousness - Irregular breathing - Increased muscle tone - Exaggerated reflexes - Vomiting, laryngospasm may occur - Dangerous stage

Goal: Pass through quickly (use IV induction agents)

NEET PG: Stage II = Excitement, dangerous (irregular breathing, laryngospasm)

docs.title: Stage III: Surgical Anesthesia
docs.text:
Divided into 4 planes:

Plane 1: - Regular respiration - Roving eyeballs - Surgery can begin

Plane 2: - Eyes central & fixed - Corneal & laryngeal reflexes lost

Plane 3: - Intercostal paralysis (diaphragmatic breathing only) - Pupil dilation begins

Plane 4: - Diaphragmatic paralysis imminent - Dangerous - near overdose

NEET PG: Stage III = Surgical anesthesia (planes 1-4)

docs.title: Stage IV: Medullary Paralysis (Overdose)
docs.text:
Characteristics: - Respiratory arrest - Cardiovascular collapse - Dilated pupils - Death if not treated

Treatment: Stop anesthetic, ventilate, circulatory support

NEET PG: Stage IV = Overdose, respiratory/cardiovascular collapse

docs.title: A. Inhalational Anesthetics
docs.text:
1. Gases: - Nitrous oxide (N₂O)

2. Volatile liquids: - Halogenated hydrocarbons: - Halothane - Isoflurane - Sevoflurane - Desflurane - Enflurane (obsolete) - Ether (obsolete)

docs.title: B. Intravenous Anesthetics
docs.text:
1. Barbiturates: - Thiopental sodium

2. Non-barbiturates: - Propofol - Etomidate - Ketamine - Benzodiazepines (midazolam)

docs.title: Minimum Alveolar Concentration (MAC)
docs.text:
Definition: Alveolar concentration at which 50% of patients do not move in response to surgical incision

Key points: - Measure of potency (↓ MAC = ↑ potency) - Additive (0.5 MAC + 0.5 MAC = 1 MAC) - Inverse of potency

Factors INCREASING MAC (resistant): - Hyperthermia - Hypernatremia - Chronic alcoholism - Drugs: Amphetamine, ephedrine, cocaine

Factors DECREASING MAC (sensitive): - Hypothermia - Hypoxia, severe anemia - Pregnancy - Elderly - Drugs: Opioids, α2-agonists, acute alcohol

NEET PG High-Yield: MAC = Measure of potency, 50% no response to incision

docs.title: Blood:Gas Partition Coefficient
docs.text:
Definition: Solubility of anesthetic in blood

Significance: - ↓ Solubility → ↑ Rate of induction & recovery (faster) - ↑ Solubility → ↓ Rate (slower)

Order (fastest to slowest): 

Desflurane (0.42) > Sevoflurane (0.65) > Isoflurane (1.4) > Halothane (2.3) > Ether (12)

NEET PG: Low blood:gas coefficient = Faster induction (desflurane fastest)

docs.title: Concentration Effect & Second Gas Effect
docs.text:
Concentration effect: ↑ Inspired concentration → ↑ Rate of rise of alveolar concentration - Used with N₂O (delivered at high %)

Second gas effect: N₂O accelerates uptake of other volatile anesthetics

NEET PG: N₂O shows concentration & second gas effects

docs.title: NITROUS OXIDE (N₂O) - "Laughing Gas"
docs.text:
Properties: - Gas (not volatile liquid) - Low potency (MAC = 105% - cannot be used alone) - Very low blood:gas (0.47) → Rapid induction & recovery - Non-flammable (supports combustion) - Sweet odor

Mechanism: NMDA receptor antagonist (like ketamine)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Analgesia: - Dental procedures - Labor analgesia - Minor procedures

2. General anesthesia (in combination): - With volatile anesthetics (↓ their requirement) - With IV agents

Concentration: 50-70% (with 30-50% O₂)

NEET PG: N₂O cannot be used alone (MAC >100%)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Diffusion hypoxia: - At end of anesthesia - N₂O diffuses out rapidly → Dilutes alveolar O₂ - Prevention: Give 100% O₂ for 5-10 min after stopping N₂O

2. Expansion of closed gas spaces ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - N₂O more soluble than N₂ - Diffuses into closed spaces faster than N₂ comes out - Contraindicated in: - Pneumothorax - Bowel obstruction - Middle ear surgery - Intracranial air (pneumocephalus) - Eye surgery with intraocular gas

3. Bone marrow depression: - Inactivates vitamin B12 - Megaloblastic anemia (prolonged use)

4. Teratogenic (avoid in early pregnancy)

NEET PG High-Yield: - N₂O contraindicated in pneumothorax, bowel obstruction (expands gas spaces) - Diffusion hypoxia prevented by 100% O₂ after stopping N₂O

docs.title: HALOTHANE
docs.text:
Properties: - Non-pungent, pleasant odor - Potent (MAC = 0.75%) - Bronchodilator - Moderate blood:gas (2.3)

Advantages: - Smooth induction (non-irritant) - Bronchodilation (useful in asthma) - Non-flammable

docs.title: Cardiovascular Effects
docs.text:
• Myocardial depression (↓ contractility)
  • Hypotension
  • Bradycardia (vagal stimulation)
  • Sensitizes heart to catecholamines → arrhythmias
  • ↓ Baroreceptor reflex

NEET PG: Halothane sensitizes heart to catecholamines (arrhythmias)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Hepatotoxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - "Halothane hepatitis" - Two types: - Type 1: Mild, transient ↑ transaminases (25%) - Type 2: Severe, fulminant hepatic necrosis (1 in 10,000) - Immune-mediated (antibodies to trifluoroacetylated proteins) - Higher risk: Repeated exposure, obesity, middle-aged women - Most serious complication

2. Malignant hyperthermia: - Genetic (ryanodine receptor mutation) - With succinylcholine - Treatment: Dantrolene

3. Uterine relaxation (↑ postpartum bleeding)

4. ↑ Intracranial pressure (cerebral vasodilation)

5. No analgesia

NEET PG High-Yield: - Halothane hepatitis = Most serious complication - Halothane + catecholamines → arrhythmias

docs.title: ISOFLURANE
docs.text:
Properties: - Pungent (irritant to airways) - Potent (MAC = 1.2%) - Low blood:gas (1.4) → faster than halothane

Advantages: - Minimal metabolism (<0.2%) → No hepatotoxicity - Does NOT sensitize heart to catecholamines - Cardiovascular stable (maintains cardiac output) - Muscle relaxation (good)

Disadvantage: - Pungent → not for inhalational induction

NEET PG: Isoflurane = No hepatotoxicity, no arrhythmias

docs.title: SEVOFLURANE
docs.text:
Properties: - Non-pungent, pleasant odor - Potent (MAC = 2%) - Low blood:gas (0.65) → Rapid induction & recovery

Advantages: - Ideal for inhalational induction (especially children) - Non-irritant - Cardiovascular stable - Minimal metabolism

Disadvantage: - Compound A (with CO₂ absorbers - soda lime) - Potentially nephrotoxic - Low fresh gas flows → ↑ Compound A

NEET PG High-Yield: Sevoflurane = Best for inhalational induction (non-pungent, rapid)

docs.title: DESFLURANE
docs.text:
Properties: - Lowest blood:gas (0.42) → Fastest induction & recovery - Pungent - MAC = 6%

Advantages: - Fastest emergence (rapid recovery) - Minimal metabolism

Disadvantages: - Pungent (airway irritation, cough, laryngospasm) - Requires special vaporizer (low boiling point)

NEET PG: Desflurane = Fastest recovery

docs.title: THIOPENTAL SODIUM
docs.text:
Classification: Ultra-short acting barbiturate

Mechanism: GABA-A receptor potentiation

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text:
Onset: Rapid (10-20 seconds - "one arm-brain circulation time")

Duration: Short (5-10 minutes) - NOT due to metabolism - Due to redistribution (highly lipid soluble) - Brain → Muscle → Fat - Recovery when brain levels ↓

Metabolism: Slow (hepatic) - hours - Hangover effect (residual sedation)

NEET PG: Thiopental short duration due to redistribution (NOT metabolism)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Induction of general anesthesia (rapid, smooth)

2. Status epilepticus (refractory)

3. ↓ Intracranial pressure (cerebral vasoconstriction)

NEET PG: Thiopental for rapid induction, status epilepticus

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Respiratory depression (dose-dependent)

2. Myocardial depression: - ↓ Contractility - Hypotension - Avoid in shock, cardiovascular instability

3. Laryngospasm, bronchospasm: - Contraindicated in asthma

4. Venous thrombosis, arterial injection: - Intra-arterial injection → Severe pain, gangrene - Treatment: Leave needle in, inject vasodilators (papaverine, tolazoline)

5. Porphyria (absolute contraindication)

6. No analgesia

NEET PG High-Yield: - Thiopental contraindicated in asthma, porphyria, shock - Intra-arterial injection → gangrene

docs.title: PROPOFOL
docs.text:
Mechanism: GABA-A receptor potentiation

Formulation: Lipid emulsion (soybean oil, egg lecithin)

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text:
Onset: Rapid (30-45 seconds)

Duration: Short (3-10 minutes) - redistribution

Metabolism: Hepatic + extrahepatic (faster than thiopental)

Recovery: Smooth, rapid (no hangover)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Induction of anesthesia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most commonly used IV induction agent - Smooth induction & recovery

2. Maintenance (continuous infusion - TIVA: Total IV anesthesia)

3. Sedation (ICU, procedures)

4. Antiemetic (↓ PONV - postoperative nausea/vomiting)

NEET PG: Propofol = Most common IV induction agent

docs.title: Advantages over Thiopental
docs.text:
• Faster recovery, no hangover
  • Antiemetic property
  • Less cardiovascular depression (safer)
  • Can be used for maintenance (infusion)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Pain on injection: - Most common complaint - Prevention: Lidocaine IV before/with propofol

2. Hypotension: - Vasodilation - Less than thiopental

3. Respiratory depression

4. **Propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS)** ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Prolonged high-dose infusion - Metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, cardiac failure - Can be fatal

5. Contraindications: - Egg/soy allergy (formulation)

6. No analgesia

7. **Supports bacterial growth (lipid emulsion) - Use strict aseptic technique - Discard after 6 hours

NEET PG High-Yield: - Propofol pain on injection (give with lidocaine) - Propofol infusion syndrome (prolonged high-dose) - Contraindicated in egg/soy allergy

docs.title: ETOMIDATE
docs.text: Mechanism: GABA-A receptor potentiation

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
Cardiovascular stability ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Minimal effect on BP, HR - Drug of choice in: - Shock - Cardiovascular disease - Hypovolemia

Cerebral protective: ↓ ICP, ↓ CMR (cerebral metabolic rate)

NEET PG: Etomidate = Most cardiovascularly stable (DOC in shock)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Adrenal suppression: - Inhibits 11β-hydroxylase - ↓ Cortisol synthesis - Even single dose - Avoid prolonged infusion

2. Myoclonus (involuntary movements)

3. Pain on injection

4. PONV (high incidence)

5. No analgesia

NEET PG High-Yield: Etomidate causes adrenal suppression

docs.title: KETAMINE - "Dissociative Anesthetic"
docs.text:
Mechanism: NMDA receptor antagonist

Unique properties: - Analgesia (strong) - Dissociative state (eyes open, but unresponsive) - Maintains airway reflexes - Bronchodilation

docs.title: Cardiovascular Effects
docs.text:
Sympathomimetic: - ↑ HR, ↑ BP, ↑ Cardiac output - Useful in shock, asthma

Mechanism: ↑ Catecholamine release, blocks reuptake

NEET PG: Ketamine ↑ BP (vs other anesthetics ↓ BP)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Induction (especially in): - Shock, hypovolemia - Asthma (bronchodilator) - Pediatric (IM route possible) - Mass casualties, disasters

2. Analgesia (chronic pain, procedural)

3. Sedation (ICU)

NEET PG: Ketamine = DOC in shock with asthma

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Emergence reactions ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Hallucinations, nightmares, delirium - During recovery - Prevention: Benzodiazepines (midazolam)

2. ↑ Intracranial pressure: - ↑ Cerebral blood flow - Contraindicated in head injury

3. ↑ Intraocular pressure: - Contraindicated in glaucoma

4. Salivation (↑ secretions)

5. Tachycardia, hypertension

NEET PG High-Yield: - Ketamine emergence reactions (give benzodiazepines) - Contraindicated in head injury, glaucoma (↑ ICP, ↑ IOP)

docs.title: BALANCED ANESTHESIA
docs.text:
Definition: Combination of multiple agents to achieve: - Unconsciousness - Analgesia - Muscle relaxation - Amnesia

Typical regimen: 1. Premedication: Benzodiazepine (anxiolysis, amnesia) 2. Induction: Propofol or thiopental (IV) 3. Muscle relaxation: Succinylcholine (intubation) → Vecuronium (maintenance) 4. Maintenance: Volatile anesthetic (sevoflurane/isoflurane) + N₂O + O₂ 5. Analgesia: Opioid (fentanyl)

Advantages: - ↓ Dose of each drug → ↓ toxicity - Better control - Faster recovery

NEET PG: Balanced anesthesia = Combination approach

docs.title: PREMEDICATION
docs.text:
Goals: 1. Anxiolysis 2. Amnesia 3. Analgesia 4. ↓ Secretions (antisialagogue) 5. ↓ Vagal reflexes 6. ↓ Gastric acidity 7. Antiemetic

Drugs:

1. Benzodiazepines (most common): - Midazolam, diazepam - Anxiolysis, amnesia, sedation

2. Anticholinergics: - Atropine, glycopyrrolate - ↓ Secretions, prevent bradycardia

3. Opioids: - Morphine, fentanyl - Analgesia, sedation

4. H2-blockers / PPIs: - Ranitidine, omeprazole - ↓ Gastric acid (aspiration risk)

5. Antiemetics: - Metoclopramide, ondansetron

NEET PG: Midazolam most common premedication (anxiolysis, amnesia)

docs.title: Inhalational Anesthetics
docs.text: Drug MAC Blood:Gas Key Feature N₂O 105% 0.47 Cannot use alone, expands gas spaces Halothane 0.75% 2.3 Hepatotoxicity, arrhythmias Isoflurane 1.2% 1.4 No hepatotoxicity, no arrhythmias Sevoflurane 2% 0.65 Best for induction (non-pungent) Desflurane 6% 0.42 Fastest recovery

docs.title: IV Anesthetics
docs.text: Drug Onset Key Use Major Adverse Effect Thiopental Fastest Rapid induction, status epilepticus Contraindicated in asthma, porphyria Propofol Fast Most common induction agent Pain on injection, PRIS Etomidate Fast Shock, CV disease Adrenal suppression Ketamine Fast Shock + asthma, pediatric Emergence reactions, ↑ ICP

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. What does MAC represent? Answer: Alveolar concentration at which 50% don't respond to incision

  2. Fastest induction & recovery inhalational agent? Answer: Desflurane (lowest blood:gas)

  3. Most serious complication of halothane? Answer: Hepatotoxicity (fulminant hepatic necrosis)

  4. Best agent for inhalational induction in children? Answer: Sevoflurane (non-pungent, rapid)

  5. Why is N₂O contraindicated in pneumothorax? Answer: Expands closed gas spaces

  6. How to prevent diffusion hypoxia? Answer: Give 100% O₂ for 5-10 min after stopping N₂O

  7. Most common IV induction agent? Answer: Propofol

  8. IV anesthetic of choice in shock? Answer: Etomidate (cardiovascular stability)

  9. Treatment for ketamine emergence reactions? Answer: Benzodiazepines (midazolam)

  10. Why is thiopental short-acting? Answer: Redistribution (NOT metabolism)

docs.title: Stages of Anesthesia (AESD)
docs.text:
• **A**nalgesia (Stage I)
  • **E**xcitement (Stage II)
  • **S**urgical (Stage III)
  • **D**eath/overdose (Stage IV)

docs.title: Thiopental Contraindications (PAS)
docs.text:
• **P**orphyria
  • **A**sthma
  • **S**hock

docs.title: Ketamine Effects (↑ Everything)
docs.text: • ↑ BP, ↑ HR, ↑ ICP, ↑ IOP, ↑ Secretions

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know stages of anesthesia (Guedel's)
  • Understand MAC concept
  • Learn blood:gas coefficients (fastest = desflurane)
  • Know N₂O contraindications (gas expansion)
  • Understand halothane hepatotoxicity
  • Learn IV anesthetic comparisons
  • Know etomidate use in shock
  • Understand ketamine unique properties
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on general anesthetics

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: ANTIHYPERTENSIVE DRUGS (Detailed) - NEET PG Study Material
Factors Determining BP
docs.text:
BP = Cardiac Output (CO) × Total Peripheral Resistance (TPR)

Cardiac Output = Heart rate × Stroke volume TPR = Vascular resistance (arteriolar tone)

docs.title: Regulatory Systems
docs.text:
1. Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS): 

Renin (kidney)
    ↓
Angiotensinogen → Angiotensin I
    ↓ (ACE)
Angiotensin II
    ↓
• Vasoconstriction (↑ TPR)
• Aldosterone release (↑ Na+/H2O retention → ↑ CO)
• ADH release

2. Sympathetic Nervous System: - ↑ HR, ↑ contractility, vasoconstriction

3. Baroreceptor Reflex

4. Kidney (volume regulation)

NEET PG: Angiotensin II = Vasoconstriction + aldosterone release

docs.title: A. Diuretics
docs.text: • Thiazides, Loop, K+-sparing

docs.title: B. Sympatholytics
docs.text:
1. Centrally acting: Clonidine, Methyldopa, Moxonidine
  2. Peripherally acting:
  3. α-blockers: Prazosin, Doxazosin
  4. β-blockers: Atenolol, Metoprolol
  5. α + β blockers: Labetalol, Carvedilol
  6. Adrenergic neuron blockers: Reserpine, Guanethidine

docs.title: C. Vasodilators
docs.text:
1. Arterial: Hydralazine, Minoxidil, Diazoxide
  2. Arterial + venous: Sodium nitroprusside

docs.title: D. Calcium Channel Blockers (CCBs)
docs.text: • Amlodipine, Nifedipine, Verapamil, Diltiazem

docs.title: E. RAAS Inhibitors
docs.text:
1. ACE inhibitors: Enalapril, Lisinopril, Ramipril
  2. ARBs: Losartan, Telmisartan, Valsartan
  3. Direct renin inhibitor: Aliskiren

docs.title: F. Others
docs.text: • Bosentan (endothelin receptor antagonist)

docs.title: THIAZIDES - First-Line
docs.text:
Examples: Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ), Chlorthalidone, Indapamide

Mechanism: - Inhibit Na+-Cl- cotransporter in distal convoluted tubule - Natriuresis → ↓ Blood volume → ↓ CO - Chronic: Vasodilation (↓ TPR) - main mechanism

Advantages: - Inexpensive - Once daily - Reduces cardiovascular events (proven) - Effective in elderly, blacks

Adverse effects: - Hypokalemia (most common) - Hyperuricemia (gout) - Hyperglycemia (↓ insulin secretion) - Hyperlipidemia (↑ LDL, ↑ TG) - Hypercalcemia (↑ Ca²⁺ reabsorption) - Hyponatremia - Erectile dysfunction

NEET PG High-Yield: - Thiazides first-line for essential hypertension - Cause hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia - Cause hypercalcemia (vs loop → hypocalcemia)

docs.title: Thiazide Combinations
docs.text:
Thiazide + K+-sparing diuretic: - Hydrochlorothiazide + Amiloride or + Spironolactone - Prevents hypokalemia

Thiazide + ACE-I/ARB: - Synergistic - ACE-I prevents hypokalemia

NEET PG: Thiazide + K+-sparing prevents hypokalemia

docs.title: METHYLDOPA
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Prodrug → Converted to α-methyl norepinephrine in CNS - Central α2 agonist (like clonidine) - ↓ Sympathetic outflow → ↓ BP

Uses: - Hypertension in pregnancy ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Safe in pregnancy - Drug of choice for chronic HTN in pregnancy - Chronic hypertension

Adverse effects: - Sedation, drowsiness - Dry mouth - Positive Coombs test (10-20%) - usually no hemolysis - Hemolytic anemia (rare) - Hepatotoxicity - Drug-induced lupus - Sexual dysfunction - Rebound hypertension (if stopped abruptly)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Methyldopa = DOC for chronic hypertension in pregnancy - Positive Coombs test (but hemolysis rare)

docs.title: CLONIDINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Central α2 agonist

Uses: - Hypertension - Opioid withdrawal - ADHD (off-label)

Adverse effects: - Sedation, dry mouth - Severe rebound hypertension (if stopped abruptly) - Bradycardia

NEET PG: Clonidine rebound hypertension (taper gradually)

docs.title: α1-Blockers
docs.text:
Examples: Prazosin, Doxazosin, Terazosin

Mechanism: Selective α1 antagonist → Vasodilation

Uses: - Hypertension (NOT first-line) - BPH (benign prostatic hyperplasia)

Adverse effects: - First-dose hypotension (severe) - Orthostatic hypotension - Reflex tachycardia (minimal)

NEET PG: α1-blockers useful for hypertension + BPH

docs.title: β-Blockers
docs.text:
Mechanism: - ↓ Cardiac output (β1 - heart) - ↓ Renin release (β1 - kidney)

First-line in: - Hypertension + IHD - Hypertension + Heart failure - Hypertension + Post-MI - Hypertension + Tachyarrhythmias

Preferred agents: - Cardioselective (β1): Atenolol, Metoprolol, Bisoprolol - α + β: Carvedilol, Labetalol

Adverse effects: - Bradycardia, heart block - Bronchospasm (avoid in asthma) - Fatigue, depression - Masks hypoglycemia - Dyslipidemia (↑ TG, ↓ HDL)

Contraindications: - Asthma, COPD - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree) - Acute heart failure

NEET PG: β-blockers first-line if IHD, heart failure, post-MI

docs.title: HYDRALAZINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Direct arterial vasodilator (exact mechanism unclear) - ↑ cGMP - ↓ TPR

Hemodynamic effects: - ↓ BP (↓ TPR) - Reflex tachycardia (↑ HR, ↑ CO) - Fluid retention

Uses: - Hypertensive emergency in pregnancy (eclampsia) - IV/IM - Safe in pregnancy - Heart failure (with nitrates - afterload reduction) - Severe hypertension (with β-blocker + diuretic)

Adverse effects: - Reflex tachycardia (prevent with β-blocker) - Fluid retention (prevent with diuretic) - Headache, flushing - Drug-induced lupus ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Dose >200 mg/day - Slow acetylators at higher risk - Anti-histone antibodies (+) - Reversible (stop drug)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Hydralazine for eclampsia (IV), safe in pregnancy - Causes drug-induced lupus (slow acetylators) - Always give with β-blocker + diuretic

docs.title: MINOXIDIL
docs.text:
Mechanism: K+ channel opener → Hyperpolarization → Vasodilation

Most potent oral vasodilator

Uses: - Refractory hypertension (resistant to other drugs) - Always with β-blocker + diuretic

Adverse effects: - Severe reflex tachycardia - Severe fluid retention - Hirsutism (hair growth) - common - Topical minoxidil for androgenic alopecia - Pericardial effusion

NEET PG High-Yield: - Minoxidil = Most potent oral vasodilator, for refractory HTN - Causes hirsutism (used topically for hair growth)

docs.title: SODIUM NITROPRUSSIDE (SNP)
docs.text:
Mechanism: NO donor → ↑ cGMP → Vasodilation (arterial + venous)

Route: IV only (very short-acting)

Uses: - Hypertensive emergency ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Fastest acting - Immediate onset - Drug of choice for most hypertensive emergencies - Acute heart failure

Monitoring: Intra-arterial BP monitoring required

Adverse effects: - Cyanide toxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - SNP → Cyanide → Thiocyanate (liver) - Prolonged infusion / renal failure → accumulation - Symptoms: Metabolic acidosis, tinnitus, confusion - Treatment: Sodium thiosulfate, hydroxocobalamin - Thiocyanate toxicity: - Prolonged use (>72 hours) - Tinnitus, blurred vision, confusion - Hypotension (excessive) - Increased ICP (cerebral vasodilation)

Contraindications: - Increased intracranial pressure - Severe renal/hepatic impairment - Leber's optic atrophy

NEET PG High-Yield: - Sodium nitroprusside = DOC for hypertensive emergency - Cyanide toxicity (treat with sodium thiosulfate) - Contraindicated if ↑ ICP

docs.title: DIAZOXIDE
docs.text:
Mechanism: K+ channel opener → Vasodilation

Route: IV bolus (not infusion)

Uses: - Hypertensive emergency (now rarely used - replaced by SNP) - Insulinoma (inhibits insulin release)

Adverse effects: - Hyperglycemia (inhibits insulin) - Fluid retention - Hirsutism

NEET PG: Diazoxide causes hyperglycemia (vs thiazides)

docs.title: Classification
docs.text:
1. Dihydropyridines (DHP): - Amlodipine, Nifedipine, Felodipine, Nicardipine - Vascular selective (peripheral vasodilation)

2. Non-dihydropyridines: - Verapamil (cardiac selective) - Diltiazem (cardiac + vascular)

NEET PG: DHP (-dipine) = Vascular, non-DHP = Cardiac

docs.title: AMLODIPINE / NIFEDIPINE (DHP)
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Block L-type Ca²⁺ channels (vascular smooth muscle) - Vasodilation → ↓ TPR → ↓ BP

Amlodipine advantages: - Long-acting (once daily) - No reflex tachycardia (slow onset) - Well tolerated

Nifedipine: - Short-acting (immediate release) - avoid (reflex tachycardia, MI risk) - Long-acting (SR/XL) - safer

Uses: - Hypertension (first-line option) - Angina - Vasospastic angina (Prinzmetal's) - DOC

Adverse effects: - Peripheral edema (most common - ankle edema) - Flushing, headache - Reflex tachycardia (short-acting nifedipine) - Gingival hyperplasia

NEET PG High-Yield: - Amlodipine long-acting, once daily, well tolerated - DHP cause peripheral edema, gingival hyperplasia

docs.title: VERAPAMIL (Non-DHP)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Blocks cardiac Ca²⁺ channels

Effects: - ↓ HR (negative chronotropy) - ↓ Contractility (negative inotropy) - ↓ AV conduction - Vasodilation (less than DHP)

Uses: - Hypertension - Angina - Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT, PSVT) - Rate control in atrial fibrillation

Adverse effects: - Constipation (most common) - Bradycardia, AV block - Heart failure (negative inotrope) - Peripheral edema

Contraindications: - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree) - Sick sinus syndrome - Severe heart failure - Concurrent β-blocker (↑ risk of heart block)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Verapamil for SVT, rate control in AF - Most common side effect = Constipation - Don't combine with β-blocker

docs.title: DILTIAZEM (Non-DHP)
docs.text:
Intermediate between verapamil & DHP

Uses: - Hypertension - Angina - Atrial fibrillation (rate control)

Adverse effects: - Less constipation than verapamil - Bradycardia, edema

docs.title: ACE INHIBITORS (ACE-I)
docs.text:
Examples: Enalapril, Lisinopril, Ramipril, Perindopril, Captopril

Mechanism: - Inhibit ACE (Angiotensin Converting Enzyme) - ↓ Angiotensin II → Vasodilation, ↓ aldosterone - ↑ Bradykinin → Vasodilation (but also cough)

Effects: - ↓ BP (vasodilation, ↓ volume) - Renoprotective (↓ intraglomerular pressure) - Cardioprotective (↓ remodeling)

docs.title: Indications (First-Line)
docs.text:
1. Hypertension ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Especially with: - Diabetes (renoprotective) - CKD (proteinuria) - Heart failure - Post-MI - LVH

2. Heart failure (all stages)

3. Post-MI (↓ remodeling, ↓ mortality)

4. Diabetic nephropathy (↓ proteinuria, slow progression)

5. CKD with proteinuria

NEET PG High-Yield: - ACE-I first-line for HTN + diabetes, CKD, heart failure, post-MI

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Dry cough ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common (10-20%) - Due to ↑ bradykinin - Switch to ARB (no cough)

2. Hyperkalemia: - ↓ Aldosterone → K+ retention - Risk: CKD, K+-sparing diuretics, NSAIDs

3. Angioedema ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Rare but serious (0.1-0.5%) - Due to ↑ bradykinin - Lip, tongue, laryngeal edema - Life-threatening (airway compromise) - Absolute contraindication for future ACE-I/ARB use - More common in blacks

4. First-dose hypotension: - Especially in volume-depleted, high-renin states

5. Acute kidney injury: - Bilateral renal artery stenosis → ARF (contraindication) - ↑ Creatinine (acceptable <30% rise)

6. Teratogenic ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Contraindicated in pregnancy - Fetal renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, growth restriction - Category D (2nd/3rd trimester)

7. Taste disturbance (captopril)

**8. Rash, proteinuria (captopril)

NEET PG High-Yield: - ACE-I most common side effect = Dry cough (↑ bradykinin) - Angioedema = Serious, absolute contraindication - Contraindicated in pregnancy, bilateral RAS - Cause hyperkalemia

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Pregnancy - Bilateral renal artery stenosis - History of angioedema - Renal artery stenosis in solitary kidney

Relative: - Hyperkalemia (>5.5 mEq/L) - Severe renal impairment

NEET PG: ACE-I contraindicated in pregnancy, bilateral RAS, angioedema history

docs.title: ANGIOTENSIN RECEPTOR BLOCKERS (ARBs)
docs.text:
Examples: Losartan, Telmisartan, Valsartan, Candesartan, Irbesartan

Mechanism: - Block AT1 receptors (Angiotensin II type 1) - Similar effects to ACE-I - No effect on bradykinin → No cough, less angioedema

docs.title: Indications
docs.text:
Same as ACE-I: - Hypertension (with diabetes, CKD, heart failure, post-MI) - Alternative to ACE-I if intolerant (cough, angioedema)

Additional: - Marfan syndrome (↓ aortic root dilation)

NEET PG: ARBs = Alternative to ACE-I (no cough)

docs.title: Advantages over ACE-I
docs.text:
• No cough (no bradykinin effect)
  • Less angioedema (but still possible)
  • Better tolerated

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
Similar to ACE-I (except cough): - Hyperkalemia - Acute kidney injury (bilateral RAS) - Teratogenic (contraindicated in pregnancy) - First-dose hypotension - Angioedema (rare)

Additional: - Losartan: ↓ Uric acid (useful in gout + HTN)

NEET PG High-Yield: - ARBs don't cause cough (vs ACE-I) - Losartan ↓ uric acid (good for HTN + gout)

docs.title: ACE-I + ARB Combination
docs.text:
NOT recommended: - ↑ Hyperkalemia - ↑ Hypotension - ↑ Acute kidney injury - No additional benefit

NEET PG: Don't combine ACE-I + ARB

docs.title: DIRECT RENIN INHIBITOR
docs.text:
ALISKIREN

Mechanism: Inhibits renin (rate-limiting step)

Uses: Hypertension (not widely used)

Adverse effects: Similar to ACE-I/ARB

NEET PG: Aliskiren = Renin inhibitor (not commonly used)

docs.title: JNC 8 GUIDELINES - First-Line Drugs
docs.text:
For general population: 1. Thiazide diuretics 2. ACE inhibitors or ARBs 3. Calcium channel blockers 4. β-blockers (if compelling indication)

Special populations: - Diabetes: ACE-I or ARB (first-line) - CKD: ACE-I or ARB (first-line) - Blacks: Thiazide or CCB (first-line) - Elderly: Thiazide or CCB - Pregnancy: Methyldopa, Labetalol, Nifedipine (long-acting)

NEET PG High-Yield: - First-line for HTN: Thiazide, ACE-I/ARB, CCB - Diabetes/CKD: ACE-I/ARB first - Blacks: Thiazide or CCB

docs.title: HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCIES
docs.text:
Definition: Severe HTN (>180/120) with end-organ damage

Examples: - Hypertensive encephalopathy - Acute stroke - Acute MI - Acute heart failure - Eclampsia - Aortic dissection

Goal: ↓ BP by 25% in first hour (not to normal - risk of ischemia)

docs.title: Drugs of Choice
docs.text:
1. Sodium nitroprusside: - DOC for most emergencies - IV infusion - Immediate onset

2. Labetalol: - Safe in pregnancy - IV bolus or infusion - Eclampsia, pre-eclampsia

3. Hydralazine: - Eclampsia (IV/IM) - Safe in pregnancy

4. Nitroglycerin: - ACS (acute coronary syndrome) - Acute heart failure

5. Esmolol: - Aortic dissection (with nitroprusside) - Ultra-short acting β-blocker

6. Nicardipine: - CCB (IV) - Cerebrovascular events

7. Fenoldopam: - Dopamine D1 agonist - ↓ BP, preserves renal flow

8. Clevidipine: - Ultra-short acting CCB (IV)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Hypertensive emergency = SNP (most), labetalol/hydralazine (pregnancy), esmolol (dissection)

docs.title: DRUG COMBINATIONS
docs.text:
Rational combinations:

1. Diuretic + ACE-I/ARB: - Synergistic - Prevents K+ loss

2. CCB (DHP) + β-blocker: - CCB vasodilation, β-blocker prevents reflex tachycardia

3. ACE-I/ARB + CCB: - Synergistic - ↓ Edema from CCB

4. Thiazide + β-blocker

AVOID: - β-blocker + verapamil/diltiazem (↑ heart block) - ACE-I + ARB (↑ hyperkalemia, no benefit) - ACE-I/ARB + K+-sparing diuretic (hyperkalemia risk)

NEET PG: Don't combine β-blocker + verapamil, ACE-I + ARB

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. First-line drug for essential hypertension? Answer: Thiazide, ACE-I/ARB, or CCB

  2. Antihypertensive safe in pregnancy (chronic HTN)? Answer: Methyldopa

  3. Drug of choice for hypertensive emergency in eclampsia? Answer: Labetalol or Hydralazine (IV)

  4. Most common side effect of ACE inhibitors? Answer: Dry cough

  5. Why switch from ACE-I to ARB? Answer: Intolerance (cough, angioedema)

  6. Drug causing drug-induced lupus? Answer: Hydralazine (high dose, slow acetylators)

  7. Antihypertensive causing hirsutism? Answer: Minoxidil

  8. Treatment for sodium nitroprusside cyanide toxicity? Answer: Sodium thiosulfate

  9. CCB causing constipation? Answer: Verapamil

  10. ARB that lowers uric acid? Answer: Losartan

docs.title: ACE-I Indications (CHAMP)
docs.text:
• **C**KD (with proteinuria)
  • **H**eart failure
  • **A**nti-remodeling (post-MI)
  • **M**yocardial infarction
  • **P**roteinuria (diabetic nephropathy)

docs.title: ACE-I Contraindications (PREG-B)
docs.text:
• **P**regnancy
  • **R**enal artery stenosis (bilateral)
  • **E**dema (angioedema history)
  • **G**ravida (pregnancy)
  • **B**ilateral RAS

docs.title: Hydralazine Side Effects (FLATR)
docs.text:
• **F**luid retention
  • **L**upus
  • **A**ngina (reflex tachycardia)
  • **T**achycardia
  • **R**eflex sympathetic activation

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know first-line antihypertensives (JNC 8)
  • Understand ACE-I mechanism & indications
  • Learn ACE-I adverse effects (cough, angioedema, hyperkalemia)
  • Know drugs safe in pregnancy (methyldopa, labetalol)
  • Understand CCB types (DHP vs non-DHP)
  • Learn hypertensive emergency drugs
  • Know drug contraindications (ACE-I in pregnancy, RAS)
  • Understand drug combinations (avoid β-blocker + verapamil)
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on antihypertensives

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (6-8 questions)

docs.title: ANTIANGINAL & ANTIARRHYTHMIC DRUGS - NEET PG Study Material
ANGINA PECTORIS - Overview
docs.text: Definition: Chest pain due to myocardial ischemia (O₂ demand > supply)

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
1. Stable angina (Exertional): - Predictable - Triggered by exertion - Relieved by rest/nitroglycerin

2. Unstable angina (ACS): - Unpredictable - At rest or minimal exertion - Medical emergency

3. Variant (Prinzmetal's) angina: - Due to coronary vasospasm - At rest (often early morning) - ST elevation on ECG

NEET PG: Variant angina = Vasospasm, ST elevation

docs.title: Determinants of Myocardial O₂ Demand
docs.text:
MVO₂ ∝ Heart rate × Contractility × Wall tension (preload + afterload)

Strategy: ↓ HR, ↓ contractility, ↓ preload, ↓ afterload

docs.title: ORGANIC NITRATES
docs.text: Examples: Nitroglycerin (GTN), Isosorbide dinitrate (ISDN), Isosorbide mononitrate (ISMN)

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
NO donors → ↑ cGMP → Smooth muscle relaxation

Effects: - Venodilation > Arteriolar dilation - ↓ Preload (↓ venous return) → ↓ MVO₂ (main mechanism) - ↓ Afterload (arteriolar dilation) - Coronary vasodilation (↑ coronary blood flow) - Redistribution of coronary flow to ischemic areas

NEET PG: Nitrates mainly ↓ preload (venodilation)

docs.title: NITROGLYCERIN (GTN)
docs.text:
Routes: - Sublingual (acute angina) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Oral (long-acting preparations) - IV (unstable angina, acute MI) - Transdermal patch - Buccal, lingual spray

Sublingual GTN: - Onset: 1-3 minutes - Duration: 10-30 minutes - DOC for acute angina attack - Dose: 0.3-0.6 mg, repeat every 5 min (max 3 doses)

NEET PG: Sublingual GTN = DOC for acute angina

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Acute angina (sublingual)

2. Prophylaxis (long-acting): - Stable angina - Unstable angina (IV)

3. Acute MI (IV - ↓ preload, ↓ afterload)

4. Acute heart failure (IV - ↓ preload)

5. Variant angina (vasospasm)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Headache ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common - Throbbing, due to cerebral vasodilation - Tolerance develops

2. Postural hypotension: - Venodilation - Reflex tachycardia

3. Tolerance ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Develops with continuous use - Prevention: Nitrate-free interval (10-12 hours/day) - Remove patch at night - Asymmetric dosing (ISDN: 8 AM, 2 PM - NOT 8 PM)

4. Methemoglobinemia: - High doses, prolonged use - Treatment: Methylene blue

5. Drug interaction: - Sildenafil (Viagra) + Nitrates → Severe hypotension - Contraindicated together - Wait 24 hours (sildenafil) or 48 hours (tadalafil)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Nitrates most common side effect = Headache - Tolerance prevented by nitrate-free interval - Contraindicated with sildenafil (severe hypotension)

docs.title: ISOSORBIDE DINITRATE (ISDN)
docs.text:
Routes: Oral, sublingual

Duration: Longer than GTN (4-6 hours)

Use: Prophylaxis of angina

Dosing: Asymmetric (to allow nitrate-free interval) - 8 AM, 2 PM (NOT 8 PM)

docs.title: ISOSORBIDE MONONITRATE (ISMN)
docs.text:
Active metabolite of ISDN

Duration: Longer (once or twice daily)

Advantage: More consistent bioavailability

docs.title: β-BLOCKERS IN ANGINA
docs.text: Examples: Atenolol, Metoprolol, Propranolol, Bisoprolol

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• ↓ Heart rate (most important)
  • ↓ Contractility
  • ↓ Blood pressure
  • → ↓ MVO₂

NEET PG: β-blockers ↓ MVO₂ by ↓ HR (main)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Stable angina ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - First-line (with aspirin) - Especially if post-MI, hypertension, arrhythmias

2. Post-MI (↓ mortality)

3. Unstable angina

NOT useful: Variant angina (may worsen vasospasm)

NEET PG: β-blockers first-line for stable angina, avoid in variant angina

docs.title: Adverse Effects & Contraindications
docs.text:
Contraindications: - Variant angina (worsens vasospasm) - Asthma, COPD - Heart block - Acute heart failure

Adverse effects: - Bradycardia, fatigue - Cold extremities - Bronchoconstriction

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text: Block L-type Ca²⁺ channels: - DHP (dihydropyridines): Vascular smooth muscle - Non-DHP: Cardiac + vascular

docs.title: DIHYDROPYRIDINES (Amlodipine, Nifedipine)
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Coronary vasodilation - Peripheral vasodilation → ↓ afterload - → ↓ MVO₂

Uses: - Variant (Prinzmetal's) angina ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (DOC) - Stable angina (if β-blockers contraindicated) - Can combine with β-blockers (safe)

NEET PG: DHP CCBs = DOC for variant angina

docs.title: NON-DIHYDROPYRIDINES (Verapamil, Diltiazem)
docs.text:
Mechanism: - ↓ HR, ↓ contractility - Vasodilation - → ↓ MVO₂

Uses: - Stable angina - Alternative to β-blockers

Contraindication: - Do NOT combine with β-blockers (↑ heart block risk)

NEET PG: Don't combine verapamil/diltiazem with β-blockers

docs.title: RANOLAZINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Inhibits late Na⁺ current in cardiac myocytes - ↓ Intracellular Ca²⁺ overload - ↓ Diastolic tension - Improves perfusion

Uses: - Chronic stable angina (add-on therapy) - When other drugs insufficient

Advantage: No effect on HR, BP (no hemodynamic changes)

Adverse effects: QT prolongation

NEET PG: Ranolazine = Add-on for chronic angina, no hemodynamic effect

docs.title: IVABRADINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Selective If (funny current) inhibitor in SA node - Pure heart rate reduction - No effect on contractility, conduction

Uses: - Chronic stable angina (alternative to β-blockers) - Heart failure with reduced EF (systolic HF)

Advantage: Can use in asthma, COPD (no bronchoconstriction)

Adverse effects: - Bradycardia - Visual disturbances (phosphenes)

NEET PG: Ivabradine = Pure HR reduction, safe in asthma

docs.title: TRIMETAZIDINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Metabolic modulator - Shifts ATP production from fatty acid → glucose oxidation - More O₂-efficient

Uses: Chronic stable angina (add-on)

docs.title: ANTIANGINAL DRUG SUMMARY
docs.text:
Drug Class Stable Angina Unstable Angina Variant Angina Nitrates ✓ (prophylaxis) ✓ (IV) ✓ β-blockers ✓ (1st line) ✓ ✗ (avoid) DHP CCBs ✓ ✓ ✓ (DOC) Non-DHP CCBs ✓ ✓ ✓ Aspirin ✓ ✓ ✗ Ranolazine ✓ (add-on) - - Ivabradine ✓ (alternative to β-blocker) - -

NEET PG: Know drug of choice for each type of angina

docs.title: VAUGHAN WILLIAMS CLASSIFICATION
docs.text:
Class I: Na⁺ channel blockers - IA: Quinidine, Procainamide, Disopyramide - IB: Lidocaine, Mexiletine - IC: Flecainide, Propafenone

Class II: β-blockers - Propranolol, Metoprolol, Esmolol

Class III: K⁺ channel blockers (↑ repolarization) - Amiodarone, Sotalol, Dofetilide, Ibutilide

Class IV: Ca²⁺ channel blockers - Verapamil, Diltiazem

Others (Unclassified): - Adenosine - Digoxin - Atropine - Magnesium

NEET PG: Vaughan Williams classification (memorize)

docs.title: QUINIDINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Na⁺ channel blocker (moderate) - K⁺ channel blocker → ↑ QT interval - Anticholinergic (vagolytic)

Effects on ECG: - ↑ PR interval - ↑ QRS duration (Na⁺ block) - ↑ QT interval (K⁺ block)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Atrial fibrillation/flutter (cardioversion, maintenance)

2. Ventricular arrhythmias (VT, VPCs)

Note: Now rarely used (replaced by safer drugs)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Cinchonism ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Tinnitus, headache, vertigo - Visual disturbances - GI upset

2. Torsades de pointes ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Polymorphic VT - Due to QT prolongation - Life-threatening - Treatment: Magnesium sulfate, isoproterenol, pacing

3. Thrombocytopenia

4. Drug interactions: - ↑ Digoxin levels (↓ renal clearance)

5. Diarrhea (most common)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Quinidine causes cinchonism (tinnitus) - Torsades de pointes (QT prolongation) treated with Mg²⁺

docs.title: PROCAINAMIDE
docs.text:
Similar to quinidine

Additional adverse effect: - Drug-induced lupus ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Very common (50-70% with chronic use) - Anti-histone antibodies (+) - Reversible

NEET PG: Procainamide causes drug-induced lupus (very common)

docs.title: LIDOCAINE (Lignocaine)
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Na⁺ channel blocker (fast on-off kinetics) - Preferentially blocks ischemic/depolarized tissue - No effect on normal tissue

Effects on ECG: - No change in QRS, QT (selective for abnormal tissue)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Ventricular arrhythmias ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Post-MI VT, VF - Digoxin-induced arrhythmias

NOT effective: Atrial arrhythmias

Route: IV only (first-pass metabolism if oral)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Lidocaine = DOC for post-MI ventricular arrhythmias - Also for digoxin-induced VT - IV only

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• CNS: Seizures (high doses), confusion
  • Hypotension
  • Bradycardia

docs.title: MEXILETINE
docs.text:
Oral analog of lidocaine

Uses: Chronic ventricular arrhythmias

docs.title: FLECAINIDE, PROPAFENONE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Potent Na⁺ channel blockers

Uses: - Supraventricular arrhythmias (AF, PSVT) - Only in structurally normal hearts

Contraindication ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Post-MI or structural heart disease - CAST study: ↑ Mortality in post-MI patients

NEET PG: Flecainide contraindicated post-MI (CAST trial)

docs.title: CLASS II: β-BLOCKERS
docs.text: Examples: Propranolol, Metoprolol, Esmolol

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• ↓ SA node automaticity
  • ↓ AV conduction
  • ↓ Sympathetic drive

Effects on ECG: - ↑ PR interval - ↓ HR

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Supraventricular arrhythmias ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - SVT, PSVT (acute & prophylaxis) - Atrial fibrillation (rate control) - Atrial flutter (rate control)

2. Ventricular arrhythmias: - Post-MI (prophylaxis) - Exercise-induced VT

3. Thyrotoxicosis-induced arrhythmias

NEET PG: β-blockers for SVT, AF rate control

docs.title: ESMOLOL
docs.text:
Ultra-short acting β1-blocker (IV)

Uses: - Perioperative tachycardia - Acute SVT - Thyroid storm

NEET PG: Esmolol = Ultra-short acting, IV

docs.title: AMIODARONE - Most Important
docs.text:
Mechanism: - K⁺ channel blocker (main) → ↑ repolarization - Also blocks: Na⁺, Ca²⁺ channels, α/β receptors (non-competitive) - Broadest spectrum antiarrhythmic

Effects on ECG: - ↑ QT interval (but rarely causes torsades) - ↑ PR, ↑ QRS

docs.title: Uses (Broadest Spectrum)
docs.text:
1. Ventricular arrhythmias ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Life-threatening VT, VF - Refractory VT/VF (ACLS protocol) - Most effective for ventricular arrhythmias

2. Supraventricular arrhythmias: - Atrial fibrillation (cardioversion, maintenance) - PSVT (including WPW)

3. Cardiac arrest (VF, pulseless VT)

NEET PG High-Yield: Amiodarone = Broadest spectrum, most effective for VT/VF

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text:
Half-life: Very long (20-100 days) - Slow onset (oral - days to weeks) - Slow elimination (persists weeks after stopping) - Loading dose required

Loading: 800-1600 mg/day × 1-2 weeks, then ↓

NEET PG: Amiodarone long half-life (20-100 days)

docs.title: Adverse Effects (Multiple Organs)
docs.text:
1. Pulmonary toxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most serious - Pulmonary fibrosis (irreversible) - Onset: Months to years - Monitor: Chest X-ray, PFTs, symptoms - Contraindicated in pre-existing lung disease

2. Thyroid dysfunction ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - High iodine content (37% iodine by weight) - Hypothyroidism OR hyperthyroidism - Monitor: TSH every 6 months

3. Hepatotoxicity: - ↑ Transaminases - Monitor: LFTs

4. Corneal microdeposits ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Almost universal (>90%) - Usually asymptomatic - Reversible

5. Blue-gray skin discoloration: - Photosensitivity - Sun exposure

6. Peripheral neuropathy, myopathy

7. Bradycardia, AV block

8. ↑ QT (but torsades rare)

9. Drug interactions: - ↑ Digoxin, warfarin levels (CYP inhibition)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Amiodarone most serious = Pulmonary fibrosis - High iodine → thyroid dysfunction - Corneal deposits (common, benign) - ↑ Digoxin & warfarin levels

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: Baseline & periodic: - Pulmonary: CXR, PFTs, symptoms - Thyroid: TSH, T3, T4 - Hepatic: LFTs - Ophthalmic: Eye exam

docs.title: SOTALOL
docs.text:
Mechanism: β-blocker + K⁺ channel blocker

Uses: - Ventricular arrhythmias - Atrial fibrillation

Adverse effects: - Torsades de pointes (↑ QT) - Bradycardia (β-blocker)

NEET PG: Sotalol = β-blocker + K⁺ blocker

docs.title: DOFETILIDE, IBUTILIDE
docs.text:
Pure K⁺ channel blockers

Uses: - Atrial fibrillation (cardioversion) - Ibutilide: IV for acute AF conversion

Adverse effect: Torsades de pointes (high risk)

docs.title: VERAPAMIL, DILTIAZEM
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Block AV node Ca²⁺ channels - ↓ AV conduction

Effects on ECG: - ↑ PR interval

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Supraventricular arrhythmias ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - PSVT (acute termination) - Verapamil DOC (IV) - Atrial fibrillation/flutter (rate control)

NOT effective: Ventricular arrhythmias

NEET PG: Verapamil (IV) = DOC for acute PSVT termination

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• WPW syndrome with AF ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
  • May ↑ ventricular rate (paradoxical)
  • Risk of VF
  • VT (may cause hemodynamic collapse)
  • Heart block
  • Heart failure

NEET PG: Verapamil contraindicated in WPW with AF

docs.title: ADENOSINE
docs.text:
Mechanism: - A1 receptor agonist - ↑ K⁺ conductance → Hyperpolarization - Slows AV conduction (transient AV block)

Pharmacokinetics: - Ultra-short half-life (<10 seconds) - Rapid IV bolus required - Metabolized by RBCs (adenosine deaminase)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. PSVT ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - DOC for acute termination - Diagnostic (unmasking flutter waves)

NOT effective: Atrial fibrillation, VT

NEET PG: Adenosine = DOC for acute PSVT (ultra-short acting)

docs.title: Administration
docs.text: Rapid IV push (6 mg → 12 mg → 12 mg if needed) - Followed immediately by saline flush - Large vein (antecubital)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
Common (but transient - seconds): - Flushing - Dyspnea, chest discomfort - Transient asystole (expected) - Bronchospasm

Contraindications: - Asthma (bronchospasm) - Heart block (2nd/3rd degree)

Drug interactions: - Dipyridamole: ↑ Effect (blocks adenosine uptake) - Theophylline: ↓ Effect (adenosine antagonist)

NEET PG: Adenosine contraindicated in asthma, potentiated by dipyridamole

docs.title: DIGOXIN
docs.text:
Uses (as antiarrhythmic): - Atrial fibrillation (rate control) - Atrial flutter (rate control) - Paroxysmal SVT

Mechanism: ↑ Vagal tone, ↓ AV conduction

NEET PG: Digoxin for AF rate control

docs.title: MAGNESIUM SULFATE
docs.text:
Uses: - Torsades de pointes ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (DOC) - Digoxin-induced arrhythmias - Hypomagnesemia

NEET PG: Mg²⁺ = DOC for torsades de pointes

docs.title: Atrial Fibrillation
docs.text:
Rate control: - β-blockers, CCBs (verapamil, diltiazem), digoxin

Rhythm control (cardioversion): - Amiodarone, dofetilide - Electrical cardioversion

Anticoagulation: Warfarin, DOACs

NEET PG: AF = Rate control (β-blocker, CCB) or rhythm control (amiodarone)

docs.title: PSVT (Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia)
docs.text:
Acute termination: 1. Vagal maneuvers (first-line) 2. Adenosine (DOC - IV) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 3. Verapamil (IV) - alternative 4. β-blockers

Prophylaxis: - β-blockers, verapamil, diltiazem

NEET PG: PSVT = Adenosine (acute DOC)

docs.title: Ventricular Tachycardia (VT)
docs.text:
With pulse: - Amiodarone (IV) - Lidocaine - Synchronized cardioversion

Pulseless VT/VF (Cardiac arrest): - Defibrillation - Epinephrine - Amiodarone (or lidocaine)

NEET PG: VT = Amiodarone (most effective)

docs.title: Torsades de Pointes
docs.text:
Treatment: - Magnesium sulfate (IV) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Isoproterenol (↑ HR) - Temporary pacing

Prevent: Stop offending drug, correct electrolytes

NEET PG: Torsades = Magnesium sulfate

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Drug of choice for acute angina attack? Answer: Sublingual nitroglycerin

  2. Drug of choice for variant (Prinzmetal's) angina? Answer: Calcium channel blockers (DHP)

  3. Most common side effect of nitrates? Answer: Headache

  4. Why is sildenafil contraindicated with nitrates? Answer: Severe hypotension

  5. Drug of choice for post-MI ventricular arrhythmias? Answer: Lidocaine (IV)

  6. Drug of choice for acute PSVT termination? Answer: Adenosine (IV)

  7. Most serious adverse effect of amiodarone? Answer: Pulmonary fibrosis

  8. Treatment for torsades de pointes? Answer: Magnesium sulfate

  9. Antiarrhythmic contraindicated in WPW with AF? Answer: Verapamil, diltiazem

  10. Antiarrhythmic causing drug-induced lupus? Answer: Procainamide

docs.title: Nitrate Adverse Effects (HMT)
docs.text:
• **H**eadache (most common)
  • **M**ethemoglobinemia
  • **T**olerance

docs.title: Amiodarone Toxicities (PULTH)
docs.text:
• **P**ulmonary fibrosis
  • **U**nderactive/overactive thyroid
  • **L**iver (hepatotoxicity)
  • **T**issue (skin, blue-gray)
  • **H**eart (bradycardia)

docs.title: PSVT Treatment (VAB)
docs.text:
• **V**agal maneuvers
  • **A**denosine
  • **B**eta-blockers (or verapamil)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know antianginal drug classes & uses
  • Understand nitrate mechanism & adverse effects
  • Learn Vaughan Williams classification
  • Know amiodarone uses & toxicities
  • Understand adenosine use in PSVT
  • Learn treatment for torsades de pointes
  • Know drug contraindications (flecainide post-MI, verapamil in WPW)
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on antianginal & antiarrhythmic drugs

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (6-8 questions)

docs.title: DEFINITION
docs.text:
Diuretics: Drugs that ↑ urine output by ↑ Na⁺ and water excretion from kidneys

Natriuresis: ↑ Na⁺ excretion Diuresis: ↑ Water excretion

docs.title: NEPHRON PHYSIOLOGY - Brief Review
docs.text:
Glomerulus (filtration)
    ↓
Proximal tubule (65% Na+ reabsorption)
    ↓
Loop of Henle (25% Na+ reabsorption)
    ↓
Distal tubule (5% Na+ reabsorption)
    ↓
Collecting duct (2-3% Na+ reabsorption)

NEET PG: Most reabsorption in proximal tubule (65%)

docs.title: By Site of Action
docs.text:
1. Proximal tubule: - Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (Acetazolamide) - Osmotic diuretics (Mannitol)

2. Thick ascending limb (Loop of Henle): - Loop diuretics (Furosemide, Bumetanide, Torsemide, Ethacrynic acid)

3. Early distal tubule: - Thiazides (Hydrochlorothiazide, Chlorthalidone, Indapamide)

4. Late distal tubule & collecting duct: - K⁺-sparing diuretics - Aldosterone antagonists (Spironolactone, Eplerenone) - ENaC blockers (Amiloride, Triamterene)

docs.title: By Efficacy
docs.text:
High-efficacy (High-ceiling): - Loop diuretics (can excrete 15-25% filtered Na⁺)

Medium-efficacy: - Thiazides (5-10% filtered Na⁺)

Low-efficacy: - K⁺-sparing, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, osmotic

NEET PG: Loop diuretics = Most potent

docs.title: LOOP DIURETICS
docs.text: Examples: Furosemide (prototype), Bumetanide, Torsemide, Ethacrynic acid

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Inhibit Na⁺-K⁺-2Cl⁻ cotransporter in thick ascending limb of Loop of Henle

Effects: 1. ↓ Na⁺ reabsorption → Natriuresis 2. ↓ Medullary tonicity (↓ concentrating ability) 3. ↓ Ca²⁺ reabsorption → Hypocalcemia 4. ↓ Mg²⁺ reabsorption → Hypomagnesemia 5. ↑ K⁺ excretion → Hypokalemia 6. ↑ Renal blood flow (prostaglandin-mediated) 7. ↑ Renin release → ↑ Aldosterone

NEET PG High-Yield: Loop diuretics cause hypocalcemia (vs thiazides → hypercalcemia)

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text:
Route: Oral, IV (rapid onset)

Onset: - IV: 5 minutes - Oral: 30-60 minutes

Duration: 2-6 hours (short-acting)

Excretion: Renal

NEET PG: Loop diuretics rapid onset (5 min IV)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Acute pulmonary edema ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - DOC - IV furosemide - Rapid venodilation + diuresis

2. Chronic heart failure: - Volume overload - Preferred in severe HF (vs thiazides)

3. Acute renal failure (oliguric): - Convert to non-oliguric (easier to manage) - No improvement in survival

4. Nephrotic syndrome: - Edema - Often combined with albumin

5. Cirrhosis with ascites: - With spironolactone

6. Hypertension: - Not first-line (short duration) - Useful in renal impairment (GFR <30)

7. Hypercalcemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Causes calciuresis - With saline hydration

8. Hyperkalemia: - Emergency (with other measures)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Furosemide = DOC for acute pulmonary edema - Loop diuretics for hypercalcemia (calciuresis) - Useful in renal failure (GFR <30)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Hypokalemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common - Can cause arrhythmias - Prevent: K⁺ supplements, combine with K⁺-sparing diuretic

2. Metabolic alkalosis: - ↑ H⁺ excretion - Contraction alkalosis

3. Hyponatremia, hypovolemia

4. Hypocalcemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↑ Ca²⁺ excretion

5. Hypomagnesemia: - ↑ Mg²⁺ excretion

6. Hyperuricemia (gout): - ↓ Uric acid excretion

7. Ototoxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Dose-related, reversible - Ethacrynic acid > Furosemide - Increased risk with: - Aminoglycosides - Rapid IV injection - Renal failure

8. Hyperglycemia: - ↓ Insulin secretion

9. Hyperlipidemia: - ↑ LDL, ↑ TG

10. Allergic interstitial nephritis: - Sulfonamide allergy (except ethacrynic acid)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Loop diuretics cause hypokalemia, hypocalcemia, ototoxicity - Ethacrynic acid = Non-sulfonamide (use if sulfa allergy)

docs.title: Drug Interactions
docs.text:
1. Aminoglycosides: - ↑ Ototoxicity - ↑ Nephrotoxicity

2. NSAIDs: - ↓ Diuretic effect - Block prostaglandin-mediated renal vasodilation

3. Digoxin: - Hypokalemia → ↑ Digoxin toxicity

4. Lithium: - ↓ Lithium excretion → toxicity

NEET PG: NSAIDs ↓ loop diuretic efficacy

docs.title: ETHACRYNIC ACID
docs.text:
Unique features: - NOT a sulfonamide (vs other loop diuretics) - Use if sulfonamide allergy - More ototoxic than furosemide

NEET PG: Ethacrynic acid = Non-sulfonamide loop diuretic

docs.title: THIAZIDE DIURETICS
docs.text: Examples: Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ), Chlorthalidone, Indapamide, Metolazone

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Inhibit Na⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter in early distal convoluted tubule

Effects: 1. ↓ Na⁺ reabsorption → Natriuresis (moderate) 2. ↑ Ca²⁺ reabsorption → Hypercalcemia (unique) 3. ↑ K⁺ excretion → Hypokalemia 4. Chronic use: Vasodilation (main antihypertensive effect)

NEET PG High-Yield: Thiazides cause hypercalcemia (opposite of loop diuretics)

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text:
Route: Oral only (NOT IV)

Onset: 1-2 hours

Duration: 12-24 hours (longer than loop)

Excretion: Renal

NEET PG: Thiazides oral only, longer duration than loop

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Hypertension ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - First-line (along with ACE-I, ARB, CCB) - Inexpensive, once daily - Preferred in: - Elderly - Blacks - Isolated systolic hypertension

2. Heart failure: - Mild to moderate - NOT in severe (loop diuretics better)

3. Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Paradoxical effect - ↓ Urine volume - Mechanism: ↓ GFR, ↑ proximal reabsorption

4. Hypercalciuria, calcium stones ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↑ Ca²⁺ reabsorption → ↓ urinary Ca²⁺

5. Osteoporosis (prevention): - ↑ Bone density (↑ Ca²⁺ retention)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Thiazides = First-line for hypertension - Nephrogenic DI (paradoxical effect) - Hypercalciuria, calcium stones

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Hypokalemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common - Arrhythmias - Prevent: K⁺ supplements, K⁺-sparing diuretic

2. Metabolic alkalosis

3. Hyponatremia: - More common than with loop (longer duration)

4. Hypercalcemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Unique to thiazides - Can unmask hyperparathyroidism

5. Hyperuricemia (gout): - ↓ Uric acid excretion

6. Hyperglycemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↓ Insulin secretion - Worsen diabetes

7. Hyperlipidemia: - ↑ LDL, ↑ TG, ↑ cholesterol

8. Hypomagnesemia

9. Erectile dysfunction

10. Sulfonamide allergy

NEET PG High-Yield: - Thiazides cause hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• Renal failure (GFR <30) - Ineffective ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
  • Use loop diuretics instead
  • Hypokalemia
  • Hypercalcemia
  • Gout (relative)

NEET PG: Thiazides ineffective if GFR <30 (use loop diuretics)

docs.title: METOLAZONE
docs.text:
Unique features: - Thiazide-like diuretic - Effective even in renal failure (unlike other thiazides) - Synergistic with loop diuretics ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Sequential nephron blockade - For refractory edema

NEET PG: Metolazone + furosemide = Refractory edema

docs.title: A. ALDOSTERONE ANTAGONISTS
docs.text: Examples: Spironolactone, Eplerenone

docs.title: SPIRONOLACTONE
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Competitive antagonist of aldosterone (mineralocorticoid receptor) - Blocks Na⁺ reabsorption, K⁺/H⁺ excretion in collecting duct

Effects: - Weak diuresis - K⁺ retention (hyperkalemia) - Weak natriuresis

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Primary hyperaldosteronism ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - DOC for treatment - Diagnostic (trial of spironolactone)

2. Heart failure ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Systolic HF (NYHA III-IV) - ↓ Mortality (RALES trial) - Low dose (25-50 mg)

3. Cirrhosis with ascites ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - DOC (often first-line) - With furosemide (ratio 100:40 mg)

4. Resistant hypertension (with other drugs)

5. Hirsutism, acne (women): - Anti-androgen effect - PCOS

NEET PG High-Yield: - Spironolactone = DOC for primary hyperaldosteronism - ↓ Mortality in heart failure (RALES trial) - DOC for cirrhotic ascites

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Hyperkalemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most serious - Especially with: ACE-I, ARBs, NSAIDs, K⁺ supplements, renal failure

2. Gynecomastia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Anti-androgen effect - Binds to androgen receptors - Common in men (10-20%)

3. Menstrual irregularities

4. Metabolic acidosis: - ↓ H⁺ excretion

NEET PG High-Yield: - Spironolactone causes hyperkalemia, gynecomastia - Don't combine with ACE-I/ARB (hyperkalemia)

docs.title: EPLERENONE
docs.text:
Selective aldosterone antagonist: - NO anti-androgen effects (no gynecomastia) - Otherwise similar to spironolactone

Uses: - Heart failure (post-MI) - Hypertension

NEET PG: Eplerenone = No gynecomastia (vs spironolactone)

docs.title: B. ENaC BLOCKERS
docs.text: Examples: Amiloride, Triamterene

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Block epithelial Na⁺ channels (ENaC) in collecting duct - Independent of aldosterone

Effects: - Weak diuresis - K⁺ retention

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Combination with thiazides/loop ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Prevent hypokalemia - Fixed-dose combinations available (HCTZ + Amiloride)

2. Lithium-induced nephrogenic DI: - Amiloride blocks Li⁺ entry (via ENaC)

3. Liddle syndrome: - Genetic ↑ ENaC activity - Amiloride specific treatment

NEET PG High-Yield: - Amiloride + thiazide prevents hypokalemia - Amiloride for lithium-induced DI, Liddle syndrome

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Hyperkalemia (most serious)

2. Metabolic acidosis

3. **Triamterene: Kidney stones, megaloblastic anemia

docs.title: CARBONIC ANHYDRASE INHIBITORS
docs.text: Example: Acetazolamide

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Inhibit carbonic anhydrase in proximal tubule

Effect: - ↓ HCO₃⁻ reabsorption → Bicarbonate diuresis - Weak diuretic - Causes metabolic acidosis

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Glaucoma ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↓ Aqueous humor production - ↓ Intraocular pressure - Oral or topical (dorzolamide)

2. Altitude sickness ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Prophylaxis & treatment - ↑ Ventilation (metabolic acidosis → respiratory compensation)

3. Metabolic alkalosis: - Correct alkalosis

4. Epilepsy (add-on): - Absence seizures

5. Pseudotumor cerebri (idiopathic intracranial hypertension): - ↓ CSF production

6. Periodic paralysis (hypokalemic)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Acetazolamide = Glaucoma, altitude sickness - Causes metabolic acidosis

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Metabolic acidosis ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↑ Cl⁻, ↓ HCO₃⁻

2. Hypokalemia

3. Paresthesias (hands, feet)

4. Renal stones: - Alkaline urine → calcium phosphate stones

5. Sulfonamide allergy

NEET PG: Acetazolamide causes metabolic acidosis, stones

docs.title: OSMOTIC DIURETICS
docs.text: Example: Mannitol

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Freely filtered, NOT reabsorbed - ↑ Tubular fluid osmolality - ↓ Water reabsorption (osmotic effect)

Effects: - Water diuresis > Na⁺ diuresis - Initial plasma expansion (draws fluid from tissues)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Cerebral edema ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↓ Intracranial pressure - DOC (along with hypertonic saline) - Head injury, stroke, tumor

2. Acute angle-closure glaucoma: - ↓ Intraocular pressure (emergency)

3. Prevent acute renal failure: - Rhabdomyolysis, hemolysis - No benefit if established ARF

4. Forced diuresis (drug overdose): - Salicylate, phenobarbital

NEET PG High-Yield: - Mannitol = DOC for cerebral edema (↓ ICP) - Also for acute glaucoma

docs.title: Route & Monitoring
docs.text:
Route: IV only (not absorbed orally)

Monitor: Serum osmolality (<320 mOsm/kg)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Volume overload ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Acute pulmonary edema - Contraindicated in heart failure - Initial plasma expansion

2. Hyponatremia, hypokalemia

3. Dehydration (excessive diuresis)

4. Headache, nausea

NEET PG High-Yield: - Mannitol contraindicated in heart failure (volume overload) - Can cause pulmonary edema

docs.title: Synergistic Combinations
docs.text:
1. Loop + Thiazide (or metolazone): - Sequential nephron blockade - Refractory edema

2. Loop (or Thiazide) + K⁺-sparing: - Prevent hypokalemia - Commonly used

3. Spironolactone + Furosemide: - Cirrhotic ascites (ratio 100:40)

NEET PG: Loop + thiazide = Refractory edema

docs.title: Avoid
docs.text: K⁺-sparing + ACE-I/ARB: - Severe hyperkalemia risk

docs.title: DIURETIC RESISTANCE
docs.text:
Causes: 1. Non-compliance 2. High Na⁺ intake 3. NSAIDs (↓ efficacy) 4. Renal failure (severe) 5. Hypoalbuminemia (nephrotic, cirrhosis)

Management: - Increase dose - Switch to IV (better bioavailability) - Combination (loop + thiazide) - Albumin infusion (if hypoalbuminemic) - Treat underlying cause

NEET PG: Diuretic resistance → Increase dose, combine drugs

docs.title: HIGH-YIELD COMPARISONS
docs.text:
Feature Loop Thiazide K⁺-sparing Site Thick ascending limb Distal tubule Collecting duct Efficacy High Moderate Low K⁺ Hypokalemia Hypokalemia Hyperkalemia Ca²⁺ Hypocalcemia Hypercalcemia - Uric acid ↑ (Hyperuricemia) ↑ (Hyperuricemia) - Renal failure Effective Ineffective (GFR <30) Caution First-line HTN NO YES NO Heart failure Severe HF Mild-moderate HF Add-on (mortality benefit)

NEET PG: Know Ca²⁺ effects (loop ↓, thiazide ↑)

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. DOC for acute pulmonary edema? Answer: IV Furosemide (loop diuretic)

  2. Diuretic causing hypercalcemia? Answer: Thiazides

  3. Diuretic for hypercalcemia? Answer: Loop diuretics (cause calciuresis)

  4. Diuretic for nephrogenic diabetes insipidus? Answer: Thiazides

  5. Diuretic ineffective in renal failure (GFR <30)? Answer: Thiazides

  6. DOC for cerebral edema? Answer: Mannitol (osmotic diuretic)

  7. Diuretic contraindicated in heart failure? Answer: Mannitol (causes volume overload)

  8. Diuretic reducing mortality in heart failure? Answer: Spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist)

  9. Diuretic causing gynecomastia? Answer: Spironolactone

  10. Diuretic for glaucoma? Answer: Acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor)

docs.title: Thiazide vs Loop - Ca²⁺ Effect
docs.text: "Thiazides are CALMer" (↑ Calcium, vs loop)

docs.title: Loop Diuretic Adverse Effects (OH DANG)
docs.text:
• **O**totoxicity
  • **H**ypokalemia
  • **D**ehydration
  • **A**lkalosis
  • **N**a⁺ loss (hyponatremia)
  • **G**out (hyperuricemia)

docs.title: Spironolactone Uses (CHAP)
docs.text:
• **C**irrhosis (ascites)
  • **H**eart failure (mortality)
  • **A**ldosteronism (primary)
  • **P**COS (hirsutism)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know diuretic classification by site of action
  • Understand loop diuretic mechanism & uses
  • Learn thiazide vs loop differences (Ca²⁺ effect)
  • Know spironolactone indications (HF, cirrhosis, aldosteronism)
  • Understand mannitol uses & contraindications
  • Learn acetazolamide uses (glaucoma, altitude)
  • Know adverse effects (hypokalemia, ototoxicity, gynecomastia)
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on diuretics

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: ANTIDIABETIC DRUGS - NEET PG Study Material
Types
docs.text:
Type 1 DM (10%): - Absolute insulin deficiency - Autoimmune destruction of β-cells - Requires insulin

Type 2 DM (90%): - Insulin resistance + relative insulin deficiency - Progressive β-cell dysfunction - Initially managed with oral drugs

NEET PG: Type 2 DM = Insulin resistance + β-cell dysfunction

docs.title: INSULIN & ANALOGS
docs.text: • Rapid-acting, Short-acting, Intermediate-acting, Long-acting

docs.title: ORAL HYPOGLYCEMIC AGENTS
docs.text:
1. Insulin Secretagogues: - Sulfonylureas (Glibenclamide, Gliclazide, Glimepiride) - Meglitinides (Repaglinide, Nateglinide)

2. Insulin Sensitizers: - Biguanides (Metformin) - Thiazolidinediones (Pioglitazone)

3. α-Glucosidase Inhibitors: - Acarbose, Voglibose, Miglitol

4. Incretin-based: - GLP-1 agonists (Exenatide, Liraglutide, Dulaglutide, Semaglutide) - DPP-4 inhibitors (Sitagliptin, Vildagliptin, Linagliptin)

5. SGLT-2 Inhibitors: - Dapagliflozin, Canagliflozin, Empagliflozin

6. Amylin analog: - Pramlintide

docs.title: Classification by Duration
docs.text:
1. Rapid-acting (Analogs): - Insulin lispro, aspart, glulisine - Onset: 5-15 min - Peak: 30-90 min - Duration: 3-5 hours - Use: Mealtime bolus, IV infusion

2. Short-acting (Regular/Soluble insulin): - Regular human insulin - Onset: 30-60 min - Peak: 2-4 hours - Duration: 6-8 hours - Use: Mealtime, IV (DKA, HHS), SC

3. Intermediate-acting: - NPH (Neutral Protamine Hagedorn) - Onset: 1-2 hours - Peak: 4-8 hours - Duration: 12-18 hours - Use: Basal insulin (twice daily)

4. Long-acting (Analogs): - Insulin glargine, detemir, degludec - Onset: 1-2 hours - Peak: No peak (peakless) - Duration: 20-24 hours (degludec: >40 hours) - Use: Basal insulin (once daily)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Only regular & rapid-acting can be given IV - Long-acting = Peakless (glargine, detemir, degludec)

docs.title: Basal-Bolus Regimen
docs.text:
Standard approach for Type 1 DM: - Basal: Long-acting insulin (glargine/detemir) once daily OR NPH twice daily - Bolus: Rapid-acting insulin (lispro/aspart) before each meal

Total daily dose: 0.5-1 unit/kg - 50% basal, 50% bolus (divided among meals)

NEET PG: Basal-bolus = Standard for Type 1 DM

docs.title: Insulin Administration
docs.text:
Routes: - SC (most common) - abdomen, thigh, arm - IV - Only regular or rapid-acting (DKA, HHS, perioperative) - Inhaled - Technosphere insulin (rarely used)

Insulin pen: Convenient, accurate dosing

Insulin pump: Continuous SC infusion (CSII)

docs.title: Adverse Effects of Insulin
docs.text:
1. Hypoglycemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common & serious - Symptoms: Sweating, tremor, palpitations, confusion, seizures, coma - Treatment: - Conscious: Oral glucose/sugar - Unconscious: IV dextrose (50%) or IM glucagon

2. Weight gain: - Anabolic effect

3. Lipodystrophy: - Lipoatrophy (fat loss) - rare (human insulin) - Lipohypertrophy (fat accumulation) - injection site rotation needed

4. Insulin allergy: - Rare (human insulin) - Local or systemic

5. Hypokalemia: - Insulin shifts K⁺ into cells - Monitor K⁺ in DKA treatment

6. Insulin resistance: - High-dose requirement (>200 units/day) - Due to antibodies (rare with human insulin)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Hypoglycemia = Most common/serious insulin complication - Treatment: Conscious = oral glucose, Unconscious = IV dextrose/IM glucagon

docs.title: SULFONYLUREAS
docs.text: Examples: Glibenclamide (Glyburide), Gliclazide, Glimepiride, Glipizide

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Close ATP-sensitive K⁺ channels on β-cell membrane: 1. K⁺ channels close 2. Depolarization 3. Ca²⁺ influx 4. Insulin release

Effect: ↑ Insulin secretion (insulin secretagogue)

NEET PG: Sulfonylureas close K⁺ channels → insulin release

docs.title: Uses
docs.text: Type 2 DM: - NOT effective in Type 1 (no β-cells) - Monotherapy or combination

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Hypoglycemia ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most serious - Glibenclamide > Glimepiride (risk) - Prolonged (long-acting) - Especially: Elderly, renal/hepatic impairment, missed meals

2. Weight gain ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↑ Insulin → anabolic effect

3. SIADH (hyponatremia): - Rare

4. Disulfiram-like reaction: - Chlorpropamide (obsolete drug)

5. Hypersensitivity: - Sulfonamide allergy

NEET PG High-Yield: - Sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia, weight gain - Contraindicated in Type 1 DM, pregnancy

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• Type 1 DM (ineffective)
  • Pregnancy (teratogenic - use insulin)
  • Severe renal/hepatic impairment
  • Sulfonamide allergy

docs.title: Drug Interactions
docs.text:
Potentiate hypoglycemia: - Alcohol, NSAIDs, sulfonamides, warfarin

Reduce effect: - Thiazides, corticosteroids, phenytoin

docs.title: MEGLITINIDES (Glinides)
docs.text: Examples: Repaglinide, Nateglinide

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Similar to sulfonylureas (close K⁺ channels) but: - Faster onset - Shorter duration - Different binding site

Effect: ↑ Insulin secretion (mealtime)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
Type 2 DM: - Postprandial hyperglycemia - Taken with meals (15-30 min before)

Advantage: - Flexible dosing (skip if skip meal) - Less hypoglycemia than sulfonylureas

NEET PG: Meglitinides = Short-acting, mealtime insulin secretagogues

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
• Hypoglycemia (less than sulfonylureas)
  • Weight gain

docs.title: METFORMIN - First-Line for Type 2 DM
docs.text: Class: Biguanide

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Multiple actions (exact mechanism unclear): 1. ↓ Hepatic gluconeogenesis (main) → ↓ glucose production 2. ↑ Peripheral glucose uptake (muscle) → ↑ insulin sensitivity 3. ↓ Intestinal glucose absorption 4. Does NOT ↑ insulin secretion (no hypoglycemia)

NEET PG: Metformin ↓ hepatic gluconeogenesis, doesn't cause hypoglycemia

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Type 2 DM ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - First-line (monotherapy) - Especially if obese (↓ weight)

2. Prediabetes (prevention)

3. PCOS: - ↑ Insulin sensitivity - ↑ Ovulation

4. Gestational diabetes (off-label, some use)

NEET PG High-Yield: Metformin = First-line for Type 2 DM

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
1. No hypoglycemia (doesn't ↑ insulin)

2. Weight neutral or ↓ weight ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Only antidiabetic that ↓ weight - ↓ Appetite

3. Cardiovascular benefits: - ↓ Mortality (UKPDS trial) - ↓ Macrovascular complications

4. ↓ HbA1c (1-2%)

5. Inexpensive

NEET PG: Metformin = Only antidiabetic that ↓ weight, no hypoglycemia

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. GI disturbances ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common (30%) - Nausea, diarrhea, abdominal discomfort - Reduce by: Start low dose, with meals, extended-release

2. Lactic acidosis ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Rare but SERIOUS (often fatal) - Risk factors: - Renal failure (eGFR <30) - Liver failure - Hypoxic states (heart failure, respiratory failure) - Alcohol abuse - Contrast dye (IV iodinated) - Sepsis, shock - Stop metformin 48 hours before contrast (restart after 48 hours if renal function normal)

3. Vitamin B12 deficiency: - Long-term use - ↓ Absorption - Monitor, supplement

NEET PG High-Yield: - Metformin most common = GI side effects - Lactic acidosis (rare, serious) - stop before contrast dye - Contraindicated in renal failure (eGFR <30)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Liver failure - Heart failure (severe, unstable) - Respiratory failure - Sepsis, shock - Alcohol abuse - Lactic acidosis history

Relative: - eGFR 30-45 (reduce dose)

NEET PG: Stop metformin 48 hours before IV contrast

docs.title: THIAZOLIDINEDIONES (TZDs, Glitazones)
docs.text: Example: Pioglitazone (Rosiglitazone withdrawn)

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
PPAR-γ (Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-gamma) agonist: - ↑ Insulin sensitivity (muscle, fat, liver) - ↑ Glucose uptake - ↓ Hepatic gluconeogenesis - Does NOT ↑ insulin secretion

NEET PG: TZDs = PPAR-γ agonist, ↑ insulin sensitivity

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
