Type 2 DM: - Combination therapy (not first-line)

Advantages: - No hypoglycemia - ↓ Triglycerides (pioglitazone)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Fluid retention, edema ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Common (5%) - Worsens heart failure

2. Heart failure ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Contraindicated in NYHA class III-IV - Black box warning

3. Weight gain ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↑ Subcutaneous fat

4. Bone fractures ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Especially in women - ↓ Bone density

5. Bladder cancer: - Pioglitazone (controversial association)

6. Hepatotoxicity: - Monitor LFTs - (Troglitazone withdrawn due to liver failure)

7. Macular edema

Onset of action: Slow (weeks to months)

NEET PG High-Yield: - TZDs cause fluid retention, weight gain, heart failure, bone fractures - Contraindicated in heart failure

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Heart failure (NYHA III-IV) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Liver disease

Relative: - Osteoporosis - Bladder cancer history

docs.title: α-GLUCOSIDASE INHIBITORS
docs.text: Examples: Acarbose, Voglibose, Miglitol

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Inhibit α-glucosidase enzymes in intestinal brush border: - ↓ Breakdown of complex carbohydrates → glucose - ↓ Glucose absorption - ↓ Postprandial hyperglycemia

NEET PG: Acarbose ↓ carbohydrate digestion → ↓ postprandial glucose

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
Type 2 DM: - Postprandial hyperglycemia - Add-on therapy

Prediabetes: - Delay progression

Advantage: - No hypoglycemia (unless with insulin/sulfonylurea) - No weight gain

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. GI disturbances ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Flatulence, diarrhea, abdominal discomfort (very common - 50-70%) - Undigested carbohydrates → colonic fermentation → gas - Limits use

2. ↑ Liver enzymes: - Monitor LFTs

Important: If hypoglycemia occurs (with other drugs), treat with GLUCOSE (NOT sucrose - won't be digested)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Acarbose causes GI side effects (flatulence, diarrhea) - Treat hypoglycemia with glucose (NOT sucrose)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• Inflammatory bowel disease
  • Intestinal obstruction
  • Cirrhosis

docs.title: GLP-1 RECEPTOR AGONISTS (Incretins)
docs.text: Examples: Exenatide, Liraglutide, Dulaglutide, Semaglutide

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Mimic GLP-1 (Glucagon-like peptide-1): 1. ↑ Glucose-dependent insulin secretion (no hypoglycemia) 2. ↓ Glucagon secretion 3. Slow gastric emptying 4. ↑ Satiety (central effect) 5. ↓ Appetite → Weight loss

NEET PG: GLP-1 agonists ↑ insulin, ↓ glucagon, ↓ appetite

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
Type 2 DM: - Add-on therapy - Especially if obese

Obesity (Liraglutide 3 mg, Semaglutide): - Significant weight loss

docs.title: Route
docs.text:
SC injection: - Daily (exenatide, liraglutide) - Weekly (exenatide LAR, dulaglutide, semaglutide)

Oral (Semaglutide): - First oral GLP-1 agonist

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
1. Weight loss ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - 3-5 kg average - Best for weight loss (with SGLT2-i)

2. No hypoglycemia (glucose-dependent)

3. Cardiovascular benefits ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↓ CV events, ↓ mortality (liraglutide, semaglutide) - Proven benefit

NEET PG: GLP-1 agonists cause weight loss, ↓ CV events

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. GI disturbances ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea (very common - 40%) - Improves with time

2. Pancreatitis ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Rare but serious - Contraindicated in pancreatitis history

3. Thyroid C-cell tumors (medullary thyroid cancer): - Animal studies (rats) - Contraindicated in personal/family history of MTC, MEN 2

4. Injection site reactions

NEET PG High-Yield: - GLP-1 agonists cause nausea (very common) - Contraindicated in pancreatitis, MTC/MEN 2 history

docs.title: DPP-4 INHIBITORS (Gliptins)
docs.text: Examples: Sitagliptin, Vildagliptin, Linagliptin, Saxagliptin

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Inhibit DPP-4 (Dipeptidyl peptidase-4): - ↓ Degradation of GLP-1 & GIP (endogenous incretins) - ↑ Incretin levels → ↑ insulin, ↓ glucagon

NEET PG: DPP-4 inhibitors ↑ endogenous GLP-1

docs.title: Uses
docs.text: Type 2 DM: - Monotherapy or combination

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
1. Well tolerated: - Minimal side effects

2. Weight neutral

3. No hypoglycemia

4. Oral (vs GLP-1 agonists - injection)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Nasopharyngitis, headache (mild)

2. Pancreatitis (rare)

3. Heart failure: - Saxagliptin, alogliptin (↑ HF hospitalizations) - Avoid in heart failure

4. Arthralgia (joint pain)

NEET PG: DPP-4 inhibitors well tolerated, some ↑ heart failure risk

docs.title: SGLT-2 INHIBITORS (Gliflozins)
docs.text: Examples: Dapagliflozin, Canagliflozin, Empagliflozin

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Inhibit SGLT-2 (Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2) in proximal tubule: - ↓ Glucose reabsorption from urine - Glycosuria (glucose in urine) - Osmotic diuresis - Insulin-independent

NEET PG: SGLT-2 inhibitors cause glycosuria, osmotic diuresis

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Type 2 DM: - Monotherapy or combination

2. Heart failure ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Heart failure with reduced EF (HFrEF) - ↓ Hospitalizations, ↓ mortality - Proven benefit (even without diabetes)

3. Chronic kidney disease: - Renoprotective (↓ albuminuria, slow progression)

NEET PG High-Yield: SGLT-2 inhibitors proven benefit in heart failure, CKD

docs.title: Advantages
docs.text:
1. Weight loss ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - 2-3 kg (calories lost in urine)

2. ↓ Blood pressure: - Osmotic diuresis

3. No hypoglycemia: - Insulin-independent

4. Cardiovascular benefits ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↓ CV death, HF hospitalizations (empagliflozin, canagliflozin, dapagliflozin)

5. Renal benefits: - ↓ Albuminuria, slow CKD progression

NEET PG: SGLT-2 inhibitors cause weight loss, ↓ BP, CV/renal benefits

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Genital mycotic infections ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Vulvovaginal candidiasis (women) - Balanitis (men) - Most common (10-15%)

2. UTI ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↑ Risk

3. Euglycemic DKA ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - DKA with normal glucose (<200 mg/dl) - Rare but serious - Stop SGLT-2i before surgery

4. Fournier's gangrene: - Necrotizing fasciitis of perineum - Rare but life-threatening

5. Hypotension (volume depletion): - Osmotic diuresis

6. Bone fractures, amputation: - Canagliflozin (controversial)

NEET PG High-Yield: - SGLT-2 inhibitors cause genital infections (most common) - Euglycemic DKA (stop before surgery)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• eGFR <30 (ineffective in severe renal failure)
  • Type 1 DM (↑ DKA risk)
  • Recurrent UTIs

docs.title: Type 1 DM
docs.text: Insulin (basal-bolus)

docs.title: Type 2 DM
docs.text:
First-line: Metformin ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

If metformin contraindicated: - Sulfonylurea (if cost concern) - DPP-4 inhibitor (well tolerated)

Add-on (if HbA1c not at goal): - If ASCVD or high CV risk: GLP-1 agonist or SGLT-2 inhibitor (CV benefit) - If heart failure: SGLT-2 inhibitor (↓ HF hospitalizations) - If CKD: SGLT-2 inhibitor or GLP-1 agonist (renoprotective) - If obesity: GLP-1 agonist or SGLT-2 inhibitor (weight loss) - Otherwise: Sulfonylurea, DPP-4i, TZD, basal insulin

NEET PG: Metformin first-line; if CV disease → GLP-1/SGLT-2; if HF → SGLT-2

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. First-line drug for Type 2 DM? Answer: Metformin

  2. Only insulin that can be given IV? Answer: Regular insulin (and rapid-acting analogs)

  3. Antidiabetic drug causing weight loss? Answer: Metformin, GLP-1 agonists, SGLT-2 inhibitors

  4. Treatment for unconscious hypoglycemia? Answer: IV dextrose or IM glucagon

  5. When to stop metformin before contrast? Answer: 48 hours before

  6. Most common side effect of metformin? Answer: GI disturbances (nausea, diarrhea)

  7. Antidiabetic contraindicated in heart failure? Answer: Thiazolidinediones (TZDs)

  8. Most common side effect of SGLT-2 inhibitors? Answer: Genital mycotic infections

  9. Antidiabetic with proven CV mortality benefit? Answer: GLP-1 agonists (liraglutide, semaglutide), SGLT-2 inhibitors (empagliflozin)

  10. If acarbose user develops hypoglycemia, give? Answer: Glucose (NOT sucrose)

docs.title: Metformin Contraindications (LASH)
docs.text:
• **L**iver failure
  • **A**lcohol abuse
  • **S**evere renal failure (eGFR <30)
  • **H**eart failure (severe)

docs.title: SGLT-2 Inhibitor Side Effects (GEU)
docs.text:
• **G**enital infections (most common)
  • **E**uglycemic DKA
  • **U**TI

docs.title: GLP-1 Benefits (WCV)
docs.text:
• **W**eight loss
  • **C**ardiovascular benefit
  • **V**omiting/nausea (side effect)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know insulin types & duration (rapid, short, intermediate, long)
  • Understand metformin mechanism, advantages, contraindications
  • Learn sulfonylurea adverse effects (hypoglycemia, weight gain)
  • Know SGLT-2 inhibitor benefits & side effects
  • Understand GLP-1 agonist uses (weight loss, CV benefit)
  • Learn when to use which drug (CV disease, HF, obesity)
  • Know treatment for hypoglycemia
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on antidiabetic drugs

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (6-8 questions)

docs.title: NSAIDs & ANALGESICS - NEET PG Study Material
Non-Opioid Analgesics
docs.text:
1. NSAIDs (Non-Selective COX Inhibitors): - Aspirin - Ibuprofen, Naproxen, Diclofenac, Indomethacin - Ketorolac, Piroxicam, Meloxicam

2. Selective COX-2 Inhibitors (Coxibs): - Celecoxib, Etoricoxib, Parecoxib

3. Para-aminophenol Derivative: - Paracetamol (Acetaminophen)

docs.title: Opioid Analgesics
docs.text: • Morphine, Codeine, Fentanyl, Tramadol, Pethidine (Meperidine)

docs.title: Adjuvant Analgesics
docs.text:
• Antidepressants (Amitriptyline)
  • Anticonvulsants (Gabapentin, Pregabalin)

docs.title: Arachidonic Acid Pathway
docs.text:
Membrane Phospholipids
        ↓ [Phospholipase A2]
Arachidonic Acid
        ↓ [Cyclooxygenase (COX)]
Prostaglandin H2 (PGH2)
        ↓
    ↙       ↓       ↘
PGE2    PGI2      TXA2
(Pain,  (Vasodilation, (Platelet
Fever,  Gastric     aggregation,
Inflam) protection) Vasoconstriction)

COX-1: Constitutive (always present) - Gastric protection (mucus, bicarbonate) - Platelet function (TXA2) - Renal blood flow

COX-2: Inducible (inflammation, pain) - Inflammation, pain, fever - Also: Renal function, vascular homeostasis

NEET PG High-Yield: COX-1 = Housekeeping (gastric protection), COX-2 = Inflammation

docs.title: MECHANISM OF NSAIDs
docs.text:
Inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX) → ↓ Prostaglandin synthesis

Effects: 1. Anti-inflammatory (↓ PGE2) 2. Analgesic (↓ PGE2 sensitization of nociceptors) 3. Antipyretic (↓ PGE2 in hypothalamus) 4. Antiplatelet (aspirin - ↓ TXA2)

NEET PG: NSAIDs inhibit COX → ↓ prostaglandins

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Irreversible COX inhibitor (acetylates enzyme): - Non-selective (COX-1 & COX-2) - Antiplatelet effect (↓ TXA2 in platelets) - Platelets cannot synthesize new COX (no nucleus) → Effect lasts 7-10 days (platelet lifespan)

NEET PG: Aspirin = Irreversible COX inhibitor, antiplatelet effect lasts 7-10 days

docs.title: Dose-Dependent Effects
docs.text:
Low dose (75-325 mg/day): - Antiplatelet effect - Preferentially inhibits COX-1 in platelets

Intermediate dose (500-1000 mg): - Analgesic, antipyretic

High dose (4-6 g/day): - Anti-inflammatory

Very high dose (>6 g/day): - Toxic (salicylism)

NEET PG: Low-dose aspirin = Antiplatelet, high-dose = Anti-inflammatory

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Antiplatelet ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Acute MI (chew 325 mg immediately) - Unstable angina, ACS - Secondary prevention (post-MI, post-stroke) - Primary prevention (high CV risk) - Stent thrombosis prevention (with clopidogrel)

2. Acute pain, fever (headache, myalgia)

3. Rheumatic fever: - Anti-inflammatory (high-dose)

4. Kawasaki disease ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - High-dose (anti-inflammatory) → Low-dose (antiplatelet)

5. Pericarditis, pleuritis

NEET PG High-Yield: - Aspirin = DOC for acute MI (antiplatelet) - Kawasaki disease (high→low dose)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. GI toxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Gastritis, peptic ulcer, bleeding - Most common serious effect - Mechanism: ↓ PGE2/PGI2 → ↓ mucus, ↓ bicarbonate, ↓ blood flow - Direct mucosal irritation - Prevention: Enteric-coated, with food, PPI

2. Bleeding ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Antiplatelet effect - ↑ Bleeding time - Stop 7-10 days before surgery - Contraindicated: Hemophilia, active bleeding, peptic ulcer

3. Reye's syndrome ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Acute encephalopathy + hepatotoxicity - Children with viral illness (influenza, varicella) - Contraindicated in children <12 years (especially with fever)

4. Hypersensitivity reactions ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Aspirin-induced asthma (bronchospasm) - Aspirin triad: Asthma + Nasal polyps + Aspirin sensitivity - Urticaria, angioedema

5. Salicylism (chronic toxicity): - Tinnitus, vertigo, headache - Nausea, vomiting - Tinnitus = Early sign

6. Acute salicylate poisoning ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Respiratory alkalosis (initial - direct CNS stimulation) - Metabolic acidosis (later - accumulation of acids) - Hyperthermia (↑ metabolism) - CNS: Confusion, seizures, coma - Treatment: - Gastric lavage (if early) - Activated charcoal - Alkaline diuresis (sodium bicarbonate - ↑ renal excretion) - Hemodialysis (severe)

7. Renal toxicity: - Acute interstitial nephritis - Analgesic nephropathy (chronic use)

8. Uric acid: - Low dose: ↓ Uric acid excretion (↑ uric acid) - High dose: ↑ Uric acid excretion (↓ uric acid)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Aspirin most common = GI toxicity - Reye's syndrome in children (contraindicated <12 years) - Aspirin triad = Asthma + polyps + aspirin sensitivity - Salicylate poisoning = Respiratory alkalosis → metabolic acidosis - Treatment: Alkaline diuresis (sodium bicarbonate)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Children <12 years (with viral illness - Reye's syndrome) - Active peptic ulcer - Hemophilia, bleeding disorders - Severe hepatic/renal impairment - Aspirin-induced asthma - Third trimester pregnancy (premature ductus closure)

NEET PG: Aspirin contraindicated in children (Reye's), peptic ulcer, bleeding disorders

docs.title: Drug Interactions
docs.text:
1. Warfarin: - ↑ Bleeding risk (antiplatelet + anticoagulant) - ↑ Warfarin effect (displaces from protein)

2. Methotrexate: - ↑ Methotrexate toxicity (↓ renal excretion)

3. Antihypertensives: - ↓ Antihypertensive effect (↓ prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation)

docs.title: IBUPROFEN
docs.text:
Properties: - Safest NSAID (GI, CV) - Reversible COX inhibitor - Short half-life

Uses: - Mild-moderate pain, fever - Dysmenorrhea - OTC (over-the-counter)

Adverse effects: - Similar to aspirin but less GI toxicity - No Reye's syndrome (safer in children >6 months)

NEET PG: Ibuprofen = Safest NSAID

docs.title: NAPROXEN
docs.text:
Properties: - Longer half-life (twice daily) - Better GI tolerability than most NSAIDs

Uses: - Arthritis, gout, dysmenorrhea

docs.title: DICLOFENAC
docs.text:
Properties: - Potent anti-inflammatory - Topical preparations available

Uses: - Arthritis, acute pain - Topical: Musculoskeletal pain

Adverse effects: - Higher CV risk than ibuprofen - Hepatotoxicity

NEET PG: Diclofenac available as topical gel

docs.title: INDOMETHACIN
docs.text:
Properties: - Very potent anti-inflammatory - Highest CNS side effects

Uses: - Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) closure ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - In neonates - ↓ PGE2 → ductus constriction - Gout (acute attack) - Ankylosing spondylitis

Adverse effects: - High GI toxicity - CNS: Headache, dizziness (common)

NEET PG High-Yield: Indomethacin = DOC for PDA closure (neonates)

docs.title: KETOROLAC
docs.text:
Properties: - Potent analgesic (comparable to morphine) - Minimal anti-inflammatory - Parenteral (IV/IM) available

Uses: - Postoperative pain ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Opioid-sparing - Acute moderate-severe pain - Renal colic

Duration: ≤5 days (↑ GI/bleeding risk if longer)

Adverse effects: - High bleeding risk - GI toxicity - Renal toxicity

NEET PG: Ketorolac = Potent parenteral NSAID for postoperative pain (max 5 days)

docs.title: PIROXICAM
docs.text:
Properties: - Very long half-life (45 hours) - once daily

Uses: - Chronic arthritis

Adverse effects: - Higher GI toxicity (long half-life)

docs.title: SELECTIVE COX-2 INHIBITORS (Coxibs)
docs.text: Examples: Celecoxib, Etoricoxib, Parecoxib

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Selective COX-2 inhibitors: - Spare COX-1 (gastric protection, platelets) - ↓ GI toxicity - No antiplatelet effect

NEET PG: COX-2 inhibitors spare COX-1 → less GI toxicity, no antiplatelet effect

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Arthritis (osteoarthritis, rheumatoid): - Especially if high GI risk

2. Acute pain: - Postoperative (parecoxib - IV) - Dysmenorrhea

Advantage: - ↓ GI toxicity (vs non-selective NSAIDs) - No effect on platelet function (safe with anticoagulants)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Cardiovascular risk ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - ↑ MI, stroke, thrombosis - Mechanism: ↓ PGI2 (vasodilation, antiplatelet) but NOT TXA2 (prothrombotic) - Rofecoxib withdrawn (2004 - ↑ CV events) - Black box warning

2. Renal toxicity: - Similar to non-selective NSAIDs - COX-2 important for renal function

3. Sulfonamide allergy (celecoxib): - Contains sulfonamide group

NEET PG High-Yield: - COX-2 inhibitors ↑ cardiovascular risk (MI, stroke) - Less GI toxicity than non-selective NSAIDs

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• Post-CABG surgery (↑ CV events)
  • Severe cardiovascular disease
  • Sulfonamide allergy (celecoxib)

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Weak COX inhibitor (mainly in CNS): - Antipyretic, analgesic - NO anti-inflammatory (no peripheral COX inhibition) - Exact mechanism unclear

NEET PG: Paracetamol = Analgesic, antipyretic, NOT anti-inflammatory

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Mild-moderate pain, fever ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - First-line for mild pain - Safest analgesic-antipyretic

2. Osteoarthritis: - First-line (better than NSAIDs for chronic use)

Advantages: - No GI toxicity - No platelet effect - No Reye's syndrome (safe in children) - Safe in asthma - Safe in pregnancy (Category B)

NEET PG: Paracetamol = Safest, first-line for pain/fever

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
Therapeutic doses: - Very safe - Rare: Rash, blood dyscrasias

Overdose ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Hepatotoxicity (potentially fatal) - Toxic dose: >10-15 g (adults), >150 mg/kg (children)

Mechanism of hepatotoxicity: 

Normal dose:
Paracetamol → Glucuronidation/Sulfation (90%)
           → CYP2E1 → NAPQI (toxic) → Glutathione conjugation (safe)

Overdose:
↑ NAPQI formation (saturated conjugation pathways)
↓ Glutathione depletion
→ NAPQI accumulates → Hepatic necrosis

Phases of paracetamol poisoning: 1. 0-24 hours: Nausea, vomiting (mild) 2. 24-72 hours: ↑ AST, ALT, PT (hepatotoxicity) 3. 72-96 hours: Hepatic necrosis, failure (jaundice, encephalopathy) 4. >4 days: Recovery or death

Treatment ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: 1. Gastric lavage (if <1 hour) 2. Activated charcoal (if <4 hours) 3. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) - Antidote - Most effective if given within 8 hours - Still beneficial up to 24 hours - Mechanism: ↑ Glutathione synthesis - IV or oral 4. Supportive care 5. Liver transplant (if fulminant hepatic failure)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Paracetamol overdose = Hepatotoxicity - Antidote = N-acetylcysteine (NAC) - best within 8 hours - NAC increases glutathione

docs.title: 1. GI Toxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Spectrum: Dyspepsia → Gastritis → Peptic ulcer → Bleeding/perforation

Risk factors: - Age >60 - Previous peptic ulcer - High-dose/long-duration - Concurrent steroids, anticoagulants - H. pylori infection

Prevention: - PPI (omeprazole) - most effective - Misoprostol (PGE1 analog) - less tolerated - COX-2 inhibitors (if low CV risk) - Take with food

NEET PG: NSAIDs cause peptic ulcer; prevent with PPI

docs.title: 2. Renal Toxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Mechanisms: - Acute kidney injury (↓ renal blood flow - ↓ PGE2) - Acute interstitial nephritis (hypersensitivity) - Analgesic nephropathy (chronic use - papillary necrosis) - Hyperkalemia (↓ renin, ↓ aldosterone) - Hyponatremia (water retention)

Risk factors: - Volume depletion, heart failure - Pre-existing renal disease - Elderly - Concurrent ACE-I, ARBs, diuretics

NEET PG: NSAIDs cause AKI (↓ renal blood flow), hyperkalemia

docs.title: 3. Cardiovascular Toxicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Effects: - Hypertension (↓ prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation) - Edema (Na⁺/water retention) - ↓ Antihypertensive efficacy - MI, stroke (especially COX-2 inhibitors) - Heart failure exacerbation

NEET PG: NSAIDs ↑ BP, ↓ antihypertensive effect

docs.title: 4. Hypersensitivity ⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Manifestations: - Bronchospasm (aspirin-induced asthma) - Urticaria, angioedema - Anaphylaxis (rare)

Cross-reactivity: Common among NSAIDs

NEET PG: NSAID hypersensitivity = Bronchospasm, urticaria

docs.title: 5. Hematological
docs.text:
• ↑ Bleeding time (antiplatelet effect)
  • Aplastic anemia (rare)

docs.title: 6. Hepatotoxicity
docs.text:
• ↑ Transaminases
  • Rare: Hepatitis

docs.title: MORPHINE - Prototype
docs.text:
Mechanism: μ-opioid receptor agonist (CNS, periphery)

Effects: - Analgesia (strong) - Sedation, euphoria - Respiratory depression - Cough suppression - GI: ↓ Motility (constipation), ↑ sphincter tone - Miosis (pinpoint pupils) - Histamine release (itching, hypotension)

NEET PG: Morphine = μ-opioid agonist, respiratory depression, miosis, constipation

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Severe pain ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Cancer pain - Postoperative pain - Acute MI (↓ pain, anxiety, preload)

2. Acute pulmonary edema: - ↓ Preload, anxiety

3. Diarrhea (codeine, loperamide)

4. Cough (codeine, dextromethorphan)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Respiratory depression ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most serious - Antidote: Naloxone (opioid antagonist)

2. Constipation ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common - No tolerance (persists with chronic use) - Prophylaxis: Laxatives

3. Nausea, vomiting: - CTZ stimulation

4. Addiction, dependence: - Physical & psychological

5. Tolerance: - Develops to analgesia, sedation, respiratory depression - NOT to miosis, constipation

6. Withdrawal syndrome: - Flu-like symptoms, craving, anxiety - NOT life-threatening (vs alcohol, benzodiazepines)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Morphine most serious = Respiratory depression (antidote: naloxone) - Most common = Constipation (no tolerance)

docs.title: TRAMADOL
docs.text:
Mechanism: Dual action - Weak μ-opioid agonist - ↑ Serotonin & norepinephrine (reuptake inhibition)

Uses: - Moderate pain - Neuropathic pain

Advantages: - Less respiratory depression than morphine - Less addiction potential

Adverse effects: - Seizures (lowers seizure threshold) - Serotonin syndrome (with SSRIs, MAOIs) - Nausea

NEET PG: Tramadol = Dual mechanism, seizures, serotonin syndrome

docs.title: NALOXONE
docs.text:
Mechanism: Pure opioid antagonist (competitive)

Uses: - Opioid overdose ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - Respiratory depression - IV/IM/Intranasal - Short half-life (1-2 hours) - may need repeated doses

NEET PG: Naloxone = Opioid antagonist for overdose

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Drug of choice for acute MI (antiplatelet)? Answer: Aspirin (chew 325 mg)

  2. Aspirin contraindicated in children because? Answer: Reye's syndrome risk

  3. Antidote for paracetamol overdose? Answer: N-acetylcysteine (NAC)

  4. NSAID for PDA closure in neonates? Answer: Indomethacin

  5. Safest NSAID for GI? Answer: Ibuprofen (or COX-2 inhibitors)

  6. NSAID safe in pregnancy? Answer: Paracetamol (Category B)

  7. Treatment for aspirin overdose? Answer: Alkaline diuresis (sodium bicarbonate), hemodialysis

  8. Most common adverse effect of morphine? Answer: Constipation

  9. Antidote for opioid overdose? Answer: Naloxone

  10. COX-2 inhibitor main disadvantage? Answer: ↑ Cardiovascular risk (MI, stroke)

docs.title: Aspirin Triad
docs.text: "ASA" (Aspirin Sensitive Asthma) - **A**sthma - **S**ensitivity to aspirin - **A**denomatous polyps (nasal)

docs.title: Paracetamol Overdose Treatment (GCAN)
docs.text:
• **G**astric lavage
  • **C**harcoal (activated)
  • **A**cetylcysteine (N-acetylcysteine)
  • **N**othing else works

docs.title: Morphine Effects (CRAMPS)
docs.text:
• **C**onstipation
  • **R**espiratory depression
  • **A**nalgesia
  • **M**iosis
  • **P**sychological dependence
  • **S**edation

docs.title: Salicylate Poisoning Phases
docs.text: "RAMA" (Respiratory Alkalosis → Metabolic Acidosis)

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know aspirin mechanism (irreversible COX inhibitor)
  • Understand aspirin adverse effects (GI, Reye's, bleeding)
  • Learn paracetamol overdose & treatment (NAC)
  • Know COX-2 inhibitor advantages & disadvantages
  • Understand NSAID adverse effects (GI, renal, CV)
  • Learn morphine effects & adverse effects
  • Know naloxone use (opioid overdose)
  • Understand indomethacin use (PDA closure)
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on NSAIDs & analgesics

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (6-8 questions)

docs.title: ANTICOAGULANTS & ANTIPLATELETS - NEET PG Study Material
Steps of Hemostasis
docs.text:
1. Vascular spasm

2. Platelet plug formation: - Adhesion (vWF, collagen) - Activation - Aggregation (fibrinogen, GPIIb/IIIa)

3. Coagulation cascade: - Extrinsic pathway (Tissue Factor, VII) - Intrinsic pathway (XII, XI, IX, VIII) - Common pathway (X, V, II → Fibrin)

4. Fibrinolysis (Plasmin)

NEET PG: Hemostasis = Platelet plug + Coagulation cascade + Fibrinolysis

docs.title: ANTICOAGULANTS (Act on Coagulation Cascade)
docs.text:
1. Parenteral: - Heparin (Unfractionated heparin - UFH) - Low molecular weight heparins (LMWH): Enoxaparin, Dalteparin - Fondaparinux (Factor Xa inhibitor) - Direct thrombin inhibitors: Bivalirudin, Argatroban, Dabigatran (oral)

2. Oral: - Vitamin K antagonists: Warfarin - Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs): - Direct thrombin inhibitor: Dabigatran - Direct Factor Xa inhibitors: Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Edoxaban

docs.title: ANTIPLATELET DRUGS
docs.text:
1. COX inhibitor: Aspirin

2. ADP receptor (P2Y12) antagonists: - Clopidogrel, Prasugrel, Ticagrelor

3. Phosphodiesterase inhibitors: - Dipyridamole, Cilostazol

4. GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors: - Abciximab, Tirofiban, Eptifibatide

docs.title: THROMBOLYTICS (Fibrinolytics)
docs.text: • Streptokinase, Alteplase (tPA), Tenecteplase

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Activates antithrombin III (AT-III): - AT-III inhibits Factor IIa (thrombin) & Factor Xa - Catalyzes the process (1000x faster)

Effect: Inhibits coagulation (prevents clot formation/extension) - Does NOT dissolve existing clots

NEET PG: Heparin activates AT-III → inhibits IIa (thrombin) & Xa

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text:
Route: IV or SC (NOT oral - large molecule, not absorbed)

Onset: Immediate (IV)

Monitoring: aPTT (activated partial thromboplastin time) - Target: 1.5-2.5× control (60-80 seconds)

Half-life: Short (1-2 hours)

Reversal: Protamine sulfate (binds heparin)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Heparin monitored by aPTT (target 1.5-2.5× control) - Antidote = Protamine sulfate

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Acute thrombosis ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) - Pulmonary embolism (PE) - Initial treatment (bridge to warfarin)

2. Acute coronary syndrome ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Unstable angina, NSTEMI - STEMI (with thrombolysis or PCI)

3. Atrial fibrillation: - Acute (before cardioversion)

4. Cardiopulmonary bypass, dialysis: - Prevent clotting in extracorporeal circuits

5. Pregnancy ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Does NOT cross placenta (large molecule) - Anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy - For DVT/PE, prosthetic valves

NEET PG High-Yield: - Heparin = Acute DVT/PE, ACS - Anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy (doesn't cross placenta)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Bleeding ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common serious effect - Antidote: Protamine sulfate (1 mg neutralizes 100 units heparin)

2. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Type I: Early (1-2 days), mild, transient, benign - Type II ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Immune-mediated (antibodies against heparin-PF4 complex) - 5-10 days after starting heparin - Thrombocytopenia (platelet <150,000 or >50% drop) - Paradoxical THROMBOSIS (venous > arterial) - Life-threatening - Management: - Stop heparin immediately - Alternative anticoagulant: Argatroban, bivalirudin, fondaparinux - NOT warfarin (↑ thrombosis risk initially) - NOT LMWH (cross-reactivity)

3. Osteoporosis: - Long-term use (>6 months) - ↓ Bone density

4. Hyperkalemia: - ↓ Aldosterone secretion

5. Hypersensitivity: - Urticaria, anaphylaxis

NEET PG High-Yield: - HIT Type II = Immune, 5-10 days, thrombocytopenia + thrombosis - Stop heparin, use argatroban (NOT warfarin, NOT LMWH)

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
aPTT (activated partial thromboplastin time): - Intrinsic + common pathway - Target: 1.5-2.5× control

Platelet count: - Monitor for HIT (every 2-3 days)

NEET PG: Monitor heparin with aPTT & platelet count

docs.title: LOW MOLECULAR WEIGHT HEPARINS (LMWH)
docs.text: Examples: Enoxaparin, Dalteparin, Tinzaparin

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Activate AT-III → Inhibit Factor Xa >> IIa (thrombin) - Smaller molecule → Preferentially inhibits Xa

NEET PG: LMWH preferentially inhibits Factor Xa

docs.title: Advantages over UFH
docs.text:
1. Predictable pharmacokinetics: - No monitoring needed (aPTT NOT reliable) - Fixed dosing (weight-based)

2. Subcutaneous (convenient): - Once or twice daily

3. Longer half-life (4-6 hours): - Less frequent dosing

4. Lower risk of HIT (but still possible)

5. Lower risk of osteoporosis

6. **Can be used outpatient

NEET PG: LMWH advantages = No monitoring, SC dosing, less HIT

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. DVT/PE treatment (outpatient): - Instead of UFH + warfarin bridge

2. ACS (NSTEMI, unstable angina)

3. DVT prophylaxis ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Postoperative (orthopedic, general surgery) - Medical patients (hospitalized)

4. Pregnancy: - Safe (doesn't cross placenta)

NEET PG: LMWH = DVT prophylaxis (postoperative), outpatient DVT treatment

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
Usually NOT needed

If needed (renal failure, obesity, pregnancy): - Anti-Xa levels (NOT aPTT) - Target: 0.6-1.0 U/ml (twice daily), 1.0-2.0 U/ml (once daily)

docs.title: Reversal
docs.text:
Partial reversal: Protamine sulfate - Less effective than for UFH (~60%)

Complete reversal: Andexanet alfa (Factor Xa decoy)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Renal failure (severe): - LMWH excreted by kidneys - ↑ Accumulation → bleeding - Prefer UFH (hepatic clearance)

NEET PG: LMWH contraindicated in severe renal failure (use UFH)

docs.title: FONDAPARINUX
docs.text:
Mechanism: Synthetic selective Factor Xa inhibitor - Via AT-III activation

Advantages: - Lowest risk of HIT (no cross-reactivity) - Once daily SC - No monitoring

Uses: - DVT/PE prophylaxis & treatment - HIT (alternative anticoagulant)

Reversal: Difficult (andexanet alfa)

NEET PG: Fondaparinux = Lowest HIT risk, use in HIT

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase: - ↓ Reduction of vitamin K - ↓ Synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X (vitamin K-dependent) - ↓ Protein C & S (anticoagulants)

Effect: Slow onset (2-7 days) - Existing factors must be depleted - Factor VII shortest half-life (6 hours) - affected first

Initial effect: Procoagulant (Protein C depleted first) - Risk of skin necrosis (Protein C deficiency) - Bridge with heparin for 5 days

NEET PG High-Yield: - Warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X) - Slow onset (2-7 days), bridge with heparin

docs.title: Pharmacokinetics
docs.text:
Route: Oral

Onset: Slow (2-7 days)

Monitoring: PT/INR (Prothrombin Time/International Normalized Ratio) - Target INR: 2-3 (most indications), 2.5-3.5 (mechanical valves)

Half-life: Long (40 hours)

Metabolism: Hepatic (CYP2C9)

NEET PG: Warfarin monitored by PT/INR (target 2-3)

docs.title: Clinical Uses
docs.text:
1. Chronic anticoagulation ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - DVT/PE (after heparin bridge, continue 3-6 months) - Atrial fibrillation (stroke prevention) - Mechanical heart valves (lifelong) - Cardiomyopathy (mural thrombus)

NEET PG: Warfarin = Chronic anticoagulation (AF, DVT/PE, mechanical valves)

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Bleeding ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most common serious effect - Reversal (depends on severity): - Minor bleeding + INR <5: ↓ Dose, monitor - INR 5-9, no bleeding: Omit dose, ↓ maintenance dose - Major bleeding or INR >9: - Stop warfarin - Vitamin K (phytonadione): - Oral (5-10 mg) - slow (12-24 hours) - IV (slower than PCC, 6-12 hours) - Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) or Fresh frozen plasma (FFP): - Immediate reversal (contains factors II, VII, IX, X) - PCC preferred over FFP

2. Skin necrosis ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Early (3-8 days) - Protein C deficiency (depleted before procoagulant factors) - Thrombosis of dermal vessels - Risk: Protein C/S deficiency, high loading dose - Prevention: Bridge with heparin, avoid loading dose

3. Teratogenicity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Fetal warfarin syndrome - Nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses, CNS abnormalities - Contraindicated in pregnancy (especially 1st trimester) - Crosses placenta

4. Purple toe syndrome: - Cholesterol microembolization - Rare

NEET PG High-Yield: - Warfarin major bleeding = Stop drug + Vitamin K (IV/oral) + PCC/FFP - Skin necrosis (Protein C deficiency) - bridge with heparin - Teratogenic - contraindicated in pregnancy

docs.title: Drug Interactions (MANY)
docs.text:
↑ Warfarin effect (↑ INR, ↑ bleeding): - Antibiotics: Metronidazole, co-trimoxazole, macrolides - Antifungals: Fluconazole, ketoconazole - NSAIDs, aspirin (also ↑ bleeding independently) - Omeprazole - Amiodarone - Alcohol (acute intoxication) - Cranberry juice

↓ Warfarin effect (↓ INR, ↑ thrombosis): - Rifampin (CYP inducer) - Carbamazepine, phenytoin (CYP inducers) - Vitamin K-rich foods (green leafy vegetables) - Alcohol (chronic use)

NEET PG: Warfarin many drug interactions (antibiotics ↑ effect, rifampin ↓ effect)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Pregnancy (teratogenic) - Active bleeding - Severe liver disease - Recent CNS/eye surgery - Hemorrhagic stroke

Relative: - Falls risk (elderly) - Non-compliance

docs.title: DIRECT ORAL ANTICOAGULANTS (DOACs)
docs.text:
Direct thrombin inhibitor: Dabigatran

Direct Factor Xa inhibitors: Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Edoxaban

docs.title: DABIGATRAN
docs.text:
Mechanism: Direct thrombin (IIa) inhibitor - Reversible, competitive

Route: Oral (prodrug)

Monitoring: NOT required (predictable pharmacokinetics)

Reversal: Idarucizumab (monoclonal antibody - specific antidote)

NEET PG: Dabigatran = Direct thrombin inhibitor, antidote = idarucizumab

docs.title: RIVAROXABAN, APIXABAN, EDOXABAN
docs.text:
Mechanism: Direct Factor Xa inhibitors

Route: Oral

Monitoring: NOT required

Reversal: Andexanet alfa (recombinant Factor Xa decoy) - Also reverses LMWH, fondaparinux

NEET PG: Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban) antidote = andexanet alfa

docs.title: Advantages of DOACs over Warfarin
docs.text:
1. No monitoring (no INR checks)

2. Rapid onset (hours vs days)

3. Predictable dose-response

4. Fewer drug interactions

5. Fewer food interactions (no dietary restrictions)

6. Lower risk of intracranial hemorrhage

7. No bridging with heparin needed

NEET PG: DOACs advantages = No monitoring, rapid onset, fewer interactions

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Atrial fibrillation (stroke prevention) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Non-valvular AF - As effective as warfarin (some superior)

2. DVT/PE (treatment & prevention)

3. VTE prophylaxis (post-orthopedic surgery)

NOT for: - Mechanical heart valves (warfarin superior, dabigatran harmful) - Severe renal failure (CrCl <30) - excreted renally

NEET PG High-Yield: - DOACs for non-valvular AF (NOT mechanical valves) - Contraindicated in severe renal failure

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Bleeding: - Similar to warfarin - Lower intracranial bleeding

2. GI upset (dabigatran): - Dyspepsia common

3. **No specific antidote (except dabigatran, rivaroxaban/apixaban): - Supportive care - Hemodialysis (dabigatran)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
• Severe renal failure (CrCl <30)
  • Mechanical heart valves
  • Active bleeding
  • Pregnancy, breastfeeding

docs.title: ASPIRIN - Covered in NSAIDs
docs.text:
Mechanism: Irreversible COX-1 inhibitor → ↓ TXA2 → ↓ Platelet aggregation

Uses: - Acute MI, ACS - Stroke (secondary prevention) - Primary prevention (high CV risk)

NEET PG: Aspirin = Antiplatelet (irreversible COX inhibitor)

docs.title: CLOPIDOGREL, PRASUGREL, TICAGRELOR
docs.text:
Mechanism: P2Y12 receptor antagonists (ADP receptor) - Block ADP-induced platelet aggregation

Clopidogrel, Prasugrel: Prodrugs (irreversible) Ticagrelor: Active drug (reversible)

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Acute coronary syndrome ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT): Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor - Minimum 12 months post-ACS/stent

2. Post-PCI (percutaneous coronary intervention): - Stent thrombosis prevention

3. Stroke (clopidogrel): - Secondary prevention (alternative to aspirin)

4. Peripheral arterial disease

NEET PG High-Yield: - DAPT = Aspirin + Clopidogrel/Ticagrelor (post-ACS, post-stent) - Duration: 12 months minimum

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Bleeding

2. Clopidogrel: - TTP (thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura) - rare

3. Ticagrelor: - Dyspnea (10-15%) - Bradycardia

4. Drug interaction: - PPIs (omeprazole) ↓ clopidogrel activation (CYP2C19 inhibition) - Use pantoprazole instead

NEET PG: Omeprazole ↓ clopidogrel effect (CYP interaction)

docs.title: DIPYRIDAMOLE
docs.text:
Mechanism: - Phosphodiesterase inhibitor → ↑ cAMP → ↓ platelet aggregation - Coronary vasodilator

Uses: - Stroke prevention (with aspirin) - Pharmacologic stress test (coronary vasodilation)

Adverse effect: Headache, hypotension

NEET PG: Dipyridamole for stroke prevention, stress test

docs.title: GPIIb/IIIa INHIBITORS
docs.text:
Examples: Abciximab, Tirofiban, Eptifibatide

Mechanism: Block GPIIb/IIIa receptor (final common pathway) - Prevent fibrinogen binding - Most potent antiplatelet

Route: IV only

Uses: - High-risk ACS (with PCI) - Acute STEMI (with PCI)

Adverse effect: Bleeding, thrombocytopenia

NEET PG: GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors = Most potent antiplatelet, IV, for high-risk ACS

docs.title: THROMBOLYTICS (Fibrinolytics)
docs.text: Examples: Streptokinase, Alteplase (tPA), Tenecteplase, Reteplase

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Activate plasminogen → plasmin: - Plasmin degrades fibrin → Dissolves clot

NEET PG: Thrombolytics activate plasminogen → plasmin → dissolves clot

docs.title: Uses
docs.text:
1. Acute STEMI ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Within 12 hours of symptom onset - If PCI not available within 90-120 min

2. Acute ischemic stroke ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Alteplase (tPA) - Within 4.5 hours - After excluding hemorrhagic stroke (CT scan)

3. Massive pulmonary embolism: - Hemodynamically unstable

4. Acute DVT (extensive, limb-threatening)

NEET PG High-Yield: - Thrombolytics for STEMI (<12 hours), stroke (<4.5 hours) - Alteplase (tPA) for stroke

docs.title: STREPTOKINASE
docs.text:
Source: Streptococcus (bacterial)

Properties: - Antigenic (antibodies develop) - Cannot repeat within 6 months - 1 year - Allergic reactions common

Cheap, widely used (developing countries)

NEET PG: Streptokinase antigenic, cannot repeat within 6 months

docs.title: ALTEPLASE (tPA - Tissue Plasminogen Activator)
docs.text:
Source: Recombinant human

Properties: - Non-antigenic - Fibrin-specific (more clot-selective) - More effective than streptokinase

Uses: - Preferred for stroke - STEMI (if available)

Expensive

NEET PG: Alteplase = tPA, preferred for stroke, fibrin-specific

docs.title: Adverse Effects
docs.text:
1. Bleeding ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Most serious - Intracranial hemorrhage (1-2% in stroke) - Antidote: - Stop drug - Tranexamic acid or Aminocaproic acid (antifibrinolytics) - FFP, cryoprecipitate

2. Reperfusion arrhythmias: - VT, VF (transient)

3. Allergic reactions (streptokinase): - Fever, rash, anaphylaxis

NEET PG: Thrombolytics main risk = Bleeding (intracranial hemorrhage)

docs.title: Contraindications
docs.text:
Absolute: - Active bleeding - Recent stroke (<3 months) - Recent surgery/trauma (<2 weeks) - Intracranial pathology (tumor, AVM, aneurysm) - Hemorrhagic stroke (any time) - Aortic dissection

Relative: - Uncontrolled hypertension (>180/110) - Recent GI bleeding - Pregnancy

NEET PG: Thrombolytics contraindicated in active bleeding, recent surgery/stroke

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Antidote for heparin overdose? Answer: Protamine sulfate

  2. Monitoring for heparin? Answer: aPTT (target 1.5-2.5× control)

  3. Monitoring for warfarin? Answer: PT/INR (target 2-3)

  4. Antidote for warfarin overdose with major bleeding? Answer: Vitamin K + PCC (or FFP)

  5. Anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy? Answer: Heparin or LMWH (don't cross placenta)

  6. HIT type II management? Answer: Stop heparin, use argatroban/fondaparinux

  7. Antidote for dabigatran? Answer: Idarucizumab

  8. Antidote for rivaroxaban/apixaban? Answer: Andexanet alfa

  9. DAPT (dual antiplatelet therapy) includes? Answer: Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel/ticagrelor)

  10. Thrombolytic for acute stroke? Answer: Alteplase (tPA) - within 4.5 hours

docs.title: Warfarin Reversal (VPF)
docs.text:
• **V**itamin K
  • **P**CC (Prothrombin Complex Concentrate)
  • **F**FP (Fresh Frozen Plasma)

docs.title: HIT Management (SAF)
docs.text:
• **S**top heparin
  • **A**rgatroban (alternative anticoagulant)
  • **F**ondaparinux (alternative)

docs.title: Vitamin K-Dependent Factors (1972)
docs.text: • Factors II, VII, IX, X (and Protein C, S)

docs.title: DOAC Antidotes
docs.text:
• **Dab**igatran → **I**darucizumab (both start with vowels)
  • Xa inhibitors → **A**ndexanet alfa (both have 'a')

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know heparin mechanism, monitoring (aPTT), antidote
  • Understand HIT (type II) - management
  • Learn LMWH advantages over UFH
  • Know warfarin mechanism, monitoring (INR), reversal
  • Understand warfarin adverse effects (bleeding, skin necrosis, teratogenic)
  • Learn DOAC advantages, antidotes
  • Know antiplatelet drugs (DAPT = aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor)
  • Understand thrombolytic uses (STEMI, stroke) & contraindications
  • Practice 50+ MCQs on anticoagulants & antiplatelets

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (6-8 questions)

docs.title: By Source
docs.text:
1. Natural (from adrenal cortex): - Glucocorticoids: Cortisol (Hydrocortisone) - Mineralocorticoids: Aldosterone

2. Synthetic: - Glucocorticoids: Prednisolone, Methylprednisolone, Dexamethasone, Betamethasone - Mineralocorticoids: Fludrocortisone

docs.title: By Duration of Action
docs.text:
Short-acting (8-12 hours): - Hydrocortisone (Cortisol)

Intermediate-acting (12-36 hours): - Prednisolone, Prednisone, Methylprednisolone

Long-acting (36-72 hours): - Dexamethasone, Betamethasone

NEET PG: Dexamethasone = Longest-acting glucocorticoid

docs.title: By Potency
docs.text:
Drug Glucocorticoid Potency Mineralocorticoid Activity Half-life Cortisol (Hydrocortisone) 1 1 8-12 hr Prednisolone 4 0.8 12-36 hr Methylprednisolone 5 0.5 12-36 hr Dexamethasone 25-30 0 36-72 hr Betamethasone 25-30 0 36-72 hr Fludrocortisone 10 125 -

NEET PG High-Yield: - Dexamethasone = Highest glucocorticoid potency, NO mineralocorticoid effect - Fludrocortisone = Highest mineralocorticoid activity

docs.title: Glucocorticoid Receptor (GR)
docs.text:
Genomic effects (slow, hours): 1. Steroid diffuses into cell (lipophilic) 2. Binds to glucocorticoid receptor (cytoplasmic) 3. Translocates to nucleus 4. Transactivation: ↑ Gene transcription (anti-inflammatory proteins) 5. Transrepression: ↓ Gene transcription (pro-inflammatory cytokines)

Non-genomic effects (rapid, minutes): - Membrane effects

NEET PG: Glucocorticoids act via nuclear receptors → gene transcription

docs.title: 1. Anti-inflammatory ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Mechanisms: - ↓ Phospholipase A2 (↓ arachidonic acid → ↓ prostaglandins, leukotrienes) - ↓ Pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-2, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ) - ↓ COX-2 expression - ↓ Vascular permeability, edema - ↓ Leukocyte migration (neutrophils, monocytes) - ↑ Anti-inflammatory proteins (lipocortin, IL-10)

NEET PG: Glucocorticoids ↓ phospholipase A2, ↓ cytokines

docs.title: 2. Immunosuppressive ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Effects: - ↓ T-cell function (↓ IL-2) - ↓ B-cell function (↓ antibody production) - Lymphopenia (redistribution) - ↓ Cell-mediated immunity - Thymic involution

NEET PG: Glucocorticoids suppress cell-mediated immunity

docs.title: 3. Metabolic Effects
docs.text:
Carbohydrate metabolism: - ↑ Gluconeogenesis (liver) - ↓ Glucose uptake (peripheral tissues) - Insulin resistance - → Hyperglycemia ("steroid diabetes")

Protein metabolism: - ↑ Protein catabolism (muscle) - → Muscle wasting, negative nitrogen balance

Fat metabolism: - Lipolysis (limbs) - Lipogenesis (face, trunk) - → Central obesity (buffalo hump, moon facies)

NEET PG: Glucocorticoids cause hyperglycemia, muscle wasting, central obesity

docs.title: 4. Bone & Calcium
docs.text:
• ↓ Bone formation (↓ osteoblast activity)
  • ↑ Bone resorption (↓ osteoclast apoptosis)
  • ↓ Ca²⁺ absorption (intestine)
  • ↑ Ca²⁺ excretion (kidney)
  • → Osteoporosis

NEET PG: Glucocorticoids cause osteoporosis (↓ bone formation, ↓ Ca²⁺ absorption)

docs.title: 5. Cardiovascular
docs.text:
• Permissive effect on catecholamines (↑ responsiveness)
  • ↑ Blood pressure (mineralocorticoid effect)

docs.title: 6. CNS
docs.text:
• Mood elevation (euphoria)
  • Insomnia
  • Psychosis (high dose)

docs.title: 7. Hematological
docs.text:
• ↑ Neutrophils (demargination, ↓ apoptosis)
  • ↓ Lymphocytes, eosinophils, basophils (redistribution, apoptosis)
  • ↑ RBCs, platelets

NEET PG: Glucocorticoids cause neutrophilia, lymphopenia, eosinopenia

docs.title: 8. GI
docs.text:
• ↑ Gastric acid, pepsin secretion
  • ↓ Mucus production
  • → ↑ Risk of peptic ulcer

docs.title: 9. Fetal Lung Maturation ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
• ↑ Surfactant production
  • Prevent respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) in preterm infants

NEET PG: Antenatal steroids for fetal lung maturity

docs.title: 1. Replacement Therapy ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Adrenal insufficiency: - Primary (Addison's disease): Hydrocortisone + Fludrocortisone - Secondary (pituitary): Hydrocortisone alone

Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH): - Hydrocortisone + Fludrocortisone

NEET PG: Addison's disease = Hydrocortisone + Fludrocortisone

docs.title: 2. Anti-inflammatory/Immunosuppressive
docs.text:
Allergic disorders: - Asthma (inhaled/systemic) - Anaphylaxis (adjunct to epinephrine) - Allergic rhinitis (topical) - Urticaria, angioedema

Autoimmune disorders: - SLE (systemic lupus) - Rheumatoid arthritis - Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's, UC) - Autoimmune hemolytic anemia - ITP (immune thrombocytopenic purpura)

Organ transplantation: - Prevent rejection (with other immunosuppressants)

Dermatological: - Eczema, psoriasis (topical) - Pemphigus, pemphigoid

Ocular: - Uveitis, optic neuritis (topical/systemic)

Renal: - Nephrotic syndrome (minimal change disease)

Respiratory: - COPD exacerbation - Pulmonary fibrosis - Sarcoidosis

NEET PG: Glucocorticoids used in asthma, SLE, RA, IBD, nephrotic syndrome

docs.title: 3. Cerebral Edema ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Dexamethasone: - Brain tumors (↓ vasogenic edema) - Bacterial meningitis (↓ inflammation)

NOT for: Head injury, stroke (not effective)

NEET PG: Dexamethasone for cerebral edema (brain tumor), bacterial meningitis

docs.title: 4. Hematological Malignancies
docs.text:
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL): - Lympholytic effect

Multiple myeloma, lymphoma: - Part of chemotherapy regimens

docs.title: 5. Fetal Lung Maturity ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
Antenatal corticosteroids: - Betamethasone or Dexamethasone (IM) - Between 24-34 weeks gestation - Risk of preterm delivery within 7 days - ↓ Respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) - ↓ Intraventricular hemorrhage, neonatal death

NEET PG High-Yield: - Antenatal steroids = Betamethasone/Dexamethasone (24-34 weeks) - Prevent RDS in preterm infants

docs.title: 6. Other Uses
docs.text:
Diagnostic: - Dexamethasone suppression test (Cushing's syndrome)

Hypercalcemia: - ↓ Ca²⁺ (vitamin D toxicity, sarcoidosis)

Chemotherapy-induced nausea (antiemetic)

Laryngeal edema (croup, post-extubation)

docs.title: Short-term Use (<3 weeks)
docs.text:
• Hyperglycemia
  • GI upset, peptic ulcer
  • Insomnia, mood changes
  • Acne
  • Increased appetite, weight gain

docs.title: Long-term Use (>3 weeks) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
docs.text:
1. Cushing's syndrome (iatrogenic): - Moon facies (round face) - Buffalo hump (fat deposition - upper back) - Central obesity (truncal) - Purple striae (abdomen) - Skin thinning, easy bruising - Hirsutism, acne

2. Metabolic: - Hyperglycemia, diabetes mellitus - Dyslipidemia - Negative nitrogen balance

3. Musculoskeletal ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Osteoporosis (most serious long-term effect) - Fractures (vertebral, hip) - Prevention: Calcium + Vitamin D, bisphosphonates - Myopathy (proximal muscle weakness) - Avascular necrosis (femoral head)

4. Cardiovascular: - Hypertension - Atherosclerosis

5. Ophthalmic ⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Posterior subcapsular cataract - Glaucoma (↑ IOP)

6. Immunosuppression: - ↑ Infection risk - Opportunistic infections (TB, fungal) - Impaired wound healing - Mask infection signs (no fever)

7. GI: - Peptic ulcer, GI bleeding

8. Psychiatric: - Psychosis, depression, mania - Insomnia

9. Growth retardation (children): - ↓ Growth hormone effect

10. HPA axis suppression ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - Adrenal suppression - Cannot respond to stress - Risk of adrenal crisis (infection, surgery, trauma)

11. Skin: - Thinning, striae, bruising - Poor wound healing

NEET PG High-Yield: - Long-term glucocorticoids = Cushing's, osteoporosis, HPA suppression - Most serious = Osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, immunosuppression

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Normal HPA axis: 

Hypothalamus (CRH)
    ↓
Pituitary (ACTH)
    ↓
Adrenal cortex (Cortisol)
    ↓
Negative feedback

Exogenous glucocorticoids: - Suppress CRH & ACTH (negative feedback) - Adrenal atrophy (lack of ACTH stimulation) - Cannot produce endogenous cortisol

Risk factors: - Duration >3 weeks (any dose) - High dose (even short duration) - Evening dosing (↑ suppression)

docs.title: Clinical Significance
docs.text:
Adrenal insufficiency if: - Sudden withdrawal (acute adrenal crisis) - Stress (infection, surgery, trauma) - cannot mount cortisol response

Symptoms of adrenal crisis: - Hypotension, shock - Hypoglycemia - Fever - Abdominal pain - Confusion

NEET PG: HPA suppression = Cannot mount stress response, risk of adrenal crisis

docs.title: Prevention & Management
docs.text:
1. Alternate-day therapy: - Single morning dose every 48 hours (intermediate-acting) - Allows HPA axis recovery on off-days - ↓ Suppression, ↓ adverse effects

2. Morning dosing: - Mimics physiological rhythm - ↓ Suppression vs evening dosing

3. Gradual tapering ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - NEVER stop abruptly (>3 weeks use) - Taper over weeks to months - Allows adrenal recovery

4. Stress dose coverage ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐: - During illness, surgery, trauma - Increase dose 2-3× or IV hydrocortisone - Prevent adrenal crisis

NEET PG High-Yield: - Glucocorticoids >3 weeks = Taper gradually (NEVER stop abruptly) - Stress dose coverage during illness/surgery

docs.title: CONTRAINDICATIONS
docs.text:
Relative (weigh risk-benefit): - Systemic fungal infection (absolute for immunosuppression) - Peptic ulcer disease - Diabetes mellitus - Osteoporosis - Psychosis - Live vaccines (avoid during immunosuppression) - Viral infections (herpes, varicella)

Note: Often benefits outweigh risks in acute situations

NEET PG: Live vaccines contraindicated during glucocorticoid therapy

docs.title: DRUG INTERACTIONS
docs.text:
CYP3A4 inducers (↓ glucocorticoid effect): - Rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine - → May need ↑ dose

CYP3A4 inhibitors (↑ glucocorticoid effect): - Ketoconazole, ritonavir - → Risk of toxicity

docs.title: Inhaled Corticosteroids (ICS)
docs.text:
Examples: Beclomethasone, Budesonide, Fluticasone

Uses: - Asthma (controller medication) - COPD

Advantages: - Topical effect (lungs) - Minimal systemic absorption (↓ adverse effects)

Adverse effects: - Oropharyngeal candidiasis (thrush) - common - Prevention: Rinse mouth after use, spacer - Hoarseness, cough - Growth retardation (children - high dose)

NEET PG: ICS cause oral thrush - prevent by rinsing mouth

docs.title: Topical Corticosteroids
docs.text:
Uses: - Eczema, psoriasis, dermatitis

Adverse effects (prolonged use): - Skin atrophy, telangiectasia - Hypopigmentation - Striae - Systemic absorption (if large area, occlusive dressing)

Potency classification: - Low: Hydrocortisone - Medium: Triamcinolone - High: Betamethasone - Very high: Clobetasol

NEET PG: Topical steroids cause skin atrophy (prolonged use)

docs.title: FLUDROCORTISONE
docs.text:
Properties: - Highest mineralocorticoid activity - Weak glucocorticoid activity

Mechanism: - Binds mineralocorticoid receptor (kidney) - ↑ Na⁺ reabsorption, ↑ K⁺ excretion - ↑ Water retention, ↑ blood volume

Uses: - Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ - With hydrocortisone - Congenital adrenal hyperplasia - Orthostatic hypotension

Adverse effects: - Hypertension - Hypokalemia - Edema

NEET PG: Fludrocortisone = Mineralocorticoid for Addison's disease

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Most potent glucocorticoid? Answer: Dexamethasone (25-30× cortisol)

  2. Glucocorticoid for cerebral edema? Answer: Dexamethasone

  3. Antenatal corticosteroid for fetal lung maturity? Answer: Betamethasone or Dexamethasone (24-34 weeks)

  4. Most common adverse effect of long-term glucocorticoids? Answer: Osteoporosis (also Cushing's features)

  5. Most serious long-term complication? Answer: HPA axis suppression, osteoporosis, immunosuppression

  6. How to prevent HPA suppression? Answer: Alternate-day therapy, morning dosing, gradual taper

  7. Treatment for Addison's disease? Answer: Hydrocortisone + Fludrocortisone

  8. Adverse effect of inhaled corticosteroids? Answer: Oropharyngeal candidiasis (thrush)

  9. How to prevent oral thrush with ICS? Answer: Rinse mouth after use, use spacer

  10. Why taper glucocorticoids gradually? Answer: Prevent adrenal crisis (HPA suppression)

docs.title: Cushing's Features (HOMBAS)
docs.text:
• **H**ypertension, Hirsutism
  • **O**besity (central), Osteoporosis
  • **M**oon facies, Myopathy
  • **B**uffalo hump, Bruising
  • **A**bdominal striae
  • **S**kin thinning, Striae

docs.title: Glucocorticoid Adverse Effects (CUSHINGOID)
docs.text:
• **C**ataract
  • **U**lcer (peptic)
  • **S**kin thinning, Striae
  • **H**ypertension, Hyperglycemia
  • **I**nfections (↑ risk)
  • **N**ecrosis (avascular - femoral head)
  • **G**rowth retardation
  • **O**steoporosis
  • **I**nsomnia, Immunosuppression
  • **D**iabetes

docs.title: HPA Suppression Risk (3-3-3)
docs.text:
• >3 weeks duration
  • 3× physiologic dose
  • Need to taper for 3× duration of use

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Know glucocorticoid potencies (dexamethasone highest)
  • Understand mechanism (genomic & non-genomic)
  • Learn pharmacological effects (anti-inflammatory, immunosuppressive, metabolic)
  • Know clinical uses (replacement, inflammatory, fetal lung maturity)
  • Understand adverse effects (Cushing's, osteoporosis, HPA suppression)
  • Learn HPA axis suppression (taper gradually, stress dose)
  • Know inhaled corticosteroids (thrush prevention)
  • Understand fludrocortisone use (Addison's disease)
  • Practice 40+ MCQs on corticosteroids

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (5-7 questions)

docs.title: RENAL PHYSIOLOGY - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Kidneys regulate: - Fluid & electrolyte balance - Acid-base balance - Blood pressure - RBC production (EPO) - Vitamin D activation - Excretion of waste products

docs.title: Nephron Structure
docs.text:
Glomerulus → PCT → Loop of Henle → DCT → Collecting Duct
               ↓         ↓           ↓          ↓
            65-70%     15-20%      5-10%      5-10%
          (Na+ reabs) (Concen)  (Fine-tune) (ADH/Aldo)

Types of Nephrons: 1. Cortical (85%): Short loop of Henle 2. Juxtamedullary (15%): Long loop, important for concentration

docs.title: Renal Blood Flow (RBF)
docs.text:
Normal RBF: 1200 ml/min (20-25% of cardiac output) Renal Plasma Flow (RPF): 600-700 ml/min

Formula: RBF = CO × 0.20-0.25

Autoregulation: Maintains constant RBF despite BP changes (80-180 mmHg)

Mechanisms: 1. Myogenic: Stretch of afferent arteriole → vasoconstriction 2. Tubuloglomerular feedback: ↑NaCl at macula densa → ↓GFR

NEET PG: Kidney has best autoregulation among all organs

docs.title: Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR)
docs.text: Normal GFR: 125 ml/min or 180 L/day Filtration Fraction (FF): GFR/RPF = 125/600 = 20%

docs.title: Starling Forces
docs.text:
Favoring Filtration: - Glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure (PGC) = 60 mmHg (main driving force)

Opposing Filtration: - Bowman's capsule hydrostatic pressure (PBS) = 18 mmHg - Glomerular capillary oncotic pressure (πGC) = 32 mmHg

Net Filtration Pressure = PGC - (PBS + πGC) = 60 - (18 + 32) = 10 mmHg

GFR = Kf × Net Filtration Pressure

docs.title: Factors Affecting GFR
docs.text:
Factor Effect on GFR Mechanism ↑ Afferent arteriole dilation ↑ ↑ PGC ↑ Efferent arteriole constriction ↑ ↑ PGC ↓ Plasma proteins ↑ ↓ πGC Urinary obstruction ↓ ↑ PBS ↓ Renal blood flow ↓ ↓ PGC ACE inhibitors ↓ Dilate efferent (↓ PGC) NSAIDs ↓ Constrict afferent (↓ PGC)

NEET PG: - Angiotensin II: Constricts efferent > afferent → Maintains GFR - ACE inhibitors: Dilate efferent → ↓ GFR (contraindicated in bilateral RAS)

docs.title: Inulin Clearance (Gold Standard)
docs.text:
• Freely filtered
  • Not reabsorbed or secreted
  • GFR = Urine [Inulin] × Urine flow / Plasma [Inulin]

docs.title: Creatinine Clearance (Clinical)
docs.text:
• Slightly overestimates GFR (small amount secreted by PCT)
  • Normal: 90-140 ml/min

Cockcroft-Gault Formula: 

CrCl = [(140 - Age) × Weight in kg] / (72 × Serum Cr)
       × 0.85 (for females)

NEET PG: Creatinine clearance overestimates true GFR (secreted in PCT)

docs.title: PROXIMAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE (PCT)
docs.text:
Reabsorbs 65-70% of filtered load

Substances Reabsorbed: - Na+ (65-70%): Na+/K+ ATPase (basolateral) - Water (65-70%): Follows Na+ (osmosis via aquaporin-1) - Glucose (100%): SGLT2 (sodium-glucose cotransporter 2) - Amino acids (100%) - Bicarbonate (80-90%): Via carbonic anhydrase - Phosphate, uric acid, K+

Substances Secreted: - Organic acids: Uric acid, drugs (penicillin, probenecid) - Organic bases: Creatinine - H+ ions

NEET PG High-Yield: - Glucose Tm (Transport maximum) = 375 mg/min - Renal threshold for glucose = 180 mg/dl - SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins): Block glucose reabsorption → glycosuria

docs.title: Descending Limb
docs.text:
• Permeable to water (aquaporin-1)
  • Impermeable to NaCl
  • Water reabsorbed → Urine becomes concentrated

docs.title: Thin Ascending Limb
docs.text:
• Permeable to NaCl
  • Impermeable to water

docs.title: Thick Ascending Limb (TAL)
docs.text:
• Impermeable to water ("diluting segment")
  • Active Na+ reabsorption via Na-K-2Cl cotransporter (NKCC2)
  • Reabsorbs 15-20% of filtered Na+
  • Creates medullary hypertonicity (countercurrent multiplier)

NEET PG: - Loop diuretics (furosemide): Block NKCC2 in TAL - TAL impermeable to water → "Diluting segment"

docs.title: DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE (DCT)
docs.text:
Reabsorbs 5-10% of filtered Na+

Mechanisms: - Na-Cl cotransporter (NCC) - Impermeable to water - Ca2+ reabsorption (stimulated by PTH)

NEET PG: - Thiazide diuretics: Block NCC in DCT - Site of Ca2+ reabsorption (PTH-dependent) - Gitelman syndrome: Defect in NCC

docs.title: COLLECTING DUCT
docs.text: Site of fine-tuning (reabsorbs 5-10% Na+)

docs.title: Principal Cells
docs.text:
Function: Na+ reabsorption, K+ secretion, water reabsorption

Transporters: - ENaC (Epithelial Na+ Channel): Na+ reabsorption - ROMK: K+ secretion - Aquaporin-2 (AQP2): Water reabsorption (ADH-dependent)

NEET PG: - Aldosterone: ↑ ENaC → ↑ Na+ reabsorption, ↑ K+ secretion - ADH (Vasopressin): Inserts AQP2 → ↑ water reabsorption - Amiloride: Blocks ENaC (K+ sparing diuretic)

docs.title: Intercalated Cells
docs.text:
Function: Acid-base balance

Type A (in acidosis): - Secrete H+ (via H+-ATPase) - Reabsorb HCO3-

Type B (in alkalosis): - Secrete HCO3- - Reabsorb H+

docs.title: Countercurrent Multiplier (Loop of Henle)
docs.text:
Function: Creates medullary hypertonicity (300 → 1200 mOsm/kg)

Mechanism: 1. TAL actively pumps out NaCl (NKCC2) 2. Water cannot follow (impermeable) 3. Interstitium becomes hypertonic 4. Descending limb loses water to interstitium 5. Urine becomes concentrated

NEET PG: Loop diuretics abolish countercurrent multiplier

docs.title: Countercurrent Exchanger (Vasa Recta)
docs.text: Function: Maintains medullary hypertonicity - Minimizes washout of solutes from medullary interstitium

docs.title: Maximum Urine Concentration
docs.text: With ADH: 1200 mOsm/kg (4× plasma) - Collecting duct permeable to water (AQP2 inserted) - Water reabsorbed into hypertonic medulla - Small volume, concentrated urine

docs.title: Maximum Urine Dilution
docs.text:
Without ADH: 50 mOsm/kg - Collecting duct impermeable to water - Water not reabsorbed - Large volume, dilute urine

NEET PG: Urine can be 24× diluted (50 mOsm) but only 4× concentrated (1200 mOsm)

docs.title: 1. ADH (Vasopressin)
docs.text:
Source: Posterior pituitary Stimulus: ↑ Plasma osmolality (>280 mOsm), ↓ Blood volume

Mechanism: - Inserts aquaporin-2 in collecting duct - ↑ Water reabsorption - Concentrated urine

NEET PG: - Central DI: No ADH production - Nephrogenic DI: Kidneys don't respond to ADH - SIADH: Excess ADH → Hyponatremia

docs.title: 2. Aldosterone
docs.text:
Source: Adrenal cortex (zona glomerulosa) Stimulus: ↑ Angiotensin II, ↑ K+, ↓ Na+

Mechanism: - ↑ ENaC in collecting duct - ↑ Na+ reabsorption, ↑ K+ secretion - ↑ Blood pressure

NEET PG: - Primary hyperaldosteronism: Hypertension, hypokalemia - Conn's syndrome: Aldosterone-secreting adenoma

docs.title: 3. PTH (Parathyroid Hormone)
docs.text: Effects on Kidney: - ↑ Ca2+ reabsorption (DCT) - ↓ Phosphate reabsorption (PCT) - ↑ 1α-hydroxylase → Active vitamin D

docs.title: 4. ANP (Atrial Natriuretic Peptide)
docs.text:
Source: Atrial myocytes Stimulus: ↑ Atrial stretch (volume overload)

Effects: - ↑ GFR (dilates afferent, constricts efferent) - ↓ Na+ reabsorption - ↓ Renin, ↓ Aldosterone - ↓ ADH - Net: Natriuresis & diuresis

docs.title: Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS)
docs.text:
↓ BP, ↓ Na+, ↑ Sympathetic
         ↓
   Juxtaglomerular cells
         ↓
      RENIN
         ↓
Angiotensinogen → Angiotensin I
                      ↓ (ACE in lungs)
                 Angiotensin II
         ┌────────┴────────┐
    Vasoconstriction   Aldosterone
    (↑ BP)             (↑ Na+, ↑ BP)

Effects of Angiotensin II: 1. Systemic vasoconstriction 2. Efferent > afferent arteriole constriction (maintains GFR) 3. ↑ Aldosterone secretion 4. ↑ ADH secretion 5. ↑ Proximal tubule Na+ reabsorption 6. ↑ Thirst

NEET PG: - ACE inhibitors: Block formation of Angiotensin II - ARBs: Block Angiotensin II receptors - Both cause: ↓ BP, ↑ K+ (less aldosterone), ↓ GFR

docs.title: Reabsorption
docs.text:
• PCT: 65% (paracellular)
  • TAL: 25% (via NKCC2)
  • DCT: 5%

docs.title: Secretion
docs.text:
• Collecting duct: Principal cells (via ROMK)
  • Regulated by aldosterone

Factors ↑ K+ Secretion: - ↑ Aldosterone - ↑ Tubular flow rate - ↑ Plasma K+ - Metabolic alkalosis

NEET PG: - Loop diuretics: ↑ K+ loss (↑ flow to collecting duct) - Aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone): Hyperkalemia

docs.title: Calcium
docs.text:
Reabsorption: - PCT: 60-70% (paracellular) - TAL: 20% (paracellular) - DCT: 10% (PTH-stimulated, active transport)

NEET PG: - Thiazides: ↑ Ca2+ reabsorption → Hypocalciuria - Loop diuretics: ↓ Ca2+ reabsorption → Hypercalciuria

docs.title: Phosphate
docs.text:
Reabsorption: - PCT: 80% (via Na-Pi cotransporter) - Inhibited by PTH & FGF-23

NEET PG: PTH causes phosphaturia (↓ phosphate reabsorption)

docs.title: H+ Secretion
docs.text:
• PCT: 80-90% HCO3- reabsorption (via carbonic anhydrase)
  • Collecting duct: H+ secretion (type A intercalated cells)

docs.title: HCO3- Reabsorption
docs.text:
Tubular lumen    |    Cell    |    Blood
                 |            |
H+ + HCO3- → H2CO3 → CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3-
                 |  (CA)      |   (CA)    |
                 |            |           → HCO3- reabsorbed

NEET PG: Acetazolamide inhibits carbonic anhydrase → ↓ HCO3- reabsorption → Alkaline urine

docs.title: Clearance Concepts - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Clearance: Volume of plasma completely cleared of a substance per unit time

Formula: C = (U × V) / P - U = Urine concentration - V = Urine flow rate - P = Plasma concentration

docs.title: Renal Handling
docs.text:
Substance Clearance vs GFR Handling Inulin C = GFR Filtered only Glucose C = 0 Filtered, 100% reabsorbed PAH C > GFR Filtered + secreted Urea C < GFR Filtered, partially reabsorbed Creatinine C ≈ GFR (slight >) Filtered, small secretion

NEET PG: - PAH clearance = Renal plasma flow (RPF) when fully secreted - Inulin clearance = GFR (gold standard)

docs.title: Diuretics - Quick Reference
docs.text: Diuretic Site Mechanism Effects Acetazolamide PCT ↓ Carbonic anhydrase Alkaline urine, ↓ HCO3- Loop (Furosemide) TAL Block NKCC2 Hypokalemia, hypocalcemia, metabolic alkalosis Thiazides DCT Block NCC Hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, metabolic alkalosis K-sparing (Amiloride) CD Block ENaC Hyperkalemia Spironolactone CD Block aldosterone Hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Normal GFR? Answer: 125 ml/min

  2. Filtration fraction? Answer: 20% (GFR/RPF)

  3. Maximum glucose reabsorption occurs at? Answer: PCT (via SGLT2)

  4. Diluting segment of nephron? Answer: Thick ascending limb

  5. ADH acts on which aquaporin? Answer: Aquaporin-2 (collecting duct)

  6. Loop diuretics block which transporter? Answer: NKCC2 (Na-K-2Cl)

  7. Site of PTH action for Ca2+ reabsorption? Answer: DCT

  8. Gold standard for GFR measurement? Answer: Inulin clearance

docs.title: PCT Reabsorption
docs.text: "PCT Gets All the Good Stuff" - 65-70% of Na+, water - 100% glucose, amino acids - 80-90% HCO3-

docs.title: Diuretics Order (Proximal to Distal)
docs.text: "A**ce **L**oopy **T**hieves **S**pared **K**idneys" - **A**cetazolamide (PCT) - **L**oop diuretics (TAL) - **T**hiazides (DCT) - **S**pironolactone (CD) - **K-sparing (CD)

docs.title: ADH Effects
docs.text: "ADH Adds H2O" - Inserts AQP2 → Water reabsorption

docs.title: Revision Checklist
docs.text:
• Understand GFR and Starling forces
  • Memorize normal values (GFR, RBF, RPF, FF)
  • Know functions of each nephron segment
  • Learn diuretic sites of action
  • Understand RAAS pathway
  • Know ADH and aldosterone mechanisms
  • Learn clearance concepts
  • Understand countercurrent mechanism
  • Practice calculations (CrCl, clearance)
  • Solve 40+ MCQs on renal physiology

Difficulty Level: High-Yield ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ NEET PG Weightage: Very High (expect 4-6 questions)

docs.title: RESPIRATORY PHYSIOLOGY - NEET PG Study Material
Overview
docs.text: Functions of Respiratory System: 1. Gas exchange (O2 and CO2) 2. Acid-base balance (CO2 excretion) 3. Phonation (voice production) 4. Olfaction (smell) 5. Heat & water loss regulation 6. Metabolism (ACE in lungs)

docs.title: Volumes (cannot be subdivided)
docs.text:
Volume Definition Value Measured by Tidal Volume (TV) Volume inspired/expired in normal breathing 500 ml Spirometry Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) Extra volume that can be inspired 3000 ml Spirometry Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) Extra volume that can be expired 1100 ml Spirometry Residual Volume (RV) Volume remaining after maximal expiration 1200 ml Body plethysmography

NEET PG: RV cannot be measured by spirometry (requires body plethysmography or helium dilution)

docs.title: Capacities (sum of 2 or more volumes)
docs.text:
Capacity Formula Value Significance Inspiratory Capacity (IC) TV + IRV 3500 ml Maximum inspiration from FRC Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) ERV + RV 2300 ml Lung volume at end of normal expiration Vital Capacity (VC) TV + IRV + ERV 4600 ml Maximum breath (inspiration → expiration) Total Lung Capacity (TLC) VC + RV (or IC + FRC) 5800 ml Total lung volume

NEET PG High-Yield: - FRC = ERV + RV: Most important capacity (gas exchange occurs here) - VC: Decreased in restrictive lung disease - RV/TLC ratio: Increased in obstructive lung disease

docs.title: Spirometry Values
docs.text:
Forced Vital Capacity (FVC): Maximum volume expired forcefully after max inspiration FEV1 (Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 sec): Volume expired in first second of FVC FEV1/FVC ratio: Normally >70% (0.7)

NEET PG: - Obstructive disease: ↓ FEV1/FVC (<70%) - Restrictive disease: Normal or ↑ FEV1/FVC, ↓ FVC

docs.title: Anatomical Dead Space
docs.text: Definition: Air in conducting airways (no gas exchange) Volume: 150 ml (≈ 2 ml/kg) Includes: Nose, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, bronchioles (up to terminal bronchioles)

docs.title: Alveolar Dead Space
docs.text: Definition: Ventilated alveoli with no/poor perfusion Normal: Negligible (≈ 0) Increased in: Pulmonary embolism, shock

docs.title: Physiological Dead Space
docs.text:
Definition: Anatomical dead space + Alveolar dead space Formula: VD/VT = (PaCO2 - PECO2) / PaCO2 (Bohr equation)

NEET PG: - Anatomical dead space: 150 ml (⅓ of tidal volume) - Alveolar ventilation = (TV - Dead space) × RR = (500-150) × 12 = 4200 ml/min

docs.title: Minute Ventilation
docs.text: Definition: Total volume breathed per minute Formula: VE = TV × RR = 500 × 12 = 6000 ml/min (6 L/min)

docs.title: Alveolar Ventilation
docs.text:
Definition: Volume reaching alveoli per minute (effective ventilation) Formula: VA = (TV - VD) × RR = (500-150) × 12 = 4200 ml/min

NEET PG: Alveolar ventilation more important than minute ventilation

docs.title: Inspiratory Muscles
docs.text:
Primary: - Diaphragm (most important - 75% of inspiration) - Nerve: Phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5) - Contraction → Descends → ↑ Vertical diameter - External intercostals - Elevate ribs → ↑ AP & transverse diameter

Accessory (in dyspnea): - Sternocleidomastoid - Scalenes - Pectoralis major/minor

NEET PG: "C3, 4, 5 keep the diaphragm alive" (phrenic nerve)

docs.title: Expiratory Muscles
docs.text:
Normal breathing: Passive (elastic recoil)

Active expiration: - Internal intercostals: Depress ribs - Abdominal muscles: Compress abdomen, push diaphragm up

docs.title: Compliance (C)
docs.text:
Definition: Change in volume per unit change in pressure Formula: C = ΔV / ΔP Normal: 200 ml/cm H2O

Increased compliance (floppy lungs): - Emphysema (loss of elastic tissue) - Easy to inflate, difficult to deflate

Decreased compliance (stiff lungs): - Pulmonary fibrosis - Pulmonary edema - ARDS - Difficult to inflate

NEET PG: Emphysema = ↑ Compliance, Fibrosis = ↓ Compliance

docs.title: Elastance
docs.text: Definition: Reciprocal of compliance (E = 1/C) Increased in: Pulmonary fibrosis (stiff lungs)

docs.title: SURFACTANT - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Produced by: Type II pneumocytes Composition: 90% lipid (dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine - DPPC), 10% protein

Functions: 1. ↓ Surface tension in alveoli 2. Prevents alveolar collapse (especially small alveoli) 3. Increases compliance 4. Prevents pulmonary edema (opposes transudation)

Laplace's Law: P = 2T/r - P = Pressure inside alveolus - T = Surface tension - r = Radius

Without surfactant: Smaller alveoli collapse (higher pressure needed) With surfactant: Surface tension ↓ more in small alveoli → Equalizes pressure

NEET PG High-Yield: - Surfactant deficiency: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (NRDS) - Treatment: Exogenous surfactant (beractant, poractant) - L/S ratio <2: Immature lungs (risk of NRDS) - Corticosteroids (betamethasone): Accelerate surfactant production

docs.title: VENTILATION-PERFUSION (V/Q) RATIO
docs.text:
Definition: Ratio of alveolar ventilation to pulmonary blood flow

Normal V/Q ratio: 0.8 (V = 4 L/min, Q = 5 L/min)

docs.title: Regional Variation
docs.text:
Apex: - High V/Q ratio (≈3) - Good ventilation, poor perfusion - High PO2, low PCO2 - Site for TB (Mycobacterium likes high O2)

Base: - Low V/Q ratio (≈0.6) - Good perfusion, better than ventilation - Low PO2, high PCO2

NEET PG: V/Q mismatch most common cause of hypoxemia

docs.title: Extreme V/Q States
docs.text:
Condition V/Q Ratio Cause Example Dead space ∞ (V, no Q) No perfusion Pulmonary embolism Shunt 0 (Q, no V) No ventilation Atelectasis, ARDS

Shunt: - Anatomical: Bronchial veins, Thebesian veins (2% of CO) - Physiological: Unventilated but perfused alveoli

NEET PG: - 100% O2 test: Hypoxemia due to shunt does NOT correct with 100% O2 - V/Q mismatch: Hypoxemia corrects with 100% O2

docs.title: Oxygen Transport
docs.text:
Forms: 1. Dissolved in plasma: 0.3 ml/100 ml blood (1.5%) 2. Bound to hemoglobin: 19.7 ml/100 ml blood (98.5%)

Total O2 content: 20 ml/100 ml (20 vol%)

Oxygen carrying capacity = Hb (g/dl) × 1.34 ml O2/g Hb O2 content = (Hb × 1.34 × SaO2) + (0.003 × PaO2)

NEET PG: Each gram of Hb binds 1.34 ml O2 when fully saturated

docs.title: Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve - HIGH YIELD
docs.text:
Shape: Sigmoid (S-shaped) P50: PO2 at which Hb is 50% saturated = 27 mmHg

Plateau portion (PO2 >60 mmHg): - Large changes in PO2 → Small changes in SaO2 - Protects against hypoxemia (high altitude)

Steep portion (PO2 <60 mmHg): - Small changes in PO2 → Large O2 release to tissues

docs.title: Right Shift (↓ O2 affinity, ↑ O2 delivery to tissues)
docs.text:
Mnemonic: "**R**ight shift = **R**elease O2 to tissues"

Causes (CADET, face Right): - **C**O2 ↑ (hypercapnia) - **A**cid ↑ (↓ pH) - **D**PG (2,3-DPG) ↑ - **E**xercise - **T**emperature ↑

NEET PG: ↑ 2,3-DPG in chronic hypoxia, high altitude

docs.title: Left Shift (↑ O2 affinity, ↓ O2 delivery)
docs.text:
Causes: - ↓ Temperature (hypothermia) - ↓ CO2, ↓ H+ (alkalosis) - ↓ 2,3-DPG - Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) - Carboxyhemoglobin (CO poisoning) - Methemoglobin

NEET PG: - Fetal Hb: Left shift (higher O2 affinity - extracts O2 from maternal blood) - CO poisoning: Left shift + ↓ O2 capacity (binds 200× more than O2)

docs.title: Bohr Effect
docs.text:
Definition: ↑ CO2 & ↓ pH → ↓ O2 affinity (right shift) Mechanism: CO2 + H+ → Allosteric change in Hb → ↓ O2 binding Significance: Facilitates O2 unloading in tissues (↑ CO2, ↓ pH)

NEET PG: Bohr effect helps O2 delivery to metabolically active tissues

docs.title: Haldane Effect
docs.text:
Definition: Deoxygenated Hb binds more CO2 than oxygenated Hb Mechanism: Deoxygenated Hb is a better buffer for H+ Significance: Facilitates CO2 loading in tissues & unloading in lungs

NEET PG: Haldane effect explains 70% of CO2 transport difference between arterial & venous blood

docs.title: Carbon Dioxide Transport
docs.text:
Forms: 1. Dissolved in plasma: 7% (0.6 mmol/L per mmHg) 2. Carbamino compounds (bound to Hb): 23% 3. Bicarbonate (HCO3-): 70% (most important)

In RBCs: 

CO2 + H2O ⇌ H2CO3 ⇌ H+ + HCO3-
   (Carbonic anhydrase)

Chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon): - HCO3- diffuses out of RBC → Cl- enters (maintain electroneutrality) - Occurs in tissues (CO2 loading) - Reversed in lungs

NEET PG: - 70% CO2 transported as HCO3- - Carbonic anhydrase: In RBCs, NOT in plasma - Chloride shift: Maintains electrical neutrality

docs.title: Medulla (Dorsal & Ventral Groups)
docs.text: Function: Basic rhythm of respiration - Dorsal respiratory group (DRG): Inspiration (stimulates phrenic nerve) - Ventral respiratory group (VRG): Expiration (active expiration)

docs.title: Pons
docs.text:
Pneumotaxic center: Limits inspiration (controls respiratory rate) Apneustic center: Prolongs inspiration

NEET PG: Medulla = Basic rhythm, Pons = Fine-tuning

docs.title: Central Chemoreceptors (Medulla)
docs.text:
Location: Ventral surface of medulla Stimulus: ↑ H+ in CSF (↑ CO2 crosses BBB → H2CO3 → H+) Response: ↑ Ventilation Contribution: 70-80% of hypercapnic drive

NEET PG: Central chemoreceptors respond to CO2 (via H+), NOT O2

docs.title: Peripheral Chemoreceptors
docs.text:
Location: - Carotid bodies (bifurcation of common carotid) - CN IX - Aortic bodies (arch of aorta) - CN X

Stimuli: 1. ↓ PO2 (most sensitive) - primary O2 sensor 2. ↑ PCO2 3. ↓ pH

Response: ↑ Ventilation Contribution: 30% of hypercapnic drive, 100% of hypoxic drive

NEET PG High-Yield: - Only peripheral chemoreceptors respond to hypoxemia - Carotid bodies: More sensitive to hypoxia than aortic bodies - Chronic hypercapnia (COPD): Lose central chemoreceptor sensitivity → Hypoxic drive predominates

docs.title: Other Receptors
docs.text:
Stretch receptors (Hering-Breuer reflex): - In airway smooth muscle - Inflation → Inhibit inspiration (prevents over-inflation)

Irritant receptors: - Rapid shallow breathing, cough, bronchoconstriction

J receptors (Juxtacapillary): - In alveolar walls - Stimulated by: Pulmonary edema, PE, pneumonia - Response: Rapid shallow breathing (tachypnea)

NEET PG: J receptors → Rapid shallow breathing in pulmonary edema

docs.title: HYPOXIA - TYPES
docs.text:
Type PaO2 CaO2 SaO2 Cause Example Hypoxic ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ Inspired O2, hypoventilation, V/Q mismatch, diffusion defect High altitude, COPD Anemic Normal ↓ Normal ↓ Hemoglobin Anemia Stagnant Normal Normal Normal ↓ Blood flow Shock, CHF Histotoxic Normal Normal Normal Cells can't use O2 Cyanide poisoning

NEET PG: - Hypoxic hypoxia: Only type with ↓ PaO2 - Anemic hypoxia: Normal PaO2 but ↓ O2 content - Cyanide poisoning: Inhibits cytochrome oxidase (can't use O2)

docs.title: Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS)
docs.text: Altitude: >2500m (8000 ft) Time: 6-12 hours after ascent Symptoms: Headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue, insomnia

docs.title: High Altitude Pulmonary Edema (HAPE)
docs.text: Mechanism: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction → ↑ Pulmonary artery pressure → Edema Features: Dyspnea, cough, frothy sputum Treatment: Descent, O2, nifedipine

docs.title: High Altitude Cerebral Edema (HACE)
docs.text:
Mechanism: Hypoxia → ↑ Cerebral blood flow → Edema Features: Ataxia, confusion, altered sensorium Treatment: Descent, O2, dexamethasone

NEET PG: Acetazolamide prevents AMS (causes metabolic acidosis → stimulates breathing)

docs.title: Acclimatization to High Altitude
docs.text:
Immediate: - ↑ Ventilation (hyperventilation) - ↑ Heart rate

Days-Weeks: - ↑ 2,3-DPG (right shift → ↑ O2 delivery) - ↑ Erythropoietin → Polycythemia - ↑ Capillary density

Months: - ↑ Mitochondria - ↑ Myoglobin

NEET PG: ↑ 2,3-DPG and polycythemia = Key adaptations

docs.title: Nitrogen Narcosis
docs.text: Cause: ↑ Nitrogen dissolves in tissues (high pressure) Depth: >30m (100 ft) Symptoms: Euphoria, impaired judgment ("rapture of the deep")

docs.title: Decompression Sickness (Bends)
docs.text:
Cause: Rapid ascent → Nitrogen bubbles form Features: - Type I: Joint pain, skin rash - Type II: CNS (paralysis), pulmonary (chokes) Treatment: Recompression (hyperbaric O2)

NEET PG: Decompression sickness prevented by slow ascent

docs.title: Practice MCQs
docs.text:
1. Anatomical dead space volume? Answer: 150 ml

  2. Alveolar ventilation formula? Answer: (TV - VD) × RR

  3. Normal V/Q ratio? Answer: 0.8

  4. What produces surfactant? Answer: Type II pneumocytes

  5. P50 of oxygen-hemoglobin curve? Answer: 27 mmHg

  6. Most common form of CO2 transport? Answer: Bicarbonate (70%)

  7. Primary stimulus for central chemoreceptors? Answer: ↑ H+ (from CO2)

  8. Only chemoreceptor responding to hypoxemia? Answer: Peripheral chemoreceptors

docs.title: Right Shift O2-Hb Curve
docs.text: "CADET, face **R**ight" - **C**O2 ↑ - **A**cid ↑ - **D**PG ↑ - **E**xercise - **T**emperature ↑

docs.title: Phrenic Nerve
docs.text: "C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive"

docs.title: CO2 Transport
docs.text: "70-23-7" - 70%: Bicarbonate - 23%: Carbamino - 7%: Dissolved

docs.title: V/Q Ratio
